topics.title: Science and Technology Behind Micro-Robot - Definition of Microrobot and Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Science and Technology Behind Micro-Robot - Definition of Microrobot and Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Microrobot

A **microrobot** is an engineered, programmable mechanical-electromechanical device whose overall characteristic dimensions lie in the range of **1 micrometer (µm) to a few millimeters (mm)**, capable of sensing, actuating, computing, and/or performing autonomous tasks at sub-millimeter scales.

From a strict MEMS/NEMS classification standpoint (per the KTU 2024 PERAT746 syllabus), microrobots are categorized as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:**
> *"A microrobot is a robotic system whose maximum characteristic dimension is less than **10 mm**, and whose manufacturing and operational principles are dominated by surface-area effects, surface-tension forces, viscous damping, and elastic continuum mechanics at the micro-scale."*

The key dimensional classification is:

| Class | Characteristic Size | Dominant Physics |
|---|---|---|
| Nanorobot | < 1 µm | Quantum, molecular |
| Microrobot | 1 µm – 1 mm | Surface tension, viscous drag, elastic deformation |
| Milli-robot | 1 mm – 10 mm | Mixed micro/macro |
| Minirobot | 10 mm – 100 mm | Macro-mechanics dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> A microrobot is **NOT** simply a "scaled-down" macro-robot. As size shrinks, **surface forces (F ∝ L²) dominate over volume/inertial forces (F ∝ L³)**. This is the foundation of "**scaling laws**" introduced by **Nakamura & Nakazawa (1993)** and later formalized by **Freund (2000)** in the context of MEMS.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ant Colony" Intuition

Imagine an ant carrying a leaf 50 times its own body weight. To a macro-observer, the ant looks impossibly strong. But this is *not* because ant muscles are magically powerful — it is because the ant lives in a world where **adhesion, capillary forces, and surface friction are enormous compared to its body weight**.

A microrobot lives in exactly such a world.

- A **macro-robot** (size ~ 0.5 m) is governed by **gravity and inertia**.
- A **microrobot** (size ~ 0.5 mm) is governed by **van der Waals forces, surface tension, electrostatic forces, and viscous drag**.

Because of this, the **structural parts of a microrobot (beams, plates, hinges, flexures) deform elastically** under extremely small forces. We therefore **cannot** analyze a microrobot using rigid-body mechanics alone — we **must** apply **linear elasticity theory** to predict its mechanical behavior. This is why **Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity** is the very first technical topic of this course.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants the KTU Examiner Expects You to Recall:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
> - Surface tension of water (at 20°C): **γ ≈ 0.072 N/m**
> - Dynamic viscosity of air (at 20°C): **μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20°C): **μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Young's modulus of silicon: **E_Si ≈ 169 GPa**
> - Young's modulus of SU-8 polymer: **E_SU8 ≈ 4.0 GPa**
> - Young's modulus of PDMS: **E_PDMS ≈ 0.5 – 3.0 MPa**

### 1.3 Why Linear Elasticity? The Bridge to Microrobot Design

Every flexible structural element of a microrobot (a cantilever beam tip, a micro-gripper finger, a swimming-flagellum rod, a walking-leg flexure) behaves as a **continuum body obeying Hooke's Law** in its linear regime. The two foundational scalar/vector/tensor quantities are:

1. **Stress (σ)** — internal force per unit area, units **Pa = N/m²**.
2. **Strain (ε)** — normalized deformation, **dimensionless**.

The proportionality between them is given by **Hooke's Law**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooke's Law (Uniaxial Form):**
> $$\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$$
> where **E** is the **Young's Modulus** (modulus of elasticity), measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.

Linear elasticity is the **mathematical framework** that predicts how a microrobot structure bends, twists, stretches, and vibrates — which directly determines its **locomotion, manipulation, and sensing performance**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Continuum Elasticity

For a deformable solid in the linear elastic regime, **three field equations** must be satisfied everywhere in the body:

#### (A) Equilibrium Equations (Newton's 2nd Law for continua)

For a body in static equilibrium with body force $f_i$ (per unit volume), the stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ must satisfy:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j} + f_i = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, 3
$$

- The **why**: A microrobot beam is rarely truly static; even quasi-static deflection satisfies this balance.
- The **how**: We sum forces on an infinitesimal cube and require the resultant to vanish.

#### (B) Kinematic / Strain-Displacement Equations (Compatibility)

The small-strain tensor $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is defined as the symmetric part of the displacement gradient:

$$
\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)
$$

- The **why**: We must link *what the material is doing* (displacement $\vec{u}$) to *how it is being stretched* (strain).
- The **how**: For a 1D bar of original length $L$ stretched to $L + \Delta L$: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$.

#### (C) Constitutive Equations (Material Law)

For a **linear, isotropic** elastic solid, the generalized Hooke's law is:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

where $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are the **Lamé constants**, and $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta. This equation, plus the previous two, is the **complete formulation of linear elastostatics**.

### 2.2 The Three Elastic Moduli — Engineering Interpretation

| Modulus | Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value (Silicon) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Resistance to uniaxial tension/compression | **169 GPa** |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ (or $\mu$) | Resistance to shape change (shear) | **65 GPa** |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | Resistance to uniform volumetric compression | **98 GPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | Lateral contraction under axial stretch | **0.28** |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for PERAT746 Module 1. Memorize the form, the units, and the inter-relationships.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma_n = F_n / A$ | $F_n$ = normal force, $A$ = area | Pa |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = F_t / A$ | $F_t$ = tangential force | Pa |
| 3 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | $\Delta L$ = elongation | dimensionless |
| 4 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \Delta x / h$ | $\Delta x$ = lateral shift, $h$ = thickness | rad |
| 5 | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | Pa |
| 6 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axl}}$ | $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}, \varepsilon_{\text{axl}}$ are lateral and axial strains | dimensionless |
| 7 | Generalized Hooke's Law (isotropic) | $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \varepsilon_{ij}$ | $\lambda, \mu$ = Lamé constants | Pa |
| 8 | Lamé → Young's Relation | $E = \mu(3\lambda + 2\mu) / (\lambda + \mu)$ | conversion | Pa |
| 9 | Lamé → Bulk Relation | $K = \lambda + \tfrac{2}{3}\mu$ | conversion | Pa |
| 10 | Lamé → Poisson Relation | $\nu = \lambda / \bigl[2(\lambda + \mu)\bigr]$ | conversion | dimensionless |
| 11 | Shear–Young–Poisson | $G = E / \bigl[2(1+\nu)\bigr]$ | conversion | Pa |
| 12 | 3D Hooke's Law (direct form) | $\varepsilon_{xx} = (1/E)\bigl[\sigma_{xx} - \nu(\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})\bigr]$ | full 3D expansion | dimensionless |
| 13 | 2D Plane Stress → 1D Effective E | $E' = E / (1 - \nu^2)$ | thin plate, $\sigma_{zz} = 0$ | Pa |
| 14 | Strain Energy Density | $U_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}$ | recoverable elastic energy per volume | J/m³ |
| 15 | Thermal Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = CTE, $\Delta T$ = temperature change | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> In all KTU board examinations, when the problem says "thin plate" or "membrane", immediately use **Plane Stress** with the effective modulus $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$. When it says "long cylinder" or "thick block" with constrained lateral strain, use **Plane Strain** with effective modulus $E'' = E\bigl[(1-\nu)/(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)\bigr]$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Microrobot Engineering

- **Cantilever beams** in AFM tips, micro-grippers, and inertia sensors are designed using $E I$ (flexural rigidity) and $\delta = PL^3 / (3EI)$ tip-deflection formulas.
- **Comb drives** in MEMS rely on in-plane stiffness $k = E t (w/L)^3$.
- **Micro-swimmer flagella** must remain flexible ($\delta/L$ high) yet elastic-recoverable — this requires low $E$ materials (PDMS) or geometric compliance.
- **Walking microrobot legs** are designed as **compliant flexures** — their deflection must be elastic (recoverable) under the gait-cycle load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **most common 14-mark derivations** in KTU ESE papers. Master them line by line.

### 3.1 Derivation I — Generalized Hooke's Law for an Isotropic Material

**Goal:** Starting from the most general 4th-order stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$, prove that for an **isotropic** material, only **two independent constants** (e.g., $\lambda$ and $\mu$) are needed.

#### Step 1 — General Linear Constitutive Law

For a linear elastic material (obeying the superposition principle), the most general stress-strain relationship is:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}
$$

The stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ has, in principle, $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 81$ components.

#### Step 2 — Apply Physical Symmetries

**(a) Stress symmetry** (conservation of angular momentum): $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$, so:

$$
C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl}
$$

**(b) Strain symmetry** (definition of small-strain tensor): $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$, so:

$$
C_{ijkl} = C_{ijlk}
$$

This reduces 81 components to **21 independent components** (the monoclinic case).

#### Step 3 — Apply Isotropic Material Symmetry

For a material with **no preferred direction** (isotropic), the stiffness tensor must be invariant under **any rotation** of the coordinate system. The only 4th-order isotropic tensor built from $\delta_{ij}$ and the identity is:

$$
C_{ijkl} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \delta_{kl} + \mu \, \delta_{ik} \, \delta_{jl} + \mu' \, \delta_{il} \, \delta_{jk}
$$

But symmetry $C_{ijkl} = C_{ijlk}$ forces $\mu = \mu'$, leaving only **two independent constants** $\lambda$ and $\mu$.

#### Step 4 — Substitute Into the General Law

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \bigl(\lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \delta_{kl} + \mu \, \delta_{ik} \, \delta_{jl} + \mu \, \delta_{il} \, \delta_{jk}\bigr) \varepsilon_{kl}
$$

Using the sifting property of the Kronecker delta $\delta_{kl}\varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{kk}$:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

This is the **generalized Hooke's Law for isotropic linear elastic materials**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Navier's Equations of Elastostatics

**Goal:** Combine equilibrium, kinematics, and constitutive law to obtain the displacement form.

#### Step 1 — Start with Equilibrium

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j} + f_i = 0
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Generalized Hooke's Law

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\bigl(\lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}\bigr) + f_i = 0
$$

Since $\lambda, \mu$ are constants (homogeneous material), we can move the derivative inside:

$$
\lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \frac{\partial \varepsilon_{kk}}{\partial x_j} + 2\mu \, \frac{\partial \varepsilon_{ij}}{\partial x_j} + f_i = 0
$$

Note that $\delta_{ij}\partial_j = \partial_i$, so:

$$
\lambda \, \frac{\partial \varepsilon_{kk}}{\partial x_i} + 2\mu \, \frac{\partial \varepsilon_{ij}}{\partial x_j} + f_i = 0
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute Strain-Displacement Relations

Replace $\varepsilon_{kk}$ with $\partial u_k / \partial x_k$ (the divergence of $\vec{u}$), and $\varepsilon_{ij}$ with the symmetric gradient:

$$
\lambda \, \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left(\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial x_k}\right) + 2\mu \, \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)\right] + f_i = 0
$$

#### Step 4 — Recognize the Vector Operators

The first term contains $\partial_i (\nabla \cdot \vec{u})$ and the second term contains the vector Laplacian $\nabla^2 u_i$ plus $\partial_i(\nabla \cdot \vec{u})$:

$$
\lambda \, \partial_i(\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) + \mu \, \nabla^2 u_i + \mu \, \partial_i(\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) + f_i = 0
$$

#### Step 5 — Collect Terms

$$
(\lambda + \mu) \, \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}(\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) + \mu \, \nabla^2 u_i + f_i = 0
$$

This is **Navier's equation of elastostatics** in vector form. In explicit index notation:

$$
\boxed{\mu \, \nabla^2 u_i + (\lambda + \mu) \, \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left(\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial x_k}\right) + f_i = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, 3}
$$

This single vector equation (or three scalar equations) is the **governing PDE for the displacement field** in any isotropic linear elastic body — including every beam, plate, and shell of a microrobot. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation III — 2D Stress Transformation (Principal Stresses)

**Goal:** Given a 2D stress state with $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$, find the normal stress on a plane rotated by angle $\theta$.

#### Step 1 — Define the Rotated Element

Consider a stress element rotated counter-clockwise by angle $\theta$ from the original $(x, y)$ axes. The new axes are $(x', y')$.

#### Step 2 — Force Balance on the Rotated Face

The normal stress on the $x'$-face of the rotated element is the projection of the traction vectors on the $x$- and $y$-faces onto the new $x'$-direction:

$$
\sigma_{x'} = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Double-Angle Identities

Using $\cos^2\theta = (1 + \cos 2\theta)/2$, $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$
\sigma_{x'} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

#### Step 4 — Find the Principal Direction (Maximum Normal Stress)

Take $\partial \sigma_{x'} / \partial \theta = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{x'}}{\partial \theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute Back to Get Principal Stresses

Using $\sin 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/R$ and $\cos 2\theta_p = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/R$ where $R = \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering application in microrobotics:** When designing a micro-cantilever with a known load, the principal-stress formula tells you the **maximum tensile stress** in the beam — which must remain below the **fracture strength of silicon (≈ 1–7 GPa)** to prevent failure.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Thermal Strain and Thermal Stress in a Constrained Beam

**Goal:** A micro-beam of length $L$ is constrained between two walls. If the temperature rises by $\Delta T$, what is the induced thermal stress?

#### Step 1 — Free Thermal Expansion

If unconstrained, the beam would expand by:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

The free thermal strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_T = \frac{\Delta L_{\text{free}}}{L} = \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Constraint

The walls prevent the expansion. By superposition, the **mechanical strain** must cancel the thermal strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} + \varepsilon_T = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = -\alpha \Delta T
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law for the Mechanical Stress

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \, \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = -E \alpha \Delta T
$$

The negative sign indicates **compressive** thermal stress. This is critical in **MEMS packaging**, where differential thermal expansion between silicon (α ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K) and the package (e.g., Kovar, α ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁶ /K) can induce **thermally-induced warpage or fracture** of the microrobot structure. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Microrobots (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram maps the taxonomy of microrobots by their **functional architecture** — locomotion principle, energy source, and application domain.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["MICROROBOT TAXONOMY"]
    rootA --> classB["BY DIMENSION"]
    rootA --> locoC["BY LOCOMOTION"]
    rootA --> applD["BY APPLICATION"]

    classB --> nanoE["NANOROBOT less than 1 micrometer"]
    classB --> microF["MICROROBOT 1 micrometer to 1 mm"]
    classB --> milliG["MILLI ROBOT 1 mm to 10 mm"]

    locoC --> swimH["SWIMMERS flagella and cilia based"]
    locoC --> crawlI["CRAWLERS surface adhesion based"]
    locoC --> walkJ["WALKERS compliant leg flexures"]
    locoC --> flyK["FLIERS electrostatic or magnetic field"]

    applD --> medL["MEDICAL drug delivery and surgery"]
    applD --> indM["INDUSTRIAL inspection and assembly"]
    applD --> envN["ENVIRONMENTAL sensing and remediation"]
    applD --> milP["DEFENSE reconnaissance and rescue"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — State of Stress at a Point (Cauchy's Stress Tensor)

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram represents the **3D stress element** at a point in a continuum. Each face carries a normal stress and two shear stresses. The full stress state requires the 2nd-order tensor $\sigma_{ij}$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["STRESS STATE AT A POINT"]
    inA --> normB["NORMAL STRESSES"]
    inA --> shearC["SHEAR STRESSES"]
    inA --> tensD["STRESS TENSOR sigma_ij"]

    normB --> nB1["sigma_xx axial X"]
    normB --> nB2["sigma_yy axial Y"]
    normB --> nB3["sigma_zz axial Z"]

    shearC --> sC1["tau_xy in XY plane"]
    shearC --> sC2["tau_yz in YZ plane"]
    shearC --> sC3["tau_xz in XZ plane"]

    tensD --> tD1["Symmetric 3x3 matrix"]
    tensD --> tD2["9 components total"]
    tensD --> tD3["6 independent due to symmetry"]
    tensD --> tD4["Governed by Hooke law and equilibrium"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Loop Formulation of Linear Elasticity

```mermaid
graph TB
    pA["INPUT displacement boundary conditions"]
    pB["INPUT traction boundary conditions"]
    pC["INPUT body force field f_i"]
    pD["INPUT material constants lambda and mu"]

    node1["EQUILIBRIUM EQUATIONS div sigma plus f equals 0"]
    node2["STRAIN DISPLACEMENT epsilon equals symmetric grad u"]
    node3["CONSTITUTIVE LAW sigma equals lambda tr epsilon I plus 2 mu epsilon"]

    out1["OUTPUT displacement field u_i x"]
    out2["OUTPUT strain field epsilon_ij x"]
    out3["OUTPUT stress field sigma_ij x"]

    pA --> node1
    pB --> node1
    pC --> node1
    pD --> node3

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> out1
    node3 --> out2
    node3 --> out3
```

### 4.4 Schematic of a Typical MEMS Cantilever Beam (Text-Based Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates a **fixed-free cantilever beam**, the most fundamental structural element in microrobotics (used in AFM tips, micro-grippers, and inertia sensors). The beam dimensions are typically $L \sim 100\text{–}500\,\mu\text{m}$, $w \sim 20\text{–}100\,\mu\text{m}$, $t \sim 1\text{–}50\,\mu\text{m}$.

```
   +------------------------------------------------------------+
   |                                                            |
   |   <-------------------- L -------------------->            |
   |                                                            |
   |   y                                                        |
   |   |                                                        |
   |   |     Fixed End (clamped)                                |
   |   v==================================================     |
   |   |##################################################|     |
   |   |##################################################|  ↑  |
   |   |##################################################|  w  |
   |   |##################################################|  ↓  |
   |   ==================================================       |
   |                                                            |
   |           Free End (load P applied here)                   |
   |           ↓                                                |
   |           P                                                |
   +------------------------------------------------------------+
   Material: Single-crystal Silicon (E = 169 GPa, ν = 0.28)
   Tip deflection: δ = P L³ / (3 E I) , where I = w t³ / 12
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU problems**: when the question says "find the tip deflection of a silicon cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, with a tip load $P$", write the answer as $\delta = \dfrac{4 P L^3}{E w t^3}$ immediately and clearly. The examiner awards full marks only if the formula and the final numerical substitution are both correct.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define a microrobot. State any two dominant physical effects at the micro-scale that differentiate microrobot design from macro-robot design.

**Model Answer:**

A **microrobot** is a robotic device whose characteristic dimensions lie in the range **1 µm to 1 mm**, designed to perform sensing, actuation, or manipulation tasks at sub-millimeter scales.

Two dominant micro-scale effects:
1. **Surface-area forces dominate over body forces.** Adhesion, surface tension, and van der Waals forces scale as $L^2$ while inertia scales as $L^3$, so as $L \to 0$, surface effects grow relatively.
2. **Viscous drag dominates over inertial motion.** At low Reynolds number ($\text{Re} \ll 1$), inertial effects are negligible and motion is governed by Stokes' drag $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two effects)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
State Hooke's Law in its uniaxial and generalized (3D isotropic) forms. Define each symbol used.

**Model Answer:**

Uniaxial Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma = E \, \varepsilon
$$

where $\sigma$ = normal stress (Pa), $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\varepsilon$ = normal strain (dimensionless).

Generalized Hooke's Law (isotropic):

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

where $\sigma_{ij}$ is the stress tensor, $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is the strain tensor, $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta, and $\lambda, \mu$ are the two independent Lamé constants.

*(3 marks: 1 for uniaxial form, 1 for generalized form, 1 for definitions)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, every Module question carries **14 marks** and provides an **internal choice**: students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the generalized Hooke's Law for a linear, isotropic elastic material starting from the general 4th-order stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$. Show clearly how the number of independent elastic constants reduces from 21 to 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon micro-cantilever beam has length $L = 200\,\mu\text{m}$, width $w = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, and thickness $t = 5\,\mu\text{m}$. A transverse point load $P = 10\,\mu\text{N}$ is applied at the free end. Calculate (i) the moment of inertia of the cross-section, (ii) the tip deflection, and (iii) the maximum bending stress at the fixed end. Given: $E_{\text{Si}} = 169\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

*(Full derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student is expected to write Steps 1 through 4, with explicit symmetry arguments.)*

**Key steps to write down for full marks:**
1. General law $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\varepsilon_{kl}$ with 81 components. [1 mark]
2. Stress symmetry $C_{ijkl}=C_{jikl}$ and strain symmetry $C_{ijkl}=C_{ijlk}$ → 21 independent components. [2 marks]
3. Isotropy requires invariance under all rotations → only 4th-order isotropic tensor built from $\delta_{ij}$ has 2 independent constants. [2 marks]
4. Final form: $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$. [2 marks]

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**(i) Moment of inertia of rectangular cross-section:**

$$
I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12}
$$

$$
I = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(125 \times 10^{-18})}{12} = \frac{6250 \times 10^{-24}}{12} = 5.208 \times 10^{-22}\,\text{m}^4
$$

**(ii) Tip deflection of cantilever under end load:**

$$
\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\delta = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \times (169 \times 10^9) \times (5.208 \times 10^{-22})}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{(10^{-5})(8 \times 10^{-15})}{3 \times (169 \times 10^9) \times (5.208 \times 10^{-22})}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{8 \times 10^{-20}}{2.641 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.03 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m} = 0.303\,\text{nm}
$$

**(iii) Maximum bending moment at fixed end:**

$$
M_{\max} = P L = (10 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{N·m}
$$

**Maximum bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \, c}{I} = \frac{M_{\max} \, (t/2)}{I} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-9})(2.5 \times 10^{-6})}{5.208 \times 10^{-22}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-15}}{5.208 \times 10^{-22}} = 9.60 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} = 9.60\,\text{MPa}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for part i, 3 for part ii, 2 for part iii)*

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the equilibrium equations, strain-displacement relations, and the generalized Hooke's law, derive **Navier's equations of elastostatics** in vector form. State the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D stress state in a micro-structure element with $\sigma_x = 80\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 40\,\text{MPa}$, and $\tau_{xy} = 30\,\text{MPa}$, calculate the **principal stresses** and the **principal angle**. Also find the **maximum shear stress**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

*(Full derivation is given in Section 3.2 of these notes. The student must write each of the 5 derivation steps.)*

**Mark allocation:**
1. Statement of equilibrium equation. [1 mark]
2. Substitution of generalized Hooke's law. [2 marks]
3. Substitution of strain-displacement relations. [2 marks]
4. Recognition of vector Laplacian and divergence. [1 mark]
5. Final Navier equation with physical interpretation of each term. [1 mark]

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Principal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Center of Mohr's circle:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60\,\text{MPa}
$$

Radius of Mohr's circle:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(80 - 40)^2 + 4(30)^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{1600 + 3600} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{5200}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2}(72.111) = 36.06\,\text{MPa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Principal angle:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(30)}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_p = 28.15^\circ}
$$

**Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = R = 36.06\,\text{MPa}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for principal stresses, 2 for principal angle, 2 for max shear stress)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in Hooke's Law problems.** Every numerical answer MUST carry units (Pa, MPa, GPa, µm, nm). A bare number = 0 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain.** For thin MEMS structures (thickness $< L/10$), use Plane Stress with $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$. For thick blocks, use Plane Strain. Mixing these up = -2 marks.
> 3. **Sign error in Poisson's ratio formula.** The minus sign in $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{axl}}$ is essential. Without it, $\nu$ would be negative for normal materials.
> 4. **Not specifying "linear" and "isotropic" before applying Hooke's law.** If the material is anisotropic (e.g., wood, single-crystal silicon in some directions), the generalized 21-constant form is required. Always state assumptions at the start.
> 5. **In derivations, skipping the symmetry argument.** The step "$C_{ijkl}$ has 21 independent components" must be EXPLICITLY justified using stress symmetry and strain symmetry — not just stated.
> 6. **Forgetting the Kronecker delta** in the generalized Hooke's law: $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$. Writing $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$ (without $\delta_{ij}$) is a CRITICAL error and loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Numerical arithmetic errors** in Mohr's circle / principal-stress problems. Always cross-check by ensuring $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Box this checklist. Revise 1 hour before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Microrobot** = robotic device with characteristic dimension **1 µm – 1 mm**; NOT a simple scale-down of a macro-robot.
- [x] At the micro-scale, **surface forces dominate volume forces** → use scaling-law analysis.
- [x] **Hooke's Law (uniaxial):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. Units: stress in Pa, strain dimensionless.
- [x] **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{axl}}$. For most metals, $\nu \in [0.25, 0.35]$; for rubber, $\nu \approx 0.49$; for cork, $\nu \approx 0$.
- [x] **Generalized Hooke's Law (isotropic):** $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$ — **NEVER** forget the $\delta_{ij}$.
- [x] **Strain tensor:** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ — always **symmetric**.
- [x] **Stress tensor:** symmetric due to angular-momentum conservation.
- [x] **Navier's equation:** $\mu\nabla^2 u_i + (\lambda+\mu)\partial_i(\nabla\cdot\vec{u}) + f_i = 0$.
- [x] **Lamé constants ↔ Engineering moduli:** $E = \mu(3\lambda+2\mu)/(\lambda+\mu)$, $\nu = \lambda/[2(\lambda+\mu)]$, $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$, $K = \lambda + 2\mu/3$.
- [x] **Plane Stress** (thin): use $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$ and $\sigma_{zz} = 0$.
- [x] **Plane Strain** (thick): use $E'' = E(1-\nu)/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$ and $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$.
- [x] **Principal stresses:** $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2 \pm \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$.
- [x] **Principal angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)$.
- [x] **Max shear stress:** $\tau_{\max} = R = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$.
- [x] **Strain energy density** (recoverable elastic): $U_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}$.
- [x] **Thermal stress** (constrained): $\sigma_T = -E\alpha\Delta T$.
- [x] **Key material values for microrobots:** Si ($E = 169$ GPa, $\nu = 0.28$), SU-8 ($E = 4$ GPa, $\nu = 0.33$), PDMS ($E \sim 1$ MPa, $\nu \approx 0.48$).
- [x] **Key physical constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 0.072$ N/m, $\mu_{\text{air}} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.
- [x] **Assumptions of linear elasticity:** small deformations, linear material, isotropic (or specified symmetry), homogeneous, no initial stress.
- [x] **The THREE governing equations of linear elastostatics:** (i) equilibrium $\partial_j\sigma_{ij}+f_i=0$, (ii) kinematics $\varepsilon_{ij}=\tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j}+u_{j,i})$, (iii) constitutive $\sigma_{ij}=C_{ijkl}\varepsilon_{kl}$.
- [x] **Why linear elasticity matters for microrobots:** every compliant flexure, cantilever, hinge, and flagellum of a microrobot is designed using these equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — STRESS: FOUNDATIONS FOR MICROROBOTIC STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition

In continuum mechanics and the analytical foundation of microrobotic structural design, **stress** is formally defined as the internal force per unit area that a continuous body experiences when subjected to an external load. It is a second-order tensor quantity that captures both the magnitude and direction of internal forces at every point inside a deformable solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formal Definition (Cauchy's Stress Principle):**
> The stress vector $\mathbf{t}^{(n)}$ acting on a plane with unit normal $\mathbf{n}$ passing through a point $P$ in a deformable body is given by:
> $$\mathbf{t}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n}$$
> where $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is the **Cauchy Stress Tensor** — a symmetric second-order tensor with units of **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**.

### 1.1 Classification of Stress at a Point

At any arbitrary point within a deformable solid, the stress can be resolved into two fundamental components relative to a chosen reference plane:

- **Normal Stress ($\sigma$)**: The component of stress acting **perpendicular** to the plane. It is further categorized as:
  - **Tensile Stress** ($\sigma_t > 0$): Causes elongation.
  - **Compressive Stress** ($\sigma_c < 0$): Causes contraction.
- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)**: The component of stress acting **parallel (tangential)** to the plane. It tends to deform the body by sliding adjacent layers.

> [!NOTE]
> In microrobotics, shear stress is particularly critical in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) flexures and compliant mechanisms, where micro-scale beams often operate in bending and torsion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spaghetti Bundle" Model

Imagine a bundle of uncooked spaghetti sticks tightly packed inside a cylindrical tube. If you push the ends of the tube inward (axial compression), every individual spaghetti strand carries a tiny compressive force distributed across its cross-section. Conversely, if you pull the ends apart (axial tension), each strand resists the pull.

Now imagine tilting the cylinder at an angle — the strands are no longer purely being pulled along their axes. Some strands slide past their neighbors. This **sliding resistance per unit area** is exactly what we call shear stress.

> [!TIP]
> **Pressure vs. Stress — The Critical Distinction:**
> Pressure ($P = F/A$) is a special case of stress where the force is purely normal and the medium is a fluid. Stress, on the other hand, is the general case applicable to solids and includes both normal AND tangential (shear) components. In microrobotics, we deal almost exclusively with **general stress states** because micro-structures are solid.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Unit of Stress (SI) | **Pascal (Pa)** = **N/m²** |
| Conversion | 1 MPa = **10⁶ N/m²**, 1 GPa = **10⁹ N/m²** |
| Typical Silicon Young's Modulus | **~169 GPa** |
| Typical Polysilicon Young's Modulus | **~150–170 GPa** |
| Typical SU-8 Young's Modulus | **~3–5 GPa** |
| Typical PDMS Young's Modulus | **~0.5–3 MPa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cauchy Stress Tensor — Generalized State of Stress

At any point in a 3D continuum, nine stress components are required to fully describe the stress state (though symmetry reduces this to **six independent components**):

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\
\tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\
\tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Because the angular momentum conservation (moment equilibrium) on an infinitesimal element requires $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$, the tensor is symmetric and has only **6 independent components**.

### 2.2 Decomposition of the Stress Tensor

The Cauchy stress tensor can be decomposed into two physically meaningful parts:

- **Hydrostatic (Volumetric / Mean) Component** — causes volume change:
  $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}}{3}$$
- **Deviatoric Component** — causes shape distortion (this is what governs yielding):
  $$\mathbf{s} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} - \sigma_m \mathbf{I}$$

### 2.3 2D Plane Stress Simplification

For thin microrobotic plates and MEMS membranes (where one dimension is much smaller than the other two), the through-thickness stresses ($\sigma_{zz}, \tau_{xz}, \tau_{yz}$) vanish, yielding:

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma}_{2D} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} \\
\tau_{xy} & \sigma_{yy}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This simplification is heavily used in analyzing micro-cantilevers, micro-diaphragms, and flexure hinges in microrobotic grippers.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE examinations. Memorize the boundary conditions and sign conventions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F_n}{A}$ | $F_n$ in N, $A$ in m², $\sigma$ in Pa |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$ | $F_t$ in N, $A$ in m², $\tau$ in Pa |
| 3 | Stress on Inclined Plane | $\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ is the angle of the inclined plane |
| 4 | Shear on Inclined Plane | $\tau_n = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$ | Resultant shear on rotated plane |
| 5 | Principal Stress (Analytic) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Eigenvalues of stress tensor |
| 6 | Principal Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Orientation of principal planes |
| 7 | Max In-Plane Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Acts on 45° planes from principal |
| 8 | Max Shear Angle | $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ | Orientation of max shear plane |
| 9 | Von Mises Stress | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | Distortion energy criterion |
| 10 | Hydrostatic (Mean) Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{3}$ (3D) or $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ (2D) | Pure volume change component |
| 11 | Traction Vector | $\mathbf{t}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n}$ | Force per unit area on plane n |
| 12 | Stress Invariant (2D) | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$, $\quad I_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ | Rotation-invariant scalars |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Microrobotics

- **Compliant Mechanism Design**: Micro-flexures in microrobotic grippers rely on calculated stress limits to achieve desired deflection without plastic deformation.
- **Fatigue Analysis**: Stress cycling in vibrating microrobotic actuators (e.g., piezoelectric or electrostatic) requires accurate peak stress determination.
- **Failure Prediction**: Von Mises stress is the gold-standard yield criterion for ductile micro-materials (metals like Ni, Au, Al used in MEMS).
- **Residual Stress Control**: Thin-film deposition in MEMS introduces residual stresses that warp micro-structures — must be quantified and minimized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal and Shear Stresses on an Inclined Plane

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a 2D rectangular element under uniaxial normal stress $\sigma_x$ applied on the left and right faces, and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on all faces (complementary shear ensures equilibrium).

We want to derive the normal and shear stress on an inclined plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry of the Inclined Section

Let $A$ be the area of the inclined plane. The area of the original vertical face is $A \cos\theta$, and that of the horizontal face is $A \sin\theta$.

### Step 2: Apply Force Equilibrium

The total force on the original element in the x-direction:
$$F_x = \sigma_x \cdot (A \cos\theta)$$

The total force in the y-direction:
$$F_y = \tau_{xy} \cdot (A \cos\theta)$$

### Step 3: Resolve Forces Along the Normal and Tangent of the Inclined Plane

The unit normal to the inclined plane is $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, and the unit tangent is $\hat{t} = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$.

Normal force on the inclined section:
$$F_n = F_x \cos\theta + F_y \sin\theta$$

Substituting:
$$F_n = \sigma_x A \cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy} A \cos\theta \sin\theta$$

Dividing by $A$:
$$\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta\cos\theta$$

### Step 4: Derive the Shear Stress on the Inclined Plane

Tangential force on the inclined section:
$$F_t = -F_x \sin\theta + F_y \cos\theta$$

Substituting:
$$F_t = -\sigma_x A \cos\theta \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} A \cos^2\theta$$

Dividing by $A$:
$$\tau_n = -\sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos^2\theta$$

### Step 5: Generalize for Biaxial Stress

For a general 2D stress state ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$), superposition yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\tau_n &= (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **stress transformation equations** — one of the most heavily tested derivations in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Principal Stresses (Eigenvalue Problem)

**Objective:** Find the angle $\theta_p$ at which shear stress on a plane vanishes (i.e., the plane experiences only normal stress).

### Step 1: Set Shear Stress to Zero

From the shear stress transformation equation, set $\tau_n = 0$:

$$0 = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta_p \cos\theta_p + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta_p - \sin^2\theta_p)$$

### Step 2: Apply Double-Angle Identities

Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$0 = \frac{(\sigma_y - \sigma_x)}{2} \sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta_p$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Principal Angle

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \\
\theta_p &= \frac{1}{2} \arctan\!\left(\frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

There are two solutions separated by **90°** (orthogonal principal planes).

### Step 4: Substitute Back to Find Principal Stresses

Substituting $\sin 2\theta_p$ and $\cos 2\theta_p$ from the tangent identity back into $\sigma_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta_p + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta_p + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta_p\cos\theta_p \\
&= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta_p
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic manipulation using the identity $\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cos 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta_p = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress

### Step 1: Differentiate the Shear Stress Equation

To find the maximum shear stress, differentiate $\tau_n$ with respect to $\theta$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d\tau_n}{d\theta} = 0$$

### Step 2: Apply the Condition

After differentiation and simplification:
$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

Comparing with the principal angle result, this gives:
$$\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum shear planes are always oriented at 45° from the principal planes.**

### Step 3: Compute the Maximum Shear Stress Value

Substituting $\theta_s$ back into $\tau_n$ yields:

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Von Mises Stress (Distortion Energy Criterion)

For a 2D stress state, the Von Mises equivalent stress is derived from the **distortion energy theory**:

### Step 1: Compute the Principal Stresses

We use $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ as derived above.

### Step 2: Apply the Von Mises Formula

For plane stress, the Von Mises equivalent stress is:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}
$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of $x, y$ Components

Using stress invariants $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ and $\sigma_1\sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{VM}^2 &= (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2 - 3(\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2) \\
&= \sigma_x^2 + 2\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 - 3\sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2 \\
&= \sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **When $\sigma_{VM} \geq \sigma_{yield}$**, the material is predicted to yield. This is the standard ductile failure criterion used in microrobot MEMS structural design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Stress Analysis Workflow for a Microrobotic Flexure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load F Applied] --> B[Identify Critical Cross-Section A]
    B --> C[Resolve F into Normal Fn and Tangential Ft]
    C --> D[Compute Normal Stress sigma = Fn per A]
    C --> E[Compute Shear Stress tau = Ft per A]
    D --> F[Construct 2D Stress Tensor sigma_xx, sigma_yy, tau_xy]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Calculate Principal Stresses sigma1 and sigma2]
    G --> H[Compute Principal Angle theta_p]
    G --> I[Compute Max Shear Stress tau_max]
    F --> J[Compute Von Mises Stress sigma_VM]
    J --> K{sigma_VM greater than sigma_yield?}
    K -->|Yes| L[FAILURE: Plastic Deformation Detected]
    K -->|No| M[Design Safe: Elastic Regime]
    H --> N[Orient Microstructure to Minimize Stress]
    I --> N
    N --> O[Final Flexure Geometry Validated]
```

## 4.2 Stress State Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Cauchy Stress Tensor sigma] --> H[Hydrostatic Component sigma_m I]
    S --> D[Deviatoric Component s]
    H --> H1[Causes Volume Change]
    H --> H2[dV per V equals 3 sigma_m per E in isotropic]
    D --> D1[Causes Shape Distortion]
    D --> D2[Governs Yielding via Von Mises]
    D2 --> D3[sigma_VM greater than sigma_y leads to failure]
```

## 4.3 Principal Stress Orientation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> Q[Compute Average Stress sigma_avg]
    P --> R[Compute Radius R = sqrt((sigma_x-sigma_y over 2) squared + tau_xy squared)]
    Q --> S[Plot Center on sigma axis at sigma_avg, 0]
    R --> T[Draw Circle of Radius R]
    S --> U[Mark Point P at sigma_x, tau_xy]
    T --> V[Circle Intersects Axis at sigma1 and sigma2]
    U --> W[Angle 2 theta_p from Center to Point P]
    W --> X[Principal Direction theta_p obtained]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The third diagram above is a conceptual flow representing Mohr's Circle construction — a graphical method for solving principal stress problems. KTU examiners often award full marks if students describe Mohr's Circle alongside the analytical formulas.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Cauchy stress tensor. Explain why it has only six independent components.**

**Model Answer:**

The Cauchy stress tensor $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is a second-order tensor that completely describes the state of stress at a point inside a deformable body. It maps the outward unit normal of any plane through the point to the traction vector on that plane via $\mathbf{t}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n}$.

In 3D, the tensor has 9 components $\sigma_{ij}$ where $i,j \in \{x,y,z\}$. However, applying **conservation of angular momentum** to an infinitesimal element requires that the net moment about the center be zero, which yields the symmetry condition:

$$\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji} \quad \text{for all } i \neq j$$

This eliminates 3 redundant off-diagonal components, leaving **6 independent components** (3 normal + 3 shear). **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between normal stress and shear stress with suitable diagrams and examples from microrobotic structures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Normal Stress ($\sigma$) | Shear Stress ($\tau$) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of Force | Perpendicular to plane | Parallel to plane |
| Deformation Effect | Elongation or contraction | Angular distortion |
| Symbol | $\sigma$ | $\tau$ |
| Microrobotic Example | Axial pull on a micro-cantilever | Torsion in a micro-gripper wrist |
| Sign Convention | Tension (+), Compression (−) | Direction-dependent |

**Example:** In a micro-cantilever beam of an AFM (Atomic Force Microscope) tip, the bending produces **tensile stress** on the upper surface and **compressive stress** on the lower surface, while **transverse shear stress** exists across the depth. **[3 Marks]**

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for normal and shear stresses on an inclined plane in a 2D stress element subjected to biaxial stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A point in a micro-flexure beam is subjected to the following in-plane stresses: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Determine:
  - (i) The principal stresses and principal angle.
  - (ii) The maximum in-plane shear stress and its orientation.
  - (iii) The Von Mises equivalent stress. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Consider a 2D element. Let an inclined plane have outward normal at angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. Let $A$ be the inclined area. The projected areas are $A\cos\theta$ (vertical face) and $A\sin\theta$ (horizontal face).

**Step 2 — Force Resolution:**
Total x-direction force: $F_x = \sigma_x A\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} A\sin\theta$
Total y-direction force: $F_y = \sigma_y A\sin\theta + \tau_{xy} A\cos\theta$

**Step 3 — Normal Stress:**
Project $F_x$ and $F_y$ along the normal $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= F_x\cos\theta + F_y\sin\theta \\
&= \sigma_x A\cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy} A\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_y A\sin^2\theta + \tau_{xy} A\sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by $A$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_n = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta + \sigma_y\sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta}$$ **[4 Marks]**

**Step 4 — Shear Stress:**
Project along the tangent $\hat{t} = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$:

$$\boxed{\tau_n = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)}$$ **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**Part (i) — Principal Stresses:**

Average stress: $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \dfrac{80+40}{2} = 60$ MPa.

Radius:
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80-40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

Principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

Principal angle:
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2(30)}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$
$$2\theta_p = 56.31° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 28.15°$$ **[3 Marks]**

**Part (ii) — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{\max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

Orientation:
$$\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45° = 28.15° + 45° = 73.15°$$
(Also valid: $-16.85°$) **[2 Marks]**

**Part (iii) — Von Mises Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{VM} &= \sqrt{80^2 - (80)(40) + 40^2 + 3(30)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{6400 - 3200 + 1600 + 2700} \\
&= \sqrt{7500} \approx 86.60 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$ **[2 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define stress. With a neat stress element diagram, explain the concept of normal stress, shear stress, and the 2D plane stress state. How is the Cauchy stress tensor expressed in 2D? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The Von Mises stress in a nickel micro-structure must not exceed **250 MPa**. If the stress state at the critical point is $\sigma_x = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 100$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 80$ MPa, determine:
  - (i) The principal stresses.
  - (ii) Whether the design is safe (use Von Mises criterion).
  - (iii) The hydrostatic and deviatoric stress components. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Definition:** Stress is the internal resistive force per unit area developed within a deformable body when subjected to external loads. Mathematically: $\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$.

**Stress Element Diagram Description:** A square element with:
- $\sigma_x$ acting normal on left/right faces (tensile outward arrows)
- $\sigma_y$ acting normal on top/bottom faces
- $\tau_{xy}$ acting tangentially on all four faces (must form complementary pairs for equilibrium)

**Plane Stress Assumption:** When the thickness of the body is small (as in MEMS membranes and micro-cantilevers), stresses $\sigma_{zz}, \tau_{xz}, \tau_{yz}$ vanish:

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma}_{2D} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} \\
\tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

with $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ due to moment equilibrium. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 100$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 250$ MPa.

**Part (i) — Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{200 + 100}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{200-100}{2}\right)^2 + 80^2} \\
&= 150 \pm \sqrt{2500 + 6400} \\
&= 150 \pm \sqrt{8900} \\
&= 150 \pm 94.34
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma_1 = 244.34 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 55.66 \text{ MPa}$$ **[3 Marks]**

**Part (ii) — Von Mises Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{VM} &= \sqrt{200^2 - (200)(100) + 100^2 + 3(80)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{40000 - 20000 + 10000 + 19200} \\
&= \sqrt{49200} \approx 221.81 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma_{VM} = 221.81$ MPa $< \sigma_{allow} = 250$ MPa, **the design is SAFE**. **[2 Marks]**

**Part (iii) — Hydrostatic and Deviatoric Stress:**

Mean stress:
$$\sigma_m = \frac{200 + 100}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

Deviatoric components:
$$
s_{xx} = \sigma_x - \sigma_m = 200 - 150 = 50 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
s_{yy} = \sigma_y - \sigma_m = 100 - 150 = -50 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
s_{xy} = \tau_{xy} = 80 \text{ MPa (unchanged)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{s} =
\begin{bmatrix}
50 & 80 \\
80 & -50
\end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}\;}
$$ **[2 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently drop the sign on shear stress when substituting into formulas. Remember $\tau_{xy}$ is signed based on the outward normal direction of the face it acts on.
> 2. **Forgetting Complementary Shear:** When only ONE face's shear arrow is drawn, equilibrium demands an equal and opposite shear on the perpendicular face — marking is deducted if omitted.
> 3. **Principal Angle Range Confusion:** $\theta_p$ can have two values separated by 90°. Always specify which one corresponds to $\sigma_1$.
> 4. **Units in Final Answer:** Stress must be reported in MPa or Pa — numerical answer without units gets **0.5 mark deducted** per KTU 2024 norms.
> 5. **Von Mises vs. Tresca:** For ductile micro-materials (Ni, Au, Al), Von Mises is used. For brittle materials (Si, ceramics), Maximum Normal Stress or Coulomb-Mohr criterion is preferred — context matters!

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stress** = Internal force per unit area; units are **Pa = N/m²**; a **second-order tensor**.
- ✅ **Two types**: Normal stress ($\sigma$) and Shear stress ($\tau$).
- ✅ **Cauchy Stress Tensor** has **9 components in 3D** but **only 6 independent** due to symmetry $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$.
- ✅ **Plane Stress** simplification applies to thin MEMS/microrobotic structures (e.g., micro-cantilevers, diaphragms, flexures).
- ✅ **Principal stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ are the **extreme normal stresses** (eigenvalues), found using the analytical formula or **Mohr's Circle**.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$, oriented at **45°** from principal planes.
- ✅ **Von Mises stress** is the **yield criterion for ductile materials** — used extensively in MEMS design safety checks.
- ✅ **Mean (hydrostatic) stress** governs volume change; **deviatoric stress** governs shape change and yielding.
- ✅ The **stress transformation equations** are the foundation for analyzing inclined planes — must be memorized.
- ✅ In microrobotics, special attention to **residual stress** from thin-film deposition and **stress concentrations** at micro-feature corners.
- ✅ Always state units explicitly and follow the sign convention: **tensile = positive, compressive = negative**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Strain

In continuum mechanics, **Strain** is a dimensionless measure of deformation representing the relative displacement between particles in a material body, normalized by a reference length. It quantifies how much a given deformation has locally occurred compared to the material's original, undeformed configuration.

Mathematically, strain is a second-order **tensor** (in 3D) that completely describes the state of deformation at a point, capturing both **stretching (normal strain)** and **angular distortion (shear strain)**.

In the context of **Microrobotics**, strain analysis is critical because:
- MEMS/NEMS compliant mechanisms operate at micron/sub-micron scales where geometric non-linearities dominate.
- Piezoelectric, electrostatic, and thermal actuators generate controlled strain fields to produce motion.
- Strain gauges embedded in microrobotic skins (e.g., for haptic feedback) convert mechanical strain into electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Strain is treated as the **kinematic dual** of stress. Whereas stress (force per unit area) describes internal force distribution, strain (displacement per unit length) describes geometric distortion. Both are second-order tensors in 3D.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rubber band** being stretched between your two hands. The original length $L_0$ increases to a new length $L$. If you mark a tiny segment of length $\Delta L_0$ on the rubber band, after stretching, that segment becomes $\Delta L$. The **normal strain** is simply how much longer that tiny segment became *relative to its original size*.

Now, imagine a **deck of cards** resting flat on a table. If you push the top card sideways while the bottom card stays fixed, the cards shear into a parallelogram shape. The change in the originally 90° angle — measured in **radians** — is the **shear strain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Strain is a *dimensionless* quantity. In SI units it has no units (though engineers often write "m/m" or "in/in" for clarity). A strain of $0.001$ is called "**one milli-strain**" and is abbreviated as $1\,\mu\varepsilon$ (micro-strain).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Small-strain threshold | $\vert \varepsilon \vert$ | $< 10^{-3}$ |
| Typical MEMS strain range | $\varepsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Silicon yield strain | $\varepsilon_y$ | $\approx 7 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Strain gauge gauge factor | $G_f$ | $\approx 2.0$ (metal), $\approx 100+$ (semiconductor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why strain is dimensionless:** It is a *ratio of two lengths* (change in length over original length). This is why strain is valid in any unit system — it is a pure number.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Strain

The general strain tensor is **symmetric** (in the absence of body couples) and has 6 independent components in 3D:

$$
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The strain tensor decomposes into two parts:

$$
\varepsilon_{ij} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)}_{\text{Symmetric part (Strain)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} - \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)}_{\text{Anti-symmetric part (Rigid-body rotation)}}
$$

#### (a) Normal (Axial) Strain — $\varepsilon_{xx}$, $\varepsilon_{yy}$, $\varepsilon_{zz}$

The relative change in length along a coordinate axis. For a 1D bar of original length $L_0$ deformed to $L$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

- **Engineering (Cauchy) strain:** $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = \Delta L / L_0$
- **True (Logarithmic / Hencky) strain:** $\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(L/L_0)$

For small deformations ($\Delta L \ll L_0$), both are approximately equal.

#### (b) Shear Strain — $\gamma_{xy}$, $\gamma_{yz}$, $\gamma_{zx}$

The change in angle (in **radians**) between two originally perpendicular line elements. The tensor shear strain is $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$, where $\gamma_{xy}$ is the **engineering shear strain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** When the question asks for "shear strain", they usually mean the engineering shear strain $\gamma$ (in radians), which is twice the tensor component $\varepsilon_{xy}$.

#### (c) Volumetric (Dilatation) Strain — $e$ or $\theta$

The fractional change in volume:

$$
e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})
$$

### 2.2 Strain Transformation

Like stress, strain transforms under a rotation of axes. The 2D transformation for normal strain along an inclined direction making angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta
$$

The shear strain transformation:

$$
\frac{\gamma_{\theta}}{2} = -\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\cos 2\theta
$$

### 2.3 Principal Strains

Principal strains are the **eigenvalues** of the strain tensor — the normal strains along directions where shear strain vanishes. They are found by solving:

$$
\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality:** Principal strains are orientation-dependent. In microrobotic flexures (e.g., a folded-beam suspension), the principal strain direction aligns with the beam's long axis, which is why crack propagation and fatigue follow predictable paths.

### 2.4 Mohr's Circle for Strain

Mohr's circle is the geometric construction for 2D strain state visualization:
- **Center:** $C = \left(\frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2},\,0\right)$
- **Radius:** $R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$
- **Maximum shear strain:** $\gamma_{\max}/2 = R$, so $\gamma_{\max} = 2R$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always plot Mohr's circle with **normal strain on x-axis** and **$\gamma/2$ (NOT $\gamma$) on y-axis**. The angle on Mohr's circle is **$2\theta$** (twice the physical angle). This is the most common place students lose marks.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Normal strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Dimensionless |
| True strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(L/L_0)$ | For finite deformation |
| Engineering shear strain | $\gamma = \Delta\phi$ (rad) | Tensor $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma/2$ |
| Volumetric strain | $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | $\text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$ |
| Strain transformation | $\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{\varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x-\varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta$ | 2D plane |
| Principal strain | $\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x-\varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ | Eigenvalue |
| Max shear strain | $\gamma_{\max} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ | In plane |
| Mohr's circle center | $C = (\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y)/2$ | x-axis intercept |
| Mohr's circle radius | $R = \sqrt{((\varepsilon_x-\varepsilon_y)/2)^2 + (\gamma_{xy}/2)^2}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| Compatibility (2D) | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_y}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{\partial^2 \gamma_{xy}}{\partial x \partial y}$ | For single-valued $u,v$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Microrobotics

- **Compliant MEMS mechanisms**: Folded-beam, crab-leg, and serpentine flexures rely on bending strain to provide low-friction, frictionless guidance. Designers calculate $\varepsilon_{\max}$ to avoid yielding silicon.
- **Piezoelectric actuators**: PZT stacks produce $\sim 0.1\%$ strain, used to drive microrobotic legs.
- **Bio-inspired strain sensing**: Microrobotic e-skin uses arrays of strain gauges or capacitive sensors to measure skin deformation for haptic perception.
- **Shape memory alloys (Nitinol)**: Martensitic phase transformation can produce recoverable strains up to $8\%$, used in microrobotic grippers.
- **Polymer flexures (PDMS, SU-8)**: Can sustain strains of $10–20\%$, enabling soft microrobots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Normal Strain from Displacement Field

**Starting setup:** Consider a 1D bar along the $x$-axis. Point $P$ at position $x$ displaces to $x' = x + u(x)$, where $u$ is the displacement field.

**Step 1 — Identify two neighboring points:**
- Point $P$: original position $x$, displaced to $x + u(x)$.
- Point $Q$ (a small distance away): original position $x + \Delta x$, displaced to $x + \Delta x + u(x + \Delta x)$.

**Step 2 — Original length between P and Q:**
$$
L_0 = \Delta x
$$

**Step 3 — Deformed length between P and Q:**
$$
L = (x + \Delta x + u(x + \Delta x)) - (x + u(x)) = \Delta x + u(x + \Delta x) - u(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Taylor expansion of $u(x + \Delta x)$:**
$$
u(x + \Delta x) = u(x) + \frac{du}{dx}\Delta x + \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2}(\Delta x)^2 + \cdots
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the deformed length:**
$$
L = \Delta x + \frac{du}{dx}\Delta x + \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2}(\Delta x)^2 + \cdots
$$

**Step 6 — Subtract original length to get the change:**
$$
\Delta L = L - L_0 = \frac{du}{dx}\Delta x + \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2}(\Delta x)^2 + \cdots
$$

**Step 7 — Form the strain ratio:**
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{du}{dx} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2}\Delta x + \cdots
$$

**Step 8 — Take the infinitesimal limit ($\Delta x \to 0$):**
All higher-order terms vanish, leaving:
$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\frac{\Delta L}{\Delta x} = \frac{du}{dx}
$$

> **Logic summary:** Strain is the *spatial gradient* of displacement. This is the foundational link between kinematics and deformation.

### 3.2 Derivation: 2D Strain Tensor from Displacement Gradient

**Step 1 — Define displacement field:** $\vec{u} = (u, v, w)$ at point $(x, y, z)$.

**Step 2 — Displacement gradient tensor:**
$$
\nabla \vec{u} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x & \partial u/\partial y & \partial u/\partial z \\ \partial v/\partial x & \partial v/\partial y & \partial v/\partial z \\ \partial w/\partial x & \partial w/\partial y & \partial w/\partial z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose into symmetric and anti-symmetric parts:**
$$
\nabla \vec{u} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\left(\nabla\vec{u} + (\nabla\vec{u})^T\right)}_{\text{Strain tensor }\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\left(\nabla\vec{u} - (\nabla\vec{u})^T\right)}_{\text{Rotation tensor }\boldsymbol{\omega}}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the symmetric part for the 2D $(x,y)$ plane:**
$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)
$$

> **Logic summary:** Symmetrization ensures that pure rigid-body rotation produces no strain. This is why a rotating disk is not "strained" — its $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \boldsymbol{0}$ even though points do move.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Strain Transformation by Mohr's Circle

**Problem:** At a point in a MEMS flexure, the strain state is:
$$
\varepsilon_x = 400\,\mu\varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon_y = -200\,\mu\varepsilon, \quad \gamma_{xy} = 600\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

Find (a) principal strains, (b) maximum in-plane shear strain, (c) orientation of principal axes.

#### (a) Principal Strains

**Step 1 — Center of Mohr's circle (average normal strain):**
$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{400 + (-200)}{2} = 100\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Mohr's circle:**
$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the half-difference:**
$$
\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{400 - (-200)}{2} = 300\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 4 — Half the shear strain:**
$$
\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} = \frac{600}{2} = 300\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into radius formula:**
$$
R = \sqrt{(300)^2 + (300)^2} = 300\sqrt{2} \approx 424.26\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Principal strains:**
$$
\varepsilon_1 = C + R = 100 + 424.26 = 524.26\,\mu\varepsilon
$$
$$
\varepsilon_2 = C - R = 100 - 424.26 = -324.26\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

#### (b) Maximum In-Plane Shear Strain

**Step 7 — Maximum engineering shear strain:**
$$
\gamma_{\max} = 2R = 2 \times 424.26 = 848.53\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

#### (c) Orientation of Principal Axes

**Step 8 — Angle to principal axis 1 (on Mohr's circle, $2\theta_p$):**
$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{\gamma_{xy}/2}{(\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y)/2} = \frac{300}{300} = 1
$$
$$
2\theta_p = 45^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_p = 22.5^\circ
$$

> **Logic summary:** The principal direction in physical space is **half** the angle measured on Mohr's circle. Always check by plugging $\theta_p$ back into the transformation equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strain Components — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STRAIN TENSOR epsilon_ij"] --> B["NORMAL STRAIN epsilon_xx epsilon_yy epsilon_zz"]
    A --> C["SHEAR STRAIN gamma_xy gamma_yz gamma_zx"]
    A --> D["VOLUMETRIC STRAIN e = tr epsilon"]
    B --> B1["1D Bar: delta L over L0"]
    B --> B2["Engineering strain: small deformation"]
    B --> B3["True strain: ln L over L0"]
    C --> C1["Angle change in radians"]
    C --> C2["Tensor: epsilon_xy = gamma_xy over 2"]
    D --> D1["Hydrostatic compression effect"]
    D --> D2["Used in bulk modulus relation"]
    A --> E["MOHRS CIRCLE 2D"]
    A --> F["PRINCIPAL STRAINS eigenvalues"]
    A --> G["COMPATIBILITY EQUATION"]
    E --> E1["Center: average normal strain"]
    E --> E2["Radius: half max shear"]
    F --> F1["Solve det epsilon minus lambda I equals 0"]
    F --> F2["Three in 3D, two in 2D"]
    G --> G1["Ensures single valued displacements"]
    G --> G2["Critical in FEM for microrobotic structures"]
    A --> H["APPLICATION IN MICROROBOTICS"]
    H --> H1["MEMS flexures and compliant mechanisms"]
    H --> H2["Piezoelectric actuator strain"]
    H --> H3["Strain gauge based tactile sensors"]
    H --> H4["Shape memory alloy grippers"]
```

### 4.2 Mohr's Circle — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Point X: epsilon_x on x-axis gamma_xy over 2 above"] --> M["Mohrs Circle Center C average epsilon"]
    P2["Point Y: epsilon_y on x-axis gamma_xy over 2 below"] --> M
    M --> P1axis["epsilon_1 at right extreme"]
    M --> P2axis["epsilon_2 at left extreme"]
    M --> P3["Top point: gamma max over 2"]
    M --> P4["Bottom point: gamma max over 2 negative"]
    P1axis -->|"2 theta_p = 45 deg"| P2axis
```

### 4.3 Strain in a MEMS Folded-Beam Flexure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L["Applied Load F at anchor"] --> FB1["Folded Beam Segment 1"]
    FB1 --> FB2["Folded Beam Segment 2"]
    FB2 --> FB3["Folded Beam Segment 3"]
    FB3 --> M["Moving Shuttle with proof mass"]
    M --> SG["Embedded Strain Gauge"]
    SG --> OP["Output Voltage delta V = Gf times epsilon times V_excite"]
    FB1 --> SF1["Axial Strain epsilon = F over kA in segment"]
    FB2 --> SF2["Bending Strain sigma = My over I"]
    FB3 --> SF3["Combined: epsilon total = epsilon axial plus epsilon bending"]
    SF1 --> DS["Design Constraint: epsilon total less than epsilon yield"]
    SF2 --> DS
    SF3 --> DS
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: Displacement → Strain → Stress

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["Displacement Field u_i"] -->|"Spatial derivative"| S["Strain Tensor epsilon_ij"]
    S -->|"Constitutive Law sigma_ij = C_ijkl times epsilon_kl"| ST["Stress Tensor sigma_ij"]
    ST --> E["Equilibrium: divergence sigma plus f = 0"]
    E --> SOL["Solve for u_i in microrobotic structure"]
    SOL --> U
    S --> P["Principal Strains and Mohr Analysis"]
    P --> FD["Failure and Fatigue Design Check"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the following terms: (i) Normal strain, (ii) Shear strain, (iii) Volumetric strain. State the units of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Normal strain (1 mark):** The ratio of change in length to the original length of a line element along a given direction.
  $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

- **(ii) Shear strain (1 mark):** The change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular line elements. Engineering shear strain is $\gamma = \Delta\phi$.

- **(iii) Volumetric strain (1 mark):** The ratio of change in volume to original volume. For small strains: $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$.

- **Units (covered implicitly):** All are **dimensionless** (pure numbers).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*At a point in a microrobotic flexure, the strain components are $\varepsilon_x = 300\,\mu\varepsilon$, $\varepsilon_y = 100\,\mu\varepsilon$, and $\gamma_{xy} = 400\,\mu\varepsilon$. Find the principal strains.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Center of Mohr's circle (1 mark):**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{300 + 100}{2} = 200\,\mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Radius (1 mark):**
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{300-100}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{400}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{(100)^2 + (200)^2} = \sqrt{50000} \approx 223.6\,\mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Principal strains (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_1 = 200 + 223.6 = 423.6\,\mu\varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon_2 = 200 - 223.6 = -23.6\,\mu\varepsilon$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]**

**(a)** Derive the 2D strain transformation equations for normal and shear strain as a function of the rotation angle $\theta$. Show that strain is a second-order tensor. (7 marks)

**(b)** A MEMS cantilever of length $L = 500\,\mu\text{m}$ and thickness $t = 5\,\mu\text{m}$ is subjected to a tip load $F = 50\,\mu\text{N}$. The Young's modulus is $E = 170\,\text{GPa}$ (silicon). Calculate (i) the maximum bending stress, (ii) the maximum strain at the fixed end, and (iii) the tip deflection. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Define original axes and rotated axes (1 mark):**
Let the original strain components be $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \gamma_{xy}$ in the $(x, y)$ frame. The rotated frame $(x', y')$ makes angle $\theta$ with the original.

**Step 2 — Express unit vectors of rotated frame in original frame (1 mark):**
$$\hat{x}' = \cos\theta\,\hat{x} + \sin\theta\,\hat{y}$$
$$\hat{y}' = -\sin\theta\,\hat{x} + \cos\theta\,\hat{y}$$

**Step 3 — Use the projection of strain in the rotated direction (2 marks):**
The normal strain along $\hat{x}'$ is the dot product of the strain tensor with $\hat{x}'$:
$$\varepsilon_{x'} = (\hat{x}')^T \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} (\hat{x}') = \varepsilon_x \cos^2\theta + \varepsilon_y \sin^2\theta + 2\varepsilon_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply double-angle identities (1 mark):**
Using $\cos^2\theta = (1+\cos 2\theta)/2$, $\sin^2\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:
$$\varepsilon_{x'} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \varepsilon_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

Since $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Shear strain transformation (1 mark):**
Following similar derivation:
$$\frac{\gamma_{x'y'}}{2} = -\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\cos 2\theta$$

**Step 6 — Tensor character (1 mark):**
The above equations match the standard second-order tensor transformation law, confirming $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is a second-order tensor.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $L = 500\,\mu\text{m} = 500 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $t = 5\,\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $F = 50\,\mu\text{N} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N}$, $E = 170 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$.
