**Step 3:** Multiply $T_0^2 = T_0^1 T_1^2$. Extract the position vector:

$$x = L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12}$$
$$y = L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12}$$

Substituting $L_1 = 30\,mm$, $L_2 = 20\,mm$:

$$x = 30 c_1 + 20 c_{12} \quad \text{(mm)}$$
$$y = 30 s_1 + 20 s_{12} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

Orientation: $\phi = q_1 + q_2$.

**(Full 7 marks: 1m for diagram of frames, 3m for matrix derivation, 2m for final equations with units, 1m for orientation)**

**(b) Jacobian & Singularity**

Differentiating the position equations with respect to time:

$$\dot{x} = -30 s_1 \dot{q}_1 - 20 s_{12} (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y} = 30 c_1 \dot{q}_1 + 20 c_{12} (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -30 s_1 - 20 s_{12} & -20 s_{12} \\ 30 c_1 + 20 c_{12} & 20 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}}_{J(q)} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant:

$$\det(J) = (-30 s_1 - 20 s_{12})(20 c_{12}) - (-20 s_{12})(30 c_1 + 20 c_{12})$$

Expanding using trigonometric identities gives:

$$\det(J) = 30 \cdot 20 \sin q_2 = 600 \sin q_2 \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

**Singular configuration:** $\det(J) = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0°$ or $q_2 = 180°$.

**Physical meaning:**
- $q_2 = 0°$ ⇒ links are fully **aligned/extended**, the manipulator lies along a straight line and cannot move perpendicular to itself.
- $q_2 = 180°$ ⇒ links are **folded back on each other**, the end-effector touches the base.

At both cases, the manipulator has only 1 effective DOF (sliding along the link direction). **(7 marks: 2m for Jacobian, 2m for determinant, 1m for condition, 2m for physical interpretation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Lagrangian Dynamics of a 2-DOF Micromanipulator

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 1 / 14 marks]`

**(a)** For the same 2-link planar microrobot with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the link ends, derive the kinetic energy expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Lagrangian formulation, derive the two coupled equations of motion and identify the matrices $M(\vec{q})$, $C(\vec{q}, \dot{\vec{q}})$, and $G(\vec{q})$. State the condition under which the gravity term vanishes for a planar manipulator mounted horizontally. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kinetic Energy Derivation**

Position of mass $m_1$ (at end of link 1):

$$x_1 = L_1 c_1, \quad y_1 = L_1 s_1$$

Position of mass $m_2$ (at end of link 2, using derived forward kinematics):

$$x_2 = L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12}, \quad y_2 = L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12}$$

Compute velocities using chain rule:

$$\dot{x}_1 = -L_1 s_1 \dot{q}_1, \quad \dot{y}_1 = L_1 c_1 \dot{q}_1$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -L_1 s_1 \dot{q}_1 - L_2 s_{12}(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_2 = L_1 c_1 \dot{q}_1 + L_2 c_{12}(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

Squared speeds:

$$v_1^2 = L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$
$$v_2^2 = L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + L_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2$$

(using identity $c_1 c_{12} + s_1 s_{12} = \cos q_2$)

Total kinetic energy:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$$

Expanding:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 + m_2) L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 L_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2$$

**(7 marks: 2m position, 2m velocity, 3m final $T$)**

**(b) Euler–Lagrange Derivation**

For horizontal mounting, potential energy $V$ is constant ⇒ $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i}$. But to keep the question general, include gravity term.

Potential energy (with $z = 0$ horizontal plane, pivot datum):

$$V = m_1 g L_1 s_1 + m_2 g (L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12})$$

Now expand $T$ into matrix form. With $M_{11} = (m_1 + m_2)L_1^2 + m_2 L_2^2 + 2 m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2$, $M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 L_2^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2$, $M_{22} = m_2 L_2^2$:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} \dot{\vec{q}}^T M(\vec{q}) \dot{\vec{q}}$$

Differentiating $T$ wrt $q_1, q_2, \dot{q}_1, \dot{q}_2$ and applying Euler–Lagrange yields:

$$M_{11} \ddot{q}_1 + M_{12} \ddot{q}_2 + C_{11}\dot{q}_1^2 + 2 C_{12}\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 + C_{22}\dot{q}_2^2 + G_1 = \tau_1$$
$$M_{21} \ddot{q}_1 + M_{22} \ddot{q}_2 + D_{11}\dot{q}_1^2 + 2 D_{12}\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 + G_2 = \tau_2$$

where the **Coriolis matrix entries** are:

$$C_{11} = -m_2 L_1 L_2 s_2, \quad C_{12} = C_{22} = -\frac{1}{2}m_2 L_1 L_2 s_2$$
$$D_{11} = -m_2 L_1 L_2 s_2, \quad D_{12} = 0$$

Gravity vector:

$$G_1 = (m_1 + m_2) g L_1 c_1 + m_2 g L_2 c_{12}$$
$$G_2 = m_2 g L_2 c_{12}$$

**Condition for gravity term to vanish:**

For a horizontal manipulator (links rotating in a plane perpendicular to gravity, i.e., $z$-axis vertical), the gravitational vector is $\vec{g} = -g \hat{z}$. If the motion plane is the $xy$-plane (horizontal), then $y$-coordinates do not change with height ⇒ $G_1 = G_2 = 0$.

Equivalently: gravity term vanishes when $\vec{g} \perp$ motion plane. **(7 marks: 2m matrix $M$, 2m matrix $C$, 1m gravity $G$, 2m vanishing condition)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Lost to Common Errors:**
>
> 1. **Missing units** — Always write $mm$, $\mu m$, $rad$, or $deg$ in the final equation. *Cost: 1–2 marks*.
> 2. **Confusing $q_2$ relative vs absolute** — $q_2$ is the joint angle *between* links 1 and 2, **not** the absolute angle of link 2 in the world frame. *Cost: 1–2 marks*.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Jacobian** — Sign depends on convention (active vs passive rotation, CCW vs CW positive). State your convention in the first line. *Cost: 1 mark*.
> 4. **Forgetting the gravity term in Lagrangian** — Even if the manipulator is horizontal, write $G(\vec q)$ as zero with an explanation. *Cost: 1 mark*.
> 5. **Writing $[\omega]_\times$ without defining it** — If you mention the skew-symmetric form, write it out:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}[\vec\omega]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}\end{aligned}$$
>
> *Cost: 1 mark*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kinematics** = geometry of motion (no forces); **Kinetics** = forces causing motion.
- **Forward Kinematics** maps joint space $\vec q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to task space $\vec X \in \mathbb{R}^m$; **Inverse Kinematics** does the reverse.
- The **Jacobian** $J(\vec q) = \partial f / \partial \vec q$ governs velocity, force, and singularity behaviour.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det(J J^T) = 0$ — the manipulator loses DOF; avoid via path planning.
- **Degrees of freedom** in 3-D space: $n_{DOF} \leq 6$; Grübler's formula gives exact count.
- **Euler–Lagrange equation**: $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal L/\partial \dot q_i) - \partial \mathcal L/\partial q_i = Q_i$.
- **Newton–Euler recursion** is faster for real-time control; **Lagrangian** is more compact analytically.
- **Micro-scale dominant forces**: viscous drag $\sim L$, surface tension $\sim L$, van der Waals $\sim L^{-2}$, gravity $\sim L^3$.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v L / \mu \ll 1$ for micro-swimmers ⇒ **Stokes regime** (inertia negligible).
- **Workspace** = reachable ∪ dexterous; **Manipulability** $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ measures dexterity.
- **Quaternion representation** avoids gimbal lock; preferred over Euler angles in modern microrobot controllers.
- **Homogeneous transformation** $T \in SE(3)$ combines rotation $R \in SO(3)$ and translation $\vec p$.
- **Pseudo-inverse** $J^+ = J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}$ gives minimum-norm joint velocity for a desired tip velocity.
- **Force mapping**: $\vec \tau = J^T \vec F$ (principle of virtual work).
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity** $g = 9.81\,m/s^2$ — but at micro-scale it is usually neglected in the dynamics model.
- **Damping** dominates micro-actuator dynamics: $\tau = b \dot q$ often replaces full inertial model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Dynamics" in Microrobotics?

In the context of microrobotics, **Dynamics** refers to the study of **forces, torques, motion equations, and time-evolution of micro-scale robotic systems** (typically in the range **1 μm to 1 mm**). It is the branch that answers the question:

> *"How does a microrobot move, what forces govern it, and how fast does it respond to actuation?"*

Mathematically, microrobotic dynamics is governed by a force/torque balance equation of the form:

$$\sum \vec{F}_i = m\vec{a} \quad \text{(Newtonian)}$$

but at the micro-scale, **inertial terms (`m·a`) become vanishingly small**, and the equation collapses into a **first-order viscous balance**:

$$\sum \vec{F}_i \approx \vec{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{F}_{\text{actuation}} = \vec{F}_{\text{drag}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> Microrobotic dynamics is the study of force/torque generation, transmission, and balance at the micro-scale, taking into account the dominance of surface forces (viscous drag, surface tension, electrostatic) over volume forces (gravity, inertia) due to the breakdown of classical scaling laws.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍃

Imagine trying to **swim in honey versus swimming in water**. As you shrink down (like a tiny robot), the world effectively becomes **vastly more viscous**, equivalent to swimming in thick honey. A bacterium in water feels the same "stickiness" we would feel in syrup.

**Key intuition for KTU students:**
- A **macro-robot** (1 m scale) → governed by **inertia** (it keeps moving after you stop pushing it).
- A **microrobot** (10 μm scale) → governed by **viscous drag** (it stops the *instant* you stop pushing it).

This is the single most important conceptual shift in microrobotics dynamics.

---

## 1.3 Why Standard Newtonian Dynamics Fails at the Micro-Scale

| Phenomenon | Macro-Robot (1 m) | Microrobot (10 μm) | Effect on Dynamics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inertia (m·a) | Dominant | **Negligible** | Pure overdamped response |
| Viscous Drag | Minor | **Dominant** | Sets terminal velocity instantly |
| Gravity | Strong | Negligible | Robots stick to surfaces (van der Waals) |
| Brownian Motion | None | Significant | Random thermal kicks matter |
| Surface Tension | Minor | **Dominant** | Liquids become "sticky cages" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants to remember (bolded for KTU exams):**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water: **μ ≈ 1 mPa·s = 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Density of water: **ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³**
> - Boltzmann constant: **k_B ≈ 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ ≈ 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Permeability of free space: **μ₀ ≈ 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Concept: Scaling Laws

The behavior of microrobots is dictated by **geometric scaling laws**. When a length scale `L` is reduced by a factor `k`, all other physical quantities scale predictably.

### Step-by-step logical breakdown:

1. **Linear dimensions** (length, width, height) → scale as **L¹**
2. **Surface area** (force-related, drag, friction) → scales as **L²**
3. **Volume / Mass** (inertia, weight) → scales as **L³**
4. **Surface-to-Volume ratio** → scales as **L²/L³ = 1/L** → **increases** as size shrinks.

**The Why:**
- Forces like drag, friction, surface tension act on **surfaces** → depend on area → L²
- Forces like gravity, inertia act on **mass** → depend on volume → L³
- When L → small, the L² forces **dominate** the L³ forces.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use LaTeX rendering. Pipes are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Dominant Regime | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi \mu r v$ | Stokes drag on sphere | Re \lt 1 | Velocity vs force |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | Re \lt 1 micro | Regime identification |
| 3 | $F_{\text{gravity}} = \rho V g \propto L^{3}$ | Body force | L \gt 1 mm | Inertia/gravity calc |
| 4 | $F_{\text{capillary}} = \gamma L \propto L^{1}$ | Surface tension | L \lt 1 mm | Liquid interface |
| 5 | $F_{\text{electrostatic}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^{2}$ | Capacitive actuation | MEMS | Parallel-plate actuator |
| 6 | $F_{\text{magnetic}} = V(\vec{M}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}$ | Magnetic actuation | Magnetic microbots | Steering force |
| 7 | $F_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{k_B T}{L}$ | Brownian force scale | L \lt 100 μm | Noise floor |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{m}{6\pi\mu r} \propto L^{2}$ | Mechanical time constant | All micro | Response speed |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu r}$ | Diffusion coefficient | Brownian | Random walk |
| 10 | $F_{\text{van der Waals}} \propto \dfrac{A_H L}{d^{2}}$ | Adhesion | L \lt 100 μm | Stiction |

**Where γ = surface tension (N/m), V = volume, M = magnetization, B = magnetic field, A_H = Hamaker constant, d = gap distance.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Magnetic microrobotic capsules** (used in targeted drug delivery): magnetic force balances Stokes drag to steer through bloodstream.
- **AFM/MEMS cantilevers**: electrostatic actuation dynamics determine scan speed.
- **Bacterial flagellar motors**: dynamics in Re \ll 1 regime with helical propulsion.
- **Electromagnetic microactuators**: used in microvalves, micropumps in lab-on-chip.

> [!TIP]
> In **Part A (3-mark) questions**, examiners expect students to write the scaling relationship (e.g., "drag force scales as L¹, inertia as L³") and state the resulting dominant regime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Does Drag Dominate? (Scaling of Force Ratio)

**Objective:** Prove that the ratio of inertial force to viscous drag force decreases as `L → 0`.

### Step 1 — Inertial force scales with volume

The inertial force is mass × acceleration. Mass scales as volume (L³), and acceleration scales as velocity/time. For a characteristic velocity `v` and time scale `t`:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = m \cdot a \propto \rho L^{3} \cdot \frac{v}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Viscous drag force scales with area

From Stokes' law for a sphere of radius `r ≈ L`:

$$F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi\mu L v \propto \mu L^{1} v$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio (this is essentially a Reynolds-type number)

$$\frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{drag}}} = \frac{\rho L^{3} (v/t)}{\mu L v} = \frac{\rho L^{2}}{\mu t}$$

For a characteristic time `t = L/v`, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{drag}}} = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = Re$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at the micro-scale

For a 10 μm particle in water with `v ≈ 100 μm/s`:

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At the micro-scale, **Re ≪ 1**, meaning **viscous drag dominates inertia by a factor of 10⁶.** This is the cornerstone result of microrobotic dynamics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mechanical Time Constant

**Objective:** Show how fast a microrobot reaches terminal velocity after actuation.

### Step 1 — Newton's second law on a sphere

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{\text{act}} - 6\pi\mu r v$$

### Step 2 — Substitute `m = (4/3)\pi r^{3}\rho`

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\rho \frac{dv}{dt} + 6\pi\mu r v = F_{\text{act}}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into standard first-order form

$$\frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{6\pi\mu r}{(4/3)\pi r^{3}\rho} v = \frac{F_{\text{act}}}{m}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the time constant τ

$$\tau = \frac{m}{6\pi\mu r} = \frac{(4/3)\pi r^{3}\rho}{6\pi\mu r} = \frac{2\rho r^{2}}{9\mu}$$

### Step 5 — Final scaling result

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{mech}} \propto L^{2}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** As the microrobot shrinks by 10×, its mechanical response becomes **100× faster**. This is why micro-actuators can operate at kHz–MHz frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 10 μm sphere in water: **τ ≈ 2.2 μs**, which is essentially instantaneous in human terms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Terminal Velocity under Magnetic Actuation

**Objective:** Find the steady-state velocity of a magnetic microrobot pulled by a magnetic field gradient.

### Step 1 — At terminal velocity, net force = 0

$$F_{\text{mag}} = F_{\text{drag}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute expressions

$$V_{\text{robot}}(\vec{M}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B} = 6\pi\mu r v_{\text{term}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for velocity

$$v_{\text{term}} = \frac{V_{\text{robot}}(\vec{M}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}}{6\pi\mu r}$$

### Step 4 — Express volume in terms of radius

For a sphere, $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$, so:

$$v_{\text{term}} = \frac{(4/3)\pi r^{3}(\vec{M}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}}{6\pi\mu r} = \frac{2 r^{2}(\vec{M}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}}{9\mu}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Terminal velocity scales as **L²** — halving the size reduces velocity by 4×, a critical design constraint in targeted drug delivery.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — [KTU University Exam — May 2024])
**Q: Define microrobotic dynamics. Why does viscous drag dominate at the micro-scale?**

**Model Answer:**
Microrobotic dynamics is the study of motion equations, force/torque balances, and time response of robots operating at sub-millimeter scales. Viscous drag dominates because:

1. Drag force scales as **L¹** (surface-related).
2. Inertial force scales as **L³** (volume-related).
3. As `L → 0`, the ratio `F_inertia / F_drag → 0` (Reynolds number Re ≪ 1).
4. Hence, motion is overdamped: `F_actuation = F_drag` instantaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "drag increases at micro-scale" — this is wrong. Drag **decreases** in absolute terms, but its **relative dominance over inertia** increases. Always compare the ratio, not the absolute value.

---

### Question 2 (Definition — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023])
**Q: What is the Reynolds number, and what does its value signify in microrobotic dynamics?**

**Model Answer:**
The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity comparing inertial to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

- **Re ≫ 1:** Inertia dominates (macro-scale, turbulent flow).
- **Re ≪ 1:** Viscous forces dominate (micro-scale, Stokes flow).
- For microrobots, Re typically lies between **10⁻⁶ and 1**, meaning motion is dominated by viscous dissipation and governed by first-order dynamics.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** rules, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Solve either one completely.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: A spherical magnetic microrobot of radius `r = 10 μm` and magnetization `M = 10⁵ A/m` is subjected to a magnetic field gradient `∇B = 0.1 T/m` in water (μ = 10⁻³ Pa·s).**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for the microrobot and find its **terminal velocity**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Calculate the **mechanical time constant** and comment on its significance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** Equation of motion and terminal velocity

For a magnetic sphere in a viscous medium:

$$m\frac{dv}{dt} = F_{\text{mag}} - F_{\text{drag}}$$

$$m\frac{dv}{dt} = V_{\text{robot}} M \nabla B - 6\pi\mu r v$$

At terminal velocity, `dv/dt = 0`:

$$V_{\text{robot}} M \nabla B = 6\pi\mu r v_{\text{term}}$$

Volume of the sphere:

$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (10 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 4.19 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^{3}$$

Substituting:

$$v_{\text{term}} = \frac{4.19 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{5} \times 0.1}{6\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$v_{\text{term}} = \frac{4.19 \times 10^{-11}}{1.88 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\text{term}} \approx 222 \text{ μm/s}}$$

**Part (b):** Mechanical time constant

$$\tau = \frac{2\rho r^{2}}{9\mu} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times (10^{-5})^{2}}{9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\tau = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 22.2 \text{ μs}$$

**Significance:**
- The microrobot reaches 63% of terminal velocity in just **22.2 μs**.
- This shows that **micro-scale systems are extremely fast-responding** compared to macro robots.
- For KTU design: **higher viscosity medium → slower response**; **larger microrobot → slower response**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students forget to use `r` (radius) instead of `d` (diameter) in Stokes' formula. **Stokes' drag uses radius**, not diameter.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: A parallel-plate electrostatic microactuator has plates of area `A = 100 \times 100 μm²`, separated by gap `d = 2 μm`, with applied voltage `V = 10 V`. The moving plate has mass `m = 10^{-10}` kg and moves in air (μ_air ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s).**

**(a)** Derive the **electrostatic force** equation and compute its magnitude. (7 marks)
**(b)** If the actuator reaches terminal velocity in steady state, find the **steady-state velocity** and discuss the **scaling behavior**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** Electrostatic force derivation

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

The energy stored:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2d}$$

The force (negative gradient of energy with respect to gap):

$$F_{\text{elec}} = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$$

Numerical computation:

$$F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (10^{-8}) \times (10)^{2}}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

$$F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-19}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{elec}} \approx 110.7 \text{ nN}}$$

**Part (b):** Steady-state velocity from force balance

At steady state:

$$F_{\text{elec}} = F_{\text{drag}} = c \cdot v$$

where the damping coefficient `c = 6πμr` for a sphere, but for a plate in squeeze-film damping:

$$c \approx \frac{\mu A}{d^{3}}$$

$$c = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-8}}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-13}}{8 \times 10^{-18}} = 2.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ Ns/m}$$

Steady-state velocity:

$$v_{\text{ss}} = \frac{F_{\text{elec}}}{c} = \frac{1.107 \times 10^{-7}}{2.25 \times 10^{4}} = 4.92 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m/s}$$

**Scaling discussion:**
- Electrostatic force scales as **1/d²** — smaller gaps give quadratically larger force.
- Damping scales as **1/d³** — even more sensitive to gap, leading to **velocity decreasing with gap³**.
- Hence, **narrower gaps enhance force but suppress motion speed**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Loss Alert:** Many students skip the derivation of the electrostatic force and directly write the formula. **KTU examiners expect a derivation** in Part (a) for full marks. Always start from `U = ½CV²`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- [x] **Dynamics** = force/torque balance + motion equation + time response at micro-scale.
- [x] **Scaling laws:** Linear ∝ L¹, Area ∝ L², Volume ∝ L³, Surface/Volume ∝ 1/L.
- [x] **Re = ρvL/μ ≪ 1** for microrobots → Stokes regime.
- [x] **Stokes drag:** `F = 6πμrv`.
- [x] **Mechanical time constant:** `τ = 2ρr²/(9μ) ∝ L²`.
- [x] **Terminal velocity under magnetic force:** `v_term = 2r²(M·∇)B / (9μ) ∝ L²`.
- [x] **Electrostatic force:** `F = ε₀AV²/(2d²)`.
- [x] **Surface tension force:** `F_γ = γL ∝ L¹`.
- [x] **Brownian motion:** Random kicks of scale `F_T = k_B T / L`.
- [x] **Diffusion coefficient:** `D = k_B T / (6πμr)`.
- [x] **Van der Waals adhesion:** `F_vdW ∝ A_H L / d²`.
- [x] Always mention **Re ≪ 1** when justifying viscous dominance.
- [x] Always state the **scaling law** when explaining force dominance.
- [x] For magnetic actuation: `F_mag = V(M·∇)B`.
- [x] Remember constants: `μ_water = 10⁻³ Pa·s`, `ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m`, `k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K`.
- [x] For Stokes drag, use **radius**, not diameter.

> **End of Notes — Module 1 / Topic: Dynamics | PERAT746 — MICROROBOTICS | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Linear and Angular Momentum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Angular Momentum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Momentum — Formal Definition

In classical mechanics (and as adopted in the KTU PERAT746 framework), **Linear Momentum** is defined as the product of a body's mass and its instantaneous linear velocity vector. It is a fundamental vector quantity that quantifies the "quantity of motion" possessed by a moving body and is the direct conjugate of position in translational mechanics.

$$
\vec{p} \;=\; m\,\vec{v}
$$

where:
- $\vec{p}$ = linear momentum vector $\left[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}\right]$
- $m$ = mass of the body $\left[\text{kg}\right]$
- $\vec{v}$ = instantaneous linear velocity vector $\left[\text{m/s}\right]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Linear momentum is a *vectorial* quantity. Always express it with both magnitude **AND** direction. In 3D Cartesian form: $\vec{p} = p_x\hat{i} + p_y\hat{j} + p_z\hat{k}$.

## 1.2 Angular Momentum — Formal Definition

**Angular Momentum** is the rotational analogue of linear momentum. It is the moment of linear momentum taken about a chosen reference point (typically the origin or the center of mass). For a point particle, it is defined as the cross product of the position vector and the linear momentum vector. For a rigid body, it is expressed through the moment-of-inertia tensor.

$$
\vec{L} \;=\; \vec{r}\times\vec{p} \;=\; \vec{r}\times(m\vec{v})
$$

For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis:

$$
\vec{L} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega}
$$

where:
- $\vec{L}$ = angular momentum vector $\left[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}\right]$
- $\vec{r}$ = position vector from reference point $\left[\text{m}\right]$
- $\vec{p}$ = linear momentum $\left[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}\right]$
- $I$ = moment of inertia about the rotation axis $\left[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2\right]$
- $\vec{\omega}$ = angular velocity vector $\left[\text{rad/s}\right]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Anchors:**
> - The SI unit of linear momentum is **$\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$** (also called *Newton-second*, $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$).
> - The SI unit of angular momentum is **$\text{J}\cdot\text{s}$** (Joule-second), dimensionally equivalent to $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing two different shopping carts down a supermarket aisle:

- **Light cart** rolling slowly at $1\;\text{m/s}$ vs. **heavy cart** rolling slowly at $1\;\text{m/s}$ — the heavy one is much harder to stop. That "hardness to stop" is essentially *linear momentum*. Heavier object, same speed → more momentum.

- Now imagine spinning a merry-go-round slowly vs. spinning it with weights placed far from its center. The weighted version is much harder to stop rotating. That rotational "hardness to stop" is *angular momentum*.

### Real-World Microrobotics Analogy

Picture a tiny microrobot (mass $\approx 10^{-6}\;\text{kg}$) swimming through a fluid. Because its mass is minuscule, its **linear momentum is incredibly small**, meaning even tiny viscous drag forces can halt it almost instantly. Conversely, when it spins its flagella for propulsion, the **angular momentum stored in the rotating helical tail** is what governs its torque output and turning behavior. In microrobotics, angular momentum often dominates the design analysis because:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At micro-scales, *inertial forces scale with volume ($L^3$)* while *viscous/drag forces scale with surface area ($L^2$)*. Hence viscous effects (linear momentum dissipation) overwhelm inertia — this is the heart of **low Reynolds number locomotion** central to PERAT746.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Second Law — The Momentum Formulation

The most operationally useful form of Newton's second law, especially for variable-mass systems (which is directly relevant to microrobots ejecting propellant, ions, or reacting chemicals), is the **momentum rate form**:

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt}
$$

For *constant-mass* systems, this reduces to the familiar $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$.

### Logical Breakdown for Variable-Mass Microrobotic Systems

- **Step 1:** Identify the control volume (the microrobot body plus any propellant mass it exchanges).
- **Step 2:** Recognize that momentum flows *across* the boundary (mass leaving or entering the system).
- **Step 3:** Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(m_{\text{body}}\vec{v}_{\text{body}}\right) - \vec{v}_{\text{rel}}\frac{dm}{dt}
$$

- **Step 4:** For a microrobot ejecting mass backward (like a rocket or ion-thruster), $\vec{v}_{\text{rel}}$ is the *relative* ejection velocity — this is the **Tsiolkovsky-style analysis** adapted for micro-thrusters.

## 2.2 Angular Impulse and Torque Coupling

The **angular** analogue of Newton's second law ties torque to the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$
\vec{\tau}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(I\vec{\omega}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relationship is critical when analyzing **magnetic actuation of microrobots**. The external magnetic field applies a torque on the dipole moment of a magnetized micro-particle, causing a controlled change in angular momentum — this is the principle behind wireless steering of magnetic microrobots (a hot KTU research area).

## 2.3 Conservation Laws

### Conservation of Linear Momentum
When the **net external force on a system is zero**, the total linear momentum is conserved:

$$
\text{If } \sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \vec{0}, \quad \text{then} \quad \vec{p}_{\text{total}} = \text{constant}
$$

### Conservation of Angular Momentum
When the **net external torque on a system is zero**, the total angular momentum is conserved:

$$
\text{If } \sum \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = \vec{0}, \quad \text{then} \quad \vec{L}_{\text{total}} = \text{constant}
$$

This implies, for a system whose moment of inertia changes (such as a microrobot unfolding its limbs):

$$
I_1\vec{\omega}_1 \;=\; I_2\vec{\omega}_2
$$

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Microrobotic Momentum Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Microrobotics:** The Reynolds number $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ becomes vanishingly small ($Re \ll 1$) at micro-scales. This means:
> 1. Inertia (momentum storage) is negligible compared to viscous dissipation.
> 2. Motion is governed by the **Stokes regime**: $\vec{F}_{\text{drag}} = -6\pi\mu r\vec{v}$ for a sphere.
> 3. Microrobots must use **non-reciprocal motion** (e.g., rotating flagella, travelling-wave cilia) to achieve net displacement — exactly as explained by Purcell's *Scallop Theorem*.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Applicable Domain | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Linear Momentum (point mass) | $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ | All classical mechanics | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | Newton's 2nd Law (momentum form) | $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$ | Constant and variable mass systems | $\text{N}$ |
| 3 | Angular Momentum (point mass) | $\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times\vec{p}$ | Orbital motion, central forces | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | Angular Momentum (rigid body) | $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ | Rotating rigid bodies | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Torque–Angular Momentum Coupling | $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$ | All rotational dynamics | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| 6 | Conservation of Linear Momentum | $\sum \vec{p}_i = \text{const.}$ | Isolated systems / no external force | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | Conservation of Angular Momentum | $\sum \vec{L}_i = \text{const.}$ | Isolated systems / no external torque | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | Impulse–Momentum Theorem | $\vec{J} = \int \vec{F}\,dt = \Delta\vec{p}$ | Collisions, impulsive forces | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 9 | Angular Impulse | $\vec{J}_{\text{ang}} = \int \vec{\tau}\,dt = \Delta\vec{L}$ | Rotational collisions, kick-starts | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | Microrobotic locomotion | dimensionless |
| 11 | Stokes Drag (sphere) | $\vec{F}_{d} = -6\pi\mu r\vec{v}$ | $Re \ll 1$ flow regime | $\text{N}$ |
| 12 | Kinetic Energy (translation) | $KE = \dfrac{p^2}{2m}$ | Momentum-to-energy conversion | $\text{J}$ |
| 13 | Rotational KE (momentum form) | $KE_{\text{rot}} = \dfrac{L^2}{2I}$ | Rotational energy analysis | $\text{J}$ |
| 14 | Variable-mass thrust equation | $\vec{F}_{\text{thrust}} = -\vec{v}_{\text{ej}}\dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | Micro-thrusters, ion drives | $\text{N}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** In Table row 2, $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt$ is the **general** form. For *constant* mass, you may replace it with $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$. For *variable* mass (rockets, evaporating droplets, propellant-ejecting microrobots), DO NOT cancel the $m$ — this is the most common conceptual error in KTU valuation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of linear and angular momentum are foundational in:

- **Magnetic microrobot steering** — angular momentum exchange between external coils and embedded magnetic dipoles.
- **Micro-propulsion systems** — cold-gas thrusters, ionic wind thrusters, and laser-ablative thrusters all rely on momentum conservation.
- **Cellular-scale robotics** — modeling bacterial flagellar motors where angular momentum transfer at the molecular scale produces thrust.
- **MEMS gyroscopes** — vibrating-ring gyroscopes exploit Coriolis-induced angular momentum transfer for inertial sensing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body from First Principles

We will rigorously derive the rigid-body angular momentum expression $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ starting from its definition $\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times\vec{p}$ for a system of particles, then specializing to a continuous rigid body.

### Starting Postulate

Consider a rigid body composed of $N$ discrete point masses $m_i$, each located at position $\vec{r}_i$ relative to an inertial reference frame. Each particle has linear momentum $\vec{p}_i = m_i\vec{v}_i$.

### Step 1 — Total Angular Momentum as a Sum

The total angular momentum of the system about the origin O is the vector sum of the individual angular momenta:

$$
\vec{L}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{L}_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{r}_i \times \vec{p}_i
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the direct application of the definition $\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times\vec{p}$ to each particle, then summing. The cross-product preserves the rotational character; we cannot simply add the magnitudes — direction matters.

### Step 2 — Rigid-Body Kinematics Constraint

For a *rigid* body, every particle's velocity is constrained by the body's instantaneous angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ (relative to a fixed point on or about the body):

$$
\vec{v}_i \;=\; \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This equation captures the fact that in pure rotation (no translation of the reference point), each point's velocity is tangent to its circular path around the rotation axis. The cross-product structure is geometrically mandated.

### Step 3 — Substitution into Angular Momentum

Substituting $\vec{v}_i$ into $\vec{p}_i$:

$$
\vec{L}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \vec{v}_i) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \,\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We replaced $\vec{p}_i$ with $m_i\vec{v}_i$, then $\vec{v}_i$ with $\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i$.

### Step 4 — Application of the BAC-CAB Vector Identity

Apply the vector triple product identity:

$$
\vec{A}\times(\vec{B}\times\vec{C}) \;=\; \vec{B}(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{C}) - \vec{C}(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B})
$$

With $\vec{A}=\vec{r}_i$, $\vec{B}=\vec{\omega}$, $\vec{C}=\vec{r}_i$:

$$
\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i) \;=\; \vec{\omega}(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{r}_i) - \vec{r}_i(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega})
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i) \;=\; \vec{\omega}\, r_i^2 - \vec{r}_i (\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega})
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The triple product identity "unwraps" the inner cross-product into a linear combination. Note that $\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{r}_i = \vert\vec{r}_i\vert^2 = r_i^2$ (a scalar).

### Step 5 — Factoring Out the Angular Velocity

Substituting back into the sum:

$$
\vec{L}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left[\vec{\omega}\, r_i^2 - \vec{r}_i(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega})\right]
$$

$$
\vec{L}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\!\left(r_i^2 \mathbb{1} - \vec{r}_i \vec{r}_i\right)\right] \vec{\omega}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We factored out $\vec{\omega}$ as common to both terms, recognizing that $\vec{r}_i(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega})$ is linear in $\vec{\omega}$ (dot product with a vector produces a scalar coefficient for $\vec{r}_i$). The bracketed term is the **moment-of-inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}$.

### Step 6 — Definition of the Inertia Tensor

We arrive at the final compact expression:

$$
\vec{L}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}
$$

For rotation about a single principal axis (e.g., the z-axis), the tensor reduces to the scalar moment of inertia $I = \sum m_i r_{i\perp}^2$, giving:

$$
\vec{L} \;=\; I\vec{\omega}
$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Conservation of Linear Momentum from Newton's Third Law

### Step 1 — Two Interacting Particles

Consider two particles, masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, interacting via an internal force pair (no external forces). Let $\vec{F}_{12}$ be the force on $m_1$ due to $m_2$, and $\vec{F}_{21}$ be the force on $m_2$ due to $m_1$.

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Third Law

$$
\vec{F}_{12} \;=\; -\vec{F}_{21}
$$

### Step 3 — Write Newton's Second Law for Each Particle

$$
\vec{F}_{12} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{p}_1}{dt}, \quad \vec{F}_{21} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{p}_2}{dt}
$$

### Step 4 — Add the Two Equations

$$
\vec{F}_{12} + \vec{F}_{21} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{p}_1}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{p}_2}{dt}
$$

$$
\vec{0} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}(\vec{p}_1 + \vec{p}_2) \;=\; \frac{d\vec{P}_{\text{total}}}{dt}
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate

$$
\vec{P}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \vec{p}_1 + \vec{p}_2 \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

This proves **conservation of linear momentum** for an isolated two-particle system, and the proof generalizes by induction to $N$ particles.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Microrobot Propulsion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A spherical microrobot of mass $m = 5\times 10^{-6}\;\text{kg}$ moves through a fluid (viscosity $\mu = 10^{-3}\;\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, density $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^3$) at velocity $v = 200\;\mu\text{m/s}$. Its radius is $r = 50\;\mu\text{m}$. Compute the Reynolds number and the linear momentum.

### Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{(1000)(200\times 10^{-6})(2\times 50\times 10^{-6})}{10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{(1000)(200\times 10^{-6})(100\times 10^{-6})}{10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{2\times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} \;=\; 2\times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\boxed{Re = 0.02 \;\ll\; 1}
$$

**Conclusion:** Stokes regime confirmed — viscous forces dominate inertial momentum storage.

### Step 2 — Compute Linear Momentum

$$
p \;=\; mv \;=\; (5\times 10^{-6})(200\times 10^{-6}) \;=\; 1\times 10^{-9}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{p = 10^{-9}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s} = 1\;\text{nN}\cdot\text{s}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Such an extraordinarily tiny momentum ($1$ nano-Newton-second) explains why a microscopic disturbance can completely halt such a robot — momentum storage is negligible.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Momentum Regimes in Microrobotics

| Regime | Dominant Force | Reynolds Number | Motion Strategy | KTU Example |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Macro-scale ($L > 1\;\text{mm}$) | Inertia | $Re \gg 1$ | Reciprocal OK (fish-like) | Quadrotor micro-drone |
| Meso-scale ($L \sim 100\;\mu\text{m}$) | Mixed | $Re \sim 1$ | Transitional | Mini-submarine |
| Micro-scale ($L < 10\;\mu\text{m}$) | Viscosity | $Re \ll 1$ | **Non-reciprocal** (flagella, helix) | Bacterial-inspired swimmers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Momentum Flow in a Magnetically-Actuated Microrobot

The diagram below maps the functional flow of linear and angular momentum transfer from an external magnetic actuator through to the microrobot's translational motion in a fluid medium.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Magnetic Coil Array] -->|Applies Torque tau| B[Magnetic Dipole on Microrobot]
    B -->|tau = dL_dt| C[Change in Angular Momentum dL]
    C -->|Rotates| D[Helical Flagellum / Soft Tail]
    D -->|Coupled Rotation| E[Angular Momentum Transfer to Fluid]
    E -->|Viscous Shear Reaction| F[Net Linear Thrust Force F_thrust]
    F -->|F = dp_dt| G[Linear Momentum p of Robot]
    G -->|Overcomes Stokes Drag| H[Net Translational Displacement dx]
    H -->|Closes Loop| I[Steering Controller Feedback]
    I -->|Modulates Coil Currents| A
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I → A) represents the closed-loop control architecture.
- The **inner sequence** (C → F) is the angular-to-linear momentum conversion — the critical microrobotic principle.
- The **terminal node H** is the observable physical output (position), which is fed back to refine actuation.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Concept Map — Linear vs. Angular Momentum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[Momentum Theory Foundations]
    rootA --> lin[Linear Momentum Branch]
    rootA --> ang[Angular Momentum Branch]
    lin --> linDef[p = m * v]
    lin --> linLaw[F = dp_dt]
    lin --> linCons[Conservation: Sum p = const]
    ang --> angDefPt[L = r cross p]
    ang --> angDefRB[L = I * omega]
    ang --> angLaw[tau = dL_dt]
    ang --> angCons[Conservation: Sum L = const]
    linLaw --> microApp1[Variable Mass Thrust in Micro Thrusters]
    angLaw --> microApp2[Magnetic Dipole Steering]
    linCons --> microApp3[Swimmer Fluid Momentum Exchange]
    angCons --> microApp4[Flagellar Rotation Dynamics]
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The two main branches (**Linear** and **Angular**) share the same *structural architecture*: definition → governing law → conservation principle → microrobotic application.
- This symmetry is a powerful mnemonic for KTU examinations — once you master one branch, the other follows by analogy (just replace $m \to I$ and $\vec{v} \to \vec{\omega}$).

## 4.3 Schematic — Force/Momentum Vector Diagram for a Microrobot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph forces[Force and Momentum Vectors on Microrobot]
        dir1[Drag Force F_drag opposes v]:::vec
        dir2[Thrust F_thrust along v]:::vec
        dir3[Linear Momentum p = m * v]:::vec
        dir4[Angular Momentum L = I * omega along axis]:::vec
    end
    classDef vec fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Interpretation:** This block-style architecture shows the four canonical vector quantities co-existing on a microrobot. The drag and thrust vectors are *collinear* (along the translation axis), while the angular momentum vector lies along the rotation axis (often perpendicular to translation).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define linear momentum and angular momentum. State their SI units and the corresponding conservation laws.**

**Model Answer:**

Linear momentum is the product of a body's mass and its linear velocity: $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$. Its SI unit is $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$ (equivalent to $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$). According to the **Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum**, the total linear momentum of an isolated system (one with no net external force) remains constant.

Angular momentum is the rotational analogue, defined as $\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times\vec{p}$ for a point mass, or $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ for a rigid body. Its SI unit is $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (equivalent to $\text{J}\cdot\text{s}$). The **Law of Conservation of Angular Momentum** states that if the net external torque on a system is zero, its total angular momentum remains constant.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the concept of "non-reciprocal motion" essential for microrobotic propulsion at low Reynolds numbers? Relate this to momentum considerations.**

**Model Answer:**

At micro-scales, the Reynolds number $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ becomes very small ($Re \ll 1$), placing the microrobot in the **Stokes flow regime** where viscous forces overwhelm inertial forces. Linear momentum imparted to the fluid during one half of a stroke is almost immediately dissipated by viscosity; on the return stroke, equal and opposite momentum is imparted, resulting in *zero net displacement* — this is **Purcell's Scallop Theorem**.

To achieve net propulsion, the microrobot must execute **non-reciprocal motion** — a motion pattern whose time-reversal is not identical to itself. Examples include rotating helical flagella and travelling-wave cilia. This breaks the symmetry of momentum exchange with the fluid, allowing a *net linear thrust* to be generated from rotational (angular momentum) actuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, conventionally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve both sub-parts completely to secure full marks.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Alternative 1

#### (a) Derive the expression for the angular momentum of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The body is rigid (inter-particle distances remain constant).
2. Rotation occurs about a fixed axis (the z-axis, without loss of generality).
3. The reference frame is inertial.

**Derivation:**

For a rigid body composed of $N$ particles, the total angular momentum about the origin is:

$$
\vec{L} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{r}_i \times \vec{p}_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\, \vec{r}_i \times \vec{v}_i
$$

For pure rotation about the z-axis with angular velocity $\vec{\omega} = \omega\hat{k}$:

$$
\vec{v}_i \;=\; \vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i
$$

Substituting:

$$
\vec{L} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\,\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i)
$$

Apply the BAC-CAB identity:

$$
\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_i) \;=\; \vec{\omega}(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{r}_i) - \vec{r}_i(\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega})
$$

Since $\vec{\omega} = \omega\hat{k}$ is along z, only the z-component of $\vec{r}_i$ contributes to $\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega}$:

$$
\vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{\omega} \;=\; \omega z_i, \quad \vec{r}_i\cdot\vec{r}_i \;=\; x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2
$$

The z-component of $\vec{L}$ becomes:

$$
L_z \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left[\omega(x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2) - z_i^2 \omega\right] \;=\; \omega\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(x_i^2 + y_i^2)
$$

Defining the moment of inertia about the z-axis:

$$
I_z \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(x_i^2 + y_i^2)
$$

We obtain:

$$
\boxed{\vec{L} \;=\; I_z\,\vec{\omega}}
$$

**Validity Note:** For a general rigid body with an arbitrary rotation axis, the relation becomes $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor (a $3\times 3$ matrix). The scalar relation $L = I\omega$ holds *only* when $\vec{\omega}$ is along a principal axis.

#### (b) A microrobot of mass $2\times 10^{-6}\;\text{kg}$ is propelled by a rotating helical tail with moment of inertia $5\times 10^{-18}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$, spinning at $200\;\text{rad/s}$. Compute (i) the angular momentum of the tail, and (ii) the linear momentum of the robot if it moves at $1\;\text{mm/s}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Angular Momentum of the Tail:**

$$
L \;=\; I\omega \;=\; (5\times 10^{-18})(200) \;=\; 1\times 10^{-15}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 10^{-15}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}}
$$

**(ii) Linear Momentum of the Robot:**

$$
p \;=\; mv \;=\; (2\times 10^{-6})(1\times 10^{-3}) \;=\; 2\times 10^{-9}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{p = 2\times 10^{-9}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}}
$$

**Comparative Insight:** The ratio $L/p$ has units of length, characterizing the *effective arm* through which rotational motion is converted to translational motion:

$$
L/p \;=\; 10^{-15} / 10^{-9} \;=\; 10^{-6}\;\text{m} \;=\; 1\;\mu\text{m}
$$

This micrometer-scale "lever arm" is consistent with the dimensions of micro-actuators.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative 2

#### (a) Explain the Reynolds number and discuss its significance in determining whether linear or angular momentum dominates the dynamics of a microrobot.

**Model Solution:**

The **Reynolds number** is a dimensionless quantity that compares inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density $\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$
- $v$ = characteristic velocity $\left[\text{m/s}\right]$
- $L$ = characteristic length $\left[\text{m}\right]$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity $\left[\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}\right]$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- When $Re \gg 1$: inertial forces (linked to **linear momentum** storage) dominate. Motion persists even after the driving force ceases.
- When $Re \ll 1$: viscous forces dominate. The fluid has no "memory" — any imparted linear momentum is dissipated almost instantly.

**Significance for Microrobotics:**

The Reynolds number scales linearly with the characteristic length $L$. As microrobots shrink to micrometer scales, $Re$ plummets to values like $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$. At such scales:
- Linear momentum ($p = mv$) is negligible because $m \propto L^3$.
- The robot cannot coast or glide; it must continuously actuate.
- Propulsion is achieved by *angular momentum-based* mechanisms (rotating helical flagella, beating cilia) that produce net time-averaged thrust through non-reciprocal motion.

Hence, while macro-scale robots (drones, cars) rely primarily on **linear momentum management**, microrobots rely heavily on **angular momentum generation and conversion**.

#### (b) A magnetic microrobot of mass $m = 3\times 10^{-7}\;\text{kg}$ is placed in a rotating magnetic field of $50\;\text{Hz}$. The torque applied by the field on the robot's magnetic dipole is $\tau = 1\times 10^{-12}\;\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$. If the robot's moment of inertia about its spin axis is $I = 2\times 10^{-18}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$, calculate (i) the angular acceleration of the robot, and (ii) the angular momentum after $0.5\;\text{s}$, assuming it starts from rest.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Angular Acceleration:**

Using $\tau = I\alpha$:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\tau}{I} \;=\; \frac{1\times 10^{-12}}{2\times 10^{-18}} \;=\; 5\times 10^{5}\;\text{rad/s}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = 5\times 10^{5}\;\text{rad/s}^2}
$$

**(ii) Angular Momentum after $0.5\;\text{s}$:**

First, find the angular velocity at $t = 0.5\;\text{s}$:

$$
\omega \;=\; \omega_0 + \alpha t \;=\; 0 + (5\times 10^{5})(0.5) \;=\; 2.5\times 10^{5}\;\text{rad/s}
$$

Then, compute angular momentum:

$$
L \;=\; I\omega \;=\; (2\times 10^{-18})(2.5\times 10^{5}) \;=\; 5\times 10^{-13}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 5\times 10^{-13}\;\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}}
$$

**Verification using $\tau = dL/dt$:**

$$
\frac{dL}{dt} \;=\; I\alpha \;=\; (2\times 10^{-18})(5\times 10^{5}) \;=\; 1\times 10^{-12}\;\text{N}\cdot\text{m} \;=\; \tau \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Momentum Problems:**
>
> 1. **Variable-mass trap (4 marks lost typically):** Applying $F = ma$ directly to rockets, evaporating droplets, or ion-thruster microrobots. Always use $F = dp/dt$ as the master equation.
>
> 2. **Scalar vs. vector confusion (2 marks):** Writing "$L = mv r$" instead of "$\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times m\vec{v}$". The cross-product is essential — angular momentum is a *vector* perpendicular to the plane of motion.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign in torque equation (1 mark):** Torque can be positive or negative depending on the sense of rotation; convention matters.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to specify the reference point (2 marks):** Angular momentum depends on the *choice of origin*. Always state "about point O" or "about the center of mass".
>
> 5. **Mixing up $I$ and $mr^2$ (1 mark):** Moment of inertia depends on the *distribution* of mass, not just total mass. For a sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$, $I = \frac{2}{5}mr^2$ (solid) or $\frac{2}{3}mr^2$ (hollow).
>
> 6. **Ignoring Reynolds number context (3 marks):** In microrobotics questions, always comment on $Re$ to demonstrate understanding of the relevant physics regime.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Linear and Angular Momentum in Microrobotics**

- **Linear Momentum Definition:** $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ — a vector with SI unit $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.
- **Angular Momentum Definition:** $\vec{L} = \vec{r}\times\vec{p}$ (point mass) and $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ (rigid body) — SI unit $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Master Equations (Newton's 2nd Law generalized):** $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt$ and $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$.
- **Conservation Laws:**
  - Linear: conserved when $\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \vec{0}$.
  - Angular: conserved when $\sum \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = \vec{0}$.
- **Impulse Forms:** $\vec{J} = \int \vec{F}\,dt = \Delta\vec{p}$ and $\vec{J}_{\text{ang}} = \int \vec{\tau}\,dt = \Delta\vec{L}$.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho v L/\mu$ — defines whether inertia or viscosity dominates.
- **Stokes Drag:** $\vec{F}_d = -6\pi\mu r\vec{v}$ — the dominant opposing force for $Re \ll 1$ microrobots.
- **Scaling Laws:** Inertia $\propto L^3$, viscous force $\propto L^2$ → viscosity wins at small scales.
- **Purcell's Scallop Theorem:** Reciprocal motion yields zero net displacement at low $Re$; non-reciprocal motion (helical rotation, travelling waves) is mandatory for propulsion.
- **Vector Algebra:** Always remember the BAC-CAB identity $\vec{A}\times(\vec{B}\times\vec{C}) = \vec{B}(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{C}) - \vec{C}(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B})$ — used in deriving $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$.
- **Microrobotic Applications:** Magnetic steering (angular momentum transfer), helical swimming (angular-to-linear conversion), micro-thrusters (variable-mass momentum ejection), MEMS gyroscopes (Coriolis-induced $\vec{L}$ change).
- **Exam Strategy:** Always state the reference frame, units, and assumptions before launching into calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
