# Module 1 — Equation of Motion

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In microrobotics, the **Equation of Motion (EOM)** is the time-dependent differential equation that governs the position, velocity, and acceleration of a micro-scale body under the influence of actuation forces, restoring forces, damping forces, and disturbance forces. For a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) microrobot actuated against a compliant suspension, the canonical form is a **second-order linear/non-linear ordinary differential equation**:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F_{\text{act}}(t) + F_{\text{dist}}(t)$$

where $m$ is the effective mass, $b$ is the total damping coefficient, $k$ is the suspension stiffness, $x(t)$ is the displacement of the moving structure, and the right-hand side contains actuation and disturbance forces.

> [!NOTE]
> At the micro-scale (characteristic length $L < 1\,\text{mm}$), **surface forces dominate volumetric forces**. Consequently, the inertia term $m\ddot{x}$ often becomes negligible, reducing the second-order EOM to a first-order differential equation. This is the single most important behavioural shift when scaling from macro to micro.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honey-Balloon in Syrup"

Imagine pushing a small rubber ball through a tub of thick honey with a spring-loaded paddle. At macro scale, the ball coasts freely once pushed (inertia dominates). At micro scale:

- The ball is so tiny that **inertia is irrelevant** — the moment you stop pushing, viscous honey halts it instantly.
- The paddle's spring tries to pull it back (restoring force).
- The honey resists motion with friction proportional to velocity (damping).
- Tiny electrostatic "stickiness" from surfaces pulls the ball sideways (surface forces).

The EOM is simply the mathematical bookkeeping of *push = spring + honey-drag + stuckness*, written for every instant $t$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantities appear repeatedly in micro-scale EOM derivations:

- **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{N·s/m}^2$
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 1.00 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N·s/m}^2$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$
- **Density of silicon (common MEMS material):** $\rho_{\text{Si}} \approx 2330\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- **Typical Reynolds number at microscale:** $Re \ll 1$ (Stokes / creeping flow regime)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The dominant behavioural characteristic of any microrobot is the **low Reynolds number regime** ($Re \ll 1$). Whenever $Re < 1$, viscous forces outweigh inertial forces and Stokes' law replaces Newton's drag relation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Force Inventory Acting on a Microrobot

To write the EOM, every force acting on the body must be catalogued. At micro-scale these forces split into four families:

1. **Actuation Forces (driving input)**
   - Electrostatic: $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,V^2$
   - Electromagnetic (Lorentz): $F_{\text{em}} = BIL$
   - Piezoelectric: $F_p = d_{33}\,E\,A$
   - Thermal (bimorph): $F_{\text{th}} \propto \Delta T$

2. **Restoring / Suspension Forces**
   - Mechanical spring: $F_s = -kx$
   - Torsional beam: $F_\theta = -k_\theta\,\theta$

3. **Damping Forces**
   - Viscous (squeeze-film): $F_d = -b\dot{x}$
   - Couette/Poiseuille flow: $F_d = -\dfrac{6\pi\mu r\,v}{1+\tfrac{3}{8}\,\lambda^{-1}}$ (Stokes-Cunningham correction)

4. **Disturbance / Surface Forces**
   - van der Waals: $F_{\text{vdW}} = -\dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^2}$
   - Capillary: $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$
   - Casimir: $F_C = -\dfrac{\pi^2 \hbar c}{240\,d^4}\,A$
   - Electrostatic stiction: $F_{\text{st}} = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are surface forces dominant at small scales?** Because surface force scales as $L^2$ while volume (inertial) force scales as $L^3$. As $L \to \mu$m, the ratio $L^2/L^3 = 1/L$ diverges, making surface forces overwhelming.

## 2.2 Why and How the EOM is Structured

The EOM is fundamentally an application of **Newton's Second Law**, $\sum F = m\ddot{x}$, but with three engineering refinements specific to microrobotics:

- **Refinement 1 — Neglect of inertia when $Re \ll 1$:** Since Stokes drag $F_{\text{Stokes}} \propto \mu r v$ and inertial force $F_{\text{inertia}} \propto \rho L^3 v/\tau$, the ratio scales as $Re = \rho v L/\mu$. For $Re < 1$, viscous drag suppresses acceleration, so $m\ddot{x} \approx 0$.
- **Refinement 2 — Linearisation around operating point:** Most restoring forces (beams, plates, diaphragms) are linear only for small displacements. The non-linear terms are expanded via Taylor series about equilibrium and higher-order terms truncated.
- **Refinement 3 — Inclusion of non-conservative forces:** Damping and actuation are non-conservative; they cannot be derived from a potential energy function, so a Newtonian (or Rayleigh's) approach is used rather than the Euler-Lagrange formulation when dissipation is present.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | Generic SDOF EOM | N, kg, m, s | Macro / MEMS |
| 2 | $b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | Reduced EOM ($Re\ll1$) | N, N·s/m, N/m | Micro swimmer |
| 3 | $F_{\text{Stokes}} = 6\pi\mu r\,v$ | Viscous drag on sphere | N | $Re \ll 1$ |
| 4 | $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(\partial C/\partial x)\,V^2$ | Electrostatic actuation | N | Comb drive |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Folded-beam spring stiffness | N/m | Flexure |
| 6 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s | 2nd order |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | 2nd order |
| 8 | $F_{\text{vdW}} = -\dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^2}$ | van der Waals adhesion | N | $d < 100\,\text{nm}$ |
| 9 | $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | Capillary adhesion | N | Humid env. |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** The EOM derived in this module is the foundational building block for designing micro-grippers (MEMS), micro-mirrors (DLP/DMD), AFM cantilevers, micro-pumps, and swimming micro-robots used in targeted drug delivery. Every closed-loop controller designed for a microrobot is built on top of this equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Generic SDOF EOM via Newton's Second Law

**Step 1.** Identify the proof mass $m$ suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ inside a viscous medium with damping coefficient $b$.

**Step 2.** Apply Newton's Second Law on the mass:

$$\sum F = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 3.** Resolve all forces acting in the direction of motion:

- Spring force: $F_s = -kx$ (opposes displacement)
- Damping force: $F_d = -b\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity)
- Applied actuation: $F_{\text{act}} = F(t)$

**Step 4.** Substitute into Newton's law:

$$F(t) - kx - b\dot{x} = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 5.** Rearrange to standard form (zero on LHS):

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

This is the **canonical SDOF EOM** for any linear micromechanical system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio

**Step 1.** Consider the homogeneous equation:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 2.** Assume exponential solution of the form $x(t) = X_0\,e^{\lambda t}$.

**Step 3.** Compute derivatives:

$$\dot{x} = \lambda X_0\,e^{\lambda t}, \qquad \ddot{x} = \lambda^2 X_0\,e^{\lambda t}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute:

$$m\lambda^2 X_0 e^{\lambda t} + b\lambda X_0 e^{\lambda t} + kX_0 e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

**Step 5.** Factor $X_0 e^{\lambda t} \neq 0$ to obtain the **characteristic equation**:

$$m\lambda^2 + b\lambda + k = 0$$

**Step 6.** Solve quadratic:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

**Step 7.** Define **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \\
\zeta &= \frac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{b}{2m\omega_n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8.** Rewrite the characteristic equation in normalised form:

$$\lambda^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n\lambda + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 9.** The three regimes follow from the discriminant:

- $\zeta > 1$ → **overdamped** (slow, no oscillation)
- $\zeta = 1$ → **critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory)
- $0 < \zeta < 1$ → **underdamped** (oscillatory decay)

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Reduced First-Order EOM at Microscale ($Re \ll 1$)

**Step 1.** Start from the canonical SDOF EOM:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

**Step 2.** Compute the ratio of inertial to viscous force magnitudes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{|F_{\text{inertia}}|}{|F_{\text{viscous}}|} &= \frac{m\ddot{x}}{b\dot{x}} \\
&= \frac{\rho L^3 \cdot v/\tau}{6\pi\mu L \cdot v} \\
&= \frac{\rho L^2}{6\pi\mu \tau}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Estimate the characteristic time $\tau \approx L/v$. Substitute:

$$\frac{|F_{\text{inertia}}|}{|F_{\text{viscous}}|} \approx \frac{\rho L v}{6\pi\mu} = \frac{Re}{6\pi}$$

**Step 4.** Since $Re \ll 1$, the inertial term is negligible:

$$m\ddot{x} \ll b\dot{x}$$

**Step 5.** Drop $m\ddot{x}$ from the EOM:

$$b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

**Step 6.** Define the mechanical time constant $\tau_m = b/k$. The first-order response is:

$$\tau_m\,\dot{x} + x = \frac{F(t)}{k}$$

**Step 7.** Solve for steady-state displacement $x_{\infty}$ under constant force $F_0$:

$$x_{\infty} = \frac{F_0}{k}$$

This is the **static deflection** — identical to the macro-scale result — even though the dynamics are now purely first-order.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — EOM for an Electrostatic Comb-Drive Actuator

**Step 1.** Identify actuation force. For a comb drive with $N$ fingers, gap $g$, overlap length $\ell$, and applied voltage $V$:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,V^2 = \frac{N\varepsilon_0\,t\,\ell}{g}\,V^2$$

where $t$ is the structural thickness.

**Step 2.** Substitute $F(t) = F_e$ into the canonical SDOF EOM:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = \frac{N\varepsilon_0\,t\,\ell}{g}\,V^2(t)$$

**Step 3.** Linearise around bias voltage $V_b$ with small signal $v(t) \ll V_b$. Use $V = V_b + v$, then $V^2 \approx V_b^2 + 2V_b v$:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = \frac{N\varepsilon_0\,t\,\ell}{g}\,(V_b^2 + 2V_b\,v)$$

**Step 4.** Separate static equilibrium $x_0 = N\varepsilon_0 t\ell V_b^2/(kg)$ from dynamic response $\delta x(t)$:

$$m\ddot{\delta x} + b\dot{\delta x} + k\,\delta x = \frac{2N\varepsilon_0\,t\,\ell V_b}{g}\,v(t)$$

**Step 5.** Define the **electrostatic gain** $K_e = 2N\varepsilon_0 t\ell V_b/g$ (N/V). The small-signal EOM becomes:

$$m\ddot{\delta x} + b\dot{\delta x} + k\,\delta x = K_e\,v(t)$$

**Step 6.** The transfer function in the Laplace domain ($s$-plane):

$$\frac{\Delta X(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_e}{ms^2 + bs + k}$$

This compact form is what every MEMS controller designer plugs into MATLAB/Simulink for closed-loop analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Microrobot EOM

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of forces and signals governing the equation of motion, instead of attempting a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Actuation Source: Voltage V or Current I"] --> B["Actuation Force F_act"]
    B --> C["Mechanical Node: Mass m with Position x"]
    D["Suspension Element: Spring Constant k"] --> C
    E["Viscous Medium: Damping b"] --> C
    F["Disturbance: F_dist"] --> C
    G["Surface Forces: vdW and Capillary"] --> C
    C --> H["Sensor: Displacement x or Velocity v"]
    H --> I["Controller Block"]
    I --> J["Plant Output: Regulated x t"]
    C --> K["Equation of Motion: m x double dot plus b x dot plus k x equals F total"]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 Damping-Regime Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Re Number Evaluated"] -->|"Re greater than 1"| B["Inertia Dominant: Second Order EOM"]
    A -->|"Re less than 1"| C["Viscous Dominant: First Order EOM"]
    B --> B1["Underdamped: Oscillatory Decay"]
    B --> B2["Critically Damped: Fastest Non Oscillatory"]
    B --> B3["Overdamped: Slow Exponential"]
    C --> C1["Stokes Drag: b equals 6 pi mu r"]
    C --> C2["Steady State: x equals F over k"]
    C --> C3["Time Constant: tau m equals b over k"]
```

## 4.3 Force Inventory Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Microrobot Proof Mass"] --> Q1["Electrostatic Fe"]
    P --> Q2["Electromagnetic F em"]
    P --> Q3["Piezoelectric F p"]
    P --> Q4["Thermal Bimorph F th"]
    P --> R1["Spring Fs equals minus kx"]
    P --> R2["Torsion F theta"]
    P --> S1["Viscous Drag Fd"]
    P --> S2["Squeeze Film F sf"]
    P --> T1["van der Waals F vdW"]
    P --> T2["Capillary F cap"]
    P --> T3["Casimir F C"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above map every term that may appear on the right-hand side of the EOM. During the exam, draw a similar free-body sketch and label every force arrow with its analytic expression.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**State and explain the generic form of the equation of motion for a single-degree-of-freedom micromechanical system. Mention the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The generic SDOF equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

- $m\ddot{x}$ represents the **inertial force** (Newton's resistance to acceleration).
- $b\dot{x}$ represents the **damping force** due to viscous drag of the surrounding medium.
- $kx$ represents the **restoring force** of the elastic suspension.
- $F(t)$ is the **net external actuation force** including any disturbance.

In microrobotics, when the Reynolds number $Re \ll 1$, the inertial term becomes negligible, reducing the system to a first-order EOM: $b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]`

**Why are surface forces dominant at the micro-scale? Justify with the help of scaling laws.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Surface forces (e.g., van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic) scale with characteristic area $L^2$, while body forces (e.g., inertia, gravity) scale with characteristic volume $L^3$. The ratio of surface to volume force scales as $1/L$. As the characteristic length $L$ decreases from macroscopic to micro-scales (e.g., from $10^{-1}\,\text{m}$ to $10^{-6}\,\text{m}$), the ratio increases by five orders of magnitude. Consequently, effects such as **stiction, adhesion, and viscous drag** dominate the dynamic behaviour of microrobots, and the EOM must include surface-force terms explicitly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a single-degree-of-freedom electrostatic comb-drive actuator. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A comb-drive has $N = 100$ fingers, gap $g = 3\,\mu\text{m}$, overlap $\ell = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, thickness $t = 20\,\mu\text{m}$, mass $m = 1\times10^{-9}\,\text{kg}$, spring constant $k = 1\,\text{N/m}$, damping $b = 1\times10^{-6}\,\text{N·s/m}$, and bias voltage $V_b = 10\,\text{V}$. Compute (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the damping ratio, and (iii) the small-signal electrostatic gain. Identify the damping regime. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1.** State the system: a proof mass $m$ suspended by a folded-flexure spring of stiffness $k$, moving in a viscous medium (damping $b$), and actuated by $N$ electrostatic comb fingers.

**Step 2.** Write the actuation force from the energy stored in the capacitor. For one finger pair:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 t \ell}{g}$$

The force on the moving shuttle is:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 t \ell}{2g}\,V^2$$

**Step 3.** For $N$ fingers engaged symmetrically (factor 2 for two sides):

$$F_e = \frac{N\varepsilon_0 t \ell}{g}\,V^2$$

**Step 4.** Apply Newton's Second Law:

$$m\ddot{x} = F_e - b\dot{x} - kx$$

**Step 5.** Substitute the expression for $F_e$:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = \frac{N\varepsilon_0 t \ell}{g}\,V^2$$

**Step 6.** Apply small-signal linearisation around $(V_b, x_0)$ where $V = V_b + v(t)$ and $x = x_0 + \delta x(t)$:

$$m\ddot{\delta x} + b\dot{\delta x} + k\,\delta x = K_e\,v(t)$$

with $K_e = 2N\varepsilon_0 t \ell V_b/g$.

**Assumptions:** Linear spring behaviour for small displacement; negligible fringing-field correction; bias voltage $V_b$ large enough that linearisation is valid; temperature and humidity constant; comb fingers operate in non-pull-in regime.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{1\times10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{10^9} \approx 3.162\times10^{4}\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \approx 5.03\,\text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{1\times10^{-6}}{2\sqrt{(1\times10^{-9})(1)}} = \frac{1\times10^{-6}}{2\times10^{-4.5}} = \frac{1\times10^{-6}}{6.325\times10^{-5}} \approx 0.0158$$

**(iii) Electrostatic gain:**

$$K_e = \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 t \ell V_b}{g}$$

Substituting values:

$$K_e = \frac{2(100)(8.854\times10^{-12})(20\times10^{-6})(50\times10^{-6})(10)}{3\times10^{-6}}$$

Numerator step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_e^{\text{num}} &= 2 \times 100 \times 8.854\times10^{-12} \times 20\times10^{-6} \times 50\times10^{-6} \times 10 \\
&= 200 \times 8.854\times10^{-12} \times 1.0\times10^{-8} \\
&= 200 \times 8.854\times10^{-20} \\
&= 1.7708\times10^{-17}
\end{aligned}$$

Denominator:

$$g = 3\times10^{-6}$$

Final gain:

$$K_e = \frac{1.7708\times10^{-17}}{3\times10^{-6}} = 5.90\times10^{-12}\,\text{N/V}$$

**Damping regime:** Since $\zeta = 0.0158 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** and will exhibit decaying oscillations before reaching steady state.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the scaling laws that govern the dominance of viscous forces over inertial forces at the micro-scale. Derive the reduced first-order equation of motion when $Re \ll 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical magnetic microrobot of radius $r = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ moves through water at velocity $v = 100\,\mu\text{m/s}$. Compute the Reynolds number and the Stokes drag force. Comment on the validity of neglecting inertia in the equation of motion. Given: $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_{\text{water}} = 1.0\times10^{-3}\,\text{N·s/m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1.** Define the Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

**Step 2.** Express the ratio of inertial force $F_i = \rho L^3 v/\tau$ to viscous force $F_v = \mu L v$:

$$\frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho L^2}{\mu \tau}$$

**Step 3.** Choose $\tau = L/v$ as the characteristic flow time. Substituting:

$$\frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho L v}{\mu} = Re$$

**Step 4.** Hence the ratio of inertial to viscous force is precisely $Re$. For $Re \ll 1$, $F_i \ll F_v$, and the EOM

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

reduces to:

$$b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

**Step 5.** Dividing by $k$:

$$\frac{b}{k}\dot{x} + x = \frac{F(t)}{k}$$

Defining $\tau_m = b/k$ as the mechanical time constant:

$$\tau_m \dot{x} + x = \frac{F(t)}{k}$$

This is the first-order linear EOM characteristic of micro-scale swimmers.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{(1000)(100\times10^{-6})(2 \times 50\times10^{-6})}{1.0\times10^{-3}}$$

where $L = 2r = 100\,\mu\text{m}$.

Compute numerator:

$$(1000)(100\times10^{-6})(100\times10^{-6}) = 1000 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-5}$$

Then:

$$Re = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

**Stokes drag force:**

$$F_{\text{Stokes}} = 6\pi\mu r v = 6\pi(1.0\times10^{-3})(50\times10^{-6})(100\times10^{-6})$$

Step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{Stokes}} &= 6\pi \times 1.0\times10^{-3} \times 5.0\times10^{-6} \\
&= 6\pi \times 5.0\times10^{-9} \\
&= 30\pi \times 10^{-9} \\
&\approx 9.42\times10^{-8}\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on inertia:** Since $Re = 0.01 \ll 1$, the system operates deep within the Stokes regime. The inertial term $m\ddot{x}$ contributes less than 1% of the viscous term and may be safely neglected. The EOM reduces to a first-order equation:

$$6\pi\mu r\,\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the comb-drive actuation force** (two comb-finger sets engaging symmetrically on either side). Examiner docks 1 mark for missing this.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega_n$ and $f_n$** — examiners want rad/s for $\omega_n$ and Hz for $f_n$; convert using $f = \omega/2\pi$.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** in part (a) derivations. Always list: linear spring regime, small displacement, isothermal, no fringing fields, no pull-in.
> 4. **Using $L$ as radius instead of diameter** in the Reynolds number. Always use the characteristic length $L = 2r$ for a sphere.
> 5. **Confusing damping ratio $\zeta$ with damping coefficient $b$** — they have different units ($\zeta$ is dimensionless).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **canonical SDOF EOM** is $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ — memorise this form.
- ✅ At micro-scale, **surface forces $\propto L^2$ dominate volumetric forces $\propto L^3$** because of the $1/L$ scaling.
- ✅ **Reynolds number $Re = \rho v L / \mu$** dictates whether inertia matters; for $Re \ll 1$, drop $m\ddot{x}$ and obtain a **first-order EOM**.
- ✅ **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; **damping ratio** $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{mk})$; **mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = b/k$.
- ✅ **Electrostatic comb-drive force** $F_e = (N\varepsilon_0 t \ell / g)\,V^2$; **small-signal gain** $K_e = 2N\varepsilon_0 t \ell V_b/g$.
- ✅ Three damping regimes: **underdamped** ($\zeta<1$), **critically damped** ($\zeta=1$), **overdamped** ($\zeta>1$).
- ✅ **Stokes drag** on a sphere: $F_{\text{Stokes}} = 6\pi\mu r v$.
- ✅ **van der Waals adhesion** at sub-100 nm gaps: $F_{\text{vdW}} = -A_H R / (6d^2)$.
- ✅ Always **linearise** nonlinear actuation (e.g., $V^2$) around the operating point for small-signal EOM.
- ✅ Common MEMS material constants to remember: $\rho_{\text{Si}} \approx 2330\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $E_{\text{Si}} \approx 169\,\text{GPa}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$.
- ✅ When asked for transfer function, write it in the $s$-domain: $X(s)/F(s) = 1/(ms^2 + bs + k)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Body Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multi Body Systems # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Microrobotics (PERAT746)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Multi-Body System (MBS)** is formally defined as: > A mechanical assembly consisting of **two or more rigid or deformable bodies (links)** interconnected by **kinematic pairs, joints, or contact constraints**, whose motion and force transmission must be solved simultaneously as a coupled system, rather than as isolated components. Mathematically, a multi-body system in microrobotics is governed by a set of coupled differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) of the form: $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{F}_{\text{contact}} + \mathbf{F}_{\text{field}} $$ where: - $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **generalized coordinate vector** representing the configuration of all bodies - $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **mass/inertia matrix** - $\mathbf{C}$ captures **Coriolis and centrifugal terms** - $\mathbf{K}$ represents **stiffness/restoring forces** - $\mathbf{Q}$ denotes **applied generalized forces** - $\mathbf{F}_{\text{contact}}$ captures **contact and adhesion forces** (critical at the micro-scale) - $\mathbf{F}_{\text{field}}$ represents **distributed field forces** (electrostatic, magnetic, van der Waals) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Multi-body systems in microrobotics are distinguished from macro-scale MBS by the **dominance of surface forces over body forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, and Casimir forces) — a phenomenon that breaks classical continuum assumptions and necessitates specialized modeling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **marionette puppet with ten strings** being operated by a single puppeteer. Each string is a "body" with its own tension, but the puppet only moves correctly when **all string tensions are coordinated**. Pull one string too hard, and the entire puppet twists awkwardly — the bodies are **coupled**. A **multi-body microrobot** behaves identically: - Each **link, joint, or micro-actuator** is a "body." - They are connected through **flexures, hinges, or contact points** that constrain their relative motion. - You cannot analyze one body in isolation because its motion **propagates forces and displacements** through the entire system. - At the micro-scale, **adhesion and surface tension** act like "sticky strings" that you must deliberately account for. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world examples in microrobotics:** > - **Serial kinematic chains:** Multi-segment continuum microrobots for minimally invasive surgery > - **Parallel mechanisms:** Micro-grippers with multiple compliant fingers > - **Swarm systems:** Coordinated micro-agents (e.g., magnetic microrobot swarms in fluid) > - **Cellular microrobots:** Modular microrobots that dock together to form larger structures ## 1.3 Classification of Multi-Body Systems in Microrobotics | Classification | Description | Typical Example | |---|---|---| | **Serial Chain** | Bodies connected end-to-end; open kinematic loop | Snake-like continuum microrobot | | **Parallel Chain** | Multiple bodies converge on a common end-effector | Stewart-platform micro-manipulator | | **Hybrid Chain** | Combination of serial and parallel structures | Multi-fingered micro-hand | | **Constrained MBS** | Bodies in contact without explicit joints | Particle swarm in magnetic field | | **Compliant MBS** | Bodies connected by elastic flexures | MEMS micro-gripper with flexure hinges | ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics at the Micro-Scale The following standard metrics are **bold-marked** as they appear in KTU board examinations: - **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** ratio of inertial to viscous forces, typically $Re \ll 1$ for microrobots in fluid - **Capillary Length ($\lambda_c$):** typically **~2.7 mm** (water); below this scale, surface tension dominates - **Bond Number ($Bo$):** ratio of gravity to surface tension; $Bo \ll 1$ for microrobots - **Knudsen Number ($Kn$):** ratio of molecular mean free path to characteristic length; relevant for gas-phase microrobotics - **Hamaker constant ($A_H$):** typically **$10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J**; governs van der Waals interactions # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Multi-Body Systems ### 2.1.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Core Concept Every body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom (DOF)**: 3 translations + 3 rotations. When bodies are connected, each **kinematic joint removes specific DOFs**. The **Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion** (also called the mobility formula) for a spatial multi-body system: $$ M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) $$ For a **planar** multi-body system: $$ M = 3(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(3 - f_i) $$ where: - $M$ = **Mobility (DOF) of the mechanism** - $n$ = number of **rigid bodies (links)**, including the ground/fixed frame - $j$ = total number of **kinematic joints** - $f_i$ = DOFs permitted by the $i^{th}$ joint ### 2.1.2 Common Joint Types and Their DOF Contributions | Joint Type | Symbol | Planar DOF $f_i$ | Spatial DOF $f_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | Revolute (pin) | R | 1 | 1 | | Prismatic (sliding) | P | 1 | 1 | | Helical (screw) | H | 1 | 1 | | Cylindrical | C | 2 | 2 | | Spherical (ball) | S | — | 3 | | Planar | E | 3 | 3 | | Universal | U | — | 2 | ### 2.1.3 Lagrangian Formulation for Multi-Body Dynamics For a multi-body system with $n$ generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$, the **equations of motion** are obtained from the **Euler–Lagrange equation**: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k} = Q_k^{\text{nc}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n $$ where: - $T$ = total **kinetic energy** of the system - $V$ = total **potential energy** (gravitational + elastic) - $Q_k^{\text{nc}}$ = **non-conservative generalized force** at coordinate $k$ - $n$ = total DOF of the multi-body system ### 2.1.4 Constrained Multi-Body Systems — DAE Form When the system has **holonomic constraints** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(\mathbf{q}) = 0$, the equations of motion become **Differential-Algebraic Equations (DAEs)**: $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{\mathbf{q}}^T \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\Phi}(\mathbf{q}, t) = \mathbf{0} $$ Here, $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ is the vector of **Lagrange multipliers** representing constraint reaction forces — critical when modeling contact between micro-bodies. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all exam-critical formulas** for Multi-Body Systems. Memorize the structure; KTU board questions frequently combine 2–3 of these in a single 14-mark problem. | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use Case | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $M = 6(n-1) - \sum (6 - f_i)$ | Spatial Kutzbach–Grübler mobility | dimensionless | | 2 | $M = 3(n-1) - \sum (3 - f_i)$ | Planar mobility | dimensionless | | 3 | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Total kinetic energy of MBS | J | | 4 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{Q}$ | Standard form of coupled EOM | N·m | | 5 | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{\mathbf{q}}^T \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{Q}$ | Constrained MBS in DAE form | N·m | | 6 | $F_{\text{vdW}} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$ | van der Waals force (sphere-flat) | N | | 7 | $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi \gamma R \cos\theta$ | Capillary adhesion force | N | | 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 9 | $Bo = \dfrac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$ | Bond number | dimensionless | | 10 | $\tau = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{tip}}$ | Joint torques from tip force via Jacobian transpose | N·m | | 11 | $\mathbf{v}_{\text{ee}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | End-effector velocity via Jacobian | m/s | | 12 | $V_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2}C V^2$ | Stored electrostatic energy | J | > [!NOTE] > **Formula caution:** In Equations 6 and 7, the **van der Waals force** uses $R$ = radius of curvature, $D$ = separation distance, $A_H$ = Hamaker constant, $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle. Do **not confuse** $D$ (separation) with $d$ (diameter) in numerical problems. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Multi-body system analysis is **the backbone** of modern microrobot design: - **Surgical microrobots:** Multi-segment continuum robots must be modeled as MBS to predict tip trajectory under magnetic actuation. - **Micro-assembly:** When a micro-gripper picks a MEMS component, the gripper + part form a transient MBS governed by adhesion. - **Swarm dynamics:** Magnetic microrobot swarms in fluid are modeled as **constrained MBS** with hydrodynamic coupling (Stokes drag). - **Compliant mechanisms:** Flexure-based micro-mechanisms are analyzed as compliant MBS using **lumped-parameter or pseudo-rigid-body models**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kutzbach–Grübler Mobility Formula (Spatial Case) ### Starting Premise Consider a spatial mechanism with $n$ rigid links (including ground) connected by $j$ joints. Each free body in 3D space has **6 DOF**, so $n$ free bodies would have $6n$ DOF. Each joint removes some DOFs. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Total DOF if all bodies were unconstrained (including ground as a body): $$ \text{Total DOF}_{\text{free}} = 6n $$ **Step 2:** Subtract DOF because the ground (one body) is fixed: $$ \text{Effective DOF}_{\text{available}} = 6n - 6 = 6(n-1) $$ **Step 3:** Each joint $i$ removes $(6 - f_i)$ DOF from the system, where $f_i$ is the DOF allowed by that joint. **Step 4:** Total removed DOF across all $j$ joints: $$ \text{Total DOF}_{\text{removed}} = \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) $$ **Step 5:** Subtract removed DOF from available DOF to obtain system mobility: $$ \begin{aligned} M &= \text{Effective DOF}_{\text{available}} - \text{Total DOF}_{\text{removed}} \\[6pt] M &= 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (Special Case — Redundant Constraints):** When the mechanism contains redundant or special geometries (parallelogram loops), a "common DOF" correction $\nu$ must be subtracted: $$ M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) + \nu $$ where $\nu$ is the number of **idle/passive DOFs** (typically 0 for generic geometry). > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The Kutzbach formula is a **necessary, not sufficient**, condition for mobility. A mechanism with $M > 0$ may still not move if joints are arranged improperly (e.g., Grashof condition for 4-bar planar linkages). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lagrangian Multi-Body Equations of Motion ### Starting Premise For a multi-body system with generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$, kinetic energy $T$ and potential energy $V$ are scalar functions of $\mathbf{q}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Express the kinetic energy of each body $i$ with mass $m_i$ and inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_i$: $$ T_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_i + \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i $$ **Step 2:** Express $\mathbf{v}_i$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ in terms of generalized coordinates using the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q})$: $$ \mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{J}_{v,i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} $$ **Step 3:** Total kinetic energy of the multi-body system: $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ m_i \dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{J}_{v,i}^T \mathbf{J}_{v,i}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i}^T \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** This yields a **quadratic form** in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$: $$ T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} $$ where the **system mass matrix** is: $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[ m_i \mathbf{J}_{v,i}^T \mathbf{J}_{v,i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i}^T \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} \right] $$ **Step 5:** Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation for each generalized coordinate $q_k$: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k} = Q_k^{\text{nc}} $$ **Step 6:** Differentiate $\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k}$: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \sum_{j} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j $$ Taking the time derivative: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) = \sum_{j} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j}\dot{M}_{kj}\dot{q}_j $$ **Step 7:** Compute the Christoffel-symbol term: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j $$ **Step 8:** Combine into compact matrix form: $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{Q}^{\text{nc}} $$ where: - $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ represents **Coriolis and centrifugal terms** - $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ represents **gravitational/elastic restoring forces** This is the **standard multi-body equation of motion** used in all microrobot dynamic simulations. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capillary Adhesion Force for Micro-Body Contact ### Starting Premise When a spherical micro-body of radius $R$ contacts a flat surface in a humid environment, a **capillary meniscus** forms a liquid bridge with surface tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** The capillary bridge exerts a force equal to the **Laplace pressure** integrated over the meniscus cross-section, plus the **vertical component of surface tension** around the contact circle. **Step 2:** For a small meniscus, the Laplace pressure contribution dominates: $$ F_{\text{Laplace}} = \pi R^2 \cdot \Delta P $$ where $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r_m}$ and $r_m$ is the meniscus radius. **Step 3:** The surface tension component around the perimeter: $$ F_{\text{tension}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta $$ **Step 4:** For typical microrobot conditions where the meniscus is thin and the perimeter term dominates, the **total capillary adhesion force** simplifies to: $$ F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta $$ **Step 5:** Verification of units: $[\gamma] = \text{N/m}$, $[R] = \text{m}$, so $[F_{\text{cap}}] = \text{N}$. ✓ **Step 6:** For a 100 μm radius microrobot in humid air ($\gamma \approx 0.072$ N/m, $\theta \approx 0$): $$ F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi (100 \times 10^{-6})(0.072)(1) \approx 4.52 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N} = 45.2\ \mu\text{N} $$ This is **comparable to or larger than the gravitational weight** of the micro-body, which is why adhesion dominates microrobot manipulation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Joint Contributions | Joint Type | $f_i$ (Spatial) | DOFs Removed | Effect on Mobility | Typical Micro-Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | Revolute (R) | 1 | 5 | Strong constraint | Flexure hinges | | Prismatic (P) | 1 | 5 | Strong constraint | Linear sliders | | Spherical (S) | 3 | 3 | Moderate constraint | Wrist joints | | Universal (U) | 2 | 4 | Strong-moderate | Compound hinges | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional
