Each node represents a computational or physical module, and arrows represent information or force transfer. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]` **Q: Define a multi-body system. List any two distinguishing features of multi-body systems in microrobotics compared to macro-scale systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A multi-body system (MBS) is an assembly of two or more rigid or deformable bodies interconnected by kinematic pairs, joints, or contact constraints, whose motion must be analyzed as a coupled system. **Two distinguishing features in microrobotics:** 1. **Surface forces dominate body forces:** Van der Waals, capillary, and electrostatic forces scale with area ($L^2$) while inertia scales with volume ($L^3$). Hence at micro-scale, adhesion dominates over weight. 2. **Low Reynolds number regime:** Fluid flow around micro-bodies is laminar and reversible (scallop theorem), making actuation strategies fundamentally different from macro-scale swimming. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two features.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]` **Q: State the Kutzbach–Grübler criterion for mobility of a spatial mechanism with 5 links and 5 revolute joints. Compute the mobility.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Kutzbach–Grübler formula (spatial): $$ M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) $$ For $n = 5$ links, $j = 5$ revolute joints with $f_i = 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 6(5-1) - 5(6-1) \\ M &= 6 \times 4 - 5 \times 5 \\ M &= 24 - 25 = -1 \end{aligned} $$ Since $M = -1 < 0$, the mechanism is a **structure (over-constrained)**, not a mechanism. (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for interpretation.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-end questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Mark distribution for this question bank follows the **KTU revised Bloom's taxonomy** with L2–L4 cognitive levels. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Derive the Kutzbach–Grübler mobility formula for a spatial multi-body system. Apply it to determine the mobility of a **Stewart-platform parallel manipulator** with 6 legs, each leg having a universal-spherical (US) pair arrangement, connected between a fixed base and a moving platform. Assume each leg has 2 bodies and the platform is 1 body. **(b)** Explain the **Lagrangian formulation** of multi-body dynamics. For a 2-link planar microrobot arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$ and lengths $l_1, l_2$, derive the equations of motion using the Lagrangian approach. Include the gravity term. ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Kutzbach–Grübler Derivation + Stewart Platform Mobility **Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 of this document. State the formula: $$ M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) $$ Explain that $n$ is the total body count (including ground), each joint removes $(6 - f_i)$ DOFs. **Stewart Platform Computation (2 marks):** - Bodies per leg = 2; total leg bodies = $6 \times 2 = 12$ - Platform = 1 body - Ground = 1 body - Total $n = 12 + 1 + 1 = 14$ bodies - Each leg has 1 universal joint ($f = 2$) and 1 spherical joint ($f = 3$) - Joints per leg = 2; total joints = $6 \times 2 = 12$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum (6 - f_i) &= 12 \text{ legs} \rightarrow 6 \text{ universal joints}, (6-2)=4 \text{ each} \\ &\quad + 6 \text{ spherical joints}, (6-3)=3 \text{ each} \\ \sum &= 6 \times 4 + 6 \times 3 = 24 + 18 = 42 \end{aligned} $$ $$ M = 6(14 - 1) - 42 = 78 - 42 = 36 $$ Wait — this is incorrect for a Stewart platform. **Correct interpretation:** The ground and platform are connected to all 6 legs simultaneously. Let us recount by considering the canonical Stewart platform topology: - Fixed base = 1 body, Moving platform = 1 body - 6 legs, each treated as **1 link** between U and S joints: 6 bodies - Total bodies $n = 1 + 1 + 6 = 8$ - Joints: 6 universal ($f=2$) + 6 spherical ($f=3$) = 12 joints $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 6(8-1) - [6 \times (6-2) + 6 \times (6-3)] \\ M &= 42 - [24 + 18] \\ M &= 42 - 42 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ A Stewart platform with 6 US legs is a **zero-DOF structure** — it must use 6 actuated prismatic legs to achieve 6 DOF. This is the classical configuration. **Mark allocation:** 1 for formula statement, 2 for Stewart platform counting, 1 for interpretation, 1 for conclusion. #### Part (b) — Lagrangian Formulation + 2-Link Planar Arm **Lagrangian theory (2 marks):** State the Lagrangian $L = T - V$ and the Euler–Lagrange equation (from Section 3.2). **2-Link Arm Derivation (5 marks):** Let $q_1 = \theta_1$, $q_2 = \theta_2$ be the joint angles. Position of link 1 center of mass: $(x_{c1}, y_{c1}) = \left(\dfrac{l_1}{2}\cos\theta_1, \dfrac{l_1}{2}\sin\theta_1\right)$ Position of link 2 center of mass: $(x_{c2}, y_{c2}) = \left(l_1\cos\theta_1 + \dfrac{l_2}{2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2), l_1\sin\theta_1 + \dfrac{l_2}{2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right)$ Velocities by time differentiation: $$ \dot{x}_{c1} = -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1, \quad \dot{y}_{c1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 $$ $$ \dot{x}_{c2} = -l_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) $$ $$ \dot{y}_{c2} = l_1\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) $$ Kinetic energy (translational + rotational, with link inertias $I_1, I_2$ about COM): $$ \begin{aligned} T = \frac{1}{2}m_1(\dot{x}_{c1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c1}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2(\dot{x}_{c2}^2 + \dot{y}_{c2}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 \end{aligned} $$ Potential energy (gravitational, taking $y = 0$ at base): $$ V = m_1 g \cdot \frac{l_1}{2}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\left[l_1\sin\theta_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right] $$ Applying Euler–Lagrange for $q_1 = \theta_1$ and $q_2 = \theta_2$ and simplifying yields the standard form: $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} $$ with: $$ \mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1/4 + m_2)l_1^2 + I_1 & (m_2 l_1 l_2/2)\cos(\theta_2) + I_2 \\ (m_2 l_1 l_2/2)\cos(\theta_2) + I_2 & (m_2 l_2^2/4) + I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Mark allocation:** 2 for Lagrangian statement, 3 for the 2-link derivation, 2 for the final matrix form. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]` **(a)** Explain the role of **surface forces** (van der Waals, capillary, and electrostatic) in multi-body microrobot interactions. Derive the capillary adhesion force for a spherical micro-body in contact with a flat surface. **(b)** A MEMS micro-gripper has 2 fingers, each modeled as a 3-body compliant mechanism. Calculate the total mobility using Kutzbach's formula. If the gripper is to manipulate a 200 μm particle, compute the capillary and van der Waals adhesion forces. Use: $R = 100\ \mu\text{m}$, $\gamma = 0.072\ \text{N/m}$, $\theta = 30°$, $A_H = 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$, $D = 5\ \text{nm}$. ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Surface Forces Theory + Capillary Derivation **Theory (4 marks):** At the micro-scale, surface-to-volume ratio increases dramatically. Surface forces (proportional to area, $L^2$) dominate volumetric forces (proportional to mass, $L^3$). The three primary surface forces in microrobotics are: - **Van der Waals (vdW):** Always present between bodies in close proximity; originates from dipole-dipole interactions. - **Capillary:** Arises from liquid meniscus in humid environments; often the dominant adhesion force. - **Electrostatic:** Present when bodies carry charge; tunable via applied voltage. **Capillary Force Derivation (3 marks):** Already provided in Section 3.3 of this document. Final result: $$ F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta $$ #### Part (b) — Mobility + Numerical Computation **Mobility Calculation (3 marks):** - 2 fingers, each with 3 bodies → 6 body-pairs (excluding ground) - Total bodies $n = 1 \text{ (ground)} + 6 = 7$ - Each finger connected by 2 joints (revolute compliant hinges, $f = 1$) - Total joints $j = 4$ $$ M = 6(7-1) - 4 \times (6-1) = 36 - 20 = 16 $$ Note: This high mobility suggests the fingers have multiple flexure DOFs — appropriate for compliant grippers. In practice, design constraints reduce effective DOF. **Numerical Force Computation (4 marks):** Capillary force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{cap}} &= 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta \\ &= 2\pi (100 \times 10^{-6}) (0.072) \cos 30° \\ &= 2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.072 \times 0.866 \\ &\approx 3.92 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N} = 39.2\ \mu\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ Van der Waals force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{vdW}} &= \frac{A_H R}{6 D^2} \\ &= \frac{(10^{-19})(100 \times 10^{-6})}{6 \times (5 \times 10^{-9})^2} \\ &= \frac{10^{-23}}{6 \times 25 \times 10^{-18}} \\ &= \frac{10^{-23}}{1.5 \times 10^{-16}} \\ &\approx 6.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{N} = 0.067\ \mu\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** $F_{\text{cap}} \approx 587 \times F_{\text{vdW}}$. The capillary force dominates in humid environments — this is the **dominant adhesion mode** for micro-gripper manipulation. **Mark allocation:** 1 for mobility formula, 2 for substituting, 2 each for the two force calculations, 1 for comparison and conclusion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to count the ground body** in the Kutzbach formula — always include it as $n$ in $6(n-1)$. > 2. **Confusing $f_i$ with DOF removed** — $f_i$ is the DOF *allowed* by the joint, so removed DOF is $(6 - f_i)$. > 3. **Mixing up coordinate frames** when writing Jacobians — always specify body-fixed vs world-fixed. > 4. **Ignoring the ground reaction** in Lagrangian problems — gravity acts on every body. > 5. **Dropping the $\cos\theta$ factor** in capillary force derivations — examiners specifically check this. > 6. **Sign errors in constraint forces** $\lambda$ — the DAE form requires careful sign convention. > 7. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing or incorrect unit in numerical answers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multi-Body Systems (PERAT746, Module 1):** - A **multi-body system** is a coupled assembly of two or more bodies with kinematic/dynamic interactions. - **Kutzbach–Grübler formula (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - \sum (6-f_i)$; **planar:** $M = 3(n-1) - \sum (3-f_i)$. - Always include the **ground body** when counting $n$ in the mobility formula. - **Lagrangian EOM for MBS:** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \mathbf{Q}^{\text{nc}}$. - **Constrained MBS** lead to **DAEs** with Lagrange multipliers $\lambda$ enforcing constraint forces. - At the **micro-scale**, surface forces (capillary, vdW, electrostatic) **dominate** body forces (gravity, inertia). - **Capillary adhesion:** $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$. - **Van der Waals (sphere-flat):** $F_{\text{vdW}} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$. - **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ governs fluid regime; $Re \ll 1$ for microrobots in fluid. - **Bond number** $Bo = \rho g L^2 / \gamma$ governs gravity vs surface tension; $Bo \ll 1$ for microrobots. - **Serial chains** are open kinematic loops; **parallel chains** converge to a common end-effector. - **Compliant MBS** use elastic flexures instead of traditional joints. - **End-effector velocity** is related to joint velocities via Jacobian: $\mathbf{v}_{\text{ee}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. - **Joint torques** from tip forces: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{tip}}$. - Always check **units** and **sign conventions** in numerical MBS problems. - **Stewart platform** with 6 US legs has $M = 0$ — needs actuation to become a 6-DOF mechanism. --- **End of Notes — PERAT746 Microrobotics, Module 1, Topic: Multi Body Systems** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme examination standards.*

topics.title: Scaling Effect - Scaling Laws - Scaling Effect on Surface and Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scaling Effect - Scaling Laws - Scaling Effect on Surface and Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling Effect (in Microrobotics)** refers to the systematic and non-linear change in the relative dominance of physical forces, material properties, and dynamic phenomena when the characteristic length (L) of a robotic system is reduced from the macro-scale to the micro/nano-scale. It is governed by **Scaling Laws**, which mathematically describe how each physical parameter transforms as a power-law function of the linear dimension.

**Scaling Laws** are the set of mathematical relationships that predict how a physical quantity (force, time, power, velocity, energy) of a geometrically similar system changes as its characteristic length is scaled by a factor $k$.

Mathematically, if a parameter $P$ has the dimensional form $[M^a L^b T^c]$, then under isotropic scaling by factor $k$ (length $L \to kL$):

$$P_{\text{new}} = k^{a+b+c} \cdot P_{\text{old}}$$

(where density $\rho$ is assumed constant; under constant density, mass scales as $k^3$).

### 1.2 The Surface-to-Volume Scaling Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Scaling Asymmetry:** Surface area scales as the **square** of the characteristic length, whereas volume scales as the **cube** of the characteristic length. Consequently, the **surface-area-to-volume (S/V) ratio scales inversely with length**.

| Quantity | Scaling Law | Power of L |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Length (L) | $L$ | $L^1$ |
| Area (A) | $L^2$ | $L^2$ |
| Volume (V) | $L^3$ | $L^3$ |
| S/V Ratio | $1/L$ | $L^{-1}$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a large iceberg (macro-scale robot) and a small ice cube (micro-scale robot), both made of the *same material* (so density is identical).

- The **iceberg** melts *slowly* because its surface (where heat exchange happens) is tiny compared to its enormous frozen bulk.
- The **ice cube** melts *very fast* because almost all of its mass is *near the surface*. Heat reaches the interior almost instantly.

Now translate this idea to a microrobot:
- **Heat dissipation** becomes a nightmare — heat is generated in the **volume** but can only escape through the **surface**.
- **Friction** (a surface phenomenon) overwhelms **inertia** (a volume/body phenomenon).
- **Surface tension** of a tiny water film can hold a microrobot hostage — a force a macro-robot would never feel.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Small-is-Different" Rule:** When you shrink a robot by 10×, its area shrinks by 100×, but its volume (and hence mass) shrinks by 1000×. Surface forces (which scale as $L^2$ or $L$) become **dominant** over body forces (which scale as $L^3$). This is the *singular* reason why microrobotics is not just "small mechanical engineering."

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density** $\rho$ [kg/m³]: Assumed constant under scaling (material property invariant).
- **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ [Pa·s] or [kg/(m·s)]: Material property; for air $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s; for water $\approx 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- **Surface tension** $\gamma$ [N/m]: For water–air interface $\approx 0.072$ N/m at 20 °C.
- **Characteristic length** $L$ [m]: The defining linear dimension of the robot.
- **Scaling factor** $k = L_{\text{micro}} / L_{\text{macro}}$: Dimensionless ratio $< 1$ for miniaturization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Geometric Scaling

When a system is scaled isotropically (all dimensions scaled equally by factor $k$):

**Pillar 1 — Linear Dimension:**
$$L_{\text{new}} = k \cdot L_{\text{old}}$$

**Pillar 2 — Surface Area:**
$$A_{\text{new}} = k^2 \cdot A_{\text{old}}$$

**Pillar 3 — Volume (and Mass at constant density):**
$$V_{\text{new}} = k^3 \cdot V_{\text{old}}$$

$$m_{\text{new}} = k^3 \cdot m_{\text{old}}$$

### 2.2 Why Surface Dominates at the Microscale

**The Surface-to-Volume Ratio Derivation:**

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{L^2}{L^3} = \frac{1}{L}$$

After scaling by $k$:

$$\left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{\text{new}} = \frac{k^2 L^2}{k^3 L^3} = \frac{1}{kL} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{\text{old}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 1 cm³ cube has $A/V = 6$ cm⁻¹. A 1 µm³ cube has $A/V = 6 \times 10^6$ m⁻¹ (or 6 µm⁻¹). For the same material, the micro-cube has *one million times* more surface per unit volume. This single fact dictates the design philosophy of every micro-actuator, micro-sensor, and micro-robot.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Force Scaling Laws

The following table is the **most important high-yield reference** for the KTU exam on this topic.

| Physical Quantity | Dimensional Form | Scaling Power (with L) | Dominant Regime |
|------------------|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Inertial Force ($F_i = ma$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^3$ | Macro |
| Gravitational Force ($F_g = mg$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^3$ | Macro |
| Viscous Drag — Stokes ($F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^1$ | Micro |
| Surface Tension Force ($F_\gamma = \gamma L$) | $M T^{-2}$ | $L^1$ | Micro |
| Electrostatic Force ($F_e = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 A$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^2$ | Micro |
| Magnetic Force ($F_m = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^2$ | Micro |
| Van der Waals Force ($F_{vdW}$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^0$ to $L^1$ | Nano |
| Elastic (Spring) Force ($F = kx$, $k \propto L$) | $M L T^{-2}$ | $L^1$ | All scales |
| Reynolds Number ($Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$) | Dimensionless | $L^1$ | Diagnostic |
| Heat Generation ($\dot{Q}_{gen} \propto V$) | $M L^2 T^{-3}$ | $L^3$ | Macro limit |
| Heat Loss (Conduction $\propto A$) | $M L^2 T^{-3}$ | $L^2$ | Micro limit |
| Natural Frequency ($\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$) | $T^{-1}$ | $L^{-1}$ | Micro |
| Power ($\dot{W} = F \cdot v$) | $M L^2 T^{-3}$ | varies | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall — No Pipes in Tables:** Note that absolute values, magnitudes, and determinant notation must never use the vertical bar symbol $\vert$ in markdown tables; we have used $\vert$ inside math-mode within table cells, but never the raw `|` separator inside cell content. This is the *only* way to keep tables parseable in KTU's PDF renderer.

### 2.4 The Surface vs. Volume Consequence Map

| Phenomenon | Macro-Dominant (Volume) | Micro-Dominant (Surface) |
|------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| Heat dissipation | Heat is dumped easily | **Thermal runaway risk** |
| Friction vs. Inertia | Inertia wins | **Friction always wins** |
| Sticking to surfaces | Negligible | **Stiction paralyzes the robot** |
| Fluid flow | Turbulent ($Re \gg 1$) | **Sticky laminar ($Re \ll 1$)** |
| Brownian motion | Averaged out | **Displaces the robot randomly** |
| Electrostatic charging | Discharges via air | **Charges persist (no arc)** |
| Wear and tear | Surface damage | **Whole robot is "surface"** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microfluidics:** Designing lab-on-chip devices requires accounting for $F \propto L$ scaling of viscous drag.
- **MEMS Design:** Comb-drive actuators use electrostatic forces (scaling $\propto L^2$) because they remain effective when inertial forces ($\propto L^3$) vanish.
- **Medical Microrobots:** Swimming in blood requires overcoming viscous (not inertial) forces — hence the design of helical and flagellar swimmers.
- **Materials Selection:** At microscale, materials behave differently because grain boundaries (a surface phenomenon) become comparable in size to the structural features.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reader's Note:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No result is stated without justification. Multi-line derivations are enclosed in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Surface-Area-to-Volume Ratio Scaling

**Given:** A cube of side $L$ has surface area $A = 6L^2$ and volume $V = L^3$.

**Step 1 — Initial ratio:**

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{6L^2}{L^3} = \frac{6}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply scaling factor $k$ to linear dimension:**

$$L' = kL, \quad A' = 6(kL)^2 = 6k^2L^2, \quad V' = (kL)^3 = k^3L^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new ratio:**

$$\frac{A'}{V'} = \frac{6k^2L^2}{k^3L^3} = \frac{6}{kL}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio of new to old:**

$$\frac{(A/V)'}{(A/V)} = \frac{6/(kL)}{6/L} = \frac{1}{k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The surface-to-volume ratio scales as $L^{-1}$. When the size is reduced by 10 ($k = 0.1$), the S/V ratio becomes **10 times larger** in absolute terms per unit volume. This is the foundational scaling law of the surface-vs-volume effect.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Force-Scaling Hierarchy

We classify forces by their exponent $n$ in $F \propto L^n$:

#### (a) Inertial Force — scales as $L^3$

**Step 1 — Definition:** $F_i = m \cdot a$

**Step 2 — Mass:** $m = \rho \cdot V \propto L^3$ (volume scales cubically)

**Step 3 — Acceleration:** $a$ is scale-invariant (it is a kinematic quantity; the time to traverse a scaled distance is not the same, but the *definition* of $a$ in terms of motion pattern is preserved)

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$F_i \propto L^3$$

#### (b) Viscous Force (Stokes Drag) — scales as $L^1$

**Step 1 — Definition:** $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$ for a sphere of radius $R$

**Step 2 — Radius:** $R \propto L$ (geometric)

**Step 3 — Velocity (for kinematic similarity):** $v \propto L/T$. If we assume Reynolds number is preserved (dynamic similarity), $v \propto L$ (to be derived below). For force scaling, we simply note that if the *same velocity* is applied, $v$ is constant.

**Step 4 — Result (constant velocity):**

$$F_d \propto L^1$$

#### (c) Surface Tension Force — scales as $L^1$

**Step 1 — Definition:** $F_\gamma = \gamma \cdot \ell$ where $\ell$ is the contact-line length

**Step 2 — Contact length:** $\ell \propto L$

**Step 3 — Surface tension coefficient:** $\gamma$ is a material constant (scale-invariant)

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$F_\gamma \propto L^1$$

#### (d) Electrostatic Force — scales as $L^2$

**Step 1 — Definition:** $F_e = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2 A$ (parallel-plate capacitor model)

**Step 2 — Electric field $E$:** Scale-invariant for the same voltage gradient

**Step 3 — Area:** $A \propto L^2$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$F_e \propto L^2$$

#### (e) Gravitational Force — scales as $L^3$

**Step 1 — Definition:** $F_g = m g = \rho V g$

**Step 2 — Volume:** $V \propto L^3$

**Step 3 — Gravitational acceleration $g$:** Universal constant (scale-invariant)

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$F_g \propto L^3$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Reynolds Number Scaling — The "Smoking Gun" of Microscale Physics

**Given:** Reynolds number $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$

**Step 1 — Define the scaling:** Length $L \to kL$, velocity $v \to v'$ (to be determined)

**Step 2 — Apply to Re:**

$$Re' = \frac{\rho v' (kL)}{\mu}$$

**Step 3 — Kinematic similarity (preserve Re if dynamic similarity is required):**

$$\frac{\rho v' (kL)}{\mu} = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} \implies v' = \frac{v}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude that velocity must *increase* by $1/k$ to preserve the flow regime.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** If we keep velocity constant while shrinking the robot, $Re$ drops as $L$. For a 1 mm robot in water moving at 1 mm/s: $Re \approx 1$. For a 1 µm "robot" in water at the same speed: $Re \approx 10^{-3}$ — the realm of **creeping flow** where inertial momentum is utterly negligible and the motion is governed by instantaneous viscous dissipation.

**Numerical example:**

A bacterium of length $L = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$ swimming in water at $v = 25 \, \mu\text{m/s}$:

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is **vanishingly small** — confirming that microorganisms (and microrobots) live in a viscous, "syrup-like" world despite being in water.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Heat Dissipation Scaling

**Given:** A body of characteristic length $L$ generates heat at a rate $\dot{Q}_{gen} \propto V$ and loses it at a rate $\dot{Q}_{loss} \propto A \cdot \Delta T$.

**Step 1 — Generation rate scaling:**

$$\dot{Q}_{gen} \propto L^3$$

**Step 2 — Loss rate scaling:**

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} \propto L^2$$

**Step 3 — Ratio (the key metric):**

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{\dot{Q}_{loss}} \propto \frac{L^3}{L^2} = L$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> [!TIP]
> For the *same volumetric power density* $p$ [W/m³], a 10× smaller device generates **10× more heat per unit surface area** than its macro counterpart. This is why CPU chips throttle (and why micro-actuators need elaborate cooling strategies or duty-cycled operation).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Time Scaling and Natural Frequency

**Step 1 — Characteristic time of a system:** Often $\tau \propto \sqrt{m/k}$ (mass-spring) or $\tau \propto L/v$.

**Step 2 — Under scaling, mass $m \propto L^3$ and stiffness $k \propto L$:**

$$\tau \propto \sqrt{\frac{L^3}{L}} = \sqrt{L^2} = L$$

**Step 3 — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{1}{\tau} \propto \frac{1}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 10× smaller resonator oscillates **10× faster**. MEMS gyroscopes and oscillators can therefore operate at MHz-to-GHz frequencies, which is impossible for macro equivalents of the same material.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Force Dominance Map

| $L$ (m) | Dominant Force(s) | Flow Regime | Example |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|---------|
| $10^{0}$ | Gravity, Inertia | Turbulent | Car, Humanoid robot |
| $10^{-2}$ | Inertia, Spring | Laminar | Insect-scale robot |
| $10^{-4}$ | Viscous, Surface tension | Low $Re$ | Bacterial flagellum |
| $10^{-6}$ | Viscous, Van der Waals, Electrostatic | Creeping flow | MEMS microrobot |
| $10^{-9}$ | Thermal noise, Quantum | Brownian | Molecular machine |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Force Scaling Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["MACRO SCALE L = 1 m"] --> nodeB["FORCES L3 Inertia Gravity"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Re >> 1 Turbulent Flow"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Heat Loss Easy V >> A"]
    nodeE["MESO SCALE L = 1 mm"] --> nodeF["FORCES L2 Electrostatic Magnetic"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Re ~ 1 Transitional"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Surface Effects Emerging"]
    nodeI["MICRO SCALE L = 1 um"] --> nodeJ["FORCES L1 Viscous Surface Tension"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Re << 1 Laminar Creeping"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Heat Loss Hard A dominates V"]
    nodeM["NANO SCALE L = 1 nm"] --> nodeN["FORCES L0 Brownian Van Der Waals"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Re = 0 Molecular Regime"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["Thermal Noise Dominant"]
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Surface-to-Volume Effect Functional Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    paramS["SCALING FACTOR k Applied to L"] --> lenOut["L_new = kL"]
    paramS --> areaOut["A_new = k2 A"]
    paramS --> volOut["V_new = k3 V"]
    paramS --> svRatio["S_to_V_new = SV divided by k"]
    svRatio --> therm["Heat Dissipation Penalty"]
    svRatio --> fric["Friction Dominates Inertia"]
    svRatio --> stick["Stiction and Adhesion Risk"]
    svRatio --> etest["Electrostatic Forces Strong"]
    therm --> design1["Use Pulsed Duty Cycle"]
    fric --> design2["Use Lubrication or Levitation"]
    stick --> design3["Use Low Surface Energy Coatings"]
    etest --> design4["Use Comb Drive or Electrostatic Actuators"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Flowchart for Micro-Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ["Designing a Microrobot"] --> q1["Is the size below 1 mm"]
    q1 -- "YES" --> q2["Identify dominant force from scaling exponent"]
    q2 --> q2a["Inertial L3 becomes negligible"]
    q2 --> q2b["Viscous L1 dominates motion"]
    q2 --> q2c["Surface L1 dominates interfaces"]
    q1 -- "NO" --> macroPath["Use conventional macro design rules"]
    q2a --> actionA["Choose actuation that scales as L1 or L2"]
    q2b --> actionB["Apply Stokes drag formulas not Newtons laws"]
    q2c --> actionC["Apply surface tension and adhesion analysis"]
    actionA --> finalOut["Microrobot Design Complete"]
    actionB --> finalOut
    actionC --> finalOut
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic Description (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** The following text-based schematic represents the functional architecture of a typical MEMS microrobot and the *interactions* between scaling-affected subsystems. A physical rendering would show interdigitated comb fingers, suspension beams, and a central proof mass.

**Block Architecture of a Micro-Scale Force-Balanced System:**

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|              MICRO-ROBOT FUNCTIONAL CORE           |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|  [INPUT: Drive Signal]                             |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [Electrostatic Comb Actuator]  <-- F_e ~ L^2     |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [Suspension Beams: Spring k ~ L]  <-- F_s ~ L     |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [Proof Mass: Inertia m ~ L^3]  <-- F_i ~ L^3      |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [Fluid Surroundings: Drag F_d ~ L]  <-- F_d ~ L   |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [Output: Displacement / Frequency]                |
+----------------------------------------------------+
         |
         v
   At microscale, F_e ~ L^2 and F_d ~ L dominate
   while F_i ~ L^3 becomes vanishingly small.
   Net behavior: spring-mass-damper, but inertia is
   often negligible → 2nd-order overdamped system.
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Scaling Law" as applied to microrobotics. Explain with one example how surface area and volume scale differently when the characteristic length is reduced by a factor of 10.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Scaling Law** is a mathematical relation describing how a physical quantity of a geometrically similar system changes when its characteristic linear dimension is multiplied by a factor $k$.

**(1 mark for definition)**

For a body of characteristic length $L$:
- Surface area $A \propto L^2$
- Volume $V \propto L^3$

**(1 mark for formulas)**

**Example:** If $L$ is reduced by 10 ($k = 0.1$):
- Area becomes $A_{\text{new}} = (0.1)^2 A_{\text{old}} = 0.01 A_{\text{old}}$ (1% of original)
- Volume becomes $V_{\text{new}} = (0.1)^3 V_{\text{old}} = 0.001 V_{\text{old}}$ (0.1% of original)

**(1 mark for numerical example with k = 0.1)**

Hence, area decreases as the square but volume decreases as the cube — the surface-to-volume ratio increases by 10×.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three forces that become dominant at the micro-scale. State their individual scaling relationships with the characteristic length $L$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

At the micro-scale, surface-related forces dominate over body forces because they scale with lower powers of $L$:

| # | Force | Scaling Relation |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Viscous (Stokes) drag | $F_d \propto L^1$ |
| 2 | Surface tension force | $F_\gamma \propto L^1$ |
| 3 | Electrostatic force | $F_e \propto L^2$ |

**(½ mark each for naming + ½ mark each for scaling relation = 3 marks)**

These forces scale slower than inertia and gravity (both $\propto L^3$), so they become relatively dominant as $L$ shrinks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Derive the scaling relationship of the surface-area-to-volume ratio with respect to the characteristic length. Explain its significance in the design of microrobots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A macro-robot of characteristic length $L = 1$ m has surface area $10$ m² and volume $1$ m³. If the robot is scaled down isotropically by a factor of $k = 0.001$ to build a micro-robot, compute:
(i) The new surface area, volume, and S/V ratio.
(ii) The ratio of heat generation to heat loss, assuming both scale as indicated in the theory.
(iii) The new natural frequency relative to the original, assuming the original was 10 Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Consider a cube of side $L$:
- Surface area: $A = 6L^2 \propto L^2$ ... (1)
- Volume: $V = L^3 \propto L^3$ ... (1)
- Ratio: $\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{6L^2}{L^3} = \dfrac{6}{L} \propto L^{-1}$ ... (1)

After scaling by factor $k$:
- $A_{\text{new}} = 6(kL)^2 = 6k^2L^2$
- $V_{\text{new}} = (kL)^3 = k^3L^3$
- $\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{V_{\text{new}}} = \dfrac{6k^2L^2}{k^3L^3} = \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{6}{L} = \dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{A}{V}\right)_{\text{old}}$ ... (1)

**Conclusion:** The S/V ratio scales as $L^{-1}$ and increases by a factor $1/k$ when the system is shrunk by $k$. ... (1)

**Significance in microrobot design:** (2 marks)
1. Heat dissipation becomes difficult because heat is generated in the volume but can only be lost through the surface.
2. Surface phenomena (friction, adhesion, surface tension) dominate over volume phenomena (inertia, gravity).
3. Material properties change because surface atoms constitute a significant fraction of the total.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $L = 1$ m, $A = 10$ m², $V = 1$ m³, $k = 0.001$.

**(i) New values:** (3 marks)

$$L_{\text{new}} = kL = 0.001 \times 1 = 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_{\text{new}} = k^2 A = (0.001)^2 \times 10 = 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 = 10 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$V_{\text{new}} = k^3 V = (0.001)^3 \times 1 = 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3 = 1 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{\text{new}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-9}} = 10^4 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

(Original S/V was $10$ m⁻¹; new S/V is $10^4$ m⁻¹ — i.e., 1000× larger, confirming the $1/k$ relationship.)

**(ii) Heat generation/loss ratio:** (2 marks)

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}} \propto L \implies \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,new}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,old}}} \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,old}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,new}}} = \frac{k^3}{k^2} = k = 0.001$$

For the same volumetric power density, the new robot generates **0.001 times** the heat per unit surface area compared to the original... Wait, re-evaluate:

For same volumetric power density, $\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,new}} / \dot{Q}_{\text{gen,old}} = k^3 = 10^{-9}$ and $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,new}} / \dot{Q}_{\text{loss,old}} = k^2 = 10^{-6}$.

So $\dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,new}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,new}}} = \dfrac{10^{-9}}{10^{-6}} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,old}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,old}}} = 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{gen,old}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss,old}}}$.

**The new robot generates 1000× more heat per unit surface than the old one** — thermal stress is severe. (2 marks)

**(iii) Natural frequency:** (2 marks)

$$\omega_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{k} \omega_{\text{old}} = \frac{1}{0.001} \times 10 \text{ Hz} = 10000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

The micro-robot oscillates **1000× faster** than the macro-robot.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** With the help of suitable derivations, compare the scaling behaviour of (i) inertial force, (ii) viscous drag force, and (iii) electrostatic force. State which force becomes dominant when a robot is shrunk from 10 cm to 100 µm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical micro-robot of radius $R = 50$ µm is moving in water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) at a velocity of $v = 100$ µm/s. Calculate the Reynolds number and state the type of flow regime. Comment on whether inertial or viscous forces dominate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Inertial force:** (2 marks)

$$F_i = m \cdot a = \rho V a = \rho \left(\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3\right) a \propto L^3$$

**(ii) Viscous drag (Stokes):** (2 marks)

$$F_d = 6\pi\mu R v \propto L^1$$

**(iii) Electrostatic force:** (2 marks)

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2 A \propto L^2$$

(For a sphere, $A \propto R^2 \propto L^2$.)

**Dominance at the new scale:** (1 mark)

Initial $L_1 = 10$ cm = $10^{-1}$ m, final $L_2 = 100$ µm = $10^{-4}$ m. Ratio $k = 10^{-3}$.

- $F_i$ becomes $10^{-9}$ of its original
- $F_e$ becomes $10^{-6}$ of its original
- $F_d$ becomes $10^{-3}$ of its original

The viscous drag decreases the least → **viscous force is dominant** at the micro-scale.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $R = 50$ µm = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, characteristic length $L = 2R = 10^{-4}$ m, $v = 100$ µm/s = $10^{-4}$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number Formula:** (1 mark)

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** (2 marks)

$$Re = \frac{(1000)(10^{-4})(10^{-4})}{10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:** (1 mark)

$$\text{Numerator} = 1000 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by viscosity:** (1 mark)

$$Re = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** (2 marks)

Since $Re = 0.01 \ll 1$, the flow regime is **creeping flow (Stokes regime)**. In this regime, viscous forces overwhelmingly dominate inertial forces; the sphere decelerates the instant the driving force is removed. This is the regime of all biological microswimmers and MEMS-scale microrobots.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Exam Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the density assumption:** When scaling mass, students often write $m \to km$ instead of $m \to k^3 m$. Always state *"assuming density is constant"* explicitly. **−1 mark penalty** if omitted.
>
> 2. **Confusing Reynolds number velocity scaling:** Many students compute $Re$ at micro-scale using the *same velocity* as macro-scale, which is correct for force-only comparison, but they then claim "inertia is preserved" — this is wrong. To preserve $Re$ as a dimensionless group, velocity must scale as $1/k$. **−1 mark** if not clarified.
>
> 3. **Mixing surface-area and volume scaling in heat problems:** Heat generation $\propto V \propto L^3$ and heat loss $\propto A \propto L^2$. The ratio is $\propto L$, not $L^2$ or $L^3$. **−2 marks** if confused.
>
> 4. **Using $\rho g$ versus $\rho V g$ in gravitational scaling:** Some students erroneously write $F_g \propto L$ (confusing with pressure). Gravity is a body force and scales as $L^3$. **−1 mark** if incorrect.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in final numerical answers:** KTU board examiners *consistently deduct* 0.5 mark for missing SI units in numerical problems.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., isotropic scaling, constant density, same material, same voltage gradient). Examiners reward 1 mark for "clearly stated assumptions" in any 7-mark derivation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Core Scaling Relations (memorize verbatim):**
  - Length $\propto L$
  - Area $\propto L^2$
  - Volume $\propto L^3$
  - Mass $\propto L^3$ (at constant $\rho$)
  - S/V ratio $\propto L^{-1}$

- **Force Scaling Hierarchy (most-to-least dominant at small L):**
  - Viscous drag $\propto L^1$ (most persistent at small scale)
  - Surface tension $\propto L^1$
  - Electrostatic $\propto L^2$
  - Magnetic $\propto L^2$
  - Inertia $\propto L^3$ (vanishes fastest)
  - Gravity $\propto L^3$ (vanishes fastest)

- **Reynolds Number Rule:** $Re \propto L$ for constant velocity. At $L \sim 100$ µm in water, $Re \sim 10^{-2}$ → **creeping flow** → inertia negligible.

- **Heat Scaling Rule:** Heat generation $\propto L^3$, heat loss $\propto L^2$. Heat generation per unit area $\propto L$ → **thermal stress increases as size shrinks**.

- **Time and Frequency Scaling:** Characteristic time $\tau \propto L$, natural frequency $\omega_n \propto L^{-1}$ → micro-resonators are **inherently faster**.

- **Key Threshold (memorize):**
  - Macro: $L > 1$ cm — inertia/gravity dominant
  - Meso: $L \sim 1$ mm — both comparable
  - Micro: $L < 100$ µm — viscous/surface dominant
  - Nano: $L < 1$ µm — Brownian/quantum dominant

- **Slogan for Viva:** *"As size shrinks, volume collapses faster than surface, so the world of the small is governed by what touches it, not what contains it."*

- **One-line answers to remember:**
  - "Surface area scales as $L^2$, volume scales as $L^3$" — universal starting point.
  - "Forces that scale as $L^1$ or $L^2$ dominate at micro-scale."
  - "At the microscale, friction always wins over inertia."

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never assume a property scales as $L$ by default. Always derive from the underlying physics formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Microrobotics**, the term **Electrical Properties** refers to the comprehensive set of measurable and analytically derivable electromagnetic and charge-transport characteristics of the structural materials, active substrates, conductive interconnects, and transduction elements that constitute a microrobotic system. These properties govern how a microrobot **stores, conducts, dissipates, and converts** electrical energy into mechanical work (actuation) or vice versa (sensing).

At the KTU 2024 Scheme level, electrical properties in microrobotics are formally categorized into the following primary phenomena:

- **Electrical Conductivity (σ)** — the intrinsic ability of a material to transport charge carriers.
- **Resistivity (ρ)** — the reciprocal opposition to charge flow.
- **Dielectric Constant / Permittivity (ε)** — the response of a material to an externally applied electric field.
- **Dielectric Loss Tangent (tan δ)** — quantification of energy dissipated as heat in a dielectric under an alternating field.
- **Piezoelectric Coefficients (d₃₃, d₃₁, g₃₃)** — coupling between mechanical strain and electric polarization.
- **Dielectric Breakdown Strength (E_bd)** — the maximum electric field a dielectric can sustain before becoming conductive.
- **Electromagnetic Susceptibility (χₑ, χₘ)** — material response to electric and magnetic fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For microrobotic systems where characteristic dimensions range from **1 µm to 1 mm**, the scaling of these electrical properties is non-trivial. Surface-to-volume ratios become dominant, and **classical bulk behavior** often fails. The **fundamental physical constant** $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m}$ (vacuum permittivity) acts as the universal baseline against which all material permittivities are normalized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden Hose Analogy 💧**
> Imagine you are watering a garden. The **water** represents **electric current (charge carriers)** flowing through a pipe. The **pipe's diameter and roughness** represent the material's **electrical conductivity/resistivity**. Now, shrink that garden to the size of a single leaf. The "pipe" is now so tiny that:
> 1. The **walls of the pipe** (surface effects) matter more than the **water inside** (bulk flow).
> 2. A tiny **leak** (tunneling/breakdown) can ruin everything.
> 3. The **water itself** moves differently because molecules bounce off the walls more often.
>
> **This is exactly what happens with electrical properties at the microscale!** The conductivity, capacitance, and piezoelectric response all deviate from macroscale textbook values.

## 1.3 Why Electrical Properties are Paramount in Microrobotics

Microrobots (sub-millimeter scale) **cannot use conventional electromagnetic motors** because the magnetic force scales with volume (∝ L³), while inertial resistance scales with mass (also ∝ L³), but **friction and surface forces** scale as L² — they dominate. Therefore, designers exploit **electric and electrostatic field-based effects**, where forces scale favorably and can be applied across micron-sized gaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Board Exams:** When answering any question on microrobics actuation/sensing, your first sentence should always state the dominant electrical property being exploited (e.g., "The actuator uses the **inverse piezoelectric effect** governed by the **d₃₃ coefficient**...").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Electrical Properties into Functional Domains

The electrical behavior of microrobotic materials can be broken down into **four interacting domains**:

### Domain A — Charge Conduction (Transport Properties)
1. **Drift of charge carriers** under an applied electric field **E**.
2. Governed by the local conductivity σ, which depends on carrier concentration *n*, charge *q*, and mobility *μ*.
3. At microscale, **surface scattering** reduces effective mobility (Mayadas–Shatzkes model).

### Domain B — Polarization & Storage (Dielectric Properties)
1. A material placed between capacitor plates stores energy via **dielectric polarization**.
2. Quantified by **relative permittivity εᵣ** and **polarization vector P**.
3. Critical for **electrostatic microactuators** (comb drives, scratch drive actuators).

### Domain C — Energy Conversion (Piezoelectric / Electrostrictive)
1. **Direct effect:** Mechanical strain → electric charge (used in **sensing**).
2. **Converse effect:** Electric field → mechanical strain (used in **actuation**).
3. Governed by the third-rank **piezoelectric tensor dᵢⱼₖ**.

### Domain D — Field-Induced Forces (Electromechanical Coupling)
1. **Maxwell stress** generates attractive/repulsive forces between charged electrodes.
2. Force density scales as **E² / (2ε)** — strong at small gaps.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the table below — it covers ~80% of numerical questions on Electrical Properties in KTU ESE.**

| # | Property / Law | Governing Equation | SI Units | Engineering Significance in Microrobotics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (local form) | $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ | A/m² | Current density in micro-interconnects |
| 2 | Conductivity (drift model) | $\sigma = n q \mu$ | S/m | Determines resistive losses in MEMS |
| 3 | Resistivity | $\rho = 1 / \sigma$ | Ω·m | Material selection for electrodes |
| 4 | Sheet Resistance (thin film) | $R_s = \rho / t$ | Ω/sq (ohms per square) | Critical for thin-film microrobot conductors |
| 5 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F (Farads) | Foundation of electrostatic actuators |
| 6 | Permittivity (relative) | $\varepsilon_r = C / C_0$ | dimensionless | Dielectric selection for capacitors |
| 7 | Energy Stored (capacitor) | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Stored energy in microrobot power systems |
| 8 | Electrostatic Force (parallel plate) | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / d^2$ | N | Pull-in / actuation force |
| 9 | Maxwell Stress Tensor | $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2$ | N/m² (Pa) | Field-induced surface traction |
| 10 | Dielectric Breakdown (Paschen-like) | $E_{bd} \approx 3 \times 10^6$ V/m (air) | V/m | Maximum operating voltage limit |
| 11 | Piezoelectric (converse) | $S = d \cdot E$ | strain (m/m) | Actuation strain in PZT, ZnO, AlN |
| 12 | Piezoelectric (direct) | $D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T E$ | C/m² | Sensor charge generation |
| 13 | Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta = \varepsilon'' / \varepsilon'$ | dimensionless | Heating in AC-driven microrobots |
| 14 | RC Time Constant | $\tau = R C$ | s | Switching speed limit |
| 15 | Skin Depth (EM shielding) | $\delta = \sqrt{2 / (\omega \mu \sigma)}$ | m | Frequency limit for conductor behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR EXAMS:** Always write units. KTU examiners routinely deduct marks for missing units. Always specify whether σ is S/m or Ω⁻¹·m⁻¹.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Microrobotics

| Application | Electrical Property Exploited | Example Microrobot |
|---|---|---|
| Walking microrobot legs | Electrostatic force ($F \propto V^2$) | Berkeley's electrostatic walker |
| Flapping-wing flyers | Piezoelectric actuation ($d_{31}$) | Harvard RoboBee |
| Capsule endoscopes | Electromagnetic coil induction | Given Imaging PillCam |
| Drug-delivery swimmers | Dielectrophoresis | Nano swimmers |
| Tactile skin sensors | Piezoresistivity | E-skin arrays |
| Energy harvesters | Piezoelectric (direct) | Vibration-powered MEMS |

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Micro-Scale Deviations

> [!NOTE]
> **Question students often ask:** *"Why don't we just use bulk values for these properties?"*
> **Answer:** Because at the microscale, three phenomena emerge that bulk theory ignores:
>
> 1. **Surface scattering of electrons** — reduces σ by 10–50% in sub-micron wires (Mayadas–Shatzkes, 1970).
> 2. **Fringing field dominance** — at small electrode gaps, the parallel-plate capacitor formula breaks down; numerical FEM is required.
> 3. **Dielectric breakdown reduction** — thin films fail at lower voltages because defect density is statistically higher per unit volume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is expanded explicitly. No step is skipped. This matches the KTU board examiner's expectation of seeing complete working for full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Micro-Capacitor

**Starting Premise (Gauss's Law):** The electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by ε₀.

### Step 1: Set Up Geometry
Consider two parallel conductive plates of area $A$ separated by a dielectric of thickness $d$ with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

Apply a potential difference $V$ across the plates. The electric field between the plates is uniform:

$$
\vec{E} = \frac{V}{d} \hat{n}
$$

**Explanation:** Uniform field arises because the plates are assumed infinite (or A ≫ d²). In microrobotics, this condition holds when the gap *d* is much smaller than the lateral plate dimension.

### Step 2: Apply Gauss's Law to a Pillbox Surface
Construct an imaginary Gaussian pillbox enclosing charge $+Q$ on the top plate:

$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}
$$

Since the displacement field $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the plate and uniform:

$$
D \cdot A = Q
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Q

$$
Q = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E A = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{V}{d} A
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Definition of Capacitance

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}
$$

**Engineering Note:** Notice that $C$ scales **inversely with d**. In microrobotics, gaps are typically **1–10 µm**, so capacitances are very small (femtofarads to picofarads).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrostatic Force on a Micro-Actuator Plate

**Starting Premise:** The work done by the electrostatic field equals the change in stored energy.

### Step 1: Compute Stored Electrostatic Energy

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

Substituting the derived expression for C:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d}
$$

### Step 2: Force is the Negative Gradient of Energy

The force in the direction of decreasing gap *d* is:

$$
F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d}\bigg|_{V=\text{const}}
$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign indicates the system does work as *d* shrinks (attractive force pulls plates together).

### Step 3: Perform the Differentiation

$$
F = -\frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d}\right]
$$

$$
F = -\left[-\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}\right] = +\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Typical Microrobot Values

Let $A = 100 \times 100 \,\mu m^2 = 10^{-8}\,m^2$, $d = 2\,\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,m$, $V = 100\,V$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air).

$$
F = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(10^{-8})(100)^2}{2(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-8})(10^4)}{8 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-16}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-4}\,N
$$

**Result:** $F \approx \mathbf{110\,\mu N}$ — sufficient to actuate a microrobot weighing milligrams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RC Time Constant for a MEMS Switching Element

### Step 1: Combine Expressions

$$
\tau = R \cdot C = R \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

### Step 2: Express Resistance via Resistivity

For a thin-film resistor of length *L*, width *w*, thickness *t*:

$$
R = \rho \frac{L}{w t}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
\tau = \rho \frac{L}{w t} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau = \frac{\rho \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L A}{w t d}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This dictates the **maximum switching frequency** $f_{max} \approx 1/(2\pi\tau)$ of any electrostatic microrobot actuator. Reducing any geometric dimension (*t*, *d*) reduces τ, enabling faster operation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Piezoelectric Constitutive Relations (KTU Advanced)

The complete linear piezoelectric coupling uses **two constitutive equations**:

$$
S = s^E \, T + d^t \, E
$$

$$
D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T \, E
$$

Where:
- $S$ = mechanical strain (second-rank tensor)
- $T$ = mechanical stress (second-rank tensor)
- $D$ = electric displacement (vector)
- $E$ = electric field (vector)
- $s^E$ = elastic compliance at constant *E* (fourth-rank)
- $d$ = piezoelectric strain coefficient (third-rank, units **C/N** or **m/V**)
- $\varepsilon^T$ = permittivity at constant *T* (second-rank)
- $d^t$ = transpose of *d*

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Form (Reduced Notation using Voigt convention):** For a thin-film material like **AlN** (aluminum nitride) commonly used in microrobotic transducers, only specific components are non-zero, simplifying the 3×6 matrix.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Property for a Given Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as the structural backbone for any 14-mark design question.**

| Design Goal | Best Electrical Property | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum actuation force | Electrostatic ($F \propto 1/d^2$) | Scales favorably at small gaps |
| Maximum actuation strain | Piezoelectric ($d_{33}$) | Direct strain-voltage coupling |
| Maximum sensitivity (sensor) | Piezoresistive ($\pi$ gauge factor) | Large ΔR/R under stress |
| Lowest power consumption | Capacitive (no DC current) | Only displacement current flows |
| Fastest response | Electromagnetic (Lorentz force) | Limited by coil inductance, not mass |
| Biocompatibility | Piezoelectric (BaTiO₃, PVDF) | Lead-free, implantable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** The diagrams below are constructed as **functional architectural flowcharts** (per the protocol) rather than physical cross-sections, ensuring 100% Mermaid rendering reliability across all KTU examination preparation platforms.

## 4.1 Master Hierarchy — Electrical Properties Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Properties in Microrobotics] --> B[Conduction Properties]
    A --> C[Storage Properties]
    A --> D[Transduction Properties]
    A --> E[Coupling Properties]

    B --> B1[Conductivity sigma]
    B --> B2[Resistivity rho]
    B --> B3[Sheet Resistance Rs]
    B --> B4[Carrier Mobility mu]

    C --> C1[Permittivity epsilon r]
    C --> C2[Loss Tangent tan delta]
    C --> C3[Breakdown Strength Ebd]

    D --> D1[Piezoelectric d33 d31]
    D --> D2[Piezoresistive pi]
    D --> D3[Electrostrictive Q]

    E --> E1[Maxwell Stress]
    E --> E2[Lorentz Force]
    E --> E3[Dielectrophoretic Force]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — How Electrical Properties Drive Microrobot Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Applied Voltage V] --> FIELD[Electric Field E Generation]
    FIELD --> STORAGE[Energy Storage in Capacitor C]
    FIELD --> FORCE[Electrostatic Force F]
    FIELD --> POLAR[Material Polarization P]
    POLAR --> STRAIN[Mechanical Strain S]
    STRAIN --> OUTPUT[Microrobot Motion or Sensing Signal]

    STORAGE -.feedback.-> EFFICIENCY[System Efficiency Eta]
    FORCE -.feedback.-> EFFICIENCY
    EFFICIENCY --> DESIGN[Final Microrobot Design]

    MATERIAL[Material Selection] --> PARAM[Choose sigma epsilon d33]
    PARAM --> FIELD
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Electrical Property for Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Design Requirement] --> Q1{Continuous force needed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Biological environment?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Precise displacement needed?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> PVDF[Use Piezoelectric PVDF]
    Q2 -- No --> PZT[Use PZT Lead Zirconate Titanate]

    Q3 -- Yes --> ACT[Use Piezoelectric d33 Mode]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{High frequency operation?}

    Q4 -- Yes --> EM[Use Electromagnetic Lorentz Force]
    Q4 -- No --> ES[Use Electrostatic Comb Drive]

    PVDF --> END[Final Material Selection]
    PZT --> END
    ACT --> END
    EM --> END
    ES --> END
```

## 4.4 Piezoelectric Coupling — Direct vs Converse Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT[Direct Effect - Sensing]
        M1[Mechanical Stress T] --> POL1[Polarization P Induced]
        POL1 --> CHARGE[Surface Charge Q]
        CHARGE --> SIG[Output Voltage Signal V]
    end

    subgraph CONVERSE[Converse Effect - Actuation]
        V2[Applied Voltage V] --> POL2[Material Polarization P]
        POL2 --> STR[Mechanical Strain S]
        STR --> MOTION[Microrobot Motion]
    end
```

## 4.5 Equivalent Circuit Model of a Microrobotic Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Voltage Source V] --> R[Series Resistance Rs]
    R --> L[Inductance L]
    L --> C[Capacitance C - Mechanical Compliance]
    C --> D[Dielectric Loss Tan Delta]
    D --> GND[Ground]

    C -.coupling.- MECH[Micromechanical Domain]
    MECH --> FORCE[Output Force F]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "draw a schematic," this equivalent circuit is **far safer to draw by hand** than attempting physical MEMS cross-sections. Examiners reward conceptual clarity over artistic detail.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define dielectric breakdown strength. Why is it critical in the design of microrobotic electrostatic actuators?**

**Model Answer:**
Dielectric breakdown strength ($E_{bd}$) is the **maximum electric field** that a dielectric material can withstand before it transitions from an insulator to a conductor, accompanied by physical damage and arcing. It is measured in **V/m**.

In microrobotic electrostatic actuators, the operating gap is on the order of **1–10 µm**, which produces very high fields even at modest voltages. If $E > E_{bd}$, the dielectric fails catastrophically, causing device destruction. Designers must ensure:

$$
E_{operating} = \frac{V}{d} < E_{bd}
$$

For air, $E_{bd} \approx 3 \times 10^6$ V/m. At $d = 2 \,\mu m$, this limits $V$ to **~6 V maximum** in air; in vacuum or with high-$\varepsilon_r$ dielectrics, higher voltages are permissible.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with one application of each in microrobotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Effect | Converse Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Mechanical stress *T* | Electric field *E* |
| Output | Electric displacement *D* (or charge) | Mechanical strain *S* |
| Governing coefficient | $d$ (piezoelectric coefficient) | Same *d* (reciprocal) |
| Microrobotic application | Vibration energy harvesting sensors | Piezoelectric walking actuators (PZT cantilevers) |
| Physical meaning | Mechanical-to-electrical conversion | Electrical-to-mechanical conversion |

**Key Point:** The coefficients are thermodynamically reciprocal, ensuring energy conservation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor with a dielectric medium and explain how this scales when the gap *d* approaches the microscale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microrobotic electrostatic actuator has 50 parallel-plate pairs, each of area $100 \times 100 \,\mu m^2$, separated by an air gap of $2\,\mu m$. If an operating voltage of 50 V is applied, calculate the total electrostatic force generated. Assume $\varepsilon_r = 1$ for air. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Two plates of area *A* separated by distance *d*, filled with dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

**Step 2 — Electric Field:**
$$
E = \frac{V}{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement Field from Gauss's Law:**
$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q \implies D \cdot A = Q
$$

**Step 4 — Constitutive Relation:**
$$
D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E
$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**
$$
Q = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{V}{d} A
$$

**Step 6 — Capacitance Definition:**
$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

**Step 7 — Microscale Discussion (2 marks):**
At small *d*, this formula remains valid **only when** the lateral dimensions ≫ *d* (typically ratio > 10:1). As *d* → 0 (sub-micron), **fringing fields** become dominant and add a parasitic capacitance term $C_{fringe} \approx 0.2 \varepsilon_0 P$, where *P* is the perimeter. Total capacitance becomes:

$$
C_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} + C_{fringe}
$$

Additionally, **quantum tunneling** between plates becomes significant below ~5 nm, eventually causing breakdown of the classical dielectric model.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of plate pairs $N = 50$
- Plate area $A = 100 \times 100 \,\mu m^2 = 10^{-8}\,m^2$
- Air gap $d = 2\,\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,m$
- Voltage $V = 50\,V$
- $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,F/m$

**Step 1 — Single Pair Force:**
$$
F_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
F_1 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(10^{-8})(50)^2}{2(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-8})(2500)}{2(4 \times 10^{-12})}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-17}}{8 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2.767 \times 10^{-6}\,N = 2.767\,\mu N
$$

**Step 3 — Total Force for 50 Pairs:**
$$
F_{total} = N \cdot F_1 = 50 \times 2.767 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{total} = 1.38 \times 10^{-4}\,N \approx 138\,\mu N}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**
This force is sufficient to drive a microrobot of mass **~14 mg** against Earth's gravity ($g = 9.81\,m/s^2$), demonstrating the practical viability of electrostatic actuation in microscale systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is piezoelectricity? Derive the constitutive equations governing the linear piezoelectric effect and identify the units of the piezoelectric coefficient *d*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PZT-5H piezoelectric bender used in a microrobot has dimensions $L = 5\,mm$, $w = 1\,mm$, $t = 0.1\,mm$, and a piezoelectric coefficient $d_{31} = -320\,pC/N$. If an electric field of $E = 10^6\,V/m$ is applied across the thickness, compute (i) the resulting strain, and (ii) the free tip displacement of the cantilever. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Piezoelectricity is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (e.g., quartz, PZT, BaTiO₃, AlN) to interconvert mechanical and electrical energy. It arises from linear electromechanical coupling in the crystal lattice.

**Derivation of Constitutive Equations (4 marks):**

Starting from thermodynamic potentials (Gibbs free energy), the independent variables are stress *T* and field *E*. Expanding strain *S* and displacement *D* as Taylor series and truncating at first order:

$$
S_{ij} = s^E_{ijkl} T_{kl} + d_{kij} E_k
$$

$$
D_i = d_{ikl} T_{kl} + \varepsilon^T_{ik} E_k
$$

Using Voigt notation (index reduction):

$$
S = s^E \, T + d^t \, E
$$

$$
D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T \, E
$$

**Units of *d* (1 mark):** From the first equation, $S$ is dimensionless, so *d* has units of $E^{-1} =$ **m/V** (or equivalently **C/N** from the second equation).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,m$, $w = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,m$, $t = 0.1 \times 10^{-3}\,m = 10^{-4}\,m$
- $d_{31} = -320 \times 10^{-12}\,C/N$
- $E = 10^6\,V/m$

**(i) Strain Calculation (3 marks):**

The strain induced by the field alone (zero stress) is:

$$
S_1 = d_{31} \cdot E
$$

$$
S_1 = (-320 \times 10^{-12})(10^6) = -3.2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_1 = -0.032\% \text{ (compressive)} }
$$

**(ii) Free Tip Displacement (4 marks):**

For a piezoelectric bimorph cantilever operating in $d_{31}$ mode, the free-tip displacement is given by:

$$
\delta = \frac{3}{2} d_{31} \frac{L^2}{t^2} \cdot V
$$

where $V = E \cdot t$ is the voltage across the thickness:

$$
V = (10^6)(10^{-4}) = 100\,V
$$

Substituting:

$$
\delta = \frac{3}{2}(-320 \times 10^{-12})\frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(10^{-4})^2}(100)
$$

$$
\delta = (1.5)(-320 \times 10^{-12})\frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-8}}(100)
$$

$$
\delta = (1.5)(-320 \times 10^{-12})(2500)(100)
$$

$$
\delta = -1.2 \times 10^{-4}\,m
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta = -120\,\mu m}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A 120 µm tip deflection from a 5 mm cantilever at just 100 V demonstrates the high efficiency of piezoelectric microrobotic actuation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $\varepsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ without units. Always write **F/m** or **C²/(N·m²)**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Multiply by N in Force Calculation (–3 marks):** When the problem states "50 plate pairs," many students compute for one pair and stop. Always **re-read the problem** for multiplier terms.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in Piezoelectric Strain (–2 marks):** $d_{31}$ for PZT-5H is **negative**. The strain is compressive. Examiners check for sign discipline.
>
> 4. **Using Bulk Resistivity for Thin Films (–3 marks):** Thin-film sheet resistance $R_s = \rho/t$ must be used; using bulk 3D resistance $R = \rho L/A$ on thin films loses marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Microscale Discussion (–2 marks):** In any derivation question, KTU expects a concluding paragraph on **how the result changes at the microscale**. Always add 1–2 lines explaining the deviation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electrical Properties (Microrobotics)**

- [ ] **Conductivity σ** has units **S/m**; resistivity ρ = 1/σ has units **Ω·m**.
- [ ] **Vacuum permittivity** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is a universal constant.
- [ ] **Capacitance formula** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — area scales **linearly**, gap scales **inversely**.
- [ ] **Electrostatic force** $F = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2 d^2)$ — scales with $1/d^2$, so **shrinking gaps dramatically boosts force**.
- [ ] **Dielectric breakdown of air** ≈ $3 \times 10^6$ V/m sets the upper voltage limit.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric coefficient** $d$ has units **m/V** or equivalently **C/N** (reciprocity).
- [ ] **Direct piezoelectric effect** = sensing; **Converse effect** = actuation.
- [ ] **Loss tangent tan δ** quantifies dielectric heating — must be minimized for energy-efficient microrobots.
- [ ] **Sheet resistance** $R_s = \rho / t$ in **Ω/sq** is the correct metric for thin-film conductors.
- [ ] **Mayadas–Shatzkes effect** reduces conductivity in sub-micron wires — bulk σ overestimates actual σ.
- [ ] **Fringing fields** add parasitic capacitance when lateral dimensions ≤ 10× gap thickness.
- [ ] **RC time constant** governs the **maximum switching frequency** of electrostatic actuators.
- [ ] For **microscale validity** of classical formulas, ensure **lateral dimension ≫ gap dimension** (rule of thumb: ratio > 10).
- [ ] **PZT** is the most common piezoelectric material; **AlN** is preferred for **CMOS-compatible** microrobots.
- [ ] Always end derivation answers with a **microscale interpretation** paragraph for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
