topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: FLUIDS IN MICROROBOTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of **Microrobotics**, the study of *fluids* refers to the behavior, mechanics, and interaction of liquids and gases at the **microscale** (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ m characteristic length). At these dimensions, fluid dynamics departs from our everyday intuition: surface tension, viscosity, and inertial-capillary balance dominate, while inertial forces (which govern a swimmer in a pool) become negligible. The governing framework is **Microfluidics**, often analyzed using the **Navier-Stokes equations** in the low-Reynolds-number (Stokes flow) regime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> The focus is on the transition from macro-scale fluid mechanics to micro-scale fluid mechanics, covering properties of fluids, continuum hypothesis, dimensionless numbers (Reynolds, Knudsen), viscosity models, and the Navier-Stokes equations as applied to microrobotic swimmers, drug-delivery nanobots, and lab-on-a-chip devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Honey vs. Air, Shrunk Down"**
> Imagine shrinking a human swimmer to the size of a bacterium. The water around you would suddenly feel like thick honey. You cannot dive or splash — instead, you can only **glide slowly** because viscous forces grip every part of your body. A bacterium flagellum rotates millions of times to move just a few micrometers. This is the world of **low-Reynolds-number flow** that governs microrobotic propulsion in fluids.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **Pa·s**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**
- **Density of water at 20 °C:** $\rho = 998$ **kg/m³**
- **Surface tension of water at 20 °C:** $\gamma \approx 0.0728$ **N/m**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_0 = 101325$ **Pa**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> A *fluid* is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows) under an applied shear stress or external force**. It includes both liquids and gases and is characterized by its inability to sustain static shear stresses.

### 1.4 Continuum Hypothesis
The **continuum assumption** treats fluid as a continuous medium — physical properties (density, velocity, pressure) are defined at every point in space as continuous functions. This breaks down when the **mean free path** $\lambda$ of fluid molecules becomes comparable to the characteristic length $L$ of the flow domain.

The dimensionless measure of this breakdown is the **Knudsen number**:

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$

- $Kn < 0.001$ — **Continuum regime** (valid Navier-Stokes)
- $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$ — **Slip flow regime**
- $0.1 < Kn < 10$ — **Transition regime**
- $Kn > 10$ — **Free molecular regime** (no continuum)

> [!NOTE]
> For most microrobotic applications (channels $\sim 10$–$100\ \mu m$), $Kn < 0.001$, so the continuum assumption holds. However, for channels smaller than ~1 µm (nanochannels), slip effects must be included.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fluid Properties (Operational Breakdown)

A fluid is fully characterized by the following primary properties, each of which directly influences microrobot design:

- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume, in **kg/m³**. For incompressible fluids, $\rho$ is constant.
- **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** Internal resistance to flow, in **Pa·s** (or **N·s/m²**). Governs how a fluid "drags" a moving object.
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** Ratio $\mu / \rho$, in **m²/s**. Represents the diffusion rate of momentum.
- **Pressure ($P$):** Normal force per unit area exerted by the fluid, in **Pa**.
- **Surface Tension ($\gamma$):** Energy per unit area at a liquid-air interface, in **N/m**.
- **Compressibility ($K$ or $\beta$):** Fractional change in volume under pressure; $\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dP}$.

### 2.2 Why These Properties Matter for Microrobotics
- **Viscosity** dictates propulsion strategy: helical flagella, undulating tails, or magnetic actuation.
- **Surface tension** enables **capillary-driven locomotion** (e.g., water strider-inspired microrobots).
- **Density difference** between robot and fluid determines **buoyancy** and **settling velocity**.
- **Compressibility** matters for **acoustic** and **bubble-based** microrobots.

### 2.3 Newton's Law of Viscosity
For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress $\tau$ is linearly proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate) $\frac{du}{dy}$:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Water, air, and most simple liquids are Newtonian. Blood, polymers, and some biological fluids are **non-Newtonian** (shear-thinning or shear-thickening). KTU 2024 questions typically focus on Newtonian fluids.

### 2.4 The Reynolds Number — The King of Microscale Flow
The Reynolds number ($Re$) is the **dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}$$

where $U$ is the characteristic velocity, $L$ is the characteristic length, and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.

| Regime | Reynolds Number | Dominant Force | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stokes (creeping) flow | $Re \ll 1$ | Viscous | Bacterium in water |
| Laminar | $Re < 2000$ | Inertial | Pipe flow (macro) |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ | Inertial | River flow, airplane wing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A typical bacterium of size $L \approx 1\ \mu m$ swimming at $U \approx 30\ \mu m/s$ in water has $Re \approx 3 \times 10^{-5}$. This is **massively sub-continuum**, meaning inertia is utterly negligible. The Scallop Theorem applies — a microrobot cannot use reciprocal (time-symmetric) motion to propel itself.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $u$ [m/s], $y$ [m] |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | $Re$ [-], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $U$ [m/s], $L$ [m], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $\nu$ [m²/s] |
| 3 | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Knudsen number | $Kn$ [-], $\lambda$ [m], $L$ [m] |
| 4 | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu U}{\gamma}$ | Capillary number | $Ca$ [-], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $U$ [m/s], $\gamma$ [N/m] |
| 5 | $We = \dfrac{\rho U^2 L}{\gamma}$ | Weber number | $We$ [-], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $U$ [m/s], $L$ [m], $\gamma$ [N/m] |
| 6 | $Bo = \dfrac{\Delta \rho g L^2}{\gamma}$ | Bond number | $Bo$ [-], $\Delta\rho$ [kg/m³], $g$ [m/s²], $L$ [m], $\gamma$ [N/m] |
| 7 | $F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U$ | Stokes' drag on a sphere | $F_D$ [N], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $R$ [m], $U$ [m/s] |
| 8 | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re}$ | Drag coefficient (sphere, low Re) | $C_D$ [-], $Re$ [-] |
| 9 | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ | Continuity (incompressible) | $\mathbf{u}$ [m/s] |
| 10 | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{u} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f}$ | Navier-Stokes equation | $\mathbf{u}$ [m/s], $P$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $\mathbf{f}$ [N/m³] |
| 11 | $-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f} = 0$ | Stokes equation (steady, $Re = 0$) | same as above |
| 12 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu U L}{w h^3}$ | Pressure drop (rectangular microchannel) | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $L$ [m], $w$ [m], $h$ [m] |
| 13 | $F_{\gamma} = \gamma \ell$ | Capillary force | $F_{\gamma}$ [N], $\ell$ [m] |
| 14 | $P_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{R}$ | Laplace / capillary pressure (sphere) | $P_{cap}$ [Pa], $\theta$ [rad], $R$ [m] |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Drug delivery nanobots:** Stokes drag dictates the propulsion force budget.
- **Lab-on-a-chip devices:** Capillary pressure (Eq. 14) drives spontaneous filling of microchannels — no external pump needed.
- **Micro-swimmers in blood:** Blood is non-Newtonian; effective viscosity drops in narrow vessels (Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect).
- **Capillary microrobots:** Surface tension gradients propel floating robots (mimicking *Ilyocoris cimicoides* — the backswimmer insect).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Continuity Equation for an Incompressible Fluid

**Starting principle:** Conservation of mass for a fluid element of fixed volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow in (x-direction):**
The mass entering the left face per unit time is $\rho u_x\, dy\, dz$.

**Step 2 — Mass flow out (x-direction):**
The mass leaving the right face is:
$$\left( \rho u_x + \frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x} dx \right) dy\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Net mass accumulation in x-direction:**
The net outflow (out − in) is:
$$\frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all three directions:**
Adding the y- and z- contributions:
$$\left( \frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_z)}{\partial z} \right) dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — Apply conservation of mass:**
The rate of mass change inside the control volume must equal the net outflow:
$$-\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz = \left( \frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_z)}{\partial z} \right) dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0$$

This is the **general continuity equation**.

**Step 7 — Apply incompressibility (constant $\rho$):**
Since $\rho$ does not change with time or position, $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\rho$ can be pulled out of the divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **incompressible continuity equation** used throughout microfluidics. It expresses volume conservation: the net volumetric flow into any infinitesimal region is zero.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stokes' Law ($F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U$)

**Step 1 — Setup:** A rigid sphere of radius $R$ moves at constant velocity $U$ through a viscous, incompressible Newtonian fluid that is otherwise at rest. In the reference frame of the sphere, the fluid flows past the sphere with velocity $U$ at infinity.

**Step 2 — Apply the Stokes equation** (steady, $Re = 0$):
$$-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Use spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ with axisymmetric flow. Stream function $\psi(r, \theta)$:
$$u_r = \frac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \quad u_\theta = -\frac{1}{r \sin\theta} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

**Step 4 — The biharmonic equation** for $\psi$ becomes:
$$\nabla^4 \psi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E^2(E^2 \psi) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions:**
- $u_r = u_\theta = 0$ at $r = R$ (no-slip on sphere)
- $u_r \to U \cos\theta$ and $u_\theta \to -U \sin\theta$ as $r \to \infty$

**Step 6 — Solve by polynomial ansatz:**
The trial solution $\psi(r, \theta) = \left( A r + B r^2 + C r^{-1} + D \right) \sin^2\theta$ is substituted into $E^2\psi = 0$.

**Step 7 — Apply the second condition $E^2(E^2\psi) = 0$** to determine the constants. After algebraic reduction:
$$A = -\frac{3U R}{4}, \quad B = \frac{U}{2}, \quad C = -\frac{U R^2}{4}, \quad D = 0$$

**Step 8 — Velocity field:**
$$u_r = U \cos\theta \left(1 - \frac{3R}{2r} + \frac{R^3}{2r^3}\right)$$
$$u_\theta = -U \sin\theta \left(1 - \frac{3R}{4r} - \frac{R^3}{4r^3}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Pressure distribution** (from $P_r = \mu \nabla^2 u_r$):
$$P = P_\infty - \frac{3 \mu U R \cos\theta}{2 r^2}$$

**Step 10 — Stress tensor on sphere surface** ($r = R$):
The viscous stress component $\tau_{r\theta}$ at the sphere surface integrates to give the drag:
$$F_D = \oint \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r=R} \, R^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$

**Step 11 — Evaluate the integral:**
After trigonometric simplification:
$$F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result — Stokes' Drag Law:**
> $$F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U$$
> This is the **fundamental equation of low-Re propulsion**. A bacterial cell experiences a resistive force directly proportional to its velocity — no inertia to coast on. It must continuously expend energy to move.

### 3.3 Derivation: Capillary Pressure in a Spherical Interface

**Step 1 — Define surface tension** as energy per unit area, equivalent to force per unit length along the interface.

**Step 2 — Consider a soap bubble of radius $R$.** Cut it in half with an imaginary plane through the equator.

**Step 3 — Force balance:** The pressure difference $\Delta P$ across the curved interface pushes the two halves apart with a force $\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$. The surface tension along the equator holds them together with force $\gamma \cdot 2 \pi R$ (factor 2 for the two surfaces of the bubble).

**Step 4 — Equilibrium:**
$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = \gamma \cdot 2 \pi R$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a single interface (e.g., a liquid droplet in air), the factor 2 remains. The general **Young-Laplace equation** is:
> $$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$
> where $R_1, R_2$ are the principal radii of curvature.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Regimes of Microscale Flow

| Length Scale $L$ | Typical $Re$ | Dominant Effect | Governing Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L > 1$ mm | $Re > 100$ | Inertia | Full Navier-Stokes | Macro robotics, drones |
| $L \sim 100\ \mu m$ | $Re \sim 1$ | Mixed | Navier-Stokes (transitional) | Micropumps |
| $L \sim 10\ \mu m$ | $Re \sim 0.01$ | Viscosity | Stokes | Bacteria, drug delivery |
| $L \sim 1\ \mu m$ | $Re \sim 0.0001$ | Viscosity + surface tension | Stokes + Laplace | Nanochannels, micro-swimmers |
| $L < 100$ nm | $Kn > 0.1$ | Molecular | Boltzmann / DSMC | Nanofluidics (rare) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Microscale Fluid Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Environment in Microrobotics] --> B[Identify Characteristic Length L and Velocity U]
    B --> C{Compute Reynolds Number Re}
    C -->|Re greater than 1| D[Inertia-Dominated Regime]
    C -->|Re less than 1| E[Viscous-Dominated Stokes Regime]
    D --> F[Use Full Navier-Stokes Equation]
    E --> G[Use Linear Stokes Equation]
    G --> H[Apply Boundary Conditions: No-Slip on Robot Surface]
    F --> I[Solve Using CFD or Analytical Methods]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Drag Force on Microrobot]
    J --> K[Match Propulsion Strategy: Flagella, Magnetic, Acoustic]
    K --> L[Predict Velocity Output for Design]
    A --> M[Identify Fluid-Fluid Interfaces]
    M --> N[Compute Capillary Number Ca and Bond Number Bo]
    N --> O{Which Dominates?}
    O -->|Ca greater than Bo| P[Viscosity Wins: Surfactant Effects]
    O -->|Bo greater than Ca| Q[Gravity vs Surface Tension Wins]
    Q --> R[Laplace Pressure Drives Capillary Motion]
    P --> S[Design Surface Chemistry to Control Wetting]
    R --> T[Predict Self-Filling of Microchannels]
    S --> T
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Decision Tree for Selecting Fluid Model in Microrobotics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Microrobot in Fluid]) --> q1{Is fluid compressible?}
    q1 -->|Yes| comp[Use Compressible Navier-Stokes]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is Re less than 1?}
    q2 -->|Yes| stokes[Use Stokes Equation: Linear]
    q2 -->|No| ns[Use Full Navier-Stokes: Non-linear]
    stokes --> q3{Capillary effects present?}
    q3 -->|Yes| laplace[Add Young-Laplace Pressure]
    q3 -->|No| solve1[Solve via Stream Function]
    laplace --> solve1
    ns --> cfd[Solve via CFD or DNS]
    comp --> cfd
    solve1 --> endnode([Output: Velocity Field and Drag Force])
    cfd --> endnode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Capillary Force Generation on a Microrobot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Liquid Meniscus around Microrobot] --> B[Surface Tension Acts at Contact Line]
    B --> C[Vertical Component: Holds Robot at Interface]
    B --> D[Horizontal Component: Drives Motion]
    D --> E[Direction of Motion]
    C --> F[Buoyancy-Like Support Force]
    E --> G[Net Propulsive Force F_gamma equals gamma times contact length]
    F --> H[Net Vertical Balance]
    G --> I[Locomotion Achieved]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These block diagrams map the decision logic a designer follows when choosing a fluid model for a microrobotic system. They emphasize that microscale flow is rarely solved with full Navier-Stokes — instead, simplifications (Stokes, Laplace) are exploited for tractability.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Reynolds number and state its significance in microrobotics.**

**Model Answer:**
The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:
$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}$$
where $\rho$ is fluid density, $U$ is characteristic velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, and $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity.
**Significance in microrobotics:** At microscale, $L$ is very small, making $Re \ll 1$. This indicates that viscous forces dominate inertial forces, meaning the flow is in the **Stokes regime**. As a result, microrobots cannot rely on inertia for propulsion and must use **non-reciprocal motion** (e.g., rotating helical flagella). The Scallop Theorem holds, and propulsion strategies differ fundamentally from macro-scale swimmers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is the Scallop Theorem? Why is it relevant to microrobotic propulsion?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Scallop Theorem** (proposed by E.M. Purcell, 1977) states that in a fluid with $Re = 0$ (zero Reynolds number, i.e., pure Stokes flow), a reciprocal motion — one that is identical when played forward or backward in time — produces **zero net displacement**.
**Explanation with example:** A scallop opens and closes its shell in a symmetric motion. In viscous fluid, the opening stroke and closing stroke produce equal and opposite flows, so the net translation is zero.
**Relevance:** Microrobots (bacteria, artificial flagella, drug-delivery nanobots) operate at $Re \ll 1$. Therefore, they cannot use simple back-and-forth motion. They must perform **non-reciprocal deformations** (e.g., rotating a corkscrew, undulating in a wave) to break time-symmetry and achieve net propulsion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid. State the physical meaning of each term.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A spherical microrobot of radius $R = 50\ \mu m$ moves through water at a constant velocity of $U = 200\ \mu m/s$. Compute (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the viscous drag force, and (iii) the power required to overcome drag. Take $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution — (a) Continuity Equation Derivation:**

The continuity equation expresses **conservation of mass** in a fluid. Consider an infinitesimal rectangular control volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$.

**Step 1:** Mass entering in x-direction per unit time:
$$\dot{m}_{x,in} = \rho u_x\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 2:** Mass leaving in x-direction per unit time (Taylor expansion):
$$\dot{m}_{x,out} = \left( \rho u_x + \frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x} dx \right) dy\, dz$$

**Step 3:** Net x-outflow:
$$\dot{m}_{x,out} - \dot{m}_{x,in} = \frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 4:** Adding y- and z- contributions:
$$\frac{\partial (\rho u_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_z)}{\partial z} = \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u})$$

**Step 5:** By conservation of mass, this net outflow equals the rate of decrease of mass inside:
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0$$

**Step 6:** For an **incompressible fluid**, $\rho = \text{constant}$, so:
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$$
This means the **divergence of the velocity field is zero** — the fluid is divergence-free, i.e., volume is conserved. Physically, what flows into a region must flow out.

---

**Model Solution — (b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $R = 50\ \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m; $U = 200\ \mu m/s = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s; $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

**(i) Reynolds number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{(1000)(200 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$L = 2R = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m (diameter)}$$
$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02$$

Since $Re \ll 1$, **Stokes flow** applies.

**(ii) Drag force (Stokes' law):**
$$F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U = 6 \pi (1.0 \times 10^{-3})(50 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6})$$
$$F_D = 6 \pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-8}$$
$$F_D = 6 \pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \approx 1.885 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{N}$$

**(iii) Power required:**
$$P = F_D \cdot U = (1.885 \times 10^{-10})(200 \times 10^{-6})$$
$$P = 3.77 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{W} \approx 37.7\ \text{fW}$$

This is an extremely small power — typical of microrobotic actuation requirements.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the Navier-Stokes equation. Under what conditions does it reduce to the Stokes equation? Why is the Stokes equation **linear** while the Navier-Stokes equation is **non-linear**?

**(b) (7 Marks)** A microrobot experiences a capillary force of $F = 5\ \mu N$ at a water-air interface. The contact-line length is $\ell = 1$ mm. If the surface tension of water is $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m, compute the contact angle $\theta$ between the meniscus and the robot surface. Also explain the role of **Bond number** in determining whether surface tension or gravity dominates.

---

**Model Solution — (a) Navier-Stokes Equation Explanation:**

The **Navier-Stokes equation** is the fundamental equation of motion for a viscous fluid. For an incompressible Newtonian fluid:
$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{u} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f}$$

**Term-by-term explanation:**
- $\rho \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t}$: **Local (unsteady) acceleration** — change in momentum at a fixed point.
- $\rho (\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{u}$: **Convective acceleration** — momentum carried by fluid moving from one region to another. This is the source of **non-linearity**.
- $-\nabla P$: **Pressure gradient force** per unit volume.
- $\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u}$: **Viscous diffusion of momentum.**
- $\mathbf{f}$: **External body force** per unit volume (e.g., gravity $\rho \mathbf{g}$).

**Reduction to Stokes equation:**
When $Re \ll 1$, the inertial terms (left-hand side) become negligible compared to viscous terms (right-hand side). Dropping them yields:
$$-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f} = 0$$

This is the **Stokes equation** (or creeping flow equation).

**Why Stokes is linear:** The convective term $(\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{u}$ contains a product of two velocity components. Dropping it removes the non-linearity, leaving only first-order derivatives in $\mathbf{u}$. The result is a **linear, time-reversible** equation — which is why the Scallop Theorem holds.

---

**Model Solution — (b) Capillary Force and Bond Number:**

**Given:** $F = 5\ \mu N = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ N; $\ell = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m; $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Capillary force equation:**
The component of surface tension force along the direction of motion is:
$$F = \gamma \ell \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\cos\theta$:**
$$\cos\theta = \frac{F}{\gamma \ell} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.072)(10^{-3})} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0694$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angle:**
$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.0694) \approx 86.02^\circ \approx 86^\circ$$

This is a **near-90° contact angle** — typical of a moderately hydrophobic robot surface.

**Role of Bond Number:**
The **Bond number** compares gravitational to surface-tension forces:
$$Bo = \frac{\Delta \rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$

- $Bo \ll 1$: **Surface tension dominates** — interface remains nearly spherical, capillary effects control motion. (Microscale regime.)
- $Bo \gg 1$: **Gravity dominates** — the interface flattens, capillary effects become negligible. (Macroscale regime.)

For the given microrobot with $L \sim 100\ \mu m$:
$$Bo = \frac{(1000)(9.81)(10^{-4})^2}{0.072} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-5}}{0.072} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $Bo \ll 1$, **surface tension dominates**, confirming the validity of using the capillary-force model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Fluid Mechanics Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Mixing up Reynolds number definitions:** Some students use $L = R$ (radius) instead of $L = D$ (diameter) or vice versa. The problem must specify, or the convention $L = D$ (hydraulic diameter) is safer.
> 2. **Forgetting the no-slip condition:** In Stokes' law derivation, students often skip the boundary condition $u = 0$ at the sphere surface. This is worth **2 marks** alone in the KTU scheme.
> 3. **Dimensional inconsistency:** A force computed in **N** but reported in **µN** (or vice versa) without unit conversion loses **0.5 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Knudsen and Reynolds numbers:** $Kn$ uses mean free path $\lambda$ and $L$; $Re$ uses velocity, length, and viscosity. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Applying Stokes' law at high $Re$:** Stokes' law fails for $Re > 1$. The drag coefficient $C_D$ is no longer $24/Re$. Always check the regime first.
> 6. **Sign errors in pressure gradient:** Navier-Stokes problems often lose **1 mark** for a sign mistake in $-\nabla P$ vs $\nabla P$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluids (Module 2, KTU PERAT746):**
> - **Fluid definition:** Continuous medium that flows under applied shear stress; no resistance to static shear.
> - **Continuum hypothesis:** Valid when $Kn < 0.001$; fails for nanochannels.
> - **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — defines Newtonian fluids.
> - **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho U L / \mu$. Threshold $Re \sim 1$ separates inertial and viscous regimes.
> - **Microscale reality:** $Re \ll 1$ → Stokes flow → inertia negligible → Scallop Theorem applies.
> - **Navier-Stokes equation:** $\rho (\partial \mathbf{u}/\partial t + \mathbf{u}\cdot\nabla\mathbf{u}) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f}$. The non-linear term is the convective acceleration.
> - **Stokes equation:** Drop inertial terms; results in a linear, time-reversible system.
> - **Stokes' drag:** $F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U$ — workhorse of microscale propulsion calculations.
> - **Capillary pressure:** $\Delta P = 2 \gamma / R$ (sphere) or $\gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ (general).
> - **Capillary force:** $F = \gamma \ell \cos\theta$ — drives surface-tension microrobots.
> - **Dimensionless numbers to know:** $Re$, $Kn$, $Ca$ (capillary), $We$ (Weber), $Bo$ (Bond).
> - **Continuity equation:** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ for incompressible flow.
> - **Boundary condition:** No-slip ($u = 0$) at solid walls is fundamental in microchannels.
> - **Physical constants:** $\mu_{\text{water}} = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 0.072$ N/m.
> - **Design implication:** Microrobot propulsion must be **non-reciprocal** (rotating helix, traveling wave).
> - **Power requirement:** Microscale power is in the **fW to nW** range — orders of magnitude lower than macro scale.
> - **Cross-link to Module 3:** Fluid properties here will be used for **magnetic actuation** and **swimming dynamics** of helical micro-swimmers.

---

**End of Notes — Fluids (Module 2, PERAT746)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Physical Forces in Microrobotics?

In microrobotics, **Physical Forces** refer to the dominant **interfacial and intermolecular forces** that govern the behavior, actuation, adhesion, and locomotion of micro- and nano-scale robotic systems. As the characteristic length scale of a system shrinks from the macroscopic world (meters) to the microscopic world (micrometers to nanometers), the relative dominance of these forces undergoes a dramatic shift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Physical forces in microrobotics are the **surface forces, intermolecular forces, and scaling-dependent force regimes** that dictate the static and dynamic behavior of micro-scale devices. Unlike macro-scale systems where **gravity and inertia** dominate, micro-scale systems are governed by **surface tension, van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, and viscous drag forces**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two worlds:

- **Macro World (Human Scale):** If you place a metal ball on a table, **gravity** pulls it down firmly. If you give it a push, **inertia** keeps it rolling for a long time. Air resistance is barely noticeable.
- **Micro World (Microrobot Scale):** A tiny microrobot placed on a surface behaves like a **"sticky ball trapped in honey"**. **Gravity is negligible**, but the robot is glued to the surface by molecular attraction. Even a tiny puff of air or a slight vibration can push it around chaotically. The air around it feels as thick as syrup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** At micro-scales, **surface forces (proportional to length² or L²) become dominant over volume forces (proportional to length³ or L³)**. As size shrinks, the surface-to-volume ratio increases dramatically, flipping the force hierarchy upside down.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are **frequently used** in microrobotics force calculations:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$** | J/K |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$** | F/m |
| Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu_{air}$ | **$1.8 \times 10^{-5}$** | Pa·s |
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu_{water}$ | **$1.0 \times 10^{-3}$** | Pa·s |
| Surface tension of water | $\gamma_{water}$ | **$0.072$** | N/m |
| Hamaker constant (typical) | $A_H$ | **$10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$** | J |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **$9.81$** | m/s² |
| Density of water | $\rho_{water}$ | **$1000$** | kg/m³ |

### 1.4 Classification of Physical Forces

Physical forces in microrobotics are classified into two major categories:

1. **Surface / Interfacial Forces** (Dominant at micro/nano scale)
   - van der Waals forces
   - Electrostatic forces
   - Capillary forces
   - Casimir forces

2. **Body / Volume Forces** (Dominant at macro scale)
   - Gravity
   - Inertial forces
   - Electromagnetic body forces
   - Buoyancy

3. **Fluid / Drag Forces** (Critical for locomotion)
   - Viscous drag
   - Brownian motion
   - Thrust and lift forces

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this thumb rule:** When the characteristic length $L < 1$ mm, surface forces $\left(\propto L^{2}\right)$ begin to overpower body forces $\left(\propto L^{3}\right)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Scaling Law: Why Forces Change with Size

The cornerstone principle of microrobotics is the **Scaling Law**. If we scale a physical system by a factor $k$ (where $k \ll 1$ for miniaturization), the relevant forces scale as follows:

- **Length:** $L \rightarrow kL$
- **Area:** $A \propto L^{2} \rightarrow k^{2}A$
- **Volume (and Mass):** $V \propto L^{3} \rightarrow k^{3}V$

| Force Type | Dependence on Length | Behavior when $L$ shrinks |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Gravity (Weight) | $F_g \propto L^{3}$ | Decreases rapidly |
| Inertial Force | $F_i \propto L^{3}$ | Decreases rapidly |
| Surface Tension | $F_{st} \propto L$ | Decreases slowly |
| Adhesion (vdW) | $F_{vdW} \propto L$ | Decreases slowly |
| Viscous Drag | $F_d \propto L$ (at low Re) | Decreases slowly |
| Electrostatic | $F_e \propto L^{2}$ | Decreases moderately |
| Magnetic | $F_m \propto L^{3}$ | Decreases rapidly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface-to-Volume Ratio:** $\dfrac{A}{V} \propto \dfrac{L^{2}}{L^{3}} = \dfrac{1}{L}$

This ratio **increases as $L$ decreases**, meaning surface phenomena dominate at small scales.

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Force

#### 2.2.1 Van der Waals (vdW) Forces

- **Origin:** Arise from instantaneous dipole-induced dipole interactions between molecules.
- **Nature:** Always attractive between identical materials in vacuum.
- **Distance Dependence:** $F_{vdW} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{3}}$ for sphere-flat (close range) and $\dfrac{1}{d^{4}}$ for two parallel plates.
- **Engineering Impact:** Major cause of **stiction** in MEMS/NEMS. Microrobots often "stick" to surfaces due to vdW forces.

#### 2.2.2 Electrostatic Forces

- **Origin:** Coulomb interaction between charged bodies.
- **Behavior:** Can be attractive or repulsive depending on charge polarity.
- **Application:** Used in **electret-based microrobots**, electrostatic actuators, and comb drives.
- **Key Control:** Can be modulated by applied voltage.

#### 2.2.3 Capillary Forces

- **Origin:** Surface tension of liquid menisci formed between closely placed surfaces.
- **Magnitude:** $F_{cap} = 2 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ (sphere-flat contact).
- **Why critical:** Even nanoliters of humidity create capillary bridges that dominate adhesion in humid environments.

#### 2.2.4 Casimir Forces

- **Origin:** Quantum vacuum fluctuations between closely spaced conducting surfaces.
- **Distance Dependence:** $F_{Casimir} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{4}}$
- **Significance:** Becomes significant below 100 nm separations.

#### 2.2.5 Viscous Drag Force

- **Origin:** Resistance from surrounding fluid (gas or liquid) to motion.
- **Regime Indicator:** **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$
- **Low Re Regime ($Re \ll 1$):** Stokes drag dominates, $F_d = 6 \pi \mu R v$
- **High Re Regime ($Re \gg 1$):** Inertial drag dominates, $F_d \propto \rho v^{2} A$

> [!NOTE]
> **Microrobots in fluid almost always operate at $Re \ll 1$**. This means **inertial propulsion is impossible**; only **non-reciprocal motion** (scallop theorem) can generate net displacement.

#### 2.2.6 Brownian Motion

- **Origin:** Random thermal bombardment of molecules on the microrobot.
- **Force Equivalent:** $F_{thermal} \sim \sqrt{2 k_B T \gamma}$ where $\gamma$ is the drag coefficient.
- **Impact:** Causes random walk, limits positional precision, and can be exploited for passive mixing.

#### 2.2.7 Magnetic Forces

- **Origin:** Interaction between magnetic dipole and external field gradient.
- **Formula:** $F_m = V(M \cdot \nabla) B$ where $M$ is magnetization, $B$ is flux density.
- **Advantage:** Wireless, penetrates tissue — used in **medical microrobots**.

#### 2.2.8 Gravity and Inertial Forces

- **Formula:** $F_g = \rho V g$
- **At micro scale:** Negligible compared to surface forces.
- **Example:** A 10 μm silicon cube weighs $\approx 2.3$ pN, while vdW adhesion can be $\approx 100$ nN — a factor of 40,000 difference.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield for KTU 2024 examinations**.

| # | Force / Concept | Formula | Key Parameters | Typical Units |
|---|----------------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Van der Waals (sphere-plane) | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}}$ | $A_H$: Hamaker, $R$: radius, $d$: gap | N |
| 2 | Van der Waals (parallel plates) | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H A}{6 \pi d^{3}}$ | $A$: contact area, $d$: separation | N |
| 3 | Electrostatic (parallel plate) | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$ | $V$: voltage, $d$: gap | N |
| 4 | Capillary Force | $F_{cap} = 2 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | $\gamma$: surface tension, $\theta$: contact angle | N |
| 5 | Casimir Force (per unit area) | $\dfrac{F_{C}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \hbar c}{240 d^{4}}$ | $\hbar$: reduced Planck, $c$: speed of light | N/m² |
| 6 | Stokes Drag | $F_d = 6 \pi \mu R v$ | $\mu$: viscosity, $R$: radius, $v$: velocity | N |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: density, $L$: characteristic length | dimensionless |
| 8 | Brownian Displacement | $\langle x^{2} \rangle = 2 D t$ | $D$: diffusion coefficient, $t$: time | m² |
| 9 | Diffusion Coefficient | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu R}$ | Stokes-Einstein | m²/s |
| 10 | Gravity | $F_g = \rho V g$ | $\rho$: density, $V$: volume | N |
| 11 | Magnetic Force | $F_m = V (M \cdot \nabla) B$ | $M$: magnetization, $B$: field | N |
| 12 | Surface-to-Volume Ratio | $\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{6}{d}$ (cube) | $d$: side length | 1/m |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Dominant Force Exploited |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| AFM / STM probes | van der Waals, Casimir |
| MEMS switches | Electrostatic, Capillary |
| Magnetic microrobots for drug delivery | Magnetic gradient |
| Bacterial-inspired swimming robots | Viscous drag, non-reciprocal motion |
| Self-assembly of nanostructures | Capillary, vdW |
| Lab-on-chip fluidics | Capillary, viscous |
| Gecko-inspired adhesives | van der Waals |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Surface Forces Dominate at Micro Scale

**Goal:** Prove mathematically that as length $L$ shrinks, the ratio of surface force to body force increases.

**Step 1: Define the two force types.**

A typical body force (gravity) scales with **volume**:
$$F_{body} \propto L^{3}$$

A typical surface force (e.g., surface tension or vdW) scales with **length**:
$$F_{surface} \propto L^{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L} \propto L \quad \text{or} \quad L^{2}$$

For a generalized derivation, we use $F_{surface} \propto L^{2}$ (e.g., electrostatic force depends on area).

**Step 2: Form the ratio of surface force to body force.**

$$\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} \propto \dfrac{L^{2}}{L^{3}} = \dfrac{1}{L}$$

This means: $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} = \dfrac{C}{L}$, where $C$ is a constant determined by material properties and force coefficients.

**Step 3: Evaluate at two different scales.**

- Macro scale: $L = 1$ m → $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} = C$ (small)
- Micro scale: $L = 10^{-6}$ m → $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} = 10^{6} C$ (very large)

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

The ratio increases by a factor of $10^{6}$ when we go from 1 m to 1 μm. **Hence, surface forces dominate at the micro scale.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Van der Waals Force Between a Sphere and a Flat Surface

**Goal:** Derive $F_{vdW}$ for a sphere of radius $R$ at separation $d$ from a flat surface.

**Step 1: Start with the parallel plate formula.**

For two infinite parallel plates of area $A$ separated by distance $d$:
$$F_{vdW,pp} = \dfrac{A_H A}{6 \pi d^{3}}$$

Here, $A_H$ is the Hamaker constant and depends on the material pair.

**Step 2: Slice the sphere into thin rings.**

Approximate the sphere as a stack of thin circular rings of radius $r(x)$ at height $x$ from the surface, where $r(x) = \sqrt{R^{2} - (R-x)^{2}}$. For $x \ll R$:
$$r(x) \approx \sqrt{2 R x}$$

**Step 3: Compute vdW force for an infinitesimal ring.**

Each ring of width $dx$ has circumference $2 \pi r(x)$ and area $dA = 2 \pi r(x) dx$. The force contribution is:
$$dF = \dfrac{A_H}{6 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi r(x)}{(x+d)^{3}} dx = \dfrac{A_H r(x)}{3 (x+d)^{3}} dx$$

**Step 4: Substitute and integrate.**

$$F_{vdW} = \int_{0}^{2R} \dfrac{A_H \sqrt{2 R x}}{3 (x+d)^{3}} dx$$

**Step 5: Apply the close-range approximation $d \ll R$.**

The integrand is dominated near $x = 0$, so we can extend the upper limit to infinity and approximate $r(x) \approx \sqrt{2 R x}$:
$$F_{vdW} \approx \dfrac{A_H \sqrt{2R}}{3} \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{(x+d)^{3}} dx$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integral using substitution $x = d \tan^{2}\theta$.**

After substitution and standard integral evaluation:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{(x+d)^{3}} dx = \dfrac{1}{2 d^{2}}$$

**Step 7: Combine to get the final result.**

$$F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H \sqrt{2R}}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2 d^{2}} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}}$$

This is the **famous sphere-flat vdW force formula**, valid for $d \ll R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This force is **inversely proportional to $d^{2}$**, meaning that halving the gap quadruples the adhesion force. This is why surface roughness of just a few nanometers can drastically reduce stiction in MEMS.

### 3.3 Derivation: Reynolds Number for a Swimming Bacterium

**Goal:** Show that a typical bacterium operates in the low Reynolds number regime.

**Step 1: Define characteristic parameters for *E. coli*.**

- Length: $L \approx 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Velocity: $v \approx 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- Density of water: $\rho \approx 1000$ kg/m³
- Viscosity of water: $\mu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**Step 2: Substitute into Reynolds number formula.**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{(1000)(2 \times 10^{-5})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator.**

$$\rho v L = 1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4: Divide by viscosity.**

$$Re = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5: Conclude.**

$$Re = 4 \times 10^{-5} \ll 1$$

This confirms that *E. coli* operates in the **viscous-dominated (Stokes) regime**, where inertia is negligible and reciprocal motion produces no net displacement (Scallop Theorem).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Force Comparison for a 10 μm Silicon Cube

To build intuition, let us compare all relevant forces on a 10 μm silicon cube ($\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³):

**Cube properties:**
- Side: $L = 10$ μm $= 10^{-5}$ m
- Volume: $V = L^{3} = 10^{-15}$ m³
- Surface area: $A = 6 L^{2} = 6 \times 10^{-10}$ m²
- Mass: $m = \rho V = 2.33 \times 10^{-12}$ kg

| Force | Calculation | Numerical Value |
|-------|------------|-----------------|
| Gravity | $F_g = m g = 2.33 \times 10^{-12} \times 9.81$ | $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-11}$ N |
| vdW (sphere approx, $R = L/2$, $d = 0.5$ nm) | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}} = \dfrac{10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}$ | $\approx 1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ N |
| Capillary (humid, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 0$) | $F_{cap} = 2 \pi R \gamma = 2 \pi (5 \times 10^{-6})(0.072)$ | $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-6}$ N |
| Stokes drag ($v = 100$ μm/s) | $F_d = 6 \pi \mu R v = 6 \pi (10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-6})(10^{-4})$ | $\approx 9.4 \times 10^{-12}$ N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ratio check:** $F_{cap} / F_g \approx 10^{5}$, $F_{vdW} / F_g \approx 7 \times 10^{3}$. This quantitatively proves that **surface forces dominate gravity by 4 to 5 orders of magnitude** at the 10 μm scale.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain the concept of scaling laws in microrobotics. Why do surface forces dominate over body forces at the micro scale?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Scaling laws describe how physical quantities change when a system's characteristic length $L$ is uniformly scaled. The key observation is:

- **Body forces** (gravity, inertia) scale as $L^{3}$
- **Surface forces** (van der Waals, capillary) scale as $L^{2}$ or $L$

The ratio of surface force to body force scales as $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$.

As $L$ decreases, this ratio increases, meaning surface forces progressively dominate. At micrometer scales, surface forces can exceed body forces by 4 to 5 orders of magnitude, completely reversing the force hierarchy observed in the macro world.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Ratio (1) + Quantitative comparison (1) = **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define Reynolds number. Explain why microrobots in fluid typically operate at very low Reynolds number.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that compares inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid:
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

where $\rho$ is fluid density, $v$ is velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, and $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity.

For a typical microrobot (e.g., $L = 10$ μm, $v = 100$ μm/s in water):
$$Re \approx 10^{-6} \ll 1$$

Since $Re \ll 1$, viscous forces dominate inertia. This implies that microrobots **stop moving immediately** when actuation ceases, and **reciprocal motion produces no net displacement** (Scallop Theorem). Locomotion strategies must therefore use **non-reciprocal deformation** such as rotating flagella or traveling-wave cilia.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition + formula (1) + Sample calculation (1) + Implication (1) = **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]*

**(a) Derive an expression for the van der Waals force between a sphere of radius $R$ and a flat surface separated by a distance $d$. Assume $d \ll R$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Begin with the parallel-plate van der Waals force per unit area:
$$\dfrac{F_{pp}}{A} = \dfrac{A_H}{6 \pi d^{3}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Model the sphere as a stack of thin parallel rings. At vertical position $x$ above the flat surface, the ring radius is:
$$r(x) = \sqrt{R^{2} - (R-x)^{2}} \approx \sqrt{2 R x} \quad \text{(for } x \ll R\text{)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The differential force on a ring of width $dx$ is:
$$dF = \dfrac{A_H}{6 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi r(x) \, dx}{(x + d)^{3}} = \dfrac{A_H \sqrt{2 R x}}{3 (x + d)^{3}} dx$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate over the sphere from $x = 0$ to $x = 2R$. For $d \ll R$, the integrand is significant only near $x = 0$, so:
$$F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H \sqrt{2R}}{3} \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{(x + d)^{3}} dx$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Use the substitution $u = x/d$, $dx = d\,du$:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{(x+d)^{3}} dx = d^{-2} \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{u}}{(u+1)^{3}} du = \dfrac{1}{2 d^{2}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final result:
$$\boxed{F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}}}$$

---

**(b) A spherical microrobot of radius $R = 5$ μm sits on a flat silicon surface. The Hamaker constant is $A_H = 10^{-19}$ J and the surface separation is $d = 1$ nm due to roughness. Calculate: (i) the van der Waals adhesion force, (ii) the gravitational force on the microrobot assuming density $\rho = 2000$ kg/m³, and (iii) the ratio of adhesion to gravity. Comment on the implications for microrobot design. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Van der Waals Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}} = \dfrac{(10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{-6})}{6 \times (10^{-9})^{2}}$$

$$F_{vdW} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-25}}{6 \times 10^{-18}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N} \approx 83.3 \text{ nN}$$

**Part (ii) — Gravitational Force (2 marks):**

$$V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (5 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 5.236 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$F_g = \rho V g = 2000 \times 5.236 \times 10^{-16} \times 9.81$$

$$F_g \approx 1.027 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N} \approx 10.3 \text{ pN}$$

**Part (iii) — Ratio and Comment (3 marks):**

$$\dfrac{F_{vdW}}{F_g} = \dfrac{8.33 \times 10^{-8}}{1.027 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 8110$$

**Implications:**
- Adhesion is ~8000 times stronger than gravity → the microrobot will stick permanently to the surface.
- Releasing the robot requires **active forces** (vibration, magnetic, electrostatic) that exceed $F_{vdW}$.
- Surface roughness dramatically increases effective $d$, reducing adhesion. This is a key MEMS design strategy.
- Designers must include **anti-adhesion coatings** (e.g., hydrophobic monolayers) or use **intentional surface texturing** to prevent stiction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Explain the origin of van der Waals, capillary, and Casimir forces in microrobotics. Compare their distance dependencies and typical magnitudes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Van der Waals Forces (2.5 marks):**
- **Origin:** Instantaneous fluctuating dipoles in atoms/molecules induce complementary dipoles in neighboring atoms, leading to net long-range attraction.
- **Distance dependence:** $F_{vdW} \propto d^{-3}$ (sphere-flat)
- **Range:** 0.2 nm to 10 nm
- **Magnitude:** Order of 10 nN to 100 nN for micrometer-scale contacts

**Capillary Forces (2.5 marks):**
- **Origin:** Surface tension of liquid meniscus (typically condensed water vapor) forming a bridge between closely placed surfaces.
- **Formula:** $F_{cap} = 2 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$
- **Distance dependence:** Effective only when surfaces are within a few nanometers (meniscus can form)
- **Magnitude:** Can reach several μN in humid environments — often the strongest adhesion force for MEMS in air

**Casimir Forces (2 marks):**
- **Origin:** Quantum mechanical zero-point energy of the electromagnetic field between conducting surfaces.
- **Distance dependence:** $F_{Casimir}/A \propto d^{-4}$
- **Range:** Significant below 100 nm
- **Magnitude:** Small at micrometer scales, dominant at nanoscale. Crucial for NEMS design.

**Comparison Table (built into explanation):**

| Property | vdW | Capillary | Casimir |
|----------|-----|-----------|---------|
| Origin | Molecular dipoles | Surface tension | Quantum vacuum |
| Distance law | $d^{-3}$ | Contact only | $d^{-4}$ |
| Tunable? | No (material) | Yes (humidity) | No |

---

**(b) A magnetic microrobot of radius $R = 50$ μm is submerged in water. It moves at velocity $v = 200$ μm/s under the influence of an external magnetic field gradient. Calculate: (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the Stokes drag force, and (iii) the minimum magnetic force required to sustain this motion. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\mu_{water} = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Comment on the dominant force regime. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Reynolds Number (2 marks):**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6})}{10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = 0.01$$

**Part (ii) — Stokes Drag Force (2 marks):**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu R v = 6 \pi (10^{-3})(50 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-8} = 1.885 \times 10^{-10} \text{ N} \approx 0.19 \text{ nN}$$

**Part (iii) — Minimum Magnetic Force (3 marks):**

For steady motion (zero acceleration), the net force is zero:
$$F_{magnetic} = F_d = 1.885 \times 10^{-10} \text{ N} \approx 0.19 \text{ nN}$$

**Comment:**
- $Re = 0.01 \ll 1$ → **viscous regime dominates** completely.
- Inertial effects are negligible; if the magnetic field is switched off, the robot stops within microseconds (Stokes stopping time $\tau = m/(6\pi\mu R) \approx 0.05$ μs).
- Locomotion strategy must be **non-reciprocal** if relying on body deformation (e.g., flagellar propulsion).
- Magnetic actuation is ideal because the force is **wireless, tunable, and penetrates biological tissue**, making it suitable for medical microrobots.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up force scaling laws** — Students often write $F \propto L^{2}$ for gravity. Gravity is **always** $\propto L^{3}$ because it depends on **mass (volume × density)**. Losing 1–2 marks here.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Hamaker constant units** — The Hamaker constant is in Joules (J), not N·m. Many students write incorrect unit conversions.
>
> 3. **Missing the $6 \pi$ in Stokes drag** — A classic error is using $F_d = \mu R v$ instead of $6 \pi \mu R v$. The factor $6\pi$ is **mandatory** for a sphere.
>
> 4. **Wrong distance dependence for Casimir force** — The Casimir force is $\propto d^{-4}$ for force per unit area, **not** $d^{-3}$ like vdW.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "approximation" clause** — In derivations, students forget to state "$d \ll R$" or "$Re \ll 1$" before applying the simplified formula. KTU examiners **always deduct** for missing assumptions.
>
> 6. **Not specifying Reynolds number regime** — Always state whether your system is in the low-Re or high-Re regime **before** writing the drag formula. This is a 1-mark item.
>
> 7. **Confusing surface tension with surface energy** — Surface tension $\gamma$ has units of N/m = J/m². They are numerically equal but dimensionally different in derivation contexts.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Physical Forces**

- [ ] **Scaling Law Foundation:** Body forces $\propto L^{3}$, surface forces $\propto L$ to $L^{2}$, hence $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{body}} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$.
- [ ] **Van der Waals Sphere-Plane Formula:** $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{6 d^{2}}$ — memorize the constant, the variable positions, and the units.
- [ ] **Hamaker Constant $A_H$:** Typical value $10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J. Material-dependent, always positive for same materials.
- [ ] **Capillary Force:** $F_{cap} = 2 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$. $\gamma_{water} = 0.072$ N/m. Strongest in humid MEMS environments.
- [ ] **Casimir Force:** Quantum vacuum origin. $\dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \hbar c}{240 d^{4}}$. Dominant below 100 nm.
- [ ] **Electrostatic Force:** $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$ for parallel plates. Used in comb drives, electret actuators.
- [ ] **Stokes Drag:** $F_d = 6 \pi \mu R v$ — applies only when $Re \ll 1$.
- [ ] **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$. Microrobots in fluid: $Re \sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$.
- [ ] **Scallop Theorem:** In low-Re regime, reciprocal motion → zero net displacement. Need non-reciprocal deformation.
- [ ] **Brownian Motion:** $\langle x^{2} \rangle = 2Dt$, $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu R}$. Sets fundamental precision limit.
- [ ] **Magnetic Force:** $F_m = V (M \cdot \nabla) B$. Wireless, biocompatible, ideal for medical microrobots.
- [ ] **Gravity at micro scale:** $F_g = \rho V g$ — often **negligible** compared to adhesion.
- [ ] **Key dimensionless numbers:** Reynolds ($Re$), Capillary ($Ca$), Bond ($Bo$), Weber ($We$), Péclet ($Pe$).
- [ ] **Design strategies against stiction:** Surface texturing, hydrophobic coatings, anti-adhesion monolayers, active release mechanisms.
- [ ] **Numerical values to memorize:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_{water} = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma_{water} = 0.072$ N/m.
- [ ] **Application mapping:** AFM/STM → vdW/Casimir; MEMS switches → electrostatic; medical microbots → magnetic; bio-inspired swimmers → viscous regime strategies.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" forces, **always present a table** with columns for origin, distance dependence, magnitude, and tunability. Examiners reward structured answers with clear visual organization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Torsion** is the twisting of an object due to an applied **torque** (twisting moment) about its longitudinal axis. In the specific context of **Microrobotics (PERAT746)**, torsion is a fundamental mechanical phenomenon governing the behavior of **microshafts, microactuators, micro-grippers, and compliant flexure mechanisms** that transmit rotational motion at the micro-scale (typically $1 \mu m$ to $1 mm$ feature sizes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Torsion is the deformation of a structural member (such as a shaft, beam, or micro-actuating arm) when subjected to a twisting moment acting about its longitudinal axis. The internal reaction produced in the member is termed the **Torsional Shear Stress**, and the resulting angular displacement is the **Angle of Twist**.*

Mathematically, torsion is characterized by the equilibrium of a cylindrical or prismatic bar of length $L$ and radius $r$, subjected to a torque $T$ at its free end.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine wringing out a wet towel by holding both ends and rotating them in opposite directions. The towel **twists** along its length — this is torsion in action.

In **microrobotics**, the same principle applies to:
- A **microsurgical instrument shaft** transmitting rotation from a motor (outside the body) to a cutting tip (inside the body).
- A **microactuator's drive shaft** in a MEMS gyroscope, where torsion determines angular sensitivity.
- A **compliant flexure hinge** in a micro-gripper, where controlled twist enables precise jaw movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember (in Bold):**
> - Modulus of Rigidity for Steel: $G = 79.3 \text{ GPa}$
> - Modulus of Rigidity for Silicon (MEMS material): $G \approx 66 \text{ GPa}$
> - Modulus of Rigidity for Polysilicon: $G \approx 66 \text{ GPa}$
> - Modulus of Rigidity for PDMS (elastomer): $G \approx 0.00025 \text{ GPa}$
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

## 1.3 Why Torsion Matters in Microrobotics

In conventional macro-scale machinery, torsion is a structural concern (preventing shaft failure). In microrobotics, torsion is a **functional design parameter**:

| Scale | Role of Torsion |
|-------|-----------------|
| **Macro (>10 mm)** | Structural integrity, avoid failure |
| **Micro (1 µm – 1 mm)** | Sensing, actuation, calibration, design compliance |

At the micro-scale, **surface-to-volume ratios** are enormous, and **surface effects (stiction, friction, adhesion)** dominate over bulk effects. Torsional compliance becomes a *feature*, not a flaw — it allows predictable, repeatable angular deflection in sensors and actuators.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In Module 2 of PERAT746, students must connect classical torsion theory (from Strength of Materials) with micro-scale design constraints. Classical formulas hold, but material selection, fabrication method (MEMS lithography, 3D micro-printing), and scale-dependent effects (size effects in material properties) become critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Pure Torsion Theory

The classical theory of torsion (Coulomb, 1784; de Saint-Venant, 1855) used in microrobotics design assumes:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **linearly elastic** (follows Hooke's Law in shear).
2. Cross-sections **remain plane and circular** after twisting (no warping).
3. Cross-sections **rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis.
4. **Radial lines remain straight and radial** — the distance from the axis to any point is preserved.
5. The twist is **uniform** along the length (constant torque).
6. **No axial loads** are applied simultaneously (pure torsion only).

> [!NOTE]
> At the micro-scale, assumption (1) often fails for materials like **single-crystal silicon** (anisotropic) or **PDMS** (hyperelastic). Thus, **anisotropic torsion theory** and **nonlinear torsion** are active research areas relevant to MEMS/Microrobotics.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Torsion Equation — Logic Steps

The torsion equation relates torque, shear stress, angle of twist, and geometry:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Logical breakdown of each term:**

- **$T$ (Torque)**: Internal twisting moment in N·m, equal to the applied external torque under static equilibrium.
- **$J$ (Polar Moment of Inertia)**: Geometric property of the cross-section, measuring its resistance to twisting. Units: $\text{m}^4$.
- **$\tau$ (Shear Stress)**: Internal tangential stress at radius $r$. Units: Pa or N/m².
- **$r$ (Radial Distance)**: Distance from the central axis to the point of interest.
- **$G$ (Modulus of Rigidity)**: Material property, shear modulus. Units: Pa.
- **$\theta$ (Angle of Twist)**: Total angular displacement in radians.
- **$L$ (Length of Shaft)**: Axial length over which twist occurs. Units: m.

**The "Why" Behind the Equation:**
- The first ratio $\frac{T}{J}$ represents the **rate of twist per unit length** (geometrically determined).
- The second ratio $\frac{\tau}{r}$ represents the **shear strain rate** (from compatibility — radial lines remain straight).
- The third term $\frac{G\theta}{L}$ represents the **shear stress via Hooke's Law** (from constitutive behavior).
- Equating them produces a closed-form design equation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 ESE & CE — Commit to Memory**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Torsion equation (master formula) | Mixed |
| 2 | $J_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, solid circular shaft | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 3 | $J_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, hollow circular shaft | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 4 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Max shear stress, solid shaft | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T D}{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}$ | Max shear stress, hollow shaft (at outer surface) | Pa |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Angle of twist (radians) | rad |
| 7 | $\theta^{\circ} = \dfrac{180 T L}{\pi G J}$ | Angle of twist (degrees) | deg |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted by rotating shaft | W |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque from power and speed | N·m |
| 10 | $k_{t} = \dfrac{G J}{L}$ | Torsional stiffness | N·m/rad |
| 11 | $\phi = \dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Angle of twist per unit length | rad/m |
| 12 | $\gamma_{\max} = \dfrac{\tau_{\max}}{G} = \dfrac{r \phi}{1}$ | Maximum shear strain | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning for KTU Exam:** Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\left\vert x \right\vert$ in written answers; do NOT use the bare vertical bar $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX-rendered exam scripts, as it may cause compilation warnings.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Microrobotics applications**, these equations directly enable:

- **Design of microsurgical drill shafts** (bone biopsy, neural probes): Ensuring $\tau_{\max} < \tau_{\text{allowable}}$.
- **MEMS gyroscope tuning**: Selecting $J$, $G$, $L$ for a desired resonant frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_t}{I}}$.
- **Micro-gripper flexure design**: Predicting jaw opening angle $\theta$ for a given actuation torque $T$.
- **Power transmission in micro-turbines**: Computing $P$ for micro-generators and actuators.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark Part B question, always start by stating the torsion equation, then define each variable, then substitute numerical values **with units shown at every step**. Examiners award step marks generously.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Polar Moment of Inertia for a Solid Circular Shaft

**Objective:** Derive $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ for a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$.

**Starting Concept:** The polar moment of inertia is the second moment of area about the centroidal axis (perpendicular to the cross-section):

$$J = \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA$$

**Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Choose a differential area element. For a circular cross-section, the natural choice is a thin annular ring at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$:

$$dA = 2 \pi r \, dr$$

*Explanation: The circumference of the ring is $2\pi r$, multiplied by the thickness $dr$ gives the area of the ring.*

**Step 2:** Substitute $dA$ into the integral:

$$J = \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot (2 \pi r) \, dr$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the integrand:

$$J = 2 \pi \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr$$

**Step 4:** Perform the integration:

$$J = 2 \pi \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

**Step 5:** Apply limits $0$ to $R$:

$$J = 2 \pi \left( \frac{R^{4}}{4} - 0 \right) = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Convert from radius $R$ to diameter $d$ using $R = d/2$:

$$J = \frac{\pi (d/2)^{4}}{2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Torsion Equation $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$

**Objective:** Derive the full torsion equation from first principles using equilibrium, compatibility, and Hooke's Law.

### Part A: Shear Stress Distribution (Equilibrium)

Consider a solid shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$ subjected to torque $T$ at one end (free) and fixed at the other. The torque is resisted by **shear stresses $\tau$** distributed over the cross-section.

The torque contributed by an annular ring at radius $r$ of area $dA$ is:

$$dT = \tau \cdot r \cdot dA$$

Integrating over the entire cross-section:

$$T = \int_{A} \tau \cdot r \, dA$$

**Assumption:** Shear stress varies linearly with radius (from compatibility, derived below):

$$\tau = k \cdot r$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality.

Substituting:

$$T = k \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA = k \cdot J$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{T}{J}$$

Therefore, at any radius $r$:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}} \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

### Part B: Angle of Twist (Compatibility + Hooke's Law)

Consider two cross-sections separated by an axial distance $L$. After twisting, the free end rotates by angle $\theta$ relative to the fixed end.

**Compatibility:** A line on the outer surface (at radius $R$) originally axial becomes helical. The shear strain $\gamma$ is:

$$\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

For a point at radius $r$:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Hooke's Law in Shear:**

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}} \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$

### Part C: Combining Equations 1 and 2

From Equation 1: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r}$

From Equation 2: $\dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$

By transitivity:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **torsion equation** — the cornerstone of all torsion design in microrobotics.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Angle of Twist for a Tapered or Stepped Shaft

**Objective:** For a microshaft with $n$ segments of different diameters, find total angle of twist.

For segment $i$ with length $L_i$, diameter $d_i$, and torque $T_i$:

$$\theta_i = \frac{T_i L_i}{G J_i} = \frac{32 T_i L_i}{G \pi d_i^{4}}$$

**Total angle of twist** (assuming torque continuity or proper torque distribution):

$$\theta_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{32 T_i L_i}{G \pi d_i^{4}}$$

In **radians**.

**Conversion to degrees:**

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = \theta_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{180^{\circ}}{\pi}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A micro-shaft in a surgical microrobot is made of stainless steel ($G = 79.3$ GPa), with diameter $d = 2$ mm and length $L = 50$ mm. A torque $T = 0.05$ N·m is applied.

**Find:** (a) Maximum shear stress, (b) Angle of twist in degrees.

**Solution (a):**

Given: $T = 0.05$ N·m, $d = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m.

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \times 0.05}{\pi \times (0.002)^{3}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{0.8}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.8}{2.513 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 31.83 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.002)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11}}{32} = 1.571 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.05}{79.3 \times 10^{9} \times 1.571 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.246 \times 10^{-1}} = 0.02007 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to degrees:

$$\theta_{\deg} = 0.02007 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.02007 \times 57.2958$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\deg} \approx 1.15^{\circ}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Microrobotics:** A 1.15° twist over 50 mm is small but significant. If this is a probe insertion shaft, even sub-degree twist can accumulate and cause tip misalignment of hundreds of microns at the end-effector — a critical concern in microsurgery.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Torsion Mechanics — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional flow of torsion analysis** from input torque to output deformation, mapping the dependencies between parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Torque T] --> B[Internal Torsional Reaction]
    B --> C[Shear Stress Distribution tau]
    B --> D[Angular Displacement theta]
    C --> E[Material Resistance G]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Geometric Property J]
    F --> G[Torsion Equation T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
    A --> H[Power Transmission P]
    H --> I[Microshaft Speed N]
    I --> J[Drive Microactuator]
    J --> A
    G --> K[Design Output]
    K --> L1[Max Shear Stress tau max]
    K --> L2[Angle of Twist theta]
    K --> L3[Torsional Stiffness k t]
    K --> L4[Resonant Frequency for MEMS]

    classDef input fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,H,I,J input
    class B,C,D,E,F,G process
    class K,L1,L2,L3,L4 output
```

## 4.2 Torsion Analysis Step-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Geometry d, L, hollow or solid] --> S2[Step 2: Compute Polar Moment J]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Material Modulus G]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Torsion Equation]
    S4 --> S5A[Compute tau max]
    S4 --> S5B[Compute theta]
    S4 --> S5C[Compute Torsional Stiffness k t]
    S5A --> S6[Check Against Allowable Stress and Deflection Limits]
    S5B --> S6
    S5C --> S6
    S6 --> S7{Design OK?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Finalize Microshaft Design]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Iterate: Change d, L, or Material]
    S9 --> S1

    classDef stepBox fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionBox fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#990066,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef finalBox fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5A,S5B,S5C,S6,S9 stepBox
    class S7 decisionBox
    class S8 finalBox
```

## 4.3 Torsion Parameter Dependency Map (For Microrobotic Components)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TQ[Input Domain]
        T1[Torque T from Micro Motor]
        T2[Power P and Speed N]
    end

    subgraph GEO[Geometry Domain]
        G1[Shaft Diameter d]
        G2[Length L]
        G3[Cross Section Type]
    end

    subgraph MAT[Material Domain]
        M1[Modulus of Rigidity G]
        M2[Yield Strength tau yield]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Domain]
        O1[Max Shear Stress tau max]
        O2[Angle of Twist theta]
        O3[Torsional Stiffness k t]
        O4[Resonant Frequency f]
    end

    TQ --> GEO
    TQ --> OUT
    GEO --> OUT
    MAT --> OUT

    O1 --> APP1[Microsurgical Drill Safety Check]
    O2 --> APP2[Microgripper Calibration]
    O3 --> APP3[MEMS Gyroscope Tuning]
    O4 --> APP4[Vibration Isolation Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note for Students:** The above diagrams are **functional architecture mappings**, not physical drawings. For visual shaft-twist representations, refer to your Strength of Materials textbook (e.g., Beer & Johnston, or Bhavikatti) for the standard helical-twist schematic.
