  4. **Scalability** — compatible with batch-fabrication processes (DRIE, LIGA).
  5. **No assembly error** — critical at micro-scale where tolerances are sub-µm.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $E = 130 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\sigma_y = 7 \times 10^9$ Pa, $L = 0.002$ m, $b = 0.0005$ m, $t = 0.00005$ m, $\theta = 8° = 0.1396$ rad.

**(i) Rotational stiffness (4 blades, $K_{\theta} = 2E b t^3 / L$):**
$$K_{\theta} = \frac{2 \times (130\times10^9) \times (0.0005) \times (0.00005)^3}{0.002}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 130\times10^9 = 2.6\times10^{11}$; $\times 0.0005 = 1.3\times10^8$; $\times (0.00005)^3 = 1.3\times10^8 \times 1.25\times10^{-13} = 1.625\times10^{-5}$.
Denominator: $0.002$.

$$K_{\theta} = \frac{1.625 \times 10^{-5}}{0.002} = 8.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m/rad}$$

$$\boxed{K_{\theta} \approx 8.13 \text{ mN·m/rad}}$$

**(ii) Maximum allowable rotation:**
$$\theta_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_y L}{2E\,t} = \frac{(7\times10^9)(0.002)}{2 \times (130\times10^9)(0.00005)}$$

Numerator: $7\times10^9 \times 0.002 = 1.4\times10^7$.
Denominator: $2 \times 130\times10^9 \times 0.00005 = 1.3\times10^7$.

$$\theta_{\max} = \frac{1.4\times10^7}{1.3\times10^7} \approx 1.077 \text{ rad} \approx 61.7°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} \approx 61.7°}$$

**(iii) Safety at $\theta = 8°$:**

Actual stress at $\theta = 8°$:
$$\sigma = \frac{2E\,t\,\theta}{L} = \frac{2 \times (130\times10^9)(0.00005)(0.1396)}{0.002}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 130\times10^9 \times 0.00005 \times 0.1396 = 1.815 \times 10^6$.
Denominator: $0.002$.

$$\sigma = \frac{1.815 \times 10^6}{0.002} = 9.07 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 907 \text{ MPa}$$

Safety factor:
$$n = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma} = \frac{7000}{907} \approx 7.7$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 7.7 \gg 1.5 \text{ (safe)}}$$

Operating at 8° is **very safe** with a safety factor of ~7.7.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the factor of 2 vs 4:** Students often write $K_{\theta} = \dfrac{E\,b\,t^3}{L}$ instead of $\dfrac{E\,b\,t^3}{3L}$ for a *cross strip*. Remember: each strip contributes $\dfrac{2EI}{L}$, and two strips add in parallel.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** when computing $\theta$ in stiffness equations. Always: $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.
> 3. **Mixing up $I$ and the strip dimensions:** $I = \dfrac{b\,t^3}{12}$ uses the *width $b$* and *thickness $t$* — students often write $L$ instead of $b$.
> 4. **Ignoring the safety factor:** Examiners expect a numerical *comment* on whether the design is safe (typically $n \geq 1.5$ for static applications).
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled sketch:** A neat, dimensioned diagram in Part (a) is worth 1–2 marks by itself in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Writing units inconsistently:** Use SI throughout (Pa, m, N, N·m). Mixing mm and m is a common deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cross Pivot** = four short blades crossed at right angles; provides 1-DOF rotation, finite COR shift.
- **Cross Strip Flexure** = two long thin strips crossed; provides 1-DOF rotation, near-zero COR shift.
- **Monolithic Cross Pivot** = single-piece construction (DRIE, EDM, laser, 2PP); eliminates friction, backlash, and assembly errors.
- **Rotational stiffness of one strip:** $k_{\theta} = \dfrac{2EI}{L}$ (antisymmetric mode).
- **Rotational stiffness of cross strip (2 strips):** $K_{\theta} = \dfrac{4EI}{L} = \dfrac{E\,b\,t^3}{3L}$.
- **Rotational stiffness of cross pivot (4 blades):** $K_{\theta} = \dfrac{2E\,b\,t^3}{L}$.
- **Lateral stiffness (cross strip):** $K_{lat} = \dfrac{2E\,b\,t^3}{L^3}$.
- **Maximum bending stress:** $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{2E\,t\,\theta}{L}$ (at the clamped root).
- **Maximum elastic rotation:** $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_y L}{2E\,t}$.
- **Design rule:** Increase $L/t$ for compliance; reduce $L/t$ for stiffness; always check $\sigma_{\max} \leq \sigma_y / n$.
- **Assumption set:** Small deflection, linear elastic, rigid clamps, Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.
- **KTU favourite question types:** Sketch + derive $K_{\theta}$ (7 marks) and numerical design problem (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexure Systems and Linear Guidance Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flexure Systems and Linear Guidance Systems # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Flexure Systems — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > A **flexure system** is a compliant (flexible) mechanical element that deforms elastically to permit a constrained, predictable, and frictionless motion. In microrobotics, flexures are the fundamental "joints" and "bearings" that replace conventional roller, ball, or sliding bearings because the small physical clearances of traditional bearings become impossible to manufacture, lubricate, or assemble at the micro-scale. Flexures exploit the **elastic deformation** of a slender, carefully shaped segment of an otherwise rigid body. The material is the same as the body itself (monolithic construction), which is one of the most important properties of flexure systems in MEMS and micro-positioning stages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight** > Flexure joints are **monolithic, frictionless, vacuum-compatible, and stiction-free**, making them indispensable in microrobotic manipulators, micro-positioning stages, probe stations, and scanning probe microscopy. ## 1.2 Linear Guidance Systems — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > A **linear guidance system** is a mechanical sub-assembly whose function is to constrain a moving platform (translator) to translate along a single straight axis while suppressing rotations and off-axis translations. In micro-robotic systems, the guide is almost always implemented using **compliant flexure elements** because conventional linear bearings are unsuitable at the micro-scale. The degrees of freedom (DoF) of an unconstrained rigid body in 2D are 3 (one translation along x, one along y, one rotation θ). A good 1-DoF linear guide must allow **only one translation** and constrain the other two DoF. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition **Flexure analogy — "the diving board that always comes back":** Imagine the springboard at a swimming pool. It is firmly clamped at one end and free at the other. When a diver steps on the free end, it bends (elastically) and returns to its original shape once the diver jumps off. It does so **without any sliding, rolling, or lubrication**. That is exactly how a flexure works. A diving board is a *leaf flexure*; a thin strip of steel that bends but never breaks. **Linear guide analogy — "the elevator shaft that prevents swaying":** Think of an elevator that must move strictly up and down. Without a guide rail, the elevator would swing sideways, tilt, and rotate. The guide rails prevent the unwanted motions but still allow the desired vertical motion. A flexure-based linear guide plays the same role — the "rails" are made of thin flexible ligaments, not sliding contact surfaces. > [!TIP] > **Why not use ball bearings at the micro-scale?** > - Manufacturing tolerances become unachievable below ~1 mm. > - Lubricant films become discontinuous (stiction). > - Contamination / particle clogging dominates friction. > - Assembly becomes impossible without precision fixtures. > Flexures elegantly sidestep *every* one of these problems. ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Young's modulus of spring steel | E | **200 × 10⁹** | Pa | | Young's modulus of beryllium copper | E | **130 × 10⁹** | Pa | | Young's modulus of aluminium 6061 | E | **69 × 10⁹** | Pa | | Yield strength of spring steel (tempered) | σ_y | **1.2 × 10⁹** | Pa | | Typical flexure thickness in MEMS | t | **5 – 200** | µm | | Typical flexure length | L | **0.5 – 20** | mm | | Typical micro-stage range | Δx | **1 – 500** | µm | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Flexure Hinges Used in Microrobotics A flexure hinge is the **atomic building block** of every compliant mechanism. The four most common types appearing in the KTU syllabus are: 1. **Leaf flexure (cantilever):** A rectangular cantilever beam clamped at one end. It allows large deflection and rotation but provides limited off-axis stiffness. 2. **Notch (hinge) flexure:** The body is narrowed by a small notch, concentrating bending at a single, well-defined location. Two main geometries: *circular* and *corner-filleted* (also called *leaf-type with fillets*). 3. **Crossed-strip pivot:** Two crossed thin strips clamped at right angles; allows rotation about a virtual centre. 4. **Living-hinge / poly-articulated:** A series of thin ligaments in series or parallel that simulate a large rotation with many small angular deflections. ## 2.2 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Concentrating strain energy:** By narrowing the cross-section at the hinge location, the designer forces almost all elastic deformation to occur in a tiny, well-defined region. This guarantees that the *input stiffness* is predictable and the *output rotation* is well defined. - **Material choice:** Flexures are typically made from **spring steel**, **beryllium copper**, or **titanium alloys** because these materials combine high yield strength (large elastic range) with high fatigue resistance. - **Symmetry exploitation:** The two halves of a flexure are usually mirror-symmetric to cancel out thermal expansion and to double the rotational range. - **Geometric stress relief:** Fillets (rounded corners) are added to reduce peak stress concentration. A sharp corner can multiply the nominal stress by a factor of 3 or more; a properly sized fillet reduces it to ~1.5. ## 2.3 Linear Guidance Architectures A linear guide must restrain the two parasitic DoF (lateral translation and rotation about the perpendicular axis) while allowing the desired DoF (axial translation). The principal flexure-based architectures are: - **Symmetric double-leaf (cantilever pair) guide:** Two parallel leaf flexures in mirror symmetry. The translator is fixed to the free ends of both flexures; the symmetry cancels parasitic rotation. - **Bridge-type (monolithic parallelogram) guide:** A monolithic block carved into a parallelogram. The four flexure ligaments (one on each corner) act like the four-bar linkage. The translator moves purely in translation. - **Compound / nested guide:** Two or more guides cascaded to suppress higher-order parasitic errors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parasitic motion** in a linear guide is the small, unintended rotation or off-axis translation that occurs alongside the desired translation. In KTU problems, the parasitic rotation **α** of a parallelogram flexure of leg length *L* due to a translation *Δx* is given by **α ≈ Δx / L** (for small deflections). A common trick to reduce this is to use **butterfly** or **compound** geometries. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Convention used below:** b = width (out-of-plane), t = thickness (in the bending direction), L = length, E = Young's modulus, R = fillet radius, ν = Poisson's ratio, F = axial force, Δx = deflection, θ = rotation, σ = bending stress. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cantilever tip deflection under end load F | $\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E\, b\, t^{3}}$ | m | Double-leaf doubles stiffness | | 2 | Cantilever bending stiffness (per leaf) | $K = \dfrac{E\, b\, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ | N/m | In-plane bending | | 3 | Maximum bending stress in cantilever | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{b\, t^{2}}$ | Pa | Occurs at fixed end | | 4 | End-rotation of cantilever | $\theta = \dfrac{6 F L^{2}}{E\, b\, t^{3}}$ | rad | Useful for notch flexures | | 5 | **Circular notch flexure rotational stiffness** | $K_{\theta} = \dfrac{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}}{9 \pi\, \sqrt{R}}$ | N·m/rad | R = fillet radius | | 6 | Circular notch flexure maximum rotation (yield) | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{2\,\sigma_{y}\, L}{E\, t}$ | rad | Approximate, L = effective bending length | | 7 | Parallelogram flexure lateral stiffness | $K_{y} = \dfrac{2 E\, b\, t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | N/m | Two parallel cantilevers | | 8 | Parallelogram flexure parasitic rotation | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$ | rad | First-order approximation | | 9 | Compound flexure stiffness (n leaves in series) | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{K}{n^{2}}$ | N/m | Springs-in-series analogy (stiffness in 1/n²) | | 10 | Stress in fillet (Inglis/Neuber) | $\sigma_{peak} = \sigma_{nom}\, K_{t}$ | Pa | $K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{t/R}$ for sharp notch | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** Notice that all absolute-value or division bars above use `\vert` / `\mid` or stacked fractions in display mode — never the raw `|` pipe — to prevent breaking the markdown table layout. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM / AFM):** Atomic-force microscopes use flexure-guided micro-scanners with sub-nanometre positioning accuracy. - **Micro-assembly workstations:** Compliant XY stages position micro-grippers during the assembly of MEMS parts. - **Optical micro-bench systems:** Flexure mounts hold optical fibres and micro-lenses without drift. - **Semiconductor lithography:** Wafer stages use flexure-based **air-bearings** combined with compliant guides to achieve nanometre straightness. - **Space deployable micro-robots:** Flexure joints are vacuum-tolerant and radiation-tolerant, ideal for planetary micro-rovers. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stiffness of a Single Leaf (Cantilever) Flexure We model the leaf as a uniform cantilever beam of length *L*, width *b*, thickness *t*, fixed at one end and loaded by a transverse point load *F* at the free end. We want the tip deflection *δ*. **Step 1 — Euler-Bernoulli moment of inertia.** For bending about the neutral axis, the rectangular cross-section gives $$ I = \frac{b\, t^{3}}{12} $$ This is the second moment of area for a rectangle bent in its *weak* direction. (Bending about the *strong* axis would use $I = t b^{3}/12$.) **Step 2 — Governing differential equation (Euler-Bernoulli).** $$ E\, I\, \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} = M(x) $$ For a tip load *F*, the bending moment at a section located distance *x* from the fixed end is $$ M(x) = F\,(L - x) $$ **Step 3 — Integrate once to obtain the slope.** $$ E\, I\, \frac{d y}{d x} = \int F (L - x)\, dx = F L x - \frac{F x^{2}}{2} + C_1 $$ Boundary condition: at the fixed end, slope = 0 → $C_1 = 0$. **Step 4 — Integrate a second time to obtain the deflection.** $$ E\, I\, y = \frac{F L x^{2}}{2} - \frac{F x^{3}}{6} + C_2 $$ Boundary condition: at the fixed end, *y* = 0 → $C_2 = 0$. **Step 5 — Evaluate at the free end (x = L).** $$ y(L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{2 E I} - \frac{F L^{3}}{6 E I} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} $$ Substituting $I = b t^{3}/12$: $$ \delta = y(L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E (b t^{3}/12)} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E\, b\, t^{3}} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for the stiffness K = F/δ.** $$ K = \frac{E\, b\, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} $$ > **Engineering takeaway:** The stiffness scales **linearly** with width *b*, **cubically** with thickness *t*, and **inversely with the cube of length *L***. This is why micro-flexures can be made very compliant simply by lengthening the hinge or thinning the cross-section. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Bending Stress in a Leaf Flexure **Step 1 — Bending stress distribution.** $$ \sigma(x, y) = \frac{M(x)\, y}{I} $$ where *y* is measured from the neutral axis. The maximum occurs at the surface, $y = \pm t/2$, at the location of maximum moment, $x = 0$ (the fixed end). **Step 2 — Maximum moment.** $$ M_{\max} = F \cdot L $$ **Step 3 — Maximum stress.** $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot (t/2)}{I} = \frac{F L \cdot t/2}{b t^{3}/12} = \frac{6 F L}{b\, t^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Yield-limited maximum load.** Setting $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{y}$ (yield strength) and solving for *F*: $$ F_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{y}\, b\, t^{2}}{6 L} $$ **Step 5 — Corresponding maximum deflection.** $$ \delta_{\max} = \frac{F_{\max}}{K} = \frac{\sigma_{y}\, b\, t^{2}}{6 L} \cdot \frac{4 L^{3}}{E b t^{3}} = \frac{2 \sigma_{y} L^{2}}{3 E\, t} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stiffness of a Circular Notch Flexure A circular notch flexure is a beam whose width is *minimised* over a small arc of radius *R* centred at the hinge axis. The minimum thickness is *t*, the width of the body is *B*, and the bending moment is approximately constant over the notch region. **Step 1 — Local second moment of area.** The narrowest section has $I_{min} = b\, t^{3}/12$ (with *b* being the out-of-plane depth of the notch, equal to the body width). For the rest of the body, the moment of inertia is much larger; we can therefore treat the notch as a localised rotational spring. **Step 2 — Compliance integral.** The compliance of a region with moment of inertia *I(x)* subjected to a constant moment *M* is $$ C = \int_{0}^{L_{n}} \frac{1}{E\, I(x)} dx $$ For a circular notch of radius *R*, the moment arm at angle *φ* is $r(\varphi) = R \sin\varphi$, and the local thickness is $t(\varphi) = 2(R \sin\varphi)$. After substituting and integrating from $0$ to $\pi/2$ (symmetry), one obtains the standard textbook result: $$ C \approx \frac{9 \pi \sqrt{R}}{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}} $$ **Step 3 — Rotational stiffness.** $$ K_{\theta} = \frac{1}{C} = \frac{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}}{9 \pi \sqrt{R}} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum rotation limited by yield.** The maximum strain at the outer fibre of the notch is $$ \varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{t/2}{\rho} $$ where *ρ* is the local radius of curvature, and equating to yield strain $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ gives the rotation limit. After algebra: $$ \theta_{\max} = \frac{2 \sigma_{y}}{E} \cdot \frac{L}{t} $$ where *L* is the effective length over which bending occurs. A more conservative form often used in KTU exam solutions is $\theta_{\max} \approx 2 \sigma_{y} L / (E t)$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parasitic Rotation of a Symmetric Parallelogram Linear Guide A parallelogram flexure has four identical ligaments of length *L*. When the translator is displaced by *Δx* along the guide axis, each ligament rotates by an angle *α*. **Step 1 — Geometric relation.** By small-angle geometry, the tip displacement of one cantilever ligament of length *L* under axial force *F* is $$ \Delta x = L \sin\alpha \approx L \alpha $$ Therefore: $$ \alpha = \frac{\Delta x}{L} $$ **Step 2 — Total parasitic rotation of the rigid translator.** The translator is attached to four ligaments, two on each side. By symmetry, the rotation of the translator is the average of the two ligament rotations on the same side. If both ligaments on a side have the same *L*, the rotation is exactly *α*. **Step 3 — Suppression by compound architecture.** For a "compound" guide made of *n* identical parallelograms in series, the *translation* is multiplied by *n* but the parasitic rotation is *unchanged* (it is purely geometric). This is the key advantage of cascaded guides: *displacement amplification without rotation amplification*. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Flexure Geometry | Application | Recommended flexure | Reason | |---|---|---| | Hinge with large rotation | Circular notch | Stress concentration is well managed, smooth bending | | Pure linear guide, small stroke | Symmetric double leaf | Simplicity, ease of manufacture | | Large stroke, low parasitic rotation | Compound parallelogram | Geometry decouples stroke from rotation | | Torsional micro-spring | Crossed-strip pivot | Pure torsional compliance | | Vacuum / cleanroom stage | Monolithic leaf flexure | No lubricants, no particle generation | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Flexure-Based Linear Guidance System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Actuator
Piezo or Voice Coil] --> B[Flexure Input Joint
Notch Hinge] B --> C[Rigid Translator Block
Carries Payload] C --> D[Flexure Output Joints
Symmetric Pair] D --> E[Linear Output Axis
Pure 1-DoF Translation] C -. parasitic rotation .-> F[Constraint By-Symmetry
Double Leaf Architecture] F --> D C -. parasitic lateral motion .-> G[Constraint By Bridge
Parallelogram Ligaments] G --> D H[Material: Spring Steel E 200 GPa] --> B H --> D ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Taxonomy of Flexure Hinge Geometries ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Flexure Hinge Family] --> B1[Leaf Cantilever] A --> B2[Notch Hinge] A --> B3[Crossed Strip Pivot] A --> B4[Polyarticulated Living Hinge] B1 --> C1[Use: large stroke, low stiffness] B2 --> C2[Use: well-defined rotation axis] B2 --> D1[Circular Fillet] B2 --> D2[Corner Fillet] B3 --> C3[Use: pure torsional compliance] B4 --> C4[Use: distributed bending, low stress] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Linear Guide Architectures ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Symmetric Double Leaf] --> A2[Translator moves in X] A2 --> A3[Parasitic Y constrained by mirror pair] A2 --> A4[Parasitic rotation constrained by mirror pair] B1[Bridge Parallelogram] --> B2[Four ligaments form closed loop] B2 --> B3[Parasitic Y constrained by ligament bending] B2 --> B4[Parasitic rotation is geometric, equal to dX over L] C1[Compound Nested Guide] --> C2[Stroke multiplied by N stages] C2 --> C3[Parasitic rotation unchanged] C2 --> C4[Stiffness reduced by factor N squared] ``` ## 4.4 Decision-Flow Diagram — Design Process for a Micro-Robotic Linear Guide ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Define stroke, payload, accuracy] --> S2{Small stroke below 50 um} S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use symmetric double-leaf flexure] S2 -- No --> S4{Large stroke above 50 um} S4 --> S5[Use compound parallelogram] S4 --> S6{Sub-nm straightness required} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Add nested compound guide] S6 -- No --> S8[Use single bridge parallelogram] S3 --> S9[Verify yield strength sigma max below sigma yield] S5 --> S9 S7 --> S9 S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Compute parasitic rotation alpha equals dX over L] S10 --> S11[End of design] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a *function*, not a physical shape. The dotted arrows represent *constraints* (parasitic-DoF suppression). The solid arrows represent *load path* or *signal flow*. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Q: Define a flexure hinge. List any two advantages of flexure hinges over conventional bearings in microrobotic systems.** **Model Answer:** A flexure hinge is a monolithic, complaint (flexible) element of a structure that allows rotation or translation through elastic deformation of a deliberately thinned region of the parent material, without any sliding, rolling, or lubrication. It replaces conventional bearings in microsystems. **Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):** 1. **Frictionless and stiction-free** motion — there are no contacting surfaces, so the motion is ideal at the micro-scale where stiction and friction dominate. 2. **Monolithic construction** — the hinge and the body are made of the same piece of material, eliminating assembly errors, backlash, and the need for lubrication. 3. **Vacuum and cleanroom compatible** — no outgassing, no particle generation, ideal for MEMS and semiconductor applications. 4. **Predictable and linear** behaviour within the elastic range, with no wear and no hysteresis. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is parasitic motion in a flexure-based linear guide? Write the expression for parasitic rotation of a symmetric parallelogram flexure of leg length L undergoing a translation Δx.** **Model Answer:** **Parasitic motion** is the unintended, secondary motion (such as rotation or off-axis translation) that accompanies the desired motion of a compliant mechanism. It arises because a flexure is not a perfect hinge. **Expression for parasitic rotation** of a symmetric parallelogram flexure: $$ \alpha \approx \frac{\Delta x}{L} $$ For small deflections, the rotation *α* (in radians) is the ratio of the axial displacement *Δx* to the ligament length *L*. A common KTU follow-up asks the student to justify the *advantage* of a compound (nested) guide: cascading *n* stages multiplies the stroke by *n* but leaves the parasitic rotation unchanged. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **A (a) Derive the expression for the bending stiffness of a single leaf flexure (cantilever) used as a hinge in a microrobotic system. State clearly the assumptions of the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli theory (1.5 marks):** 1. The beam material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic. 2. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending. 3. Shear deformation and rotary inertia are neglected (slender beam). 4. The deflection is small compared to the beam length. 5. The cross-section does not warp. **Derivation (5.5 marks) — recap of Section 3.1:** For a cantilever of length *L*, width *b*, thickness *t*, fixed at one end, loaded transversely by *F* at the free tip, the moment of inertia is $$ I = \frac{b\, t^{3}}{12} $$ The governing equation is $E I\, y''(x) = M(x)$ with $M(x) = F(L - x)$. Integrating twice with the boundary conditions $y(0) = 0$ and $y'(0) = 0$: $$ E I\, y(x) = \frac{F L x^{2}}{2} - \frac{F x^{3}}{6} $$ Evaluating at $x = L$: $$ \delta = y(L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E\, b\, t^{3}} $$ Therefore the bending stiffness is $$ K = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E\, b\, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} $$ This is the required expression. Note the cubic dependence on thickness and the inverse-cubic dependence on length. --- **(b) A micro-positioning stage uses a double-leaf flexure of length L = 10 mm, width b = 8 mm, and thickness t = 0.2 mm, made of spring steel with E = 200 GPa. The stage must support a vertical payload of 2 N with a maximum allowable tip deflection of 50 µm. Determine (i) the actual tip deflection, (ii) the maximum bending stress, and (iii) whether the design is safe given a yield strength σ_y = 1.2 GPa. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Tip deflection (3 marks).** For a double-leaf flexure, stiffness is doubled: $$ K = \frac{2\, E\, b\, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} = \frac{E\, b\, t^{3}}{2 L^{3}} $$ Substituting values (all in SI units): - L = 10 × 10⁻³ m - b = 8 × 10⁻³ m - t = 0.2 × 10⁻³ m - E = 200 × 10⁹ Pa - F = 2 N $$ K = \frac{200\times10^{9} \cdot 8\times10^{-3} \cdot (0.2\times10^{-3})^{3}}{2 \cdot (10\times10^{-3})^{3}} $$ Computing step by step: $$ t^{3} = 0.2\times10^{-3}\cdot 0.2\times10^{-3}\cdot 0.2\times10^{-3} = 8\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^{3} $$ $$ L^{3} = 1\times10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{3} $$ $$ K = \frac{200\times10^{9} \cdot 8\times10^{-3} \cdot 8\times10^{-12}}{2 \cdot 1\times10^{-9}} = \frac{200 \cdot 8 \cdot 8 \times 10^{9-3-12}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{12800 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.4 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m} $$ Therefore: $$ \delta = \frac{F}{K} = \frac{2}{6.4 \times 10^{6}} = 3.125 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.3125\,\mu\text{m} $$ **This is well within the 50 µm limit. ✓** **(ii) Maximum bending stress (2 marks).** $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 F L}{b\, t^{2}} = \frac{6 \cdot 2 \cdot 10\times10^{-3}}{8\times10^{-3} \cdot (0.2\times10^{-3})^{2}} $$ $$ t^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^{2} $$ $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-2}}{32 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{0.12}{3.2 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.75 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa} = 375\,\text{MPa} $$ **(iii) Safety check (2 marks).** The factor of safety is $$ n = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{3.75 \times 10^{8}} = 3.2 $$ **The design is safe with a factor of safety of 3.2. ✓** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **B (a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a circular notch flexure hinge. Derive the expression for its rotational stiffness. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch description (1.5 marks):** A circular notch flexure is a beam whose side walls are cut into a circular arc of radius *R*, leaving a minimum cross-sectional thickness *t* at the centre of the notch. The body of the beam has width *B ≫ t*. The notch is symmetric about the hinge axis. The body material outside the notch is essentially rigid; nearly all bending is concentrated in the notched region. **Working (2 marks):** When a moment *M* is applied across the notch, the thinned region deforms elastically. Because the second moment of area $I(x) = b t(x)^{3}/12$ is smallest at the centre of the notch, the strain energy density peaks there. Most of the relative rotation between the two sides of the hinge occurs within the notch, so the hinge behaves as a **localised rotational spring** with stiffness $K_\theta$. **Derivation (3.5 marks) — recap of Section 3.3:** The rotational compliance is the integral of the local bending compliance along the notch arc: $$ C = \int \frac{1}{E\, I(x)} dx $$ For a circular notch, the local thickness is $t(x) = 2 R \sin(x/R)$ and $dx = R\, d\varphi$. Substituting and integrating from 0 to $\pi/2$ (and doubling by symmetry): $$ C \approx \frac{9 \pi \sqrt{R}}{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}} $$ The rotational stiffness is the inverse: $$ K_{\theta} = \frac{1}{C} = \frac{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}}{9 \pi \sqrt{R}} $$ This is the required expression. Note the $t^{5/2}$ dependence and the $R^{-1/2}$ dependence: *thicker* and *smaller fillet radius* both increase the stiffness. --- **(b) A circular notch flexure made of beryllium copper (E = 130 GPa, σ_y = 800 MPa) has b = 5 mm, t = 0.3 mm, R = 1 mm. The hinge must support a moment of 0.05 N·m. Compute (i) the rotation produced, (ii) the maximum elastic rotation limited by yield, and (iii) the factor of safety on rotation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Rotation produced (2.5 marks).** $$ K_{\theta} = \frac{2 E\, b\, t^{5/2}}{9 \pi \sqrt{R}} $$ Compute step by step: $$ t^{5/2} = (0.3\times10^{-3})^{5/2} = (3\times10^{-4})^{2.5} $$ $$ (3\times10^{-4})^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ (3\times10^{-4})^{0.5} = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2} = 1.732 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ t^{5/2} = 9 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.732 \times 10^{-2} = 1.559 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{5/2} $$ $$ \sqrt{R} = \sqrt{1\times10^{-3}} = 3.162 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^{1/2} $$ $$ K_{\theta} = \frac{2 \cdot 130\times10^{9} \cdot 5\times10^{-3} \cdot 1.559\times10^{-9}}{9 \pi \cdot 3.162\times10^{-2}} $$ Numerator: $2 \cdot 130 \cdot 5 \cdot 1.559 \times 10^{9-3-9} = 2026.7 \times 10^{-3} = 2.027$ Denominator: $9 \pi \cdot 3.162\times10^{-2} = 9 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 3.162\times10^{-2} = 0.8937$ $$ K_{\theta} = \frac{2.027}{0.8937} = 2.268\,\text{N·m/rad} $$ Therefore the rotation is: $$ \theta = \frac{M}{K_{\theta}} = \frac{0.05}{2.268} = 0.0220\,\text{rad} = 1.26^{\circ} $$ **(ii) Maximum elastic rotation (2 marks).** $$ \theta_{\max} \approx \frac{2 \sigma_{y} L}{E t} $$ For a notch flexure, the effective bending length *L* is taken equal to the fillet radius *R* (a common KTU convention) — so *L = R = 1 mm*: $$ \theta_{\max} = \frac{2 \cdot 800 \times 10^{6} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-3}}{130 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.3 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1600}{39} = 41.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad} = 2.35^{\circ} $$ **(iii) Factor of safety on rotation (2.5 marks).** $$ n_{\theta} = \frac{\theta_{\max}}{\theta} = \frac{41.0 \times 10^{-3}}{22.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.86 $$ **The hinge operates with a rotational factor of safety of ~1.86. For long-term reliability, KTU examiners usually expect a factor of safety ≥ 2; the designer should consider a slightly thicker notch or a smaller moment.** --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in KTU valuation on this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4 vs 3:** Many students confuse the *clamped-clamped* and *cantilever* formulas for tip deflection. The single-leaf cantilever tip deflection is $4FL^{3}/(Ebt^{3})$; the *clamped-clamped* centre deflection is $FL^{3}/(48EI)$ — completely different. State the boundary conditions explicitly. > 2. **Confusing *b* and *t*:** In a leaf flexure, *b* is the **out-of-plane** width and *t* is the **in-plane bending** thickness. Swapping them is the single most common cause of wrong numerical answers. > 3. **Neglecting parasitic rotation in parallelogram guides:** KTU asks *what limits* the accuracy of a parallelogram flexure. The correct answer is its geometric parasitic rotation $\alpha = \Delta x / L$, NOT friction. > 4. **Forgetting units:** The expression $K = E b t^{3}/(4 L^{3})$ requires all quantities in **metres** (or all in mm — be consistent). Mixing mm and m gives answers off by factors of 10⁶. > 5. **Incorrect moment of inertia:** The flexure cross-section is *rectangular*; the moment of inertia is $b t^{3}/12$, not $\pi t^{4}/32$ (which is for a circular wire — wrong entirely here). > 6. **Not stating Euler-Bernoulli assumptions:** A 1.5-mark question often asks for the assumptions; skipping them costs easy marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] A **flexure** is a monolithic, frictionless elastic joint used in microrobotics to replace conventional bearings. - [x] A **linear guidance system** constrains a translator to a single DoF using flexure ligaments. - [x] **Leaf (cantilever) flexure stiffness:** $K = E b t^{3}/(4 L^{3})$; stress: $\sigma_{\max} = 6 F L/(b t^{2})$. - [x] **Circular notch flexure rotational stiffness:** $K_{\theta} = 2 E b t^{5/2}/(9 \pi \sqrt{R})$. - [x] **Parallelogram flexure parasitic rotation:** $\alpha = \Delta x / L$. - [x] **Compound (cascaded) flexure** multiplies stroke by *n* but leaves parasitic rotation unchanged. - [x] Material choice: **spring steel** (E = 200 GPa) and **beryllium copper** (E = 130 GPa) are the most common. - [x] Stress concentration at sharp corners is mitigated using **fillet radii**; typical stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{t/R}$. - [x] Flexures are inherently **monolithic**, **frictionless**, **vacuum-compatible**, and **wear-free**. - [x] The governing assumptions for nearly all KTU flexure problems are the **Euler-Bernoulli beam assumptions** (slender, linear-elastic, small-deflection, plane-sections-remain-plane). - [x] **Units consistency** is the single biggest source of numerical errors in flexure problems — convert all dimensions to metres before substituting. - [x] **Factor of safety** is typically evaluated as the ratio $\sigma_y / \sigma_{\max}$ for load, or $\theta_{\max} / \theta$ for rotation; KTU expects FoS ≥ 2 for static micro-robotic applications.

topics.title: Introduction to Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Microrobotics**, an **Actuator** is formally defined as a compliant micro-scale transduction element that converts an input energy domain (electrical, magnetic, thermal, chemical, optical, or biochemical) into a controlled mechanical output — typically in the form of **displacement (stroke)**, **force/torque**, or **vibration** — at the sub-millimetre scale, while satisfying the stringent dimensional, power, and force-density constraints imposed by the operating environment.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PERAT746 – Module 3), an actuator in a microrobot is the **"muscle"** of the system. Without a competent actuator, locomotion, manipulation, sensing, and task-execution become physically impossible. The actuator is therefore the **single most critical subsystem** that defines the feasibility envelope of any microrobot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT746, Module 3):**
> An actuator in microrobotics must be evaluated against four canonical metrics: **(1) Work Output / Stroke, (2) Force/Torque Density, (3) Bandwidth/Response Time, and (4) Power Efficiency**. Memorise these four pillars — they appear every year in either Part A or Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a human body without muscles — the brain is perfect, the nervous system is intact, but the body cannot move a single finger. That is exactly what happens to a microrobot without an actuator. The on-board processor, the microcontroller, and the sensors can all be brilliant, but if the actuator cannot physically push, pull, twist, or vibrate the robot in a controlled way, the microrobot is just an inert "chip on a substrate."

A more useful everyday analogy:

| Biological Counterpart | Robot Counterpart | Energy Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bicep muscle | Electrostatic comb drive | Electric field |
| Heart muscle | Piezoelectric cantilever | Electric field (strain) |
| Cell flagellum | Magnetic micro-swimmer | Magnetic field |
| Inchworm locomotion | SMA (Shape Memory Alloy) micro-arm | Thermal field |

The **crucial difference** at the micro-scale is that *the laws of physics change their relative dominance*. Friction, viscosity, and surface forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary) overtake inertial forces. Therefore, an actuator that works perfectly at the macro-scale (e.g., a DC motor) often **fails catastrophically** when shrunk to the micro-scale — and this is the central motivation for studying microrobotic actuators as a separate discipline.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These constants appear repeatedly in KTU derivation problems. Keep them on your formula-sheet finger-tips.

- Permittivity of free space: $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{F/m}$
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\text{H/m}$
- Standard power-density benchmark for a "good" microrobotic actuator: **$\geq 100 \; \text{W/cm}^3$**
- Bandwidth benchmark for micro-swimmers: **$\geq 10 \; \text{Hz}$**
- Viscosity of water at 25 °C: $\eta = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Actuator Choice is Hard at the Micro-Scale

The selection of a microrobotic actuator is governed by the **scaling of dominant forces** as the characteristic length $L$ of the robot shrinks. Let us break this into structured logic steps:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the scaling law of every relevant force:**
   - Electrostatic force $F_e \propto L^2$ (acts on surfaces/areas).
   - Magnetic force $F_m \propto L^3$ (acts on volumes).
   - Inertial (gravitational) force $F_g \propto L^3$ (volume mass).
   - Viscous drag force $F_v \propto L \cdot v$ (linear in size and velocity).

2. **Step 2 — Compare surface vs. volume forces:**
   - At the macro-scale, **volume forces dominate** (gravity, inertia).
   - At the micro-scale, **surface forces dominate** (electrostatic, van der Waals, surface tension).

3. **Step 3 — Match the actuator principle to the dominant force regime:**
   - If surface forces dominate → choose **electrostatic** or **surface-tension** actuators.
   - If remote force application is needed → choose **magnetic** actuation.
   - If precision displacement is needed → choose **piezoelectric** (sacrificing stroke for accuracy).

4. **Step 4 — Validate against the four canonical metrics:**
   - Stroke, force density, bandwidth, and efficiency must all be within tolerance.
   - Compromise is the rule, not the exception.

5. **Step 5 — Verify fabrication compatibility:**
   - The actuator must be compatible with CMOS/MEMS processes (photolithography, etching, deposition).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Reasoning Template:** Whenever an exam question asks "which actuator is best for X?", always answer in this 4-part structure — *dominant force regime → actuator physics → metric trade-off → fabrication fit*. Examiners reward this logical scaffolding.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you will need for this topic. The vertical-bar symbol is deliberately written as `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Typical Unit | Micro-Scale Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Surface-area to volume ratio | $S/V \propto 1/L$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Increases as size shrinks — favours surface-effect actuators. |
| 2 | Electrostatic force on a parallel-plate capacitor | $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0\,A\,(V/d)^2$ | N | Scales as $L^2$ — excellent at micro-scale. |
| 3 | Electrostatic pressure | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 E^2$ | Pa | Independent of gap size for fixed $E$. |
| 4 | Magnetic force on a magnetised volume | $F_m = V_m\,(M \cdot \nabla) B$ | N | Scales as $L^3$ — dominant only if volume is magnetised. |
| 5 | Magnetic pressure | $P_m = B^2 / (2\mu_0)$ | Pa | Independent of gap size for fixed $B$. |
| 6 | Power density | $\rho_p = P / V_{actuator}$ | $\text{W/cm}^3$ | Target $\geq 100 \; \text{W/cm}^3$ for mobile microrobots. |
| 7 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v L / \eta$ | dimensionless | $Re \ll 1$ at the micro-scale — flow is reversible/Stokesian. |
| 8 | Viscous drag (low-Re sphere) | $F_d = 6\pi \eta R v$ | N | Stokes' law governs micro-swimmer propulsion. |
| 9 | Resonance frequency | $f_r = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | As $L \to \mu\text{m}$, $f_r$ moves to kHz–MHz. |
| 10 | Energy stored in a capacitor | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Electrostatic actuators store energy in $C$. |
| 11 | Strain in piezoelectric material | $S = d_{33}\,E$ | dimensionless | $d_{33}$ is the relevant piezo-charge constant. |
| 12 | SMA phase-transition temperature | $T_t$ (depends on alloy, e.g. NiTi $\approx 70\,^\circ\text{C}$) | °C | Thermal actuator trigger temperature. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is used in:
- **Targeted drug delivery** — selecting a magnetic actuator to swim through blood.
- **Micro-assembly in watch/electronics industries** — picking electrostatic comb drives.
- **In-vivo minimally invasive surgery** — choosing piezo-ultrasonic actuators for precision.
- **Microfluidic lab-on-chip systems** — using thermal or piezoelectric pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why does this matter?"** Because at KTU valuation, an examiner gives full marks only when the student explains *why* an actuator is chosen for a *specific* micro-application, not merely names it. Always tie the actuator principle back to a physical scale argument.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Electrostatic Forces Dominate at the Micro-Scale

**Goal:** Prove mathematically that the ratio of electrostatic force to gravitational (inertial) force grows as $L$ shrinks.

**Step 1 — Write the two forces for a cube of side $L$:**

The electrostatic force on the surface $A = L^2$ at field $E$:

$$F_e = P_e \cdot A = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 E^2 \cdot L^2$$

The gravitational force on the volume $V = L^3$ with density $\rho$:

$$F_g = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot L^3 \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio:**

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 E^2 \cdot L^2}{\rho \cdot L^3 \cdot g}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the algebra explicitly:**

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{\epsilon_0 E^2}{2\,\rho\,g\,L}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the scaling:**

As the characteristic length $L \to 0$, the ratio $F_e/F_g \to \infty$. This proves that **electrostatic actuation becomes increasingly dominant as size shrinks**.

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check (KTU-style):**

Take $E = 10^7 \; \text{V/m}$ (typical breakdown limit), $\rho = 2330 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (silicon), $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, and $L = 10\;\mu\text{m} = 10^{-5}\; \text{m}$:

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})\,(10^7)^2}{2\,(2330)\,(9.81)\,(10^{-5})}$$

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{14}}{4.572 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{2}}{4.572 \times 10^{-1}} = 1.936 \times 10^{3}$$

Thus, **electrostatic force is roughly 2000× stronger than gravity at the 10-micron scale** — a quantitative proof of the qualitative claim.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power-Density Optimisation of a Micro-Actuator

**Goal:** Show how to maximise the volumetric power density of an electrostatic comb-drive.

**Step 1 — Define output mechanical power:**

For a comb drive moving at velocity $v$ against a load $F$:

$$P_{out} = F \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the electrostatic force expression:**

Using $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 A\,(V/d)^2$ and $v = \dot{x}$:

$$P_{out} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 A\,(V/d)^2 \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Normalise by actuator volume $V_{act} = A \cdot d$ (plate-separation factor):**

$$\rho_p = \frac{P_{out}}{V_{act}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 A\,(V/d)^2 \cdot v}{A \cdot d}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic simplification:**

$$\rho_p = \frac{\epsilon_0\,V^2\,v}{2\,d^3}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and optimisation:**

Power density grows as $1/d^3$ — so **shrinking the gap $d$** is the single most effective design lever. This is why MEMS fabrication aims for sub-micron gaps. It also grows quadratically with driving voltage $V$ — explaining why microrobotic actuators are often driven at the highest safe voltage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Limit from Viscous Drag (Stokes Regime)

**Goal:** Establish the upper bound on operating frequency of a micro-swimmer moving through water.

**Step 1 — Equate driving force to viscous drag:**

For a sphere of radius $R$ at velocity $v$ in a viscous fluid:

$$F_{drive} = 6\pi \eta R v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute sinusoidal motion $v = \omega x_0$:**

$$F_{drive} = 6\pi \eta R \omega x_0$$

**Step 3 — The inertial term $m\omega^2 x_0$ vanishes when $Re \ll 1$:**

At low Reynolds number, $F_{inertia} \approx 0$, so the only restoring impedance is viscous. The power delivered per cycle is:

$$P_{cycle} = \pi \eta R \omega^2 x_0^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum $\omega$ before actuator saturates:**

Setting $P_{cycle}$ equal to the actuator's maximum sustainable power $P_{max}$:

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{max}}{\pi \eta R x_0^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in realistic numbers:**

Take $\eta = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $R = 50\;\mu\text{m}$, $x_0 = 5\;\mu\text{m}$, and $P_{max} = 1\;\mu\text{W}$ (typical for a micro-swimmer):

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{\pi \cdot (8.9 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{3.494 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{2.862 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\omega_{max} \approx 5.35 \times 10^{5} \; \text{rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{max} \approx 85 \; \text{kHz}$$

**Interpretation:** Even a tiny 1-microwatt actuator can oscillate up to ~85 kHz at this scale — orders of magnitude faster than macro-scale muscle (~10 Hz). This is why **micro-swimmers can beat their flagella thousands of times per second**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing an Actuator for a Given Application

| Application | Dominant Force Regime | Recommended Actuator | Trade-off Accepted |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| In-vivo blood-vessel swimmer | Viscous, low Re | Magnetic (external Helmholtz coil) | Lower efficiency, but wireless |
| AFM nano-positioner | Inertial, surface | Piezoelectric stack | Tiny stroke, ultra-high resolution |
| On-chip micro-pump | Surface / capillary | Electrostatic or thermal | High voltage, low flow rate |
| Crawling micro-insect on water | Surface tension | SMA wire or electrostatic | Slow speed, high force density |
| Optical tweezer-driven micro-tool | Optical / surface | Optical (LASER) actuation | Very low force, no electrical contact |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How an Actuator Sits Inside a Microrobot

The diagram below maps every functional block of a microrobot, showing how the actuator interfaces with the rest of the system. Notice that **the actuator is the only block that physically bridges the on-board electronics and the external environment**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Energy Source] --> B[Power Conditioning Block]
    B --> C[Microrobot Onboard Power Bus]
    C --> D[Microcontroller / Control ASIC]
    D --> E[Actuator Driver Stage]
    E --> F[Microrobotic Actuator Core]
    F --> G[Mechanical Output Stage]
    G --> H[External Environment]
    H --> I[Sensor Feedback Loop]
    I --> D
    F --> J[Heat Dissipation Path]
    C --> J
    K[Fabrication Process Constraint] -.-> F
    K -.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = power / signal flow.
- Dashed arrows = design-time constraints (not real-time signals).
- The **closed loop** `D → E → F → G → H → I → D` is the canonical mechatronic control loop.
- `J` (heat dissipation) is a critical path often forgotten by students — at micro-scale, Joule heating cannot be ignored.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Microrobotic Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Microrobotic Actuators] --> F1[Field Driven]
    ROOT --> M1[Material Driven]
    ROOT --> O1[Other / Hybrid]
    F1 --> F1A[Electrostatic Comb Drive]
    F1 --> F1B[Magnetic Microactuator]
    F1 --> F1C[Electromagnetic Coil Microactuator]
    M1 --> M1A[Piezoelectric Cantilever]
    M1 --> M1B[Shape Memory Alloy Wire]
    M1 --> M1C[Conducting Polymer IPMC]
    O1 --> O1A[Thermal Bimorph]
    O1 --> O1B[Optical LASER Driven]
    O1 --> O1C[Chemical Fuel Driven]
    ROOT --> S1[Selection Criteria]
    S1 --> S1A[Stroke]
    S1 --> S1B[Force Density]
    S1 --> S1C[Bandwidth]
    S1 --> S1D[Power Efficiency]
    S1 --> S1E[Fabrication Compatibility]
```

## 4.3 Functional State Machine — Actuator Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: Energy Input On
    Charging --> Ready: Threshold Reached
    Ready --> Stroke: Trigger Command
    Stroke --> Hold: Lock Signal
    Stroke --> Recovery: Spring Back / Reset
    Hold --> Stroke: Re-trigger
    Recovery --> Ready: Reset Complete
    Ready --> Fault: Overcurrent Detected
    Fault --> Idle: Reset Pulse
    Hold --> Idle: Power Off
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state-machine is the **mental model examiners expect** when you describe an actuator's duty cycle in an exam answer. Linking it to the four canonical metrics (stroke, force, bandwidth, efficiency) is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a microrobotic actuator. List the four canonical performance metrics used to evaluate it. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A microrobotic actuator is a micro-scale transduction element that converts an input energy form (electrical, magnetic, thermal, chemical, or optical) into a controlled mechanical output (displacement, force, torque, or vibration) at the sub-millimetre scale, satisfying the dimensional, force-density, and power constraints of the microrobotic operating environment.

The four canonical performance metrics are:

1. **Stroke / Work Output** — the maximum displacement or mechanical work delivered.
2. **Force / Torque Density** — force per unit actuator volume, ideally $\geq 100 \; \text{W/cm}^3$.
3. **Bandwidth / Response Time** — the maximum operating frequency, often kHz–MHz at micro-scale.
4. **Power Efficiency** — the ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power.

### Q2. State why electrostatic actuators become more attractive as the characteristic length of a microrobot shrinks below 100 micrometres. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

At the micro-scale, the **surface-area-to-volume ratio** scales as $S/V \propto 1/L$, which means surface-based forces grow relative to volume-based forces. Electrostatic force scales as $L^2$ (surface phenomenon) while gravitational/inertial forces scale as $L^3$ (volume phenomenon). Therefore the ratio $F_e/F_g \propto 1/L \to \infty$ as $L \to 0$, making electrostatic actuation increasingly dominant. This is also why **comb drives**, **capacitive transducers**, and **electrostatic micro-motors** are the workhorses of MEMS microrobotics.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** Derive the scaling relation showing that the electrostatic-to-gravitational force ratio grows as the characteristic length $L$ decreases. Use a representative cube of side $L$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. The electrostatic force on a cube of side $L$ with surface area $A = L^2$ subjected to a field $E$ is given by:

$$F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 E^2 \cdot L^2$$

2. The gravitational force on the same cube with density $\rho$ is:

$$F_g = \rho \cdot L^3 \cdot g$$

3. Forming the ratio:

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{\epsilon_0 E^2 L^2}{2 \rho g L^3} = \frac{\epsilon_0 E^2}{2 \rho g L}$$

4. Therefore $F_e/F_g \propto 1/L$. As $L \to 0$, the ratio diverges, proving electrostatic dominance at small scales.

5. **Numerical substitution:** With $E = 10^7 \; \text{V/m}$, $\rho = 2330 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $L = 10\;\mu\text{m}$:

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{14})}{2 \times 2330 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.94 \times 10^{3}$$

Thus the electrostatic force is ~2000× the gravitational force at $L = 10\;\mu\text{m}$.

**(b)** Using the four canonical performance metrics, compare piezoelectric and electrostatic actuators for a microrobot with $L = 50\;\mu\text{m}$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

| Metric | Electrostatic Actuator | Piezoelectric Actuator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stroke | $1 - 10\;\mu\text{m}$ (limited by pull-in) | $0.01 - 1\;\mu\text{m}$ (smaller stroke) |
| Force Density | $0.1 - 10 \; \text{kN/m}^2$ | $10 - 100 \; \text{kN/m}^2$ |
| Bandwidth | kHz range | MHz range (very fast) |
| Power Efficiency | $10 - 40\%$ | $30 - 90\%$ (higher) |
| Drive Voltage | $50 - 200 \; \text{V}$ | $1 - 10 \; \text{V}$ (low) |
| Fabrication | CMOS-compatible | Requires PZT sputtering |

**Conclusion:** For a $50\;\mu\text{m}$ microrobot, electrostatic actuators are preferred when **stroke** is critical, while piezoelectric actuators are preferred when **force density and bandwidth** are critical. The choice is application-specific.

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a magnetic microrobotic actuator. Derive the expression for the magnetic force on a magnetised volume $V_m$ in a non-uniform field $B$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A magnetic micro-actuator uses an externally applied magnetic field gradient to exert a body force on a permanently or temporarily magnetised micro-component embedded in the microrobot. Because the field is generated externally (e.g., by a Helmholtz or Maxwell coil pair), the microrobot itself needs no on-board power for actuation — a major advantage for in-vivo and tethered-free applications.

**Derivation:**

The magnetic energy stored in the magnetised volume is:

$$U = -\int_{V_m} \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{B} \; dV$$

For a uniformly magnetised body in a slowly varying field:

$$U \approx -\mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{B} \cdot V_m$$

The force is the negative gradient of this energy:

$$\mathbf{F}_m = -\nabla U = V_m \, (\mathbf{M} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{B}$$

This proves that the magnetic force scales with the **volume** $V_m \propto L^3$ of the magnetised region. Hence magnetic actuation is strong only when sufficient volume is available.

**(b)** A spherical micro-swimmer of radius $R = 50\;\mu\text{m}$ oscillates with amplitude $x_0 = 5\;\mu\text{m}$ in water. If the actuator delivers a maximum power of $1\;\mu\text{W}$, compute the maximum oscillation frequency $f_{max}$. Use $\eta = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. Power dissipated per cycle in the Stokes regime:

$$P_{cycle} = \pi \eta R \omega^2 x_0^2$$

2. Equating to actuator maximum:

$$P_{max} = \pi \eta R \omega_{max}^2 x_0^2$$

3. Solve for $\omega_{max}$:

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{max}}{\pi \eta R x_0^2}}$$

4. Substitute numbers:

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{\pi \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{3.494 \times 10^{-18}}} \approx 5.35 \times 10^{5} \; \text{rad/s}$$

5. Convert to Hz:

$$f_{max} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{2\pi} \approx \frac{5.35 \times 10^{5}}{6.283} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{4} \; \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 85 \; \text{kHz}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1-microwatt actuator can drive the swimmer at up to ~85 kHz, demonstrating the **inherently high bandwidth** of micro-scale actuation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Marks are deducted every time a numerical answer is presented without SI units.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why" step.** Writing "electrostatic is better at micro-scale" without deriving $F_e/F_g \propto 1/L$ loses 2–3 marks. Always show the math.
> 3. **Confusing $\epsilon_0$ with $\epsilon_r \epsilon_0$.** In vacuum/air, only $\epsilon_0$ applies; in a dielectric, you must multiply.
> 4. **Treating Stokes and inertial regimes as interchangeable.** If $Re \ll 1$, you **must** use Stokes' drag, not Newton's drag. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Mixing up stroke vs. work output.** Stroke is displacement (m); work output is force × displacement (J). They are not the same metric.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A microrobotic actuator is a micro-scale energy transducer producing controlled mechanical output.
- **Four canonical metrics:** Stroke, Force Density, Bandwidth, Power Efficiency.
- **Scaling rule of thumb:** Surface forces scale as $L^2$, volume forces scale as $L^3$. Surface forces win at the micro-scale.
- **Electrostatic actuators:** Best when $L < 100\;\mu\text{m}$, stroke-critical, CMOS-compatible, but require high voltage.
- **Magnetic actuators:** Best for wireless/in-vivo applications; scale as $L^3$, so volume matters.
- **Piezoelectric actuators:** Best for ultra-precise, high-bandwidth, high-force applications; stroke is the limitation.
- **SMA actuators:** Best for large stroke, slow cycling; thermally triggered, e.g., NiTi at ~70 °C.
- **Conducting polymer (IPMC) actuators:** Best for low-voltage, wet-environment (bio-medical) operation.
- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  - $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 A E^2$
  - $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\epsilon_0 E^2$
  - $F_m = V_m (\mathbf{M} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{B}$
  - $P_m = B^2 / (2\mu_0)$
  - $F_d = 6\pi \eta R v$
  - $Re = \rho v L / \eta \ll 1$ at micro-scale
- **Fabrication compatibility** is a silent but mandatory criterion — CMOS/MEMS compatibility often decides the actuator choice.
- **Always close the loop:** Sensor feedback → Controller → Driver → Actuator → Environment → Sensor.
- **State machine of an actuator:** Idle → Charging → Ready → Stroke → Recovery → Ready. Know this cycle.
- **In any KTU exam answer, always end with a numerical sanity-check** — examiners reward it with 1–2 grace marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles of Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Principles of Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Design Principles of Actuators

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Microrobotics**, an **actuator** is a transduction element that converts a non-mechanical input energy form — typically electrical, magnetic, thermal, chemical, or optical — into a precisely controlled mechanical output (force, displacement, strain, or vibration) at the micro- or nano-scale. The **Design Principles of Actuators** constitute the set of physical, geometric, and material constraints that govern the selection, sizing, and integration of such transducers to deliver a target mechanical work output under the unique scaling laws of the micro-domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** The examiner expects you to explicitly link the choice of actuation physics to the **dominant scaling law** that affects it most. The two cardinal relationships are:
> - Electrostatic force scales as $\propto L^2$ (favourable at small scale)
> - Magnetic force scales as $\propto L^2$ (with field), but mass $\propto L^3$ (unfavourable)
> - Electrostatic pressure is **independent** of scale (a key KTU "favourite" fact).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle vs. Engine" Analogy

Think of a **macroscale robot arm** as a large diesel engine. It is powerful, heavy, and inefficient at low speeds. Now imagine shrinking it to the size of an ant. The engine's piston weighs almost nothing, but the **friction from the air**, the **stiffness of the materials**, and the **surface forces** become dominant. In microrobotics, the design engineer is no longer asking *"How strong is my motor?"* but rather *"Which physical law is most forgiving at this scale?"*. The **Design Principles** are the engineer's rules-of-thumb for picking the right physical law (electrostatic, piezoelectric, magnetic, thermal) so the ant-sized robot can actually move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Insight:** As the characteristic length $L$ shrinks, **surface forces (electrostatic, surface tension, viscous drag) scale as $L^2$ while volume forces (inertia, weight) scale as $L^3$**. Therefore, the "strongest" actuator at the microscale is **NOT** the one that is strongest at the macroscale — it is the one whose force-generation mechanism scales most gently with shrinking dimensions.

### 1.3 The Three Engineering Metrics of a Microactuator (Mandatory Vocabulary)

Every actuator in a KTU microrobotics question is judged by exactly three numbers. Memorise these:

| Metric | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Force / Torque** | $F$ or $\tau$ | Output mechanical load capacity | $\mu$N or nN·m |
| **Stroke (Displacement)** | $\delta$ | Maximum travel range | $\mu$m or nm |
| **Bandwidth** | $f_{-3dB}$ | Maximum operating frequency | kHz or MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Density = $F \cdot \delta$ per unit volume.** This is the single most important figure-of-merit when comparing two microactuators. The KTU examiner will frequently test this.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Scaling Laws of Microactuation

The micro-domain is governed by dimensionless numbers. A KTU 2024 answer that does **not** reference these numbers is considered incomplete. The four cardinal numbers are:

### 2.1.1 The Reynolds Number (Inertia vs. Viscosity)

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, L}{\mu}$$

where $\rho$ is fluid density, $v$ is characteristic velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, and $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity. At the microscale, $L \to 10^{-6}$ m, so **$Re \ll 1$** and viscous forces dominate. There is **no turbulence** in microrobotic flows.

### 2.1.2 The Capillary Number (Viscous vs. Surface Tension)

$$Ca = \frac{\mu \, v}{\gamma}$$

where $\gamma$ is surface tension. Surface tension ($\sim$ 0.072 N/m for water) is a "volume-independent" force that becomes enormous relative to weight at small $L$.

### 2.1.3 The Knudsen Number (Continuum Validity)

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$

where $\lambda$ is the mean free path of air molecules ($\sim 65$ nm at STP). For $L < 10\,\mu$m, $Kn > 0.1$ and the **continuum assumption breaks down** — a key KTU pitfall when students blindly apply Navier-Stokes equations at the micro-scale.

### 2.1.4 The Peclet Number (Thermal vs. Convective Mass Transport)

$$Pe = \frac{v \, L}{D}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient. Used in micro-fluidic actuator analysis.

## 2.2 Energy & Force Density of Major Actuation Principles

The KTU board requires you to compare **at least three** physical principles in any 14-mark design question. Below is the **Core Analytical Matrix** that should be reproduced from memory.

### 2.2.1 Electrostatic Actuation (Comb Drives, Parallel Plate)

A parallel-plate capacitor with area $A$, gap $g$, and voltage $V$ has an attractive normal force given by:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \, \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{g^2} \, V^2$$

The energy stored is $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$, so the **pressure** (force per area) is:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, V^2}{2 g^2}$$

**Crucial KTU Insight:** Notice that the **pressure is independent of the linear scale $L$** — it depends only on $V$ and $g$. This is why electrostatic actuators are the **workhorse of MEMS** (e.g., the ADI accelerometers).

### 2.2.2 Piezoelectric Actuation

For a piezoelectric stack of length $L_p$, thickness $t$, and applied field $E$:

$$\delta = d_{33} \, V = d_{33} \, E \, t$$

The blocked force is:

$$F_b = \frac{A \, d_{33} \, E \, Y}{1} = A \, Y \, S_{max}$$

where $Y$ is Young's modulus and $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge constant. Typical $d_{33}$ for PZT: **$300 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V**. The energy density is:

$$w_{piezo} = \frac{1}{2} Y \, S^2 \quad \text{with } S \leq 0.001 \text{ (strain limit)}$$

### 2.2.3 Magnetic Actuation (Lorentz-Force Coils)

For a current-carrying conductor of length $\ell$ in a flux density $B$:

$$F = B \, I \, \ell$$

The magnetic pressure scales as:

$$P_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

For $B = 1$ T, $P_{mag} \approx 4 \times 10^5$ Pa — comparable to the electrostatic pressure only at **kV-level voltages**.

### 2.2.4 Thermal Actuation (Bimorph / Shape-Memory)

Bimorph deflection under differential thermal expansion:

$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t (4 + 6 \frac{t_1}{t_2} + 4 (\frac{t_1}{t_2})^2 + \frac{E_1}{E_2} (\frac{t_1}{t_2})^3 + \frac{E_2}{E_1} (\frac{t_2}{t_1})^3)}$$

The power consumption for heating is $P = k \Delta T$, where $k$ is the thermal conductance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of equations you are expected to know for the ESE. No vertical bars `|` are used inside cells; the `\vert` and `\mid` notation is used to prevent markdown-table breakage.**

| S.No | Actuation Principle | Output Force | Output Stroke | Energy Density | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrostatic Comb Drive | $F = \frac{n \, \varepsilon_0 \, t \, V^2}{g}$ | $\delta = 0 \text{ to } \tfrac{2g}{3}$ (pull-in limit) | $\tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 \approx 4.4 \,\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ (air, 3 kV/$\mu$m) | Pull-in instability at $\delta = g/3$ |
| 2 | Parallel Plate Capacitor | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g^2} V^2$ | Limited by pull-in at $g/3$ | Same as above | Pull-in instability |
| 3 | Piezoelectric Stack | $F_b = A \, Y \, d_{33} \, E$ | $\delta = d_{33} \, V$ | $\sim 10^5 \,\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ | High voltage, small strain $\sim 0.1\%$ |
| 4 | Lorentz-Force Coil | $F = B I \ell$ | Limited by flexure design | $\tfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ | Requires $B > 0.1$ T |
| 5 | Thermal Bimorph | $F \propto E t^2 \Delta T$ | $\delta \propto L^2 \Delta T$ | High ($\sim 10^6 \,\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ ideal) | Slow bandwidth ($\sim 100$ Hz) |
| 6 | Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) | $F \propto A \sigma_{yield}$ | $\delta \leq 8\%$ strain | Moderate | Cyclic fatigue, hysteresis |
| 7 | Dielectric Elastomer (DEA) | $F \propto \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E^2 A$ | $\delta \leq 100\%$ strain | $\sim 10^6 \,\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ | Requires pre-stretch, high voltage |

## 2.4 Design Selection Flowchart (Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> The selection is governed by the **target application**: Is the microrobot operating in air or fluid? What is the required force? What is the bandwidth?

- **High force, small stroke, in-vivo fluid environment** → **Piezoelectric (PZT)** or **Magnetic**.
- **High stroke, low force, on-chip integration** → **Electrostatic comb drive**.
- **Large deflection, low bandwidth, dry environment** → **Thermal bimorph / SMA**.
- **Soft, bio-compatible, large strain** → **Dielectric elastomer / IPMC (ionic polymer-metal composite)**.

The **real-world engineering utility** of these principles spans atomic-force-microscope (AFM) nanopositioners (piezo), flying insect-sized aerial microrobots (electrostatic/Flapping-wing resonance), and capsule endoscopes (magnetic actuation from external Helmholtz coils).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pull-in Voltage of a Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Actuator

This is the **single most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU PERAT746. Every step is reproduced below.

**Statement:** A movable plate of a parallel-plate capacitor is suspended by a linear spring of stiffness $k$. Derive the pull-in voltage $V_{PI}$ and the corresponding stable-stroke limit.

**Step 1 — Energy formulation.** The total potential energy of the system is the sum of the electrical energy stored in the capacitor and the mechanical spring energy:

$$U_{total}(g) = \frac{1}{2} C(g) V^2 + \frac{1}{2} k (g_0 - g)^2$$

For a parallel plate, $C(g) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}$, so substituting:

$$U_{total}(g) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g} + \frac{1}{2} k (g_0 - g)^2$$

**Step 2 — Force balance.** The net force on the movable plate (positive direction = decreasing $g$) is obtained by differentiating with respect to $g$:

$$F_{elec} = -\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2} - k(g_0 - g)$$

Setting this to zero gives the **equilibrium condition**:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2} = k(g_0 - g)$$

**Step 3 — Stability condition.** For a stable equilibrium, the small-signal stiffness must be positive. Differentiating the net force again with respect to $g$:

$$\frac{\partial F_{elec}}{\partial g} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^3} + k$$

**Instability (pull-in)** occurs when this derivative first becomes zero, i.e.:

$$k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^2}{g_{PI}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Co-solve with equilibrium.** Substituting the stability condition back into the equilibrium equation and eliminating $V_{PI}$:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^2}{2 g_{PI}^2} = k(g_0 - g_{PI})$$

Dividing this by the stability condition:

$$\frac{g_{PI}}{2} = (g_0 - g_{PI}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad g_{PI} = \frac{2 g_0}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Pull-in voltage.** Substituting $g_{PI} = \tfrac{2 g_0}{3}$ back into the stability condition:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Result:** The maximum stable stroke of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator is **one-third of the initial gap**. This is the "famous pull-in rule" and is examinable as a direct numerical-substitution question (3 marks) or as part of a 14-mark design problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Scaling of Electrostatic vs. Magnetic Force

**Step 1 — Define the length scale.** Let $L$ be the characteristic linear dimension. Then area $A \propto L^2$ and gap $g \propto L$.

**Step 2 — Electrostatic force scaling.** From $F_e = \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2}$, with $V$ held constant:

$$F_e \propto \frac{L^2}{L^2} = L^0 \quad \text{(for fixed field strength)}$$

With $V$ scaled as $V \propto L$ (fixed field): $F_e \propto L^2$. With $V$ held constant, the **electrostatic force per unit area is independent of $L$** — this is the reason MEMS works.

**Step 3 — Magnetic force scaling.** From $F_m = B I \ell$, the current density $J$ is bounded by the resistivity heating: $J \propto 1/L$ for adiabatic scaling (since the cooling surface $\propto L^2$ and volume heating $\propto L^3$). So $I = J A \propto (1/L) \cdot L^2 = L$, hence:

$$F_m \propto L \cdot L = L^2$$

**Step 4 — Compare to weight.** The weight scales as $W = \rho g L^3$. The force-to-weight ratio:

$$\frac{F_e}{W} \propto \frac{1}{L^3} \quad \text{(improves at small scale!)}$$

$$\frac{F_m}{W} \propto \frac{1}{L} \quad \text{(improves at small scale, but less aggressively)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is **why electrostatic actuation dominates at the micro-scale**, and why building a magnetic motor smaller than $\sim 1$ mm is extremely inefficient. This single derivation is worth 14 marks if set as a Part-B question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Actuator for a Given Microrobotic Application

The following matrix is a high-yield exam tool. It maps **application constraints** to the **correct physical principle**.

| Application | Required Force | Required Stroke | Bandwidth | Environment | Recommended Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AFM tip scanner | $\sim 1\,\mu$N | $\sim 10\,\mu$m | $> 10$ kHz | Air/Vacuum | **Piezoelectric stack** | High stiffness, fast, vacuum compatible |
| Flying microrobot (Harvard RoboBee) | $\sim 100\,\mu$N per wing | $\sim 90^\circ$ rotation | $> 100$ Hz | Air | **Piezoelectric bimorph** | High bandwidth, low power |
| Capsule endoscope | $\sim 1$ mN | $>> 1$ cm | $< 1$ Hz | In-vivo fluid | **External magnetic field** | Wireless, biocompatible |
| Optical MEMS mirror (DLP) | $\sim 10\,\mu$N | $\sim 10^\circ$ | kHz | Air | **Electrostatic comb** | CMOS-compatible, fast |
| Soft crawling microrobot | $\sim 100\,\mu$N | $\sim 1$ mm | $< 10$ Hz | Wet | **Dielectric elastomer / IPMC** | Large strain, soft body |
| AFM tip in SEM | Variable | nm | DC-1 kHz | Vacuum | **Piezo tube scanner** | UHV compatibility |
| Lab-on-chip valve | $\sim 1$ mN | $\sim 50\,\mu$m | $< 100$ Hz | Fluid | **Thermal bimorph or SMA** | High force, large stroke |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design-Flow of a Microactuator

The following diagram maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows when designing an actuator for a microrobotic platform. This is the substitute for a physical schematic (microrobotic actuators are too small to draw at board-level) and is fully compliant with the Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B[Operating Environment?]
    B --> C1[In-Vivo Fluid]
    B --> C2[In-Air / On-Chip]
    B --> C3[Vacuum / Cleanroom]

    C1 --> D1[Need Wireless Control?]
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Magnetic Actuation]
    D1 -- No --> E2[Pneumatic or IPMC]

    C2 --> F1[Required Stroke]
    F1 -- Small under 10 um --> G1[Electrostatic Comb Drive]
    F1 -- Large above 10 um --> G2[Piezoelectric Stack or Bimorph]

    C3 --> H1[UHV Compatible]
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Piezoelectric Tube Scanner]
    H1 -- No --> I2[Thermal Bimorph with Shielding]

    E1 --> J1[Apply Lorentz Force F = B I L]
    E2 --> J2[Apply Hydration / Voltage Hydration Coupling]
    G1 --> J3[Apply F = n eps0 t V2 over g]
    G2 --> J4[Apply delta = d33 V]
    I1 --> J5[Apply 3 axis Tube Scanner Geometry]
    I2 --> J6[Apply delta proportional to L squared Delta T]

    J1 --> K1[Validate: F over W Ratio]
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    J4 --> K1
    J5 --> K1
    J6 --> K1

    K1 --> L1{Force sufficient?}
    L1 -- No --> M1[Re-scale: Increase L or Use Lever Amplification]
    L1 -- Yes --> N1[Validate Bandwidth and Power]

    N1 --> O1[Final Selection and Fabrication]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Closed-Loop Microactuator

This block diagram shows how a microactuator is integrated into a feedback loop. Even "open-loop" microrobotic actuators are typically operated inside such a control loop to compensate for hysteresis (especially in piezo and SMA).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Reference Command] --> SUM[Junction Summation Node]
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller: PID or H infinity]
    CTRL --> AMP[High Voltage Amplifier]
    AMP --> ACT[MICROACTUATOR: Electrostatic or Piezo or Magnetic]
    ACT --> OUT[Mechanical Output: Force or Stroke]
    OUT --> LOAD[Microrobot Linkage or End Effector]
    LOAD --> SEN[Displacement or Force Sensor]
    SEN --> FBK[Feedback Signal]
    FBK --> SUM
    ACT -. Pull-in or Saturation Limit .-> LIM[Nonlinearity Block]
    LIM -. Hysteresis Compensation .-> CTRL
```

## 4.3 Force vs. Stroke Performance Envelope (Block Visualization)

The following diagram conveys the **qualitative trade-off** that every KTU 14-mark answer must discuss. The reader should mentally populate this with the numerical values from the formula sheet in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "High Force, Low Stroke"
        A1[Piezoelectric Stack] 
        A2[Magnetic Voice Coil]
    end
    subgraph "High Stroke, Low Force"
        B1[Electrostatic Comb Drive]
        B2[Piezoelectric Bimorph]
    end
    subgraph "High Force AND High Stroke, Low Bandwidth"
        C1[Thermal Bimorph]
        C2[Shape Memory Alloy]
    end
    subgraph "High Stroke, Moderate Force, Soft Body"
        D1[Dielectric Elastomer]
        D2[IPMC]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "diagonal" nature of this trade-off is the **central design conflict** of all actuation. There is **no single actuator** that simultaneously maximises force, stroke, bandwidth, and efficiency. KTU questions on "compare and contrast" are testing whether the student understands this fundamental compromise.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style**

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Explain why electrostatic actuation becomes increasingly attractive as the characteristic length of a microactuator is reduced from the millimeter to the micrometer scale. State the relevant scaling law.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The electrostatic force on a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$ and gap $g$ at voltage $V$ is $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g^2} V^2$. Since the electrostatic force scales as $L^2$ while the inertial weight scales as $L^3$ (with characteristic length $L$), the **force-to-weight ratio scales as $1/L$**, meaning that **electrostatic actuation becomes more efficient as the device shrinks**. Additionally, the breakdown field of air ($3 \times 10^6$ V/m) is independent of scale, so the achievable pressure ($\sim 40$ kPa) is preserved. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the figure of merit "energy density" for a microactuator and give the typical value for a piezoelectric material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Energy density is the mechanical work output per unit volume of the actuator, defined as $w = \tfrac{F \cdot \delta}{V_{actuator}}$, with units of J/m³. For piezoelectric PZT (lead zirconate titanate), the typical energy density is of the order of $10^5 \,\tfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$, which is roughly two to three orders of magnitude higher than that of an electrostatic actuator ($\sim 4.4 \,\tfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ in air at 3 V/nm). **(3 marks)**

---
