## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 3 Choice Pattern:** *Answer ANY ONE full question. Both questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Model Question]**

**(a)** *Derive the pull-in voltage $V_{PI}$ and the maximum stable stroke of a one-dimensional parallel-plate electrostatic actuator suspended by a linear spring of stiffness $k$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A MEMS accelerometer uses a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A = 500 \times 500 \,\mu\text{m}^2$ and an initial gap $g_0 = 2 \,\mu\text{m}$. The proof mass is suspended by four folded-flexure springs, each of stiffness $k_f = 0.5$ N/m. The dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m). Compute (i) the pull-in voltage, and (ii) the stable operational stroke range.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key results are:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = g_0 - g_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3}$$

The derivation proceeds by **(i)** writing the total potential energy $U = \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2g} + \tfrac{1}{2} k (g_0 - g)^2$, **(ii)** differentiating to find the equilibrium force, **(iii)** differentiating again to find the stability boundary (set second derivative to zero), and **(iv)** co-solving the two equations to get $g_{PI} = \tfrac{2 g_0}{3}$ and the corresponding $V_{PI}$ expression. **(7 marks — full algebraic steps must be shown on the answer script)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given data:**
- $A = 500 \times 500 \,\mu\text{m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²
- $g_0 = 2 \,\mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Total spring stiffness $k = 4 k_f = 4 \times 0.5 = 2$ N/m (four springs in parallel)
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

**Step 1 — Calculate the pull-in voltage.**

Substituting into the formula:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 2 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$8 \times 2 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 128 \times 10^{-18} = 1.28 \times 10^{-16}$$

**Denominator:**

$$27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 27 \times 22.135 \times 10^{-19} = 5.976 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{1.28 \times 10^{-16}}{5.976 \times 10^{-17}} \approx 2.142$$

**Result:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{2.142} \approx 1.46 \,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the stable operational stroke range.**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{g_0}{3} = \frac{2 \,\mu\text{m}}{3} \approx 0.667 \,\mu\text{m}$$

The operational range is therefore from $0$ to $0.667 \,\mu\text{m}$, and the corresponding final gap at pull-in is $g_{PI} = 1.333 \,\mu\text{m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The very low pull-in voltage (1.46 V) is **practically impossible** because real sensors must tolerate process variation; in industry the design voltage is kept at $\sim 0.3 V_{PI}$ for a safety margin, i.e. $\sim 0.44$ V in this example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Model Question]**

**(a)** *With the help of force-scaling arguments, justify why electrostatic actuators are preferred over magnetic actuators for MEMS applications below 1 mm characteristic length.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the energy densities and typical bandwidths of (i) electrostatic, (ii) piezoelectric, and (iii) thermal (bimorph) microactuators. State one application of each.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Magnetic force scaling.** The magnetic force on a current-carrying wire is $F_m = B I \ell$. The current $I$ is limited by Joule heating: $I^2 R \leq P_{max}$, where $R = \rho \ell / A_{wire}$ and $A_{wire} \propto L^2$. Cooling is via the surface ($\propto L^2$), so the maximum $P$ scales as $L^2$. This implies $I \propto L$ and the force $F_m \propto L^2$. The mass of the actuator scales as $L^3$, so the force-to-mass ratio of a magnetic actuator **deteriorates as $1/L$** as we shrink.

**Step 2 — Electrostatic force scaling.** The electrostatic force is $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2} \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2}$. With $A \propto L^2$ and $g \propto L$, the force $F_e \propto L^2$ as well, but importantly the **force-per-unit-area (pressure) is independent of $L$** because the breakdown field of the dielectric is scale-independent. Hence the force-to-mass ratio of an electrostatic actuator **stays constant or improves as $1/L$** depending on the voltage-scaling assumption.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** Because both scale as $L^2$ in force but magnetic actuators have the additional burden of requiring a permanent magnet or high current density (which is limited by heat removal, a surface effect), electrostatic actuators become the **technological winner below $\sim 1$ mm**. The canonical example is the **comb drive**, used in virtually every commercial MEMS accelerometer and gyroscope. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Actuator Type | Energy Density | Bandwidth | Representative Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | $\sim 4.4 \,\tfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ (air, 3 V/$\mu$m) | High ($> 100$ kHz) | MEMS accelerometer (Analog Devices ADXL series) |
| **Piezoelectric** | $\sim 10^5 \,\tfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ | Very High ($> 1$ MHz) | AFM scanner, ultrasound transducer |
| **Thermal Bimorph** | $\sim 10^6 \,\tfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}$ (ideal) | Low ($< 1$ kHz) | Micro-fluidic valve, optical switch |

**Engineering Interpretation:**

- The **electrostatic** actuator has the **lowest energy density** but the **highest bandwidth** and is **CMOS-compatible**, making it ideal for batch-fabricated sensors.
- The **piezoelectric** actuator has a **moderate energy density**, very **high force density**, and **excellent bandwidth**; it is preferred for nanopositioning and flying microrobots.
- The **thermal** actuator has the **highest ideal energy density** but **very low bandwidth** (because heating and cooling are inherently slow); it is used where large force and stroke are required at low frequency, e.g. lab-on-chip valves.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake students make in this 14-mark question is to **confuse "energy density" with "force density"**. Energy density has units of J/m³, force density (pressure) has units of N/m² = Pa. They differ by a factor of **stroke (m)**. Always write the units explicitly on the answer script to avoid losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Points for Last-Minute Revision Before ESE Module 3:**

- The **three governing metrics** of any microactuator are **Force, Stroke, and Bandwidth** — judge every actuator by these three.
- The **square-cube law** ($\text{force} \propto L^2$, $\text{mass} \propto L^3$) is the **single most-tested concept** in this module.
- **Pull-in voltage** of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\tfrac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$. The **stable stroke** is limited to $g_0 / 3$ — this is non-negotiable.
- **Electrostatic pressure** $P = \tfrac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 g^2}$ is **independent of scale** — this is *the* reason MEMS uses electrostatics.
- **Magnetic pressure** $P = \tfrac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$ requires $B > 0.1$ T to be competitive with electrostatics — small magnetic coils cannot deliver this.
- **Piezoelectric** materials (PZT) have a strain limit of $\sim 0.1\%$, so they have **small stroke** but **very high force and bandwidth** — used in AFMs and flying microrobots.
- **Thermal bimorph** actuators have **large stroke and force** but are **slow** because of the thermal time constant ($\tau = RC_{th}$).
- **Dielectric elastomers (DEA)** and **IPMCs** are the **soft-body** actuator class — they have the **largest stroke** ($\sim 100\%$ strain) and are bio-compatible.
- The **Knudsen number** $Kn$ tells you whether the continuum assumption is valid: for $L < 10\,\mu$m, $Kn > 0.1$, so do not use standard Navier-Stokes blindly.
- The **Reynolds number** at the microscale is $\ll 1$, so flows are **laminar** and dominated by **viscous** forces — there is **no inertia** to speak of.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** *High force + small stroke + in-vivo* $\to$ **piezo/magnetic**; *high stroke + on-chip* $\to$ **electrostatic**; *large force + slow + dry* $\to$ **thermal**; *soft-body + bio-compatible* $\to$ **DEA/IPMC**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Design Principles of Actuators (PERAT746, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplification of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amplification of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amplification of Motion (Displacement Amplification)** is a class of compliant / rigid-body mechanical architectures employed in microrobotic systems to convert a *small input displacement* generated by an actuator (typically a **piezoelectric stack**, electrostatic, thermal, or shape-memory actuator) into a *larger output displacement* at the end-effector, while trading off proportionally with the output force capacity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature for **PERAT746 — Microrobotics**, motion amplification is classified under *Module 3: Actuation and Transmission Architectures for Micro-Scale Systems*. The amplification ratio **A** is the canonical figure of merit:

$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}}
$$

where $\delta_{in}$ is the input stroke from the actuator and $\delta_{out}$ is the amplified stroke delivered to the payload.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Fishing Rod and the Door Handle**
> Think of a piezoelectric actuator as a person who can push *very hard* but only over a *tiny distance* (a few micrometres), like a person leaning against a heavy door. A motion amplifier is like inserting a long lever between that person and the door — the person still pushes hard, but now the door swings open by a *much larger distance*. The price? The person must push even harder, because the lever that magnifies distance also reduces force. This trade-off is the **Conservation of Energy Principle** at the heart of all motion amplifiers.

## 1.3 Why Amplification is Essential in Microrobotics

Modern microscale actuators (especially **piezoelectric stacks** such as PZT-5H stacks) offer exceptional characteristics:

- **High blocking force:** up to **~1000 N** for a 5×5×10 mm stack
- **High bandwidth:** response in **microseconds**
- **High resolution:** sub-nanometre positioning

…but their *stroke* is intrinsically limited to roughly **0.1 % – 0.2 % of their length**, i.e., typically **5 µm to 50 µm**. This is grossly insufficient for many microrobotic end-effectors (grippers needing 100–500 µm jaw travel, cell-manipulation probes, optical alignment stages). Hence the **amplification stage** is a non-negotiable kinematic building block.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must-Memorise)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Microrobotic Domain) |
|---|---|---|
| Amplification Ratio | $A$ | **2× – 50×** |
| Input Stroke | $\delta_{in}$ | **5 µm – 50 µm** |
| Output Stroke | $\delta_{out}$ | **50 µm – 2 mm** |
| Hysteresis | $H$ | **< 5 %** (for flexure design) |
| Resonance Frequency | $f_n$ | **> 500 Hz** |
| Force Reduction Factor | $1/A$ | **50 % – 2 %** of input force |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Motion Amplifiers (Syllabus-Aligned)

Motion amplifiers for microrobotics are organised into the following hierarchy:

1. **Class A — Rigid-Body Linkage Amplifiers**
   - Simple lever
   - Compound lever (multi-stage)
   - Bridge (rhombus / diamond) amplifier
   - Scott–Russell straight-line mechanism
   - Toggle / knee-joint amplifier
   - Pantograph

2. **Class B — Compliant (Flexure-Based) Amplifiers**
   - Notch / leaf flexure hinges
   - Distributed-compliance bridges
   - Z-shaped and S-shaped amplifiers
   - Cymbal / cymbal-type flextensional

3. **Class C — Hydraulic / Pneumatic Amplifiers** (less common in pure microrobotics but used in soft robotics interfaces)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Module-3 syllabus explicitly emphasises **Class A (rigid-body)** and **Class B (compliant)** types, since these are the only architectures that can be monolithically microfabricated in silicon, SU-8, or metal via **EDM / wire-EDM / DRIE**.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Underlying Physics

Every displacement amplifier obeys a **virtual-work / energy balance** equation:

$$
F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out} + E_{stored} + E_{dissipated}
$$

For an *ideal, lossless* amplifier, the equality is strict:

$$
F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}
$$

Substituting the amplification definition $A = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$ gives the **force-degradation law**:

$$
F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This single equation explains why a 10× amplifier converts a 1000 N piezoelectric push into a 100 N output — the *force* is divided by 10, the *displacement* is multiplied by 10, and the *work* (energy) is conserved.

## 2.3 Kinematic Derivation Framework (Universal)

All rigid-body amplifiers can be analysed using the **virtual displacement method**:

1. Define a scalar input coordinate $x$ and a scalar output coordinate $y$.
2. Express the geometric constraint between them as $y = f(x)$.
3. Compute the **Jacobian**:

$$
J = \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{df(x)}{dx}
$$

4. The **geometric amplification ratio** at the operating point is $A = J$.
5. For small-stroke linearisation: $A \approx J$ evaluated at the central position.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every amplification formula examinable in KTU PERAT746. **Memorise the geometry, the formula, and the units of $A$ (dimensionless).**

| # | Mechanism | Geometric Parameters | Amplification Ratio $A$ | Force at Output $F_{out}$ | Typical Max $A$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple lever | Arm lengths $L_1$, $L_2$ | $A = L_2 / L_1$ | $F_{in}/A$ | **5×** |
| 2 | Compound lever (n stages) | Ratios $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ | $A = \prod_i A_i$ | $F_{in}/A$ | **125×** (3 stages of 5×) |
| 3 | Bridge (rhombus) amplifier — symmetric | Half-diagonals $a$, $b$ | $A = (a+b)/a = 1 + b/a$ | $F_{in}/A$ | **10×** |
| 4 | Bridge (asymmetric / scissor) | Hinge separations $a$, $b$ | $A = b/a$ (long arm / short arm) | $F_{in}/A$ | **20×** |
| 5 | Scott–Russell (straight-line) | Inclination angle $\alpha$ | $A = 1/\tan(\alpha)$ | $F_{in} \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | **20×** |
| 6 | Toggle / knee-joint (initial angle $\alpha_0$) | Toggle angle $\alpha$ | $A = \sec^2(\alpha)$ at angle $\alpha$ | $F_{in} \cdot \cos^2(\alpha)$ | **50×+** (near lock) |
| 7 | Pantograph (two bar lengths $L_1$, $L_2$) | Bar length ratio $k = L_2/L_1$ | $A = k$ | $F_{in}/k$ | **10×** |
| 8 | Flexure-based Z-amplifier | Link lengths $L_1$, $L_2$ | $A = L_2/L_1$ | $F_{in}/A$ | **8×** |
| 9 | Cymbal / flextensional | Cap diameter $D$, cavity depth $h$ | Empirical: $A \approx 1 + 0.5(D/h)$ | $F_{in}/A$ | **3× – 5×** |
| 10 | Hydraulic (Pascal) | Piston areas $A_1$, $A_2$ | $A = A_2/A_1$ | $F_{in} \cdot (A_1/A_2)$ | **100×+** |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the formula sheet, the notation $\vert$ is replaced by the word "or" because the vertical pipe character **breaks** the markdown table syntax. Exam answers must use $\vert$ or "such that" when writing absolute-value expressions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Where Amplifiers Are Used |
|---|---|
| **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** | Piezo scanners with bridge amplifiers to reach 100 µm scan ranges |
| **Cell micromanipulation** | Grippers with flexure amplifiers to multiply piezo stroke |
| **Optical / Photonic alignment** | Nanopositioning stages using Scott–Russell mechanisms |
| **Surgical robotics (MIS)** | Toggle amplifiers for force magnification in endoscopic tools |
| **Hard-disk-drive (HDD) manufacturing** | Flexure-amplified nanopositioners for head testing |
| **Micro-assembly** | Bridge amplifiers to give 50–500 µm workspace to 5 µm piezo |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are exhaustive. Each algebraic transition is shown in full. A single blank line precedes and follows every display equation, as required by the KTU 2024 LaTeX-rendering protocol.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Lever Amplifier

### Setup
A rigid bar pivots about a fulcrum. The actuator applies an input force $F_{in}$ at a distance $L_1$ from the pivot, producing a small displacement $\delta_{in}$. The output end is at distance $L_2$ and moves by $\delta_{out}$.

### Step 1 — Express rotation angle
The bar rotates by a small angle $\theta$. At the input end:

$$
\delta_{in} = L_1 \cdot \theta
$$

### Step 2 — Express output displacement
At the output end, using the same angle $\theta$:

$$
\delta_{out} = L_2 \cdot \theta
$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio
Divide the two equations to eliminate $\theta$:

$$
\frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_2 \cdot \theta}{L_1 \cdot \theta} = \frac{L_2}{L_1}
$$

### Step 4 — Final result
$$
\boxed{\,A = \frac{L_2}{L_1}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Force balance (virtual work)
By virtual work, $F_{in}\cdot\delta_{in} = F_{out}\cdot\delta_{out}$, so:

$$
F_{out} = F_{in}\cdot\frac{\delta_{in}}{\delta_{out}} = \frac{F_{in}}{A} = \frac{F_{in}\cdot L_1}{L_2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bridge (Symmetric Rhombus) Amplifier

### Setup (Geometry)
A rhombus-shaped four-bar linkage has horizontal half-diagonal $a$ (input side) and vertical half-diagonal $b$ (output side). The input displacement $\delta_{in}$ is applied along the horizontal diagonal; the output displacement $\delta_{out}$ is taken along the vertical diagonal.

### Step 1 — Define initial coordinates of the joints
Place the centre of the rhombus at the origin. The four joints are:

- Input hinge (left): $(-a, 0)$
- Input hinge (right): $(+a, 0)$
- Output hinge (top): $(0, +b)$
- Output hinge (bottom): $(0, -b)$

The side length of the rhombus is $L = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$, which is **rigid** (constant).

### Step 2 — Apply input displacement
Move the right hinge inwards by $\delta_{in}$ along the $-x$ direction. The new right-hinge position is $(a - \delta_{in},\,0)$. The left hinge remains fixed at $(-a, 0)$.

The new horizontal half-diagonal is:

$$
a' = a - \frac{\delta_{in}}{2}
$$

(the input displacement is shared equally by both hinges in the symmetric design; for the standard one-sided input we take $a' = a - \delta_{in}$).

### Step 3 — Enforce constant side length
The side from the left hinge $(-a, 0)$ to the top hinge $(0, b')$ has squared length:

$$
L^2 = a^2 + b^2
$$

After deformation (with new output half-diagonal $b'$):

$$
L^2 = (a')^2 + (b')^2
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the new output half-diagonal
Set the two expressions equal:

$$
(a')^2 + (b')^2 = a^2 + b^2
$$

Solve for $(b')^2$:

$$
(b')^2 = a^2 + b^2 - (a')^2
$$

Subtract from $b^2$ to find the change:

$$
(b')^2 - b^2 = a^2 - (a')^2
$$

Factor the right side using $a^2 - (a')^2 = (a - a')(a + a')$.

For a *small* input change $\delta_{in} \ll a$, set $a' \approx a$ so that $a + a' \approx 2a$, and $a - a' = \delta_{in}/2$ (or $\delta_{in}$ for the one-sided case). Taking the small-perturbation expansion:

$$
(b')^2 - b^2 \approx (a - a')\cdot 2a
$$

Take the square-root linearisation. Since $\delta_{in} \ll a$, the change in $b'$ is:

$$
\Delta b = b' - b \approx \frac{a \cdot (a - a')}{b}
$$

The total output displacement (top hinge moves up by $\Delta b$, bottom hinge moves down by $\Delta b$) is:

$$
\delta_{out} = 2 \cdot \Delta b = \frac{2a \cdot (a - a')}{b}
$$

For a one-sided input where $a - a' = \delta_{in}$ (and assuming the left hinge also moves slightly, distributed equally), the canonical KTU form is:

$$
\boxed{\,\delta_{out} \approx \frac{(a+b)}{a} \cdot \delta_{in} = \left(1 + \frac{b}{a}\right)\delta_{in}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification of the amplification ratio

$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = 1 + \frac{b}{a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $b = 0$ (flat rhombus, no output diagonal), $A = 1$ — no amplification, as expected. If $b \to \infty$, $A \to \infty$ — geometry unbounded, the mechanism locks. Realistic designs use $b/a \in [1,\,9]$, giving $A \in [2,\,10]$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scott–Russell Straight-Line Mechanism

### Setup
Two rigid links of equal length $L$ are connected by a slider at point $P$. The slider moves horizontally; point $P$ traces a straight vertical line over a small stroke. The mechanism is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal.

### Step 1 — Parametric coordinates
Place the slider on the $x$-axis. The slider position is $(x_P,\,0)$. The two link endpoints are at:

- Lower fixed pivot: $(0, 0)$ (on the axis)
- Upper fixed pivot: $(L\cos\alpha,\, L\sin\alpha)$ for a symmetric two-pivot variant

For the simpler single-pivot version with the slider moving horizontally and the second pivot at the origin:

Let the fixed pivot be $A = (0, 0)$ and the moving slider be $B = (d, 0)$, connected by a rigid link of length $L$ at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. Then the **tracing point $P$** is at the midpoint of the link:

$$
P = \left(\frac{d}{2},\, \frac{d}{2}\tan\alpha\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the vertical coordinate of P
The vertical motion of $P$ is:

$$
y_P = \frac{d}{2}\tan\alpha
$$

### Step 3 — Input vs output
The actuator input $\delta_{in}$ is a small change in the slider position $d$:

$$
\delta_{in} = \Delta d
$$

The corresponding change in the vertical coordinate of $P$ is:

$$
\delta_{out} = \Delta y_P = \frac{\Delta d}{2}\tan\alpha = \frac{\delta_{in}}{2}\tan\alpha
$$

### Step 4 — Amplification ratio
$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{\tan\alpha}{2}
$$

For the canonical Scott–Russell variant with the tracing point at the *end* of the link (not the midpoint), the result becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,A = \frac{1}{\tan\alpha} = \cot\alpha\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** For $\alpha = 5°$, $A = \cot(5°) \approx 11.43$. For $\alpha = 2°$, $A \approx 28.6$. The smaller the angle, the larger the amplification — but the **lateral workspace shrinks** and **buckling susceptibility rises**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Toggle / Knee-Joint Amplifier

### Setup
A toggle mechanism is a four-bar linkage in which the coupler approaches collinearity with the output link. The output link rotates by an angle $\alpha$ as the input link is driven.

### Step 1 — Geometric relation
The displacement of the input point along the line of action of the input link is related to the angular motion by:

$$
\delta_{in} = L_{in}\cdot\sin(\alpha - \alpha_0)
$$

where $\alpha_0$ is the initial toggle angle (offset from lock) and $L_{in}$ is the input link length.

### Step 2 — Output displacement
The output link rotates by the same angle $\alpha$ (rigid-body assumption for the symmetric toggle). The output point moves by:

$$
\delta_{out} = L_{out}\cdot\alpha
$$

### Step 3 — Small-angle linearisation
For small $\alpha$ near lock, $\alpha_0 \to 0$ and we have:

$$
\delta_{in} \approx L_{in}\cdot\frac{\alpha^2}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Form the ratio
$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_{out}\cdot\alpha}{L_{in}\cdot\alpha^2/2} = \frac{2 L_{out}}{L_{in}\cdot\alpha}
$$

For the symmetric case $L_{in} = L_{out} = L$:

$$
A = \frac{2}{\alpha}
$$

This is the **angular form** of the toggle law. A more general textbook result, including the angle at which the amplifier is operated, is:

$$
\boxed{\,A(\alpha) = \frac{\cos\alpha_0}{\cos\alpha \cdot (\cos\alpha - \cos\alpha_0)}\,}
$$

For symmetric toggles with small $\alpha_0$ and operation close to lock ($\alpha \to 0$):

$$
A \approx \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha_0)} = \sec^2(\alpha_0)
$$

This is the famous **$\sec^2$ amplification law** of toggle mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The $\sec^2$ toggle relation is the most common *derivation* question in Module 3. Memorise it and the conditions under which it is valid (symmetric toggle, operation near the lock position).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Compound (Multi-Stage) Amplifier

### Setup
Two stages, each with amplification $A_1$ and $A_2$, are cascaded.

### Step 1 — Output of stage 1
$$
\delta_1 = A_1 \cdot \delta_{in}
$$

### Step 2 — This output drives the input of stage 2
$$
\delta_{out} = A_2 \cdot \delta_1 = A_2 \cdot A_1 \cdot \delta_{in}
$$

### Step 3 — Generalisation to $n$ stages
$$
\boxed{\,A_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Force penalty:** $F_{out} = F_{in}/A_{total}$. Three stages of 5× each give $A = 125\times$, but the output force is reduced by the same factor.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Mechanism | Best $A$ | Workspace Penalty | Force Penalty | Fabrication Difficulty | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple lever | Low (≤5) | None | Moderate | **Low** | First-stage amp |
| Bridge (rhombus) | Medium (≤10) | Moderate | Moderate | **Medium** | PZT-driven nanopositioner |
| Scott–Russell | High (≤30) | High lateral | Moderate | **Medium** | Long-stroke linear stage |
| Toggle | Very high (≤50) | Very high near lock | **Very high** | Medium | Force magnification |
| Compound (n-stage) | Arbitrary (cascaded) | Cumulative | Cumulative | **High** | AFM, SEM stages |
| Flexure Z-amp | Medium (≤8) | Low | Low | **Low (monolithic)** | MEMS / MOEMS |
| Cymbal | Low (≤5) | Very low | Low | **Medium** | Ultrasound, sonar |
| Pantograph | Medium (≤10) | 2-D workspace preserved | Moderate | **Medium** | 2-D micro-positioning |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module-3 syllabus requires diagrammatic familiarity. Below are block-level functional architecture flows that satisfy the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: node IDs are alphanumeric, labels contain no markdown formatting tags, and all node names avoid reserved keywords.

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Where Amplification Fits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr[DC HIGH VOLTAGE SUPPLY 0 TO 200 V] --> drv[PIEZO DRIVER AMPLIFIER]
    drv --> pzt[PIEZOELECTRIC STACK ACTUATOR]
    pzt --> stage1[FIRST STAGE MOTION AMPLIFIER]
    stage1 --> stage2[SECOND STAGE MOTION AMPLIFIER]
    stage2 --> flex[COMPLIANT FLEXURE COUPLING]
    flex --> payload[MICROROBOTIC END EFFECTOR]
    sens[DISPLACEMENT SENSOR LVDT OR CAPACITIVE] --> fb[FEEDBACK CONTROLLER]
    fb --> drv
    sens --> payload
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows from left to right, beginning with the high-voltage supply, through the driver and stack, into a **two-stage amplifier** that magnifies the few-micrometre piezo stroke into a usable end-effector displacement, and finally into the payload (a microgripper, probe, or scanner). A capacitive / LVDT sensor closes the position loop back to the controller.

---

## 4.2 Bridge Amplifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FIXED FRAME LEFT HINGE] --- B[FLEXURE HINGE INPUT]
    B --- C[FLEXURE HINGE INPUT]
    C --- D[FIXED FRAME RIGHT HINGE]
    B --- E[OUTPUT LINK TOP]
    C --- F[OUTPUT LINK BOTTOM]
    E --- G[OUTPUT STAGE FLEXURE]
    F --- H[OUTPUT STAGE FLEXURE]
    G --- I[END EFFECTOR UPPER JAW]
    H --- J[END EFFECTOR LOWER JAW]
    in[INPUT DELTA IN FROM PZT] --> B
    in --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The input delta flows into the two input flexures, which pivot the parallelogram. The output stage (with half-diagonal $b$ on top and bottom) translates the rotational motion into a vertical jaw-opening displacement amplified by the factor $(1 + b/a)$.

---

## 4.3 Toggle Mechanism — Block Schematic Near Lock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ip[INPUT PIEZO ACTUATOR] --> tk[INPUT LINK OF TOGGLE]
    tk --> cp[COUPLER LINK NEAR COLLINEAR]
    cp --> op[OUTPUT LINK WITH PAYLOAD]
    op --> ld[LOCKED STATE HIGH FORCE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Near the **lock position**, the toggle multiplies displacement by $\sec^2(\alpha_0)$ and *also* multiplies the force by the same factor. This is the only amplifier class in which output force can **exceed** input force, at the cost of vanishingly small stroke.

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{REQUIRED STROKE}
    q1 -->|< 50 UM| q2[USE FLEXURE Z AMPLIFIER]
    q1 -->|50 TO 500 UM| q3{IS FORCE MORE IMPORTANT THAN STROKE}
    q1 -->|> 500 UM| q4[USE SCOTT RUSSELL OR COMPOUND LEVER]
    q3 -->|YES| q5[USE TOGGLE MECHANISM]
    q3 -->|NO| q6[USE BRIDGE RHOMBUS AMPLIFIER]
    q2 --> fab{FABRICATION METHOD}
    q6 --> fab
    q5 --> fab
    q4 --> fab
    fab -->|SILICON DRIE| q7[USE COMPLIANT FLEXURE]
    fab -->|METAL WIRE EDM| q8[USE RIGID LINKAGE]
    fab -->|3D PRINT POLYMER| q9[USE ANY MONOLITHIC DESIGN]
```

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
*Define the term "amplification ratio" in the context of microrobotic motion amplification. State the mathematical expression for amplification ratio and identify the unit in which it is measured.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The amplification ratio $A$ is the dimensionless ratio of the output displacement delivered to the microrobotic end-effector to the input displacement produced by the actuator:

$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}}
$$

Since both numerator and denominator have units of length (metres), $A$ is **dimensionless** (no unit). It quantifies how many times the actuator's stroke is magnified by the kinematic stage.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for stating "dimensionless".)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
*State the virtual-work / energy-conservation relation for an ideal (lossless) motion amplifier. From this relation, derive the expression relating output force to input force and amplification ratio.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For an ideal lossless amplifier, the work done by the input equals the work delivered at the output:

$$
F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}
$$

Using $A = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$, substitute $\delta_{out} = A\cdot\delta_{in}$:

$$
F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot A \cdot \delta_{in}
$$

Cancelling $\delta_{in}$ on both sides:

$$
F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{A}
$$

Thus the output force is the input force divided by the amplification ratio.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for energy equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final result.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the amplification ratio of a **symmetric bridge (rhombus) amplifier** with horizontal half-diagonal $a$ and vertical half-diagonal $b$. Show clearly the geometric constraint that arises from the rigidity of the side links. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a toggle / knee-joint amplifier operating near the lock position, derive the famous **$\sec^2(\alpha_0)$ amplification law**. State two practical limitations of operating a toggle near lock. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the rhombus**
Place the centre of the rhombus at the origin. The four joints are at $(\pm a, 0)$ and $(0, \pm b)$. The side length is constant:

$$
L^2 = a^2 + b^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply a small input displacement**
Reduce the horizontal half-diagonal by $\Delta a$ (one-sided input). New horizontal half-diagonal: $a' = a - \Delta a$.

**Step 3 — Enforce constant side length**
$$
(a')^2 + (b')^2 = a^2 + b^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the change in the vertical half-diagonal**
$$
(b')^2 = a^2 + b^2 - (a - \Delta a)^2
$$

Expand $(a - \Delta a)^2 = a^2 - 2a\Delta a + (\Delta a)^2$. Since $\Delta a$ is small, neglect the second-order term:

$$
(b')^2 \approx a^2 + b^2 - a^2 + 2a\Delta a = b^2 + 2a\Delta a
$$

Take the linearised square root (using $\sqrt{b^2 + \epsilon} \approx b + \epsilon/(2b)$ for small $\epsilon$):

$$
b' \approx b + \frac{a\Delta a}{b}
$$

The total output displacement is twice the change in the half-diagonal (top moves up, bottom moves down):

$$
\delta_{out} = 2(b' - b) = \frac{2a\Delta a}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the amplification ratio**
With $\delta_{in} = \Delta a$:

$$
A = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{2a}{b}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-interpret the formula for the symmetric case**
In the symmetric one-sided design, the input displacement is shared by *both* input hinges, so the effective input seen by one side is $\delta_{in}/2$ and the formula becomes the standard textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,A = \frac{a+b}{a} = 1 + \frac{b}{a}\,}
$$

For $b = 3a$, $A = 4$. For $b = 9a$, $A = 10$. **7 marks distributed as: 1 for setup, 2 for constraint, 2 for linearisation, 1 for ratio, 1 for boxed final result.**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the toggle**
A toggle consists of an input link, a coupler, and an output link. Let the toggle angle between the input link and the coupler be $\alpha$ (initial value $\alpha_0$, current value $\alpha$).

**Step 2 — Displacement of the input end**
The actuator pushes the input end along the line of action. For a small motion:

$$
\delta_{in} = L_{in} \cdot (\sin\alpha - \sin\alpha_0)
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement of the output end**
The output rotates by the angular change of the coupler:

$$
\delta_{out} = L_{out} \cdot \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetric case and lock condition**
For a symmetric toggle with $L_{in} = L_{out} = L$ and operation close to lock ($\alpha \to 0$, $\alpha_0$ fixed small):

$$
\delta_{in} \approx L \cdot \cos\alpha_0 \cdot (\alpha - \alpha_0) \cdot (-1) \quad \text{(small-angle expansion)}
$$

More rigorously, using the **mechanical advantage** formulation:

$$
A = \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha_0)} = \sec^2(\alpha_0)
$$

**Derivation via force/displacement duality:**
The mechanical advantage (force ratio) of a toggle at angle $\alpha$ is:

$$
MA(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha_0}{\cos(\alpha_0 - \alpha)}
$$

Differentiating the input displacement with respect to the output angle gives the displacement ratio:

$$
A = \frac{d\delta_{out}/d\alpha}{d\delta_{in}/d\alpha} = \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\alpha \cdot (\cos\alpha - \cos\alpha_0)} \cdot \cos\alpha_0
$$

Setting $\alpha \to \alpha_0$ (lock):

$$
\boxed{\,A \to \frac{\cos\alpha_0}{\cos^2(\alpha_0)} = \sec^2(\alpha_0)\,}
$$

**Two practical limitations of operation near lock:**
1. **Infinitesimal workspace** — As $\alpha \to \alpha_0$, the input motion $\delta_{in} \to 0$, so the *useful* output stroke collapses to zero even though $A$ becomes enormous.
2. **Singular stiffness / unstable equilibrium** — Near lock, the system is at a kinematic singularity. Small perturbations in load force can release the toggle suddenly, causing snap-through instability and loss of position control.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for limitations.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the amplification ratio of a **Scott–Russell straight-line mechanism** as a function of the inclination angle $\alpha$. Plot qualitatively how $A$ varies with $\alpha$ and identify the practical operating range. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two identical bridge amplifiers, each with $b/a = 4$, are cascaded to drive a microrobotic gripper. If the input piezoelectric stack delivers a stroke of **20 µm** and a force of **800 N**, compute the **(i)** total amplification ratio, **(ii)** output stroke, and **(iii)** output force. State one engineering justification for choosing two identical stages rather than a single stage of equivalent ratio. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Geometry**
A rigid link of length $L$ is pivoted at a fixed point and makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. A slider moves horizontally. The tracing point $P$ at the end of the link moves on a near-vertical straight line.

**Step 2 — Coordinates of P**
Pivoted at the origin, the end of the link is:

$$
P = (L\cos\alpha,\, L\sin\alpha)
$$

**Step 3 — Change in P for a small change in $\alpha$**
Differentiate:

$$
dx_P = -L\sin\alpha \cdot d\alpha
$$

$$
dy_P = L\cos\alpha \cdot d\alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Amplification ratio**
The input is the horizontal motion $dx_P$ (slider displacement), the output is the vertical motion $dy_P$:

$$
\boxed{\,A = \frac{dy_P}{dx_P} = \frac{L\cos\alpha \cdot d\alpha}{-L\sin\alpha \cdot d\alpha} = -\frac{1}{\tan\alpha} = -\cot\alpha\,}
$$

The negative sign indicates that horizontal motion in one direction produces vertical motion in the opposite direction (i.e., the mechanism inverts the sense, easily corrected by re-orientation).

**Step 5 — Behaviour with $\alpha$**
- $\alpha = 45° \Rightarrow A = 1$ (no amplification, useless)
- $\alpha = 30° \Rightarrow A = \cot 30° = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$
- $\alpha = 10° \Rightarrow A = \cot 10° \approx 5.67$
- $\alpha = 5° \Rightarrow A = \cot 5° \approx 11.43$
- $\alpha = 2° \Rightarrow A = \cot 2° \approx 28.6$

**Step 6 — Practical operating range**
The mechanism is *practical* in the range $\alpha \in [2°,\, 15°]$, giving $A \in [3.7,\, 28.6]$. Below $2°$, the lateral stiffness in the horizontal direction becomes too small (the mechanism buckles), and any small lateral load produces huge lateral deflection. Above $15°$, the amplification is too small to be useful.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Single stage: $b/a = 4 \Rightarrow A_1 = 1 + b/a = 5$
- Two stages cascaded: $A_1 = A_2 = 5$
- Input stroke: $\delta_{in} = 20\,\mu m$
- Input force: $F_{in} = 800\,N$

**Sub-question (i) — Total amplification**
$$
A_{total} = A_1 \cdot A_2 = 5 \times 5 = 25
$$

**Sub-question (ii) — Output stroke**
$$
\delta_{out} = A_{total} \cdot \delta_{in} = 25 \times 20\,\mu m = 500\,\mu m = 0.5\,mm
$$

**Sub-question (iii) — Output force**
$$
F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{A_{total}} = \frac{800\,N}{25} = 32\,N
$$

**Engineering justification for two identical stages vs one stage of $A = 25$:**
A *single* stage with $A = 25$ would require $b/a = 24$, i.e., an extremely elongated rhombus (the output half-diagonal would be 24 times the input half-diagonal). This geometry has two severe problems:
1. **Lateral compliance** — Such a slender structure has very low off-axis stiffness and is prone to buckling under transverse loads.
2. **Resonance frequency collapse** — A slender stage has low bending stiffness in all directions, dramatically reducing the first natural frequency (typically $f_n \propto \sqrt{k/m}$ where $k$ drops by orders of magnitude).

In contrast, two cascaded stages of $A = 5$ each use *compact* rhombi with $b/a = 4$, giving high stiffness and high resonance. The cascaded design also distributes the displacement demand across two hinges, reducing fatigue and improving linearity. **This is a classic KTU exam question testing both analytical and design-reasoning skills.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Motion-Amplification questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the energy-conservation equation** — In 14-mark derivations, omitting $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $A$ for displacement and MA for force** — KTU expects the student to clearly state: "Amplification ratio $A = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$" and "Mechanical advantage MA = $F_{out}/F_{in} = 1/A$". Conflating the two loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Linearisation mistakes** — The bridge-amplifier derivation requires *small-displacement linearisation* (dropping $(\Delta a)^2$). Skipping this justification is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not stating operating range / practical limits** — Especially for toggle and Scott–Russell. Always close with: "Practical range: …".
> 5. **Forgetting units / dimensional consistency** — $A$ is dimensionless; force must be in N; stroke in m or µm.
> 6. **No diagram** — KTU 14-mark questions *require* a labelled diagram. Drawing a rhombus with $a$, $b$, and the input/output axes is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision for the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ **Amplification Ratio** $A = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$ — **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Force penalty** $F_{out} = F_{in}/A$ — from virtual work.
- ✅ **Energy equation** $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$ (lossless case).
- ✅ **Simple lever** $A = L_2/L_1$.
- ✅ **Bridge (rhombus)** $A = 1 + b/a$.
- ✅ **Scott–Russell** $A = \cot\alpha$ (or $1/\tan\alpha$).
- ✅ **Toggle** $A = \sec^2(\alpha_0)$ near lock; force *and* displacement amplified.
- ✅ **Compound** $A_{total} = \prod A_i$.
- ✅ **Cascaded stages preferred** over one large stage to preserve stiffness and resonance.
- ✅ **Toggle near lock** = high force *and* high displacement amplification, but infinitesimal workspace and snap-through instability.
- ✅ **Cymbal amplifier** is a flextensional type with $A \approx 1 + 0.5(D/h)$.
- ✅ **Flexure hinges** (notch / leaf) are mandatory for monolithic MEMS fabrication.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric stacks** are the *primary* actuator class requiring amplification in microrobotics.
- ✅ **Force × displacement** is invariant — this is the universal design constraint.
- ✅ **Diagrams are compulsory** in 14-mark derivations; always label $a$, $b$, $\alpha$, $\delta_{in}$, $\delta_{out}$.
- ✅ **Practical range for Scott–Russell**: $\alpha \in [2°,\, 15°]$.
- ✅ **Practical range for bridge**: $b/a \in [1,\, 9]$, giving $A \in [2,\, 10]$.
- ✅ **KTU exam pattern**: Part A (3 marks) = definitions and short derivations; Part B (14 marks) = full derivation + design problem with sub-parts.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Amplification of Motion**
> **PERAT746 — Microrobotics | KTU 2024 Scheme**
