<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> Define *Displacement Amplification Ratio* and *Force Amplification Ratio* for a planar motion amplifier. State the relationship between them for a lossless amplifier.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Displacement Amplification Ratio (DAR)** is defined as the ratio of output displacement to input displacement:

$$
A_d = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}}
$$

The **Force Amplification Ratio (FAR)** is the ratio of output force to input force:

$$
A_f = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}}
$$

For an **ideal lossless amplifier**, by the **Virtual Work Principle** $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$, so:

$$
\boxed{A_f \cdot A_d = 1}
$$

A high DAR therefore always implies a low FAR — motion and force amplification are mutually exclusive in a lossless planar amplifier.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> List any three advantages of using flexure-hinge compliant amplifiers over conventional pin-jointed amplifiers in microrobotic systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No friction, backlash, or lubrication requirement** — Flexure hinges deform elastically and have no contacting sliding surfaces, eliminating stick-slip and wear at micro-scale.
2. **Monolithic fabrication** — The amplifier can be fabricated in a single piece with the actuator (e.g., silicon, SU-8, or polymer), reducing assembly errors.
3. **High precision and repeatability** — Deformation is governed entirely by Hooke's law, so motion is deterministic and sub-micron accurate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently *forget* to state the assumption (lossless / frictionless) before writing $A_f \cdot A_d = 1$. Always declare the assumption explicitly.
> 2. In bridge-amplifier problems, students mix up $a$, $b$, and $c$. Memorize: $a$ and $b$ are *half-arms to the hinges*, $c$ is the *side member*.
> 3. Marks are often lost for omitting **units** in numerical answers and for **not drawing the free-body / kinematic sketch**.
> 4. For force-displacement trade-off derivations, examiners expect the **Virtual Work Principle** to be cited by name.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the displacement amplification ratio of a **bridge-type planar amplifier** with input half-arm $a$, output half-arm $b$ and side member length $c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bridge-type amplifier has $a = 2$ mm, $b = 2$ mm, $c = 6$ mm. The actuator supplies a force of $F_{in} = 5$ N over an input displacement of $\delta_{in} = 10\ \mu$m. Compute (i) DAR, (ii) FAR, (iii) output displacement, (iv) output force, and (v) output stiffness if input stiffness is $k_{in} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ N/m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B1(a)

**Step 1 — Define geometry.** The rhombus bridge has side members of length $c$ connecting the input and output vertices through flexure hinges at distances $a$ and $b$ from the respective vertices.

**Step 2 — Kinematic relation.** A small input displacement $\delta_{in}$ at the input vertex causes a corresponding change in the horizontal diagonal. By small-angle linearization of the rhombus geometry, the vertical output displacement is:

$$
\delta_{out} = \delta_{in}\cdot\frac{(a+c)(b+c)}{a\,b}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain DAR.**

$$
\boxed{A_d = \frac{(a+c)(b+c)}{a\,b}}
$$

For the symmetric case $a = b$, this reduces to:

$$
A_d = \left(1 + \frac{c}{a}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Note on Virtual Work.** By Virtual Work, the corresponding FAR is $A_f = 1/A_d$.

#### Model Solution — B1(b)

**Given:** $a = 2$ mm, $b = 2$ mm, $c = 6$ mm, $F_{in} = 5$ N, $\delta_{in} = 10\ \mu$m $= 10^{-5}$ m, $k_{in} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ N/m.

**(i) DAR:**

$$
A_d = \left(1 + \frac{c}{a}\right)^2 = \left(1 + \frac{6}{2}\right)^2 = (1 + 3)^2 = 16
$$

**(ii) FAR:**

$$
A_f = \frac{1}{A_d} = \frac{1}{16} = 0.0625
$$

**(iii) Output displacement:**

$$
\delta_{out} = A_d \cdot \delta_{in} = 16 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 160\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**(iv) Output force:**

$$
F_{out} = A_f \cdot F_{in} = 0.0625 \times 5\ \text{N} = 0.3125\ \text{N} \approx 0.313\ \text{N}
$$

**(v) Output stiffness:**

$$
k_{out} = \frac{k_{in}}{A_d^{\,2}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{16^{2}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{256} = 1953.125\ \text{N/m} \approx 1.95 \times 10^{3}\ \text{N/m}
$$

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Scott-Russell straight-line linkage** as a planar motion amplifier. State its key advantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micro-positioning stage uses a compound lever formed by cascading two simple levers. The first lever has $A_{d1} = 4$ and the second has $A_{d2} = 5$. The actuator delivers $F_{in} = 2$ N and $\delta_{in} = 20\ \mu$m. Compute the overall DAR, overall FAR, output displacement and output force. Comment on the design trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B2(a)

**Sketch description:** The Scott-Russell linkage consists of two equal-length rigid links $AB$ and $CD$ connected at their midpoints by a third rigid link $BC$. Point $A$ is fixed (ground pivot), point $D$ moves along a horizontal slot, and point $C$ traces an exact straight line perpendicular to $AD$.

**Working:**
- The input motion is applied at point $D$ (or $C$).
- Through the geometric constraint that $AB = BC = CD$ and $A$ is fixed, point $C$ moves in a straight line — a pure translation.
- Because the output is pure translation (not a rotation), parasitic angular errors at the output are eliminated.

**Displacement amplification:** For an input displacement $\delta_{in}$ at $D$, the output displacement at $C$ is given by:

$$
\delta_{out} = 2\,\delta_{in}\cdot\frac{L_{BC}}{L_{AB}} = 2\,\delta_{in}
$$

(when the linkage is symmetric).

**Key advantage:** **Pure straight-line output motion** — ideal for micro-positioning stages where any angular error would translate into planar tracking errors.

#### Model Solution — B2(b)

**Overall DAR (cascaded product):**

$$
A_d = A_{d1}\cdot A_{d2} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Overall FAR (using duality):**

$$
A_f = \frac{1}{A_d} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05
$$

**Output displacement:**

$$
\delta_{out} = A_d \cdot \delta_{in} = 20 \times 20\ \mu\text{m} = 400\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Output force:**

$$
F_{out} = A_f \cdot F_{in} = 0.05 \times 2\ \text{N} = 0.1\ \text{N}
$$

**Design trade-off comment:**
The compound lever achieves a massive 20× motion amplification, but the output force drops to just 0.1 N. The original 2 N actuator force has been "diluted" by a factor of 20. Additionally, the overall stiffness at the output is reduced to $1/A_d^{\,2} = 1/400$ of the input stiffness, making the stage very compliant and sensitive to external disturbances. For a microrobot, this trade-off is acceptable when the output task is *displacement-dominated* (e.g., cell positioning) but is unacceptable when the task is *force-dominated* (e.g., micro-machining).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DAR:** $A_d = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$. **FAR:** $A_f = F_{out}/F_{in}$.
- **Virtual Work (lossless):** $F_{in}\,\delta_{in} = F_{out}\,\delta_{out}$, hence $A_d \cdot A_f = 1$.
- **Simple lever:** $A_d = L_2/L_1$. **Bridge amplifier (symmetric):** $A_d = (1 + c/a)^2$. **Bridge (general):** $A_d = (a+c)(b+c)/(a\,b)$.
- **Output stiffness scales inversely with $A_d^{2}$:** $k_{out} = k_{in}/A_d^{2}$. Higher amplification → softer output.
- **Cascaded amplifiers multiply DAR:** $A_d^{total} = A_d^{1}\cdot A_d^{2}\cdot\ldots\cdot A_d^{n}$.
- **Compliance beats pin joints at micro-scale:** no friction, no backlash, no lubrication, monolithic fabrication.
- **Buckling check is mandatory** for slender amplifier arms: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L^2$.
- **Trade-off summary:** motion amplification ↔ force amplification ↔ stiffness reduction.
- **Always declare assumptions** (lossless, small-angle, symmetric) before derivations in KTU exams.
- **Draw the free-body / kinematic sketch** before writing any derivation — partial marks depend on it.
- **Common amplifier families:** simple lever, bridge/diamond, Scott-Russell, toggle/knee, compound lever, tendon trunk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrostatic Actuator:** A microscale actuation device that converts electrical potential energy into controlled mechanical displacement or force by exploiting the **Coulombic attraction** (or repulsion) between oppositely charged conductive structures separated by a dielectric medium (typically air or vacuum).

In the context of KTU Microrobotics (PERAT746), electrostatic actuators are classified under **electromagnetic-field-driven actuators** (alongside piezoelectric actuators), and represent the **most widely fabricated** microactuation principle in silicon-based MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) due to their compatibility with CMOS fabrication processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Sticky Balloon Analogy"**
>
> Imagine rubbing a balloon on your hair. The balloon becomes electrically charged and is *attracted* to your hand or the wall. The closer it gets, the stronger the pull — until it suddenly snaps onto the surface. An **electrostatic actuator works on the exact same principle**, but in a controlled, engineered microscale geometry:
> - **The two plates** of a tiny capacitor play the role of the balloon and the wall.
> - **A voltage applied between the plates** plays the role of the rubbing-induced charge.
> - **The "snap"** you observe at a critical voltage is called the **pull-in instability** — the most important (and exam-favorite) phenomenon in this module.

### 1.3 Why Electrostatic Actuation Dominates Microrobotics

| Property | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Voltage (electrical field), **not** current |
| **Power consumption (static)** | Near **zero** — current flow is negligible |
| **Typical operating voltage** | **5 V to 100 V** |
| **Response time** | Microseconds (very fast) |
| **Force scaling** | F ∝ Area → favorable at microscale |
| **Fabrication compatibility** | Excellent with **silicon micromachining** |
| **Biomedical suitability** | High (no magnetic interference) |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Insight:** Electrostatic forces scale as **F ∝ L²** (area), whereas inertial forces scale as **F ∝ L³** (mass). As device dimension **L** shrinks to micrometers, **surface forces dominate volume forces** — this is precisely why electrostatic actuation is preferred in microrobotics.

### 1.4 Classification of Electrostatic Actuators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus-Mandated Categories (Module 3):**
> 1. **Parallel-Plate Actuator** — vertical out-of-plane motion.
> 2. **Comb-Drive Actuator** — lateral in-plane motion.
> 3. **Scratch-Drive Actuator (SDA)** — stepwise micro-motion using electrostatic deformation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The complete operational chain of an electrostatic actuator is summarized below.

- **Step 1 — Charge Accumulation:** When a voltage **V** is applied across two conductive electrodes separated by a dielectric gap **d**, opposite charges (+Q and −Q) accumulate on the facing surfaces, creating an electric field **E**.
- **Step 2 — Capacitive Energy Storage:** The structure behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor storing electrostatic energy **U** proportional to **V²**.
- **Step 3 — Force Generation via Energy Gradient:** Any infinitesimal displacement **dx** that *increases* the capacitance reduces the system energy. The negative gradient of energy with respect to displacement produces a mechanical force **F** that always acts to **close the gap**.
- **Step 4 — Mechanical Work Output:** The force performs work against the elastic restoring force of a suspension beam (spring constant **k**), producing a stable deflection **x**.
- **Step 5 — Pull-In Instability (Critical Limit):** Beyond a threshold voltage **V_PI**, the electrostatic force grows faster (∝ 1/d²) than the linear spring restoring force (∝ d), and the movable plate **snaps** uncontrollably onto the fixed plate.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel-plate capacitance | C | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F (Farads) |
| 2 | Stored electrostatic energy | U | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Joules |
| 3 | Electrostatic force (general) | F_e | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Newtons |
| 4 | Electrostatic force (parallel plate) | F_e | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$ | Newtons |
| 5 | Spring restoring force | F_s | $F_s = k x$ | Newtons |
| 6 | Stable deflection | x | $x = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k d^{2}}$ | Meters |
| 7 | Pull-in voltage | V_{PI} | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_{0}^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Pull-in displacement | x_{PI} | $x_{PI} = \dfrac{d_0}{3}$ | One-third of gap |
| 9 | Comb-drive capacitance (n fingers) | C_{comb} | $C_{comb} = \dfrac{2 n \varepsilon_0 t}{g}$ | F, per unit overlap |
| 10 | Comb-drive force | F_{comb} | $F_{comb} = \dfrac{n \varepsilon_0 t V^{2}}{g}$ | Independent of x |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** Note that the **pull-in voltage formula** has a factor of **8/27 ≈ 0.296** which is the classic KTU-exam numerical. The pull-in always occurs at **one-third** of the initial gap, never at one-half — this is a frequent **wrong-answer trap**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Comb drives** are the workhorse of MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers (e.g., Analog Devices ADXL series).
- **Scratch drive actuators** are used in optical MEMS for fine alignment of micro-mirrors in projectors and barcode scanners.
- **Parallel-plate micromirrors** enable adaptive optics, DLP (Digital Light Processing) projectors, and endomicroscopy probes.
- **Micro-grippers** for cell manipulation in biomedical microrobotics use electrostatic fingers for precise sub-micron gripping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** Gauss's Law applied to the uniform field between two large parallel plates of area **A** separated by a small gap **d**, with free-space permittivity **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**.

**Step 1.** The surface charge density on each plate is

$$\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 2.** Applying Gauss's Law to a rectangular pillbox enclosing one plate gives the electric field magnitude between the plates:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 3.** The potential difference between the plates is the line integral of the field across the gap:

$$V = \int_0^d E \, dx' = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \, d}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 4.** By definition, capacitance $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$. Substituting the expression for V:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q d}{\varepsilon_0 A}} \\
  &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comment:** Adding a dielectric of relative permittivity **ε_r** simply multiplies the result by **ε_r**. This is the canonical starting point for every electrostatic actuator derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrostatic Force from Energy Method (Co-energy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** The electrostatic force at constant voltage equals the *positive* gradient of stored electrical co-energy $W' = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ with respect to the generalized displacement **x**. This is the energy-method formulation required by KTU Module 3.

**Step 1.** Express the co-energy of the system at fixed voltage **V**:

$$W'(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^{2}$$

**Step 2.** The generalized electrostatic force is the partial derivative:

$$F_e = \left.\frac{\partial W'}{\partial x}\right|_{V=\text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 3.** For a parallel-plate geometry where **x** represents the plate gap **d**, the capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$, so:

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 4.** The negative sign indicates the force acts to *reduce* the gap (attractive). Taking the magnitude:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_e &= \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^{2}} \\
    &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The force is proportional to **V²**, hence it is **direction-insensitive** — it acts whether V is positive or negative (only the magnitude matters). This is the key reason why electrostatic actuators can be driven by **AC** or **bipolar DC** without loss of force directionality.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pull-In Voltage (Static Stability Limit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** Pull-in occurs when the electrostatic force curve and the linear spring restoring force curve become *tangent* (one shared solution, no longer two intersections). Mathematically: force equality + zero slope difference.

**Step 1.** Define the movable plate's equilibrium position **x** measured from the initial gap **d₀**:

$$\text{Initial gap} = d_0 - x$$

**Step 2.** Force equilibrium between spring and electrostatic force:

$$k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x)^{2}}$$

**Step 3.** Stability requires that an infinitesimal increase in **x** must produce a net *restoring* force, i.e.:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[kx\right] > \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2(d_0 - x)^{2}}\right]$$

This simplifies to:

$$k > \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(d_0 - x)^{3}}$$

**Step 4.** At the **critical pull-in point**, the two conditions (equilibrium + boundary of stability) hold simultaneously. Setting them equal at the critical displacement:

$$k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{(d_0 - x_{PI})^{3}}$$

Dividing by the equilibrium equation and substituting, the algebra yields the canonical result:

$$x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}$$

**Step 5.** Substituting back into the equilibrium equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \frac{d_0}{3} &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{2 \left(d_0 - \dfrac{d_0}{3}\right)^{2}} \\
k \cdot \frac{d_0}{3} &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{2 \left(\dfrac{2d_0}{3}\right)^{2}} \\
k \cdot \frac{d_0}{3} &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{2 \cdot \dfrac{4 d_0^{2}}{9}} \\
k \cdot \frac{d_0}{3} &= \frac{9 \,\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{8 \, d_0^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Solving for **V_{PI}**:

$$\boxed{\,V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27 \, \varepsilon_0 A}}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Cheat:** The fraction $\dfrac{8}{27} \approx 0.296$. Some students wrongly write it as $\dfrac{8}{9}$ — please memorize the **27** in the denominator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electrostatic Actuator System

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **signal flow** and **energy conversion** chain of a generic electrostatic actuator system as used in KTU Module 3 case studies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voltage Source V_dc or V_ac] --> B[Drive Electronics and Waveform Shaper]
    B --> C[Electrode Pair Fixed Plate and Movable Plate]
    C --> D[Electric Field E and Charge Q on Plates]
    D --> E[Coulomb Attractive Force F_e]
    E --> F{Spring Suspension with Stiffness k}
    F --> G[Mechanical Displacement x]
    G --> H[Output Work to Microrobot Linkage]
    E --> I{Pull-in Condition Check}
    I --> J[Stable Operation x less than d0 by 3]
    I --> K[Instability Snap to Contact x greater than d0 by 3]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture of Three KTU-Mandated Actuator Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parallel Plate Actuator] --> P2[Out of Plane Vertical Motion]
    P2 --> P3[Force depends on gap d squared]
    C1[Comb Drive Actuator] --> C2[In Plane Lateral Motion]
    C2 --> C3[Force independent of displacement]
    S1[Scratch Drive Actuator] --> S2[Stepwise Inchworm Motion]
    S2 --> S3[Uses Bus Electrode and Footplate Deformation]
```

### 4.3 Working Cycle of a Scratch Drive Actuator (SDA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Phase 1 Apply Voltage Footplate Flattens Against Substrate] --> ST2
    ST2[Phase 2 Hold Voltage Move Bus Electrode Forward by Delta x] --> ST3
    ST3[Phase 3 Release Voltage Footplate Recovers and Scratches Forward] --> ST4
    ST4[Phase 4 Repeat Cycle Net Displacement per Cycle is Delta x]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the SDA is exam-relevant:** It enables **unlimited continuous micro-motion** (unlike comb drives which are limited by the finger overlap length) and is the basis of many optical MEMS alignment platforms studied in PERAT746.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an electrostatic actuator. List any three advantages it offers over electromagnetic actuation at the microscale.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** An electrostatic actuator is a device that produces mechanical force/displacement through the attractive Coulomb force between oppositely charged conductors separated by a dielectric, when a voltage is applied between them.

**Three advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Near-zero static power consumption** — no current flows through the capacitor gap under steady-state conditions.
2. **Fast response** — limited only by the electrical RC time constant (microseconds), unlike thermal or magnetic actuators.
3. **Force scales favorably at microscale** — force ∝ Area (L²) while inertial mass ∝ L³, making electrostatic actuation dominant below ~1 mm.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is pull-in instability in a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator? State the pull-in displacement as a fraction of the initial gap.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-in instability (2 marks):** It is the phenomenon in which, beyond a critical applied voltage (the *pull-in voltage V_PI*), the electrostatic attractive force grows faster (∝ 1/d²) than the linear mechanical restoring force (∝ x). The plates **lose stable equilibrium** and snap together uncontrollably. Stable operation is therefore limited to displacements **less than one-third** of the initial gap.

**Pull-in displacement (1 mark):**
$$x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the working principle of a **comb-drive actuator**. Derive an expression for its lateral force and show that the force is **independent of displacement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has plate area **A = 200 × 10⁻⁶ m²**, initial gap **d₀ = 2 μm**, suspension stiffness **k = 1 N/m**, and dielectric is air (ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m). Calculate the **(i)** pull-in voltage and **(ii)** stable operating voltage for a displacement of **0.4 μm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Working principle and force derivation (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction (2 marks):** A comb-drive actuator consists of two interdigitated sets of conductive fingers — one set fixed (stator) and one set movable (rotor), suspended by a folded-flexure spring of stiffness **k**. The fingers form **n** parallel-plate capacitor pairs of overlap length **x**, finger thickness **t**, and finger-to-finger gap **g**.

> **Working (2 marks):** When voltage V is applied, all finger gaps experience the same electric field. The lateral attractive force on each finger pair sums across **n** pairs. Because the overlap area $A = t \cdot x$ is perpendicular to the *lateral* motion, the capacitance gradient is **constant** with respect to displacement.

> **Derivation (3 marks):** Capacitance of one finger pair (with two facing surfaces) per unit displacement:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> C &= \frac{2 \,\varepsilon_0 \, t \, x}{g} \\
> \frac{dC}{dx} &= \frac{2 \,\varepsilon_0 \, t}{g} \quad (\text{constant}) \\
> F_{comb} &= \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{n \,\varepsilon_0 \, t \, V^{2}}{g}
> \end{aligned}$$

> The force has **no x-dependence** — this is the unique advantage of comb drives, enabling **large stable travel ranges**.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

> **Given:**
> $A = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}$, $d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $k = 1\,\text{N/m}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$, $x = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$.

> **(i) Pull-in voltage (4 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> V_{PI} &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \, k \, d_0^{3}}{27 \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{27 \times 1770.8 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{\frac{64 \times 10^{-18}}{47811.6 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{1.339 \times 10^{-3}} \\
>       &\approx 0.0366\,\text{V} \;(\text{as a scalar coefficient})
> \end{aligned}$$
> **Recomputing with correct unit handling:** Substituting purely into $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^{3} / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> V_{PI} &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{\frac{64 \times 10^{-18}}{4.78 \times 10^{-14}}} \\
>       &= \sqrt{1.339 \times 10^{-3}} \\
>       &\approx 36.6\,\text{mV (theoretical, sub-1 V indicates extremely compliant design)}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **(ii) Voltage for x = 0.4 μm (3 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> x &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k d^{2}}, \quad \text{where } d = d_0 - x = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} \\
> V &= \sqrt{\frac{2 k x \, d^{2}}{\varepsilon_0 A}} \\
>   &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times (1.6 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
>   &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.4 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-18}}{1770.8 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
>   &= \sqrt{\frac{2.048}{1770.8}} \\
>   &= \sqrt{1.156 \times 10^{-3}} \\
>   &\approx 34.0\,\text{mV}
> \end{aligned}$$

> Since the operating voltage (34.0 mV) **< pull-in voltage** (36.6 mV), the operation is **stable**. ✔

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a **Scratch Drive Actuator (SDA)** with the help of a phase diagram. List two advantages and one disadvantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **pull-in voltage** expression for a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator. Explain why **stable operation** is limited to **one-third** of the initial gap. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) SDA working (7 marks):**

> **Phase 1 – Charge and Flatten (2 marks):** A voltage pulse is applied between the *bus electrode* and the *substrate*. The SDA's flexible footplate (an elongated plate) is pulled down and **flattens** against the substrate due to electrostatic attraction, increasing the contact area.

> **Phase 2 – Advance (2 marks):** While voltage is held, the entire SDA structure (attached to the bus) translates forward by a small step **Δx** via an external actuator or by the deformation itself. The bus electrode moves, but the footplate remains adhered (electrostatically clamped).

> **Phase 3 – Release and Scratch (2 marks):** Voltage is removed. The footplate's elastic restoring force makes it *spring back* to its curved shape. Because the footplate's tip is now in front of the bus electrode, the recovery motion "**scratches**" the footplate forward by approximately the same **Δx**.

> **Phase 4 – Repeat (1 mark):** The cycle repeats at kHz rates, accumulating unlimited micro-displacement.

**Advantages (included in 7 marks):**
- Unlimited continuous travel range (no finger-overlap limitation).
- Very large force per unit area at small voltages.

**Disadvantage:**
- Stepwise motion — not suitable for smooth analog control; requires pulsed waveforms.

---

**(b) Pull-in derivation — already fully derived in SECTION 3.3 (7 marks):**

> The student is expected to reproduce the canonical derivation:
> 1. State equilibrium $kx = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2(d_0 - x)^2}$ (2 marks).
> 2. Apply stability condition $k = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(d_0 - x)^3}$ at the critical point (2 marks).
> 3. Divide the two to obtain $x_{PI} = d_0/3$ (2 marks).
> 4. Substitute back to obtain $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ (1 mark).

> **Why one-third limit (qualitative, included in marks):** The electrostatic force curve is **convex** (∝ 1/d²) while the spring force curve is **linear**. Two intersections exist only while the linear line is steeper than the tangent to the curve at the second intersection. Geometrically, this tangent point always lies at exactly **d₀/3**, beyond which the curves have no common solution and the system is unstable.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong pull-in coefficient (8/27 vs 8/9):** Most frequent error. Memorize $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^{3} / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the ε₀ in vacuum vs ε_r ε₀ in dielectric:** Always check whether the gap medium is air (ε_r = 1) or a specific dielectric.
> 3. **Confusing force direction:** Electrostatic force is **always attractive** (pulls plates together) regardless of voltage polarity — do not write "repulsive" unless specifying like charges.
> 4. **Using energy derivative incorrectly:** At constant **V**, force = +∂W'/∂x. At constant **Q**, force = −∂W/∂x. Students often mix these.
> 5. **Units of ε₀:** Always **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**, not μF/m.
> 6. **Pull-in vs pull-out:** Pull-in is the *collapse* voltage; pull-out (release) voltage is **lower** due to surface stiction — both are sometimes asked.
> 7. **Comb-drive force unit confusion:** Force is in **Newtons**, but for a single-finger pair, the value may be sub-μN — clearly state the **number of fingers n**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Definition:** Electrostatic actuator = Coulomb-force-driven displacement device.
- ☐ **Operating regime:** Always below pull-in voltage for analog control.
- ☐ **Coulomb's force:** $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^{2} / (2 d^{2})$ — quadratic in V, inverse-square in d.
- ☐ **General force formula:** $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \cdot dC/dx$ — energy method.
- ☐ **Pull-in voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^{3} / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ — must know the **8/27** coefficient.
- ☐ **Pull-in gap ratio:** $x_{PI} / d_0 = 1/3$ — fundamental geometric constant.
- ☐ **Comb drive:** Lateral force $\propto n, t, V^{2}$, **independent of x** — unique advantage.
- ☐ **Scratch drive:** Stepwise inchworm motion — unlimited travel, but pulsed operation.
- ☐ **Scaling law:** Surface forces ∝ L²; volume forces ∝ L³ → electrostatic wins at micro-scale.
- ☐ **Energy efficiency:** Near-zero static current → ideal for battery-powered microrobots.
- ☐ **Limitations:** Small absolute force at very small scales; high voltages (>50 V) often needed; vulnerable to **dielectric breakdown** and **pull-in stiction**.
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** MEMS gyroscopes, accelerometers, DLP mirrors, micro-grippers, optical switches, RF MEMS capacitors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal-based Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal-based Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermal-based Actuator** in microrobotics is a class of microactuator that converts **thermal energy** (manifested as a controlled temperature change $\Delta T$) into **mechanical work** (displacement, force, or strain) through a coupled **thermo-mechanical transduction** mechanism. The thermal energy is most commonly generated via **Joule self-heating** ($\text{I}^2\text{R}$ dissipation) by passing an electric current through a resistive element, although external heaters, laser irradiation, and chemical exothermic reactions are also employed in specialized microrobotic implementations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, thermal actuators are categorized into four principal families:

| Family | Transduction Mechanism | Key Material |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Martensite–Austenite phase transformation | NiTi (Nitinol) |
| **Thermal Bimorph (Bimetal)** | Differential thermal expansion of two bonded layers | Si/SiO₂, Al/Si |
| **Thermal Expansion (Axial)** | Bulk linear thermal expansion | Polymers, waxes, liquids |
| **Thermally Responsive Polymer** | Glass-transition induced modulus change | PDMS, hydrogel composites |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 scope on thermal actuators emphasizes the *coupled multiphysics* (electrical → thermal → mechanical) energy flow and the **constitutive modeling** required to predict micro-scale deflection and force output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Thermostat

Imagine the bimetallic coil inside a vintage **wall thermostat**. Two metals (say, brass and invar) are bonded along their length. When the room gets warm, brass expands more than invar. Because they are welded together and cannot slide, the strip *bends* — physically moving a lever that clicks the air-conditioner ON. Now scale this exact idea down to the size of a human hair, drive it with a tiny resistive current, and you have a **micro thermal bimorph actuator** — a fundamental building block of micro-robotics, micro-fluidics, and MEMS.

The SMA variant works like a *muscle*: train a NiTi wire to "remember" a shape, deform it cold, then heat it (above its activation temperature ~$70$–$110^\circ\text{C}$) and it *snaps back*, lifting a load. This is analogous to a human muscle fiber contracting on a nerve impulse — but here the "impulse" is heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2K^4}$
> - Resistivity of Nitinol: $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega \cdot m}$
> - Austenite finish temperature (typical NiTi): $A_f \approx \mathbf{90^\circ C}$
> - Maximum recoverable strain (SMA): $\varepsilon_L \approx \mathbf{8\%}$ (single crystal), $\mathbf{6\%}$ (polycrystalline)
> - Young's modulus of austenite NiTi: $E_A \approx \mathbf{70\ GPa}$

---

## 1.3 Why Thermal Actuation in Microrobotics?

The motivation is two-fold:

1. **High work-density**: SMA produces work density ($\sim 10^7\ \text{J/m}^3$) comparable to biological muscle and orders of magnitude higher than electrostatic or piezoelectric microactuators. This is critical for untethered, battery-powered microrobots.
2. **Simple fabrication and low-voltage drive**: A two-terminal resistive element is the simplest electrode geometry in microfabrication, eliminating the need for complex comb-finger or stack electrode topologies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off Pitfall:** Thermal actuators are inherently **slow** (bandwidth typically $1$–$100\ \text{Hz}$) due to thermal time constants and **energy-inefficient** (round-trip efficiency often $< 10\%$). The KTU examiner will frequently test whether you can articulate these limitations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coupled Multiphysics Chain

Every thermal actuator is governed by a sequential three-domain conversion:

$$\boxed{\text{Electrical Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Joule Heating}} \text{Thermal Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Thermo-mechanical Coupling}} \text{Mechanical Work}}$$

Step-by-step physics:

1. **Electrical domain**: A current $I$ flows through a resistive element of resistance $R$. The dissipated Joule power is:
$$P_{elec} = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$
2. **Thermal domain**: The temperature rise $\Delta T$ above ambient is governed by the heat balance:
$$P_{elec} = h A_s \Delta T + m c_p \frac{dT}{t} + \sigma \varepsilon A_s (T^4 - T_\infty^4)$$
where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient, $A_s$ the surface area, and $\varepsilon$ the emissivity. At microscale, conduction to the substrate **dominates** over convection/radiation.
3. **Mechanical domain**: The temperature rise produces a strain $\varepsilon_T$ either through phase transformation (SMA) or differential expansion (bimorph), generating force $F = k \delta$ and displacement $\delta$.

## 2.2 SMA Phase Transformation — The Core Concept

Nitinol's behavior is dictated by a **solid-state phase transformation** between two crystal structures:

- **Austenite (A)**: High-temperature, high-symmetry B2 cubic phase — the "parent" or "memory" phase.
- **Martensite (M)**: Low-temperature, monoclinic B19′ phase — easily twinned and deformable.

The four critical transformation temperatures are:

- $M_s$ = Martensite start (on cooling)
- $M_f$ = Martensite finish (on cooling)
- $A_s$ = Austenite start (on heating)
- $A_f$ = Austenite finish (on heating)

The hysteresis is typically $A_f - M_s \approx 20$–$50^\circ\text{C}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day reference** the KTU valuation key expects you to reproduce.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = I^2 R = V^2/R$ | Joule heating power | W |
| 2 | $\tau_{th} = \dfrac{m c_p}{h A_s}$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ | Thermal strain (linear) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | Free thermal expansion | m |
| 5 | $\xi = \dfrac{1 - e^{a_c(T - A_s) + b_c \sigma}}{1 + e^{a_c(T - A_s) + b_c \sigma}}$ | Martensite volume fraction (Tanaka, cooling) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma - \sigma_0 = E(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_0) + \Omega \xi$ | SMA constitutive law | Pa |
| 7 | $\delta_{bimorph} = \dfrac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t (4 + 6n + 4n^2 + \frac{E_1^*}{E_2^*}n^3 + \frac{E_2^*}{E_1^*}n^{-1})}$ | Tip deflection of bilayer | m |
| 8 | $F_{block} = \dfrac{E_1 A_1 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{1 + \frac{E_1 A_1}{E_2 A_2}}$ | Blocked force of bimorph | N |
| 9 | $\sigma_{SMA}^{max} = E_A \cdot \varepsilon_L$ | Maximum SMA recovery stress | Pa |
| 10 | $f_{bw} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_{th}}$ | Approx. usable bandwidth | Hz |

In the bimorph equation, $n = t_1/t_2$ is the thickness ratio, and $E_i^* = E_i / (1 - \nu_i^2)$ is the **plane-strain modulus**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal actuators have conquered three KTU-relevant application domains:

- **Untethered microrobotics**: SMA wires are the *only* actuator technology with high enough work-density to power sub-cm crawling/swimming robots (e.g., MIT's milli-motein, EPFL's RoBeetle).
- **Microfluidics**: Thermopneumatic and phase-change actuators pump fluids in lab-on-chip devices.
- **Optical MEMS**: Micro-mirror tilt and zoom-lens translation are routinely achieved by V-shaped thermal bimorphs fabricated in standard SOI-MUMPs processes.

---

## 2.5 The Tanaka Constitutive Model (Detailed in §3)

The **Tanaka model** (1990) is the canonical SMA constitutive law tested at KTU. It treats the martensite volume fraction $\xi$ as the internal state variable.

For the **forward (heating, A → M reverse)** transformation:

$$\xi = \frac{1 - e^{a_A(T - A_s) + b_A \sigma}}{1 + e^{a_A(T - A_s) + b_A \sigma}}, \quad A_s \le T \le A_f$$

For the **reverse (cooling, A → M forward)** transformation:

$$\xi = \frac{1 - e^{a_M(T - M_s) + b_M \sigma}}{1 + e^{a_M(T - M_s) + b_M \sigma}}, \quad M_f \le T \le M_s$$

The material constants $a_A, a_M$ (K⁻¹) and $b_A, b_M$ (MPa⁻¹) are experimentally fitted from DSC and isobaric tests.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mandate (KTU 2024):** You are expected to show *every* algebraic step. The following derivations are written with full expansion to model the **valuation key answer script**.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tip Deflection of a Thermal Bimorph Cantilever

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a bilayer cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, with layer thicknesses $t_1$ and $t_2$. Material 1 has thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha_1$, Young's modulus $E_1$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu_1$. Material 2 has the corresponding $\alpha_2, E_2, \nu_2$. The beam is stress-free at temperature $T_0$ and subjected to a uniform temperature change $\Delta T = T - T_0$.

The *plane-strain* biaxial moduli are:
$$E_i^* = \frac{E_i}{1 - \nu_i^2}, \quad i = 1, 2$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decompose strain into mechanical + thermal parts.**

In each layer, the total strain $\varepsilon_i$ is the sum of the curvature-induced bending strain $\varepsilon_{b,i}(y)$ and the free thermal strain $\alpha_i \Delta T$:

$$\varepsilon_i(y) = \varepsilon_{b,i}(y) + \alpha_i \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Express the bending strain as a function of distance $y$ from the neutral axis.**

By Euler–Bernoulli kinematics, the bending strain varies linearly with $y$:

$$\varepsilon_{b,i}(y) = \varepsilon_0 + y \kappa$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the mid-plane axial strain and $\kappa$ is the curvature.

**Step 3 — Apply force and moment equilibrium.**

The axial force resultant $N$ in the cross-section must be zero (no external axial load):
$$N = \int_{0}^{t_1} E_1^* (\varepsilon_0 + y \kappa - \alpha_1 \Delta T) w \, dy + \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + t_2} E_2^* (\varepsilon_0 + y \kappa - \alpha_2 \Delta T) w \, dy = 0$$

The bending moment $M$ must be zero as well (no external moment):
$$M = \int_{0}^{t_1} E_1^* (\varepsilon_0 + y \kappa - \alpha_1 \Delta T) y w \, dy + \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + t_2} E_2^* (\varepsilon_0 + y \kappa - \alpha_2 \Delta T) y w \, dy = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 system for $\varepsilon_0$ and $\kappa$.**

Defining the normalized thickness ratio $n = t_1/t_2$ and the total thickness $t = t_1 + t_2$, the (well-known) symbolic solution is:

$$\kappa = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T (1 + n)^2}{t \left[ 4(1+n)^2 + (1 + nE_1^*/E_2^*)(n^{-2} + 2n^{-1}E_1^*/E_2^* + E_1^{*2}/E_2^{*2}) \right]}$$

For equal-thickness layers ($t_1 = t_2$, $n = 1$) and equal plane-strain moduli ($E_1^* = E_2^*$), this simplifies to:

$$\kappa_{eq} = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t \cdot 8} = \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 t}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the tip deflection.**

For small deflections, the cantilever tip displacement is:

$$\delta = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2}$$

Substituting the simplified curvature:

$$\boxed{\delta_{eq} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{8 t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** For a polysilicon/gold bilayer with $L = 500\ \mu m$, $t = 2\ \mu m$, $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = 14\ \text{ppm/K}$, $\Delta T = 100\ \text{K}$:
> $\delta = \dfrac{3 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 14 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100}{8 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.56 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 65.6\ \mu m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SMA Constitutive Equation (Brinson Variant)

### 3.2.1 Tanaka's Starting Point

The Clausius–Duhem inequality for an isothermal, rate-independent SMA yields the rate form:

$$\dot{\sigma} = E \dot{\varepsilon} + \Omega \dot{\xi} + \Theta \dot{T}$$

where $\Omega$ is the **transformation tensor** and $\Theta$ is the thermoelastic tensor.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Integrate the rate form.**

Integrating from reference state $(\sigma_0, \varepsilon_0, T_0, \xi_0)$ to current state $(\sigma, \varepsilon, T, \xi)$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma} d\sigma = E \int_{\varepsilon_0}^{\varepsilon} d\varepsilon + \Omega \int_{\xi_0}^{\xi} d\xi + \Theta \int_{T_0}^{T} dT$$

**Step 2 — Perform each integral.**

$$\sigma - \sigma_0 = E (\varepsilon - \varepsilon_0) + \Omega (\xi - \xi_0) + \Theta (T - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Set reference state to pure austenite.**

In the standard convention, the reference state is taken at $\xi_0 = 0$ (pure austenite, $T > A_f$, $\sigma = 0$). The internal variable $\xi$ is the **detwinned martensite fraction** (Brinson 1993):

$$\xi = \xi_T + \xi_D$$

where $\xi_T$ is the stress-induced (transformation) martensite and $\xi_D$ is the temperature-induced (detwinned) martensite.

**Step 4 — Introduce the maximum transformation strain $\varepsilon_L$.**

The transformation tensor magnitude is $\Omega = -E \varepsilon_L$. This is the **maximum recoverable strain** that an SMA can produce upon full detwinning and reversion.

**Step 5 — Write the final Brinson constitutive equation.**

$$\boxed{\sigma - \sigma_0 = E(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_0) + E \varepsilon_L \xi + \Theta (T - T_0)}$$

The thermoelastic term is usually written $\Theta = E \alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the SMA thermal expansion coefficient (small, typically $\sim 10^{-6}/\text{K}$).

**Step 6 — Linearize for small deviations.**

For an actuator cycled about a fixed state, $\xi$ is held constant and we obtain the elastic relation $\dot\sigma = E\dot\varepsilon + E\alpha \dot T$, confirming that the SMA behaves as a conventional (but pre-strained) elastic material once the transformation is complete.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Time Constant and Bandwidth

### 3.3.1 Lumped-Parameter Heat Balance

For a microcantilever with mass $m$, specific heat $c_p$, surface area $A_s$, convective coefficient $h$, and electrical input $P_{elec}$:

$$m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = P_{elec} - h A_s (T - T_\infty)$$

### 3.3.2 Solve the First-Order ODE

Let $\Delta T = T - T_\infty$ and define the time constant $\tau_{th} = m c_p / (h A_s)$:

$$\tau_{th} \frac{d\Delta T}{dt} + \Delta T = \frac{P_{elec}}{h A_s}$$

The solution for a step input $P_{elec} = P_0$ at $t = 0$ with $\Delta T(0) = 0$ is:

$$\Delta T(t) = \frac{P_0}{h A_s} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

**Steady-state temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T_\infty = \frac{P_0}{h A_s}$$

The thermal bandwidth is conventionally taken as the frequency at which the response amplitude falls to $70.7\%$ of the DC value:

$$f_{bw} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{th}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical reality check:** A $1\ \text{mm}^3$ silicon beam with $h = 200\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ has $\tau_{th} \approx 1.5\ \text{s}$, giving $f_{bw} \approx 0.1\ \text{Hz}$. This is the **fundamental reason** thermal actuators are slow.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | SMA (Nitinol) | Thermal Bimorph (Si/Au) | Thermal Expansion (Wax) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Work density | $10^7\ \text{J/m}^3$ | $10^5\ \text{J/m}^3$ | $10^6\ \text{J/m}^3$ |
| Stroke | $4$–$8\%$ strain | $10$–$50\ \mu m$ | $100\ \mu m$ – $1\ \text{mm}$ |
| Bandwidth | $0.1$–$10\ \text{Hz}$ | $1$–$100\ \text{Hz}$ | $< 0.5\ \text{Hz}$ |
| Voltage drive | $0.5$–$5\ \text{V}$ | $5$–$20\ \text{V}$ | $3$–$12\ \text{V}$ |
| Energy efficiency | $1$–$10\%$ | $< 1\%$ | $< 5\%$ |
| Fabrication | Wire bonding | CMOS-compatible | Polymer casting |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermal Actuator

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete energy flow, signal flow, and feedback pathways of a generic thermal microrobotic actuator. All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of markdown formatting inside labels.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["POWER SOURCE"] --> A2["CURRENT LIMITER"]
    A2 --> A3["PWM DRIVER MODULE"]
    A3 --> B1["RESISTIVE HEATING ELEMENT"]
    B1 --> B2["JOULE HEAT GENERATION NODE"]
    B2 --> C1["THERMAL MASS TEMPERATURE RISE"]
    C1 --> C2["HEAT LOSS TO SUBSTRATE"]
    C1 --> C3["HEAT LOSS BY CONVECTION"]
    C1 --> C4["HEAT LOSS BY RADIATION"]
    C1 --> D1["THERMO MECHANICAL COUPLING ZONE"]
    D1 --> D2["SMA PHASE TRANSFORMATION"]
    D1 --> D3["BIMORPH BENDING ACTION"]
    D1 --> D4["POLYMER SWELLING ACTION"]
    D2 --> E1["RECOVERY STRESS OUTPUT"]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1["MECHANICAL WORK OUTPUT"]
    F1 --> F2["MICROROBOT LINKAGE"]
    F2 --> F3["END EFFECTOR PAYLOAD"]
    C1 --> G1["TEMPERATURE SENSOR"]
    G1 --> A3
```

## 4.2 State Diagram of the SMA Transformation Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AusteniteHi
    AusteniteHi --> Cooling: T decreases past Ms
    Cooling --> TwinnedMartensite: T reaches Mf
    TwinnedMartensite --> Loading: External stress applied
    Loading --> DetwinnedMartensite: Stress exceeds sigma critical
    DetwinnedMartensite --> Heating: Joule heating applied
    Heating --> AusteniteRecovered: T exceeds Af
    AusteniteRecovered --> AusteniteHi: Stress removed
    AusteniteRecovered --> AusteniteHi: Shape memory restored
```

## 4.3 Physical-Schematic Block: U-Shaped V-Beam Thermal Bimorph

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["ANCHOR PAD 1"] --> S2["COLD ARM LENGTH L1"]
    S2 --> S3["V APEX JUNCTION"]
    S3 --> S4["HOT ARM LENGTH L2"]
    S4 --> S5["ANCHOR PAD 2"]
    S2 -.-> T1["HIGH ALPHA LAYER TOP"]
    S2 -.-> T2["LOW ALPHA LAYER BOTTOM"]
    S4 -.-> T1
    S4 -.-> T2
    T1 --> H1["METALLIZATION HEATER"]
    H1 --> P1["APEX DEFLECTION OUT"]
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control Hierarchy for an SMA Microrobot Joint

```mermaid
graph TB
    CMD["DESIRED POSITION CMD"] --> ERR["ERROR COMPUTATION"]
    FB["ENCODER FEEDBACK"] --> ERR
    ERR --> PID["PID CONTROLLER BLOCK"]
    PID --> PWM["PWM SIGNAL GENERATION"]
    PWM --> DRV["H BRIDGE DRIVER"]
    DRV --> SMA["SMA ACTUATOR WIRE"]
    SMA --> JNT["ROBOTIC JOINT OUTPUT"]
    JNT --> FB
    JNT --> LOAD["PAYLOAD MECHANICAL LOAD"]
    TH["THERMAL SENSOR ARRAY"] --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block is an *energy or signal domain transition*. The KTU valuation key rewards students who annotate their diagrams with the governing equation beside each block (e.g., next to the JOULE HEAT GENERATION NODE, write $P = I^2 R$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Distinguish between the four critical transformation temperatures $M_s, M_f, A_s, A_f$ of a shape memory alloy. Why is the hysteresis gap $A_f - M_s$ important in SMA actuator design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- $M_s$ — **martensite start**: temperature on cooling at which the austenite begins transforming to martensite (1 mark).
- $M_f$ — **martensite finish**: temperature on cooling at which transformation is complete (0.5 mark).
- $A_s$ — **austenite start**: temperature on heating at which martensite begins reverting to austenite (0.5 mark).
- $A_f$ — **austenite finish**: temperature on heating at which full parent phase is restored (0.5 mark).
- **Hysteresis importance**: A larger hysteresis gap $A_f - M_s$ means the SMA is more thermally stable and less susceptible to accidental phase switching due to ambient temperature fluctuations; however, it also means *higher* heating power and slower cycle times for the actuator (0.5 mark).

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample]

**Q: What is meant by the "thermal time constant" $\tau_{th}$ of a microrobotic actuator? State its mathematical expression and explain how it limits the actuator bandwidth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The thermal time constant is the time required for the actuator temperature to reach $63.2\%$ (i.e., $1 - e^{-1}$) of its steady-state value after a step electrical input.
- **Expression (1 mark):** $\tau_{th} = m c_p / (h A_s)$, derived from the lumped heat-balance ODE.
- **Bandwidth limitation (1 mark):** The mechanical response cannot be faster than the thermal response, so the usable closed-loop bandwidth is approximately $f_{bw} \approx 1 / (2\pi \tau_{th})$. This is why thermal microrobotic actuators are typically limited to $0.1$–$10\ \text{Hz}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, every Part B question offers an internal choice between two sub-questions of equal cognitive load and mark distribution.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]

**A microcantilever thermal bimorph actuator** is fabricated from a bilayer of polysilicon (top, $t_1 = 2\ \mu m$) and gold (bottom, $t_2 = 1\ \mu m$) on a silicon substrate. The cantilever is $L = 600\ \mu m$ long and $w = 100\ \mu m$ wide. The relevant material properties are:

- Polysilicon: $E_1 = 160\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu_1 = 0.22$, $\alpha_1 = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{K}^{-1}$
- Gold: $E_2 = 78\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu_2 = 0.44$, $\alpha_2 = 14.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{K}^{-1}$

A Joule-heating current is applied, raising the structure uniformly to $\Delta T = 150\ \text{K}$ above the ambient.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the curvature $\kappa$ of the bimorph and compute its tip deflection $\delta$ in micrometres.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** If the convective heat-transfer coefficient between the cantilever and surrounding air is $h = 250\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and the specific heat $c_p = 700\ \text{J/kg·K}$ (silicon-dominant), determine the steady-state electrical power required to maintain $\Delta T = 150\ \text{K}$ and the thermal time constant. Comment on the suitability of this actuator for a $5\ \text{Hz}$ closed-loop microrobot.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Compute plane-strain moduli.**

$$E_1^* = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.22^2} = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{0.9516} = 1.681 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}$$

$$E_2^* = \frac{78 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.44^2} = \frac{78 \times 10^9}{0.8064} = 9.673 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters.**

$n = t_1 / t_2 = 2 / 1 = 2$; $t = t_1 + t_2 = 3\ \mu m = 3 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$; $L = 600\ \mu m = 6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$; $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = -11.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{K}^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the general bimorph curvature formula.**

The full Timoshenko expression (taking the neutral axis at the interface for clarity, layer 1 from $0$ to $t_1$ and layer 2 from $-t_2$ to $0$):

$$\kappa = \frac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T (1 + n)^2}{t \left[ 4(1+n)^2 + (1 + n E_1^*/E_2^*)(n^{-2} + 2n^{-1}E_1^*/E_2^* + E_1^{*2}/E_2^{*2}) \right]}$$

Compute the modulus ratio:

$$m = \frac{E_1^*}{E_2^*} = \frac{1.681 \times 10^{11}}{9.673 \times 10^{10}} = 1.737$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

- Numerator: $6 \times (-11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 150 \times (1 + 2)^2 = -6 \times 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 \times 9 = -0.09396\ \text{K}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}\text{K} \to -9.396 \times 10^{-2}$
- Denominator term A: $4(1+2)^2 = 4 \times 9 = 36$
- Denominator term B: $(1 + 2 \times 1.737)(2^{-2} + 2 \times 2^{-1} \times 1.737 + 1.737^2) = (1 + 3.474)(0.25 + 1.737 + 3.017) = 4.474 \times 5.004 = 22.39$
- Denominator total: $3 \times 10^{-6} \times (36 + 22.39) = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 58.39 = 1.752 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$

$$\kappa = \frac{-0.09396}{1.752 \times 10^{-4}} = -536.5\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

The negative sign indicates bending toward the polysilicon side (which has lower expansion).

**Step 5 — Tip deflection.**

$$\delta = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2} = \frac{536.5 \times (6 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2} = \frac{536.5 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-7}}{2} = 9.66 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 96.6\ \mu m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split (a):** Plane-strain moduli — 1 mark; substitution — 2 marks; curvature evaluation — 2 marks; tip deflection — 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the cantilever mass.**

The volume is dominated by silicon. Taking an effective single-layer approximation: $V \approx L \cdot w \cdot t = 6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{m}^3$. Using silicon density $\rho_{Si} = 2330\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$m = 1.8 \times 10^{-13} \times 2330 = 4.194 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state power.**

The steady-state power balances convective loss to ambient:

$$P_{ss} = h A_s \Delta T = 250 \cdot (2 L w + 2 w t) \cdot 150 \approx 250 \cdot 2 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 150$$

$$P_{ss} = 250 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 150 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = 4.5\ \text{mW}$$

(Using only the top surface for order-of-magnitude; full top + bottom + side area: $A_s \approx 1.26 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2$, giving $P_{ss} \approx 4.7\ \text{mW}$.)

**Step 3 — Thermal time constant.**

$$\tau_{th} = \frac{m c_p}{h A_s} = \frac{4.194 \times 10^{-10} \times 700}{250 \times 1.26 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{2.936 \times 10^{-7}}{3.15 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 9.32 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 9.3\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth and 5 Hz comment.**

$$f_{bw} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{th}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 9.3 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 17.1\ \text{Hz}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The bandwidth ($\approx 17\ \text{Hz}$) is **more than three times the required $5\ \text{Hz}$**, so this actuator *is* suitable for a 5 Hz closed-loop microrobotic joint, though the on-board power budget of 4.5 mW continuous may be problematic for an untethered battery-powered design (a duty-cycled drive would be advisable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split (b):** Mass calculation — 1 mark; steady-state power — 2 marks; time constant — 2 marks; bandwidth and comment — 2 marks.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Re-adapted]

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the **Brinson constitutive equation** for a shape memory alloy starting from the Clausius–Duhem inequality. Clearly state each term, the role of the martensite fraction $\xi$, and the physical interpretation of the transformation constant $\varepsilon_L$.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** A $100\ \mu m$ diameter Nitinol wire ($E_A = 70\ \text{GPa}$, $\varepsilon_L = 0.06$, $\rho = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega \cdot m$) of length $L_w = 50\ \text{mm}$ is to be used as an SMA actuator in a microrobotic gripper. Determine (i) the **maximum recovery force** the wire can produce, (ii) the **Joule heating current** required to achieve an activation temperature of $90^\circ\text{C}$ from a $25^\circ\text{C}$ ambient, assuming $h = 300\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and 100% martensite-to-austenite conversion at the steady state, and (iii) the **time** to reach 63.2% of the steady-state temperature rise.

---

#### Model Solution to (a): *(Outline of the derivation — full algebra was given in §3.2)*

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Clausius–Duhem rate form:
$$\dot\sigma = E \dot\varepsilon + \Omega \dot\xi + \Theta \dot T$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify the physical roles — $E \dot\varepsilon$: elastic lattice strain; $\Omega \dot\xi$: phase-transformation contribution; $\Theta \dot T$: thermal expansion.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Set reference state to pure austenite ($\xi_0 = 0$, $\sigma_0 = 0$, $T_0 > A_f$).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Introduce the transformation tensor magnitude $\Omega = -E \varepsilon_L$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Integrate to obtain:
$$\sigma = E \varepsilon + E \varepsilon_L \xi + E \alpha (T - T_0)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpret $\varepsilon_L$: it is the **maximum residual strain** that can be recovered by heating; it represents the lattice distortion between fully detwinned martensite and parent austenite. For Nitinol, $\varepsilon_L \approx 6\%$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Geometry and cross-section.**

Wire diameter $d = 100\ \mu m = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, so cross-section $A = \pi d^2/4 = \pi \times 10^{-8}/4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$. Lateral surface area $A_s = \pi d L_w = \pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 = 1.571 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$.

**Step 2 (i) Maximum recovery force.**

$$\sigma_{max} = E_A \cdot \varepsilon_L = 70 \times 10^9 \times 0.06 = 4.2 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa}$$

$$F_{max} = \sigma_{max} \cdot A = 4.2 \times 10^9 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-9} = 32.99\ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{max} \approx 33\ \text{N}}$$

This is *remarkably high* for a $100\ \mu m$ wire — illustrating the famous **work density** of SMA.

**Step 3 (ii) Heating current.**

Required temperature rise: $\Delta T = 90 - 25 = 65\ \text{K}$.

Steady-state power: $P_{ss} = h A_s \Delta T = 300 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-5} \times 65 = 0.3063\ \text{W}$.

Wire resistance: $R = \rho L_w / A = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 / 7.854 \times 10^{-9} = 6.366\ \Omega$.

From $P_{ss} = I^2 R$:

$$I = \sqrt{\frac{P_{ss}}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.3063}{6.366}} = \sqrt{0.04812} = 0.219\ \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 219\ \text{mA}}$$

**Step 4 (iii) Thermal time constant.**

Wire mass $m = \rho_{mass} V$. For Nitinol, $\rho_{mass} \approx 6450\ \text{kg/m}^3$; $V = A L_w = 7.854 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.05 = 3.927 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^3$. So $m = 6450 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-10} = 2.533 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg}$. With $c_p \approx 500\ \text{J/kg·K}$:

$$\tau_{th} = \frac{m c_p}{h A_s} = \frac{2.533 \times 10^{-6} \times 500}{300 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.267 \times 10^{-3}}{4.712 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.269\ \text{s}$$

**Time to reach 63.2% of steady-state** (by definition): $t_{63.2\%} = \tau_{th} \approx 0.27\ \text{s}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split (b):** Geometry — 1 mark; maximum force — 2 marks; resistance and current — 2 marks; time constant — 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the plane-strain correction** $E^* = E/(1-\nu^2)$ in bimorph problems — costs up to 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\delta = \kappa L$ instead of $\delta = \kappa L^2/2$** — a 0.5-mark deduction that is recurrent.
> 3. **Mixing up the sign convention** of $\xi$: in the Brinson model, $\xi$ is the *detwinned martensite fraction*, not the total martensite. The contribution to stress is $E \varepsilon_L \xi$ (positive sense for tensile transformation).
> 4. **Forgetting to include all three surface area contributions** (top, bottom, sides) in $\tau_{th}$ — examiners commonly test whether you can recognize when one face is bonded to the substrate (in which case convection from that side is suppressed).
> 5. **Stating "thermal actuators are slow" without numerical justification** — always cite $\tau_{th}$ and $f_{bw}$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before entering the exam hall):**

- ✅ Thermal actuators transduce **Joule heat → temperature rise → mechanical strain/force** through a coupled multiphysics chain.
- ✅ The three dominant thermal-loss mechanisms are **conduction** (to substrate), **convection** (to ambient), and **radiation** (Stefan–Boltzmann). At microscale, conduction typically dominates.
- ✅ The **thermal time constant** $\tau_{th} = m c_p / (h A_s)$ sets the *fundamental bandwidth limit* of the actuator. Bandwidth $f_{bw} \approx 1 / (2\pi \tau_{th})$.
- ✅ The **Timoshenko bimorph equation** gives the curvature $\kappa$ of a bilayer cantilever; the tip deflection is $\delta = \kappa L^2 / 2$.
- ✅ Always compute **plane-strain moduli** $E^* = E / (1-\nu^2)$ before plugging into the bimorph formula.
- ✅ **SMA four temperatures** $M_s, M_f, A_s, A_f$ form a hysteresis loop; the difference $A_f - M_s$ governs thermal stability.
- ✅ The **Brinson constitutive equation** is $\sigma = E \varepsilon + E \varepsilon_L \xi + E \alpha (T - T_0)$; $\xi$ is the detwinned martensite fraction; $\varepsilon_L \approx 6\%$ for Nitinol.
- ✅ **SMA work density** ($\sim 10^7\ \text{J/m}^3$) is the highest of any microactuator class — *the* reason it is preferred for untethered microrobots.
- ✅ The **steady-state Joule power** is $P_{ss} = h A_s \Delta T = I^2 R$.
- ✅ **Trade-off mantra**: High work density ↔ Low bandwidth ↔ Low efficiency. State this explicitly in any design question.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control of SMA** is non-trivial due to hysteresis and slow thermal response; **PID with temperature feedback** is the standard industrial solution.
- ✅ **Numerical values to memorize:** $A_f \approx 90^\circ\text{C}$ (typical NiTi); $\sigma_{max} \approx 4.2\ \text{GPa}$ for $E_A \cdot \varepsilon_L = 70 \times 0.06$; $\rho_{Nitinol} \approx 1.0\ \mu\Omega\cdot m$.
- ✅ **Mention application context** in every long answer: SMA → untethered micro-robot locomotion; bimorph → optical MEMS, micro-fluidic valves; thermopneumatic → lab-on-chip pumps.
- ✅ **Common unit conversion pitfall:** $1\ \text{ppm/K} = 10^{-6}\ \text{K}^{-1}$. Always write expansions in scientific notation to avoid valuation errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Memory Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shape Memory Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs)** are a unique class of smart metallic materials that can recover their original, undeformed shape upon heating above a specific transformation temperature or upon removal of an applied mechanical load. They exhibit two extraordinary solid-state phenomena — the **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** and **Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)** — both rooted in a reversible solid-to-solid phase transformation between a high-temperature parent phase called **Austenite** (B2 cubic, ordered) and a low-temperature product phase called **Martensite** (B19' monoclinic for Nitinol).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT746, Module 3):**
> SMAs are classified as "active smart materials" because they can directly convert thermal energy into mechanical work (or vice-versa) without intermediate transducers — making them ideal for **wireless, miniature, and high-power-density micro-actuators** in microrobotic systems.

The phenomenon is governed by a **diffusionless (military/displacive) martensitic transformation**, where atoms cooperatively shift by distances smaller than an interatomic spacing. No atomic diffusion or chemical change occurs — only a crystallographic re-arrangement.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bent Paperclip" Analogy:**
> Imagine a paperclip made of Nitinol. At room temperature, you can bend it into any arbitrary shape (e.g., the letter "U"). The moment you drop it into **hot water (above ~70 °C)**, it instantly snaps back to its original manufactured shape (a straight clip). The atoms "remember" the original austenite geometry and shear cooperatively to restore it. As it cools back in air, it stays in the new shape — ready to be re-deformed again. This is the **One-Way Shape Memory Effect (OWSME)**.

A more sophisticated analogy is a **human muscle**:
- Heating → muscle **contracts** (actuation stroke)
- Cooling → muscle **relaxes** (reset)
- This cyclic thermal contraction is exactly how SMA micro-actuators generate linear motion in microrobots.

## 1.3 Key Material Properties (Standard Metrics for Nitinol)

- **Density (ρ):** ~**6.45 g/cm³**
- **Young's Modulus (Austenite):** ~**30–83 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus (Martensite):** ~**20–41 GPa**
- **Transformation Strain (recoverable, ε_L):** up to **~8 %** (single crystal) / **~6 %** (polycrystalline wire)
- **Hysteresis Width (Austenite–Martensite):** typically **20–50 °C** (training reduces it)
- **Resistivity:** ~**80–100 μΩ·cm** (austenite), ~**70–100 μΩ·cm** (martensite)
- **Power Density (work output per volume):** up to **~10⁷ J/m³** — orders of magnitude higher than human muscle.
- **Latent Heat of Transformation:** ~**25 J/g**

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Nomenclature:**
> - **A → Austenite (A):** Parent phase, high-symmetry cubic, stable at high T / low σ.
> - **M → Martensite (M):** Product phase, lower-symmetry monoclinic, stable at low T / high σ.
> - **Twinned Martensite (Self-accommodated):** Multiple variants cancel net macroscopic strain.
> - **Detwinned Martensite (Re-oriented):** Variants aligned with applied stress — yields macroscopic strain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Solid-State Phenomena

### 2.1.1 Shape Memory Effect (SME)
The SME is a **thermally-induced** recovery. The cycle is:
1. **Cool** austenite below **M_f** (martensite finish) → twinned martensite forms, no macroscopic shape change.
2. **Apply stress** below M_f → detwinning occurs; martensite variants re-orient, producing large inelastic strain (ε_tr).
3. **Heat** above **A_f** (austenite finish) → complete reverse transformation back to parent austenite, **recovering all the inelastic strain**.

> **Why does it work?** Because the parent austenite has only ONE possible crystal orientation, while martensite has **24 possible variants** (in Nitinol's B2 → B19' transformation). The original macroscopic shape is uniquely encoded in the parent phase.

### 2.1.2 Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)
Above **A_f**, applying a mechanical stress induces martensite **directly** (stress-induced martensite, SIM). Upon unloading, the martensite becomes thermodynamically unstable and reverts to austenite — the strain spontaneously recovers. **No temperature change required.** This produces a flag-shaped stress–strain hysteresis loop and is the principle behind superelastic orthodontic archwires and endovascular stents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clausius-Clapeyron Slope:** The critical stress to induce martensite is a **linear function of temperature**, with slope dσ/dT. This is the key engineering lever — you can "tune" the actuation temperature by simply varying the applied stress (or vice-versa).

## 2.2 The Four Critical Transformation Temperatures

| Symbol | Full Name | Physical Meaning |
|:------:|:----------|:-----------------|
| **M_s** | Martensite Start | Cooling: martensite begins to form |
| **M_f** | Martensite Finish | Cooling: transformation to martensite complete |
| **A_s** | Austenite Start | Heating: austenite begins to form |
| **A_f** | Austenite Finish | Heating: transformation to austenite complete |

A **thermal hysteresis** exists: A_s > M_s (typically by 20–50 °C).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Master the equations below — they appear in nearly every Part-B question on SMAs. Memorize the *meaning* of every symbol, not just the equation.

| # | Equation | Description / Engineering Use |
|:--|:---------|:------------------------------|
| 1 | $H^{curie} = H^{A} + \dfrac{dH}{dT}\,(T - T_{0})$ | Clausius–Clapeyron linearization of phase boundary in σ–T plane |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{\,T\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}\,}$ | Thermodynamic slope of stress-induced transformation line |
| 3 | $\sigma^{M} = \sigma^{M}_{0} + C^{M}\,(T - M_{s})$ | Critical stress for detwinning / martensite reorientation |
| 4 | $\xi = \xi^{T} + \xi^{S}$ | Brinson decomposition: total martensite fraction = thermal part + stress-induced part |
| 5 | $\sigma - \sigma_{0} = E(\xi)\,\bigl(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{0}\bigr) + \Omega(\xi)\,\xi + \Theta\,(T - T_{0})$ | Brinson 1-D constitutive law (master equation) |
| 6 | $E(\xi) = E^{A} + \xi\,(E^{M} - E^{A})$ | Linear rule-of-mixtures elastic modulus |
| 7 | $\Omega(\xi) = -\varepsilon^{L}\,E(\xi)$ | Phase transformation modulus (constant simplification) |
| 8 | $\Theta = E(\xi)\,\alpha$ | Thermoelastic coupling term |
| 9 | $\xi = \dfrac{1-\xi_{A}}{2}\,\cos\!\bigl[a^{A}(T - M_{f} - T_{0})\bigr] + \dfrac{1+\xi_{A}}{2}$ | Cosine-based transformation kinetics (cooling, T < M_s) |
| 10 | $\xi = \dfrac{1-\xi_{S}}{2}\,\cos\!\bigl[a^{S}(\sigma - \sigma^{M}_{s} - C^{M}(T-M_{s}))\bigr] + \dfrac{1+\xi_{S}}{2}$ | Cosine kinetics for stress-induced transformation |
| 11 | $\xi = \dfrac{\xi_{S}}{2}\,\bigl\{\cos\!\bigl[a^{A}(T - A_{s} - \sigma/C^{A})\bigr] + 1\bigr\}$ | Reverse transformation kinetics (unloading/heating) |
| 12 | $W = \sigma\,\varepsilon^{L}\,V$ | Mechanical work output per SMA element of volume V |
| 13 | $f = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{0}\,\Delta T}$ | Thermal actuation strain rate (linear SMA spring/wire) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Escape in Tables:** Where absolute value is needed in the body, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (LaTeX math delimiters) — the raw `|` character breaks markdown table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-robotics:** Sub-millimeter wire actuators, micro-grippers, biomimetic fish/worm locomotion, cell-manipulation tweezers.
- **Medical Devices:** Self-expanding Nitinol stents (SME), orthodontic wires (pseudoelasticity), guidewires, bone staples.
- **Aerospace:** SMA-driven adaptive wing morphing, vibration dampers, deployable space structures.
- **Civil:** Reinforcing concrete tendons that "heal" cracks on heating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clausius–Clapeyron Slope for the SMA Phase Boundary

### Motivation
We want an analytical expression that tells us: *if I increase the applied stress by Δσ, by how much does the equilibrium transformation temperature shift?* This is the foundation of SMA actuator design.

### Setup (Gibbs Free-Energy Balance at Phase Equilibrium)
At the phase boundary, the **chemical Gibbs free energies of austenite and martensite are equal**:

$$
G^{A}(T, \sigma) = G^{M}(T, \sigma)
$$

Consider an infinitesimal movement of the boundary along the σ–T plane:

$$
dG^{A} = dG^{M}
$$

### Step 1 — Apply the total differential of G
For each phase, the general thermodynamic differential is:

$$
dG = -S\,dT + V\,d\sigma
$$

Applied to both phases:

$$
-S^{A}\,dT + V^{A}\,d\sigma = -S^{M}\,dT + V^{M}\,d\sigma
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange terms
Move the entropy terms to the left and volume terms to the right:

$$
(V^{A} - V^{M})\,d\sigma = (S^{A} - S^{M})\,dT
$$

### Step 3 — Define the transformation entropy and volume change
Let $\Delta V = V^{A} - V^{M}$ and $\Delta S = S^{A} - S^{M}$. Therefore:

$$
\Delta V\,d\sigma = \Delta S\,dT
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the slope
Divide both sides by $dT \,\Delta V$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to measurable quantities
By definition, $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$ (latent heat relation at equilibrium). The volumetric strain per unit volume of transformation is $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr} = \Delta V / V_{0}$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta V} = \frac{\Delta H}{T\,V_{0}\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}}
$$

Per unit volume ($V_{0}=1$):

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{d\sigma}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}}\;}
$$

### Interpretation
- **Positive sign:** higher stress raises the transformation temperature (martensite is favoured at high σ).
- **Magnitude order:** For Nitinol, $d\sigma/dT \approx 6$–$8$ MPa/°C — meaning a 10 °C temperature shift corresponds to a 60–80 MPa shift in transformation stress.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Brinson 1-D Constitutive Equation

### Step 1 — Start from generalized thermo-mechanical law
The general 1-D incremental form for an SMA is:

$$
d\sigma = E\,d\varepsilon + \Omega\,d\xi + \Theta\,dT
$$

where $\Omega$ is the **transformation tensor** (energy associated with the phase change) and $\Theta$ the **thermoelastic tensor**.

### Step 2 — Decompose martensite fraction
Following Brinson (1993), the total martensite fraction $\xi$ (range 0 to 1) is split into a thermally-induced part $\xi^{T}$ (twinned/self-accommodated) and a stress-induced part $\xi^{S}$ (detwinned):

$$
\xi = \xi^{T} + \xi^{S}
$$

This split is essential because only the detwinned fraction contributes to macroscopic shape change.

### Step 3 — Integrate under linear-elastic assumption
Integrating the differential form (with constant $E$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$) from the reference state $(0, \varepsilon_{0}, \xi_{0}, T_{0})$:

$$
\sigma - \sigma_{0} = E(\xi)\,\bigl(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{0}\bigr) + \Omega(\xi)\,\bigl(\xi - \xi_{0}\bigr) + \Theta\,(T - T_{0})
$$

### Step 4 — Express the transformation modulus
The recoverable maximum strain is $\varepsilon^{L}$ (the transformation strain at full detwinning). The widely-used simplification is:

$$
\Omega(\xi) = -\varepsilon^{L}\,E(\xi)
$$

The minus sign is required because an increase in martensite fraction produces a *compressive* (recoverable) component in the stress.

### Step 5 — Rule-of-mixtures for elastic modulus

$$
E(\xi) = E^{A} + \xi\,(E^{M} - E^{A})
$$

### Step 6 — Final Master Equation

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma - \sigma_{0} = E(\xi)\,\bigl(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{0}\bigr) - \varepsilon^{L}\,E(\xi)\,\xi + E(\xi)\,\alpha\,(T - T_{0})\;}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Transformation Pathways

| Pathway | Driving Variable | Initial Phase | Final Phase | Governing Cosine Kinetics |
|:--------|:-----------------|:--------------|:------------|:---------------------------|
| **Cooling** (no stress) | $T$ ↓ from M_s to M_f | Austenite (ξ=0) | Twinned Martensite | Eq. 9 in Formula Sheet |
| **Loading** (T < A_f) | $\sigma$ ↑ at constant T | Austenite/Twinned M | Detwinned Martensite | Eq. 10 |
| **Unloading** (T > A_f) | $\sigma$ ↓ at constant T | SIM Martensite | Austenite (recovered) | Eq. 11 (reverse) |
| **Heating** (post-loaded) | $T$ ↑ above A_f | Detwinned Martensite | Austenite (SME recovery) | Eq. 11 |

## 3.4 Numerical Sanity Check (Typical Nitinol Wire)

For a Nitinol wire actuator (length 10 mm, diameter 100 μm):
- $V = \pi r^{2} L = \pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^{2}(10 \times 10^{-3}) = 7.85 \times 10^{-11}$ m³
- $\varepsilon^{L} = 0.06$, $\sigma = 200$ MPa
- $W = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon^{L} \cdot V = 200 \times 10^{6} \times 0.06 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-11} = 9.4 \times 10^{-4}$ J

> Although small in absolute terms, this energy is delivered from a sub-millimeter wire, yielding a power density of $W/V \approx 12$ MJ/m³ — outperforming mammalian muscle by ~40×.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stress–Temperature Phase Diagram & Transformation Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Start Austenite A"] --> A2["Cool below Ms"]
    A2 --> A3["Twinned Martensite MT"]
    A3 --> A4["Apply Stress Sigma"]
    A4 --> A5["Detwinned Martensite MD"]
    A5 --> A6["Heat above Af"]
    A6 --> A1
    A1 --> B1["Apply Stress at T greater than Af"]
    B1 --> B2["Stress Induced Martensite SIM"]
    B2 --> B3["Remove Stress"]
    B3 --> A1
    A3 --> C1["Pseudoelastic Cycle"]
    C1 --> A1
    A1 --> D1["Thermal Hysteresis Width Approx 30 C"]
    D1 --> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The vertical loop (A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A6 → A1) is the **Shape Memory Effect**.
> - The right branch (A1 → B1 → B2 → B3 → A1) is **Pseudoelasticity** at T > A_f.
> - The hysteresis is explicitly tagged as node D1.
