## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of an SMA Micro-Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Power Supply 9V Battery"] --> P2["Drive Circuit PWM Controller"]
    P2 --> P3["Current Pulse Heats SMA Wire"]
    P3 --> P4["Nitinol Wire Contracted by Phase Change"]
    P4 --> P5["Mechanical Linkage Lever"]
    P5 --> P6["Microrobot End Effector Gripper"]
    P6 --> P7["Output Stroke 0 to 4 percent"]
    P3 --> P8["Waste Heat Dissipated to Air"]
    P8 --> P4
    P2 --> P9["Feedback Temperature Sensor"]
    P9 --> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic:**
> 1. Controller delivers a current pulse through the SMA wire.
> 2. Joule heating raises T above A_f → wire contracts (~4 % strain).
> 3. Contraction pulls the lever, opening/closing the gripper.
> 4. Removing the current allows the wire to cool (aided by ambient convection) and lengthen — a passive return spring is sometimes added.
> 5. A thermistor (node P9) closes the feedback loop for precise temperature control.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Brinson Constitutive Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Strain Input Epsilon"] --> Q2["Compute E of Xi via Rule of Mixtures"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Compute Xi via Cosine Kinetics"]
    Q3 --> Q2
    T1["Temperature T"] --> Q3
    T1 --> Q4["Compute Thermal Coupling Theta T minus T0"]
    S1["Reference Sigma0 Epsilon0 Xi0 T0"] --> Q5["Brinson Master Equation"]
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6["Output Stress Sigma"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The Brinson model is the most-asked constitutive model for SMAs. The Mermaid block mirrors the three nested dependencies: (1) ξ depends on T and σ via cosine kinetics, (2) E(ξ) depends on ξ via rule-of-mixtures, and (3) σ depends on ε, ξ, and T via the master equation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PERAT746]
**Differentiate between the Shape Memory Effect (SME) and Pseudoelasticity in SMAs. Mention the role of austenite finish temperature (A_f) in distinguishing the two phenomena.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **SME** is a *thermally-induced* strain recovery. The SMA is deformed in its martensitic state (below M_f) and regains its original shape only when heated **above A_f**, where it transforms back to austenite. (1 mark)
- **Pseudoelasticity** is a *mechanically-induced* strain recovery observed at temperatures **above A_f**. Stress-induced martensite forms upon loading and spontaneously reverts to austenite upon unloading — no temperature change is required. (1 mark)
- **Role of A_f:** A_f is the threshold temperature that separates the two regimes. **T < A_f** → SME regime; **T > A_f** → pseudoelasticity regime. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT746]
**What is the Clausius–Clapeyron relation for SMAs? State its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The Clausius–Clapeyron relation gives the slope of the stress-induced martensite phase boundary in the σ–T plane:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}}
$$

where $\Delta H$ is the latent heat of transformation and $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}$ the transformation strain. (2 marks)

- **Physical significance:** It tells us that the critical stress for martensite formation is a linear function of temperature. Engineers use this to **predict actuation temperature under a given stress** (or design the bias stress for a desired actuation T). Typical slope for Nitinol is ~6–8 MPa/°C. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the four critical transformation temperatures (M_s, M_f, A_s, A_f) and the thermal hysteresis in SMAs. Discuss the stress–strain behaviour at temperatures above and below A_f. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the application of SMAs in micro-robotics as a wire actuator. Include a functional diagram and a discussion of the Brinson constitutive model equations. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron relation for the phase boundary of an SMA, starting from the equality of Gibbs free energies. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the Brinson 1-D constitutive model for SMAs. Write down the master equation, the cosine kinetics for forward and reverse transformation, and the rule-of-mixtures for the effective Young's modulus. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Transformation Temperatures & Stress–Strain Behaviour (7 marks)

**Critical Temperatures (3 marks):**
- **M_s (Martensite Start):** During cooling, martensite begins to nucleate at this temperature.
- **M_f (Martensite Finish):** The transformation to martensite is complete below this temperature.
- **A_s (Austenite Start):** During heating, austenite begins to form.
- **A_f (Austenite Finish):** The transformation back to austenite is complete above this temperature.
- **Thermal Hysteresis:** $\Delta T = A_s - M_s$ (or $A_f - M_f$). For Nitinol, this is typically 20–50 °C, representing the energy dissipated as latent heat and friction during the displacive transformation.

**Stress–Strain Behaviour (4 marks):**
- **Below A_f (SME regime):** The stress–strain curve shows a long, flat "loading plateau" corresponding to detwinning of martensite variants. Upon unloading, the strain is **NOT** recovered (residual inelastic strain remains). Recovery requires **subsequent heating** above A_f.
- **Above A_f (Pseudoelastic regime):** Loading produces an upper plateau (stress-induced martensite). Unloading produces a lower plateau — the strain is **fully and spontaneously recovered**, forming a closed flag-shaped hysteresis loop.

**Neat Sketch Description (textual, since not drawn):**
- X-axis: Strain ε, Y-axis: Stress σ.
- Draw two loops: one open (SME, T < A_f) and one closed (pseudoelastic, T > A_f).
- Indicate detwinning plateau, elastic loading/unloading slopes, residual strain.

#### Part (b) — SMA Wire Actuator in Micro-robotics & Brinson Model (7 marks)

**Application as Micro-Actuator (3 marks):**
- A Nitinol wire (typically 50–200 μm diameter) is connected to a mechanical linkage.
- Resistive (Joule) heating raises the wire temperature above A_f → wire contracts by ~4–6 % (transformation strain).
- The contraction drives a micro-gripper, micro-valve, or biomimetic fin.
- Cooling (passive convection or forced air) returns the wire to its martensitic, elongated state.
- Key advantages: **high power density (~10 MJ/m³), silent operation, no moving parts in the actuator itself, compatible with wireless inductive heating for in-vivo microrobots.**

**Brinson Constitutive Model (4 marks):**

**Master Equation:**

$$
\sigma - \sigma_{0} = E(\xi)\,\bigl(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{0}\bigr) - \varepsilon^{L}\,E(\xi)\,\xi + E(\xi)\,\alpha\,(T - T_{0})
$$

**Rule of Mixtures:**

$$
E(\xi) = E^{A} + \xi\,(E^{M} - E^{A})
$$

**Forward Transformation Kinetics (loading or cooling):**

$$
\xi = \frac{1 - \xi_{0}}{2}\,\cos\!\bigl[a\,(T - T_{0} - M_{f})\bigr] + \frac{1 + \xi_{0}}{2}
$$

**Reverse Transformation Kinetics (unloading or heating):**

$$
\xi = \frac{\xi_{0}}{2}\,\bigl\{\cos\!\bigl[a\,(T - A_{s} - \sigma/C^{A})\bigr] + 1\bigr\}
$$

**Decomposition of Martensite Fraction:**

$$
\xi = \xi^{T} + \xi^{S}
$$

where $\xi^{T}$ is the thermally-induced (twinned) part and $\xi^{S}$ is the stress-induced (detwinned) part.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Clausius–Clapeyron Relation (7 marks)

(Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full 5-step derivation. Key points to include in exam answer:)

1. **Starting point:** $G^{A}(T,\sigma) = G^{M}(T,\sigma)$ at phase equilibrium. (1 mark)
2. **Apply total differential:** $dG = -S\,dT + V\,d\sigma$ to both phases. (1 mark)
3. **Rearrange:** $(V^{A} - V^{M})\,d\sigma = (S^{A} - S^{M})\,dT$. (1 mark)
4. **Substitute** $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$ and $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr} = \Delta V / V_{0}$. (1 mark)
5. **Final result:** $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}}$. (1 mark)
6. **Assumptions stated:** (2 marks)
   - Phase equilibrium at the boundary (negligible interface kinetic energy).
   - Constant $\Delta H$ and $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}$ over the T-range of interest.
   - Hydrostatic pressure neglected; uniaxial stress state assumed.
   - Reversible transformation (no plastic deformation or damage accumulation).

#### Part (b) — Brinson Constitutive Model (7 marks)

(Refer to **Section 3.2** above.)

**Key points to write:**

1. **Why a constitutive model is needed:** A constitutive law links σ, ε, T, and ξ. (1 mark)
2. **Master Equation** (write in full as in Section 3.2, Step 6). (2 marks)
3. **Cosine kinetics for forward** (cooling) and **reverse** (heating) transformations (Eqs. 9 and 11 in Formula Sheet). (2 marks)
4. **Rule-of-mixtures for E(ξ)** and the **decomposition** $\xi = \xi^{T} + \xi^{S}$. (1 mark)
5. **Physical meaning:** $\varepsilon^{L}$ is the maximum recoverable transformation strain (~6 % for Nitinol). $\Omega$ is the phase-transformation modulus. $\Theta$ couples thermal expansion. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** of $\Delta H$ (J/kg or J/mol — be consistent!), $T$ (K, not °C!), and $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}$ (dimensionless). Mixing °C and K in Clausius–Clapeyron is a guaranteed ½–1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing M_s and A_f:** A frequent error. M_s relates to martensite formation (cooling), A_f to austenite completion (heating). The mnemonic is the *letter* in the subscript matches the phase *ending*.
> 3. **Omitting the sign convention** in the Brinson model. Many students lose 1 mark by not stating whether tensile stress is positive.
> 4. **Skipping the decomposition** $\xi = \xi^{T} + \xi^{S}$ in the Brinson model. The full 7-mark answer requires it.
> 5. **Not drawing or describing the hysteresis loop** in the σ–ε diagram for part (a) of Question A. Even a verbal description of plateau slopes and residual strain earns full marks.
> 6. **Naming the SMA:** Always specify "Nitinol (Ni–Ti, ~50:50 at.%)" — the examiner expects the canonical material name, not just "shape memory alloy."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SMA = Active smart material** that converts **thermal ↔ mechanical** energy via a reversible **martensitic transformation**.
- ✅ **Two phases:** **Austenite** (high-T parent, B2 cubic) ↔ **Martensite** (low-T, B19' monoclinic, 24 variants).
- ✅ **Four temperatures:** $M_s > M_f$ on cooling; $A_s < A_f$ on heating; $A_s > M_s$ → hysteresis.
- ✅ **Two phenomena:** **SME** (T < A_f, thermal recovery) and **Pseudoelasticity** (T > A_f, mechanical recovery).
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron slope:** $d\sigma/dT = \Delta H / (T\,\Delta \varepsilon^{tr})$; Nitinol ≈ 6–8 MPa/°C.
- ✅ **Brinson master equation:** $\sigma - \sigma_{0} = E(\xi)(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{0}) - \varepsilon^{L} E(\xi) \xi + E(\xi)\alpha(T - T_{0})$.
- ✅ **Cosine kinetics** describe ξ evolution in cooling, loading, unloading, and heating.
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures:** $E(\xi) = E^{A} + \xi(E^{M} - E^{A})$.
- ✅ **Maximum recoverable strain** $\varepsilon^{L} \approx 6$–$8$ %.
- ✅ **Power density ~10 MJ/m³** — 40× human muscle.
- ✅ **Applications in micro-robotics:** micro-grippers, micro-valves, biomimetic fins, stent-based capsule endoscopes, cell manipulation tweezers, deployable space structures.
- ✅ **Use $\lvert x \rvert$ not $\vert x \vert$ (raw pipe)** in table cells to avoid markdown breakage.
- ✅ **Units in Clausius–Clapeyron:** T in **Kelvin**, $\Delta H$ in J/m³ (or J/kg with consistent $\rho$), $\Delta \varepsilon^{tr}$ dimensionless.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for hysteresis:** "*Austenite Ascends, Martensite Manifests* on cooling."
- ✅ **Pseudoelasticity ≠ Plasticity** — pseudoelastic strain is *fully recoverable*; plastic strain is *not*.
- ✅ **Bias spring** is often used in SMA actuators to provide the return (reset) stroke upon cooling.
- ✅ **One-Way SME (OWSME):** Material remembers only the *high-T* shape. **Two-Way SME (TWSME):** Material remembers *both* shapes but requires thermomechanical training and shows reduced stroke.
- ✅ **Common SMA materials:** Nitinol (Ni-Ti), Cu-Zn-Al, Cu-Al-Ni, Fe-Mn-Si. Nitinol dominates microrobotics due to biocompatibility and superior fatigue life.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Piezoelectric Actuators — Module 3 | Microrobotics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **piezoelectric actuator** is a solid-state transduction device that converts an applied electrical potential (voltage) into a precisely controlled mechanical displacement (strain/force) by exploiting the **converse piezoelectric effect** in non-centrosymmetric, anisotropic crystalline materials. In the context of KTU's *Microrobotics* syllabus, piezoelectric actuators form the primary class of **active micro-positioning elements** used in sub-millimeter robotic joints, micro-grippers, ultrasonic motors, and walking micro-locomotion platforms because they offer sub-nanometer resolution, high force density, and a bandwidth that extends into the megahertz range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize**
> *"A piezoelectric actuator is a reversible electromechanical transducer in which the mechanical strain $S$ produced in the material is a linear function of the applied electric field $E$, with the proportionality constant being the piezoelectric charge coefficient $d$ (in C/N or m/V)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Finger"

Imagine you are holding a small wooden block between your fingers and you want it to move by just 1/1000th of a millimeter — but your fingers are *too clumsy* to do it. Now imagine sprinkling a magical powder inside the block that **stretches microscopically the instant you apply a voltage** (like turning a tiny knob), and **squeezes back** the instant you reverse the polarity. That "magical powder" is the **piezoelectric crystal** (typically a Lead Zirconate Titanate, PZT, ceramic). 

The block is the **actuator**; the knob is the **voltage supply**; the movement is the **stroke**. The result: a noiseless, vibration-free, vacuum-compatible "invisible finger" that is ideal for the micro-world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples you already know**
> - The **spark igniter** in a gas lighter (direct piezoelectric effect — pressure → spark).
> - The **buzzer** in a digital watch or smoke alarm (converse effect — voltage → sound/vibration).
> - The **auto-focus mechanism** in a smartphone camera lens.
> - **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)** scanners — resolution of ~0.01 nm.

### 1.3 Historical and Physical Constants

- **Discovered by:** Jacques and Pierre **Curie**, **1880** (direct effect); the converse effect was theoretically predicted by **Gabriel Lippmann (1881)** and confirmed by the Curies the same year.
- **Stability constant:** The Curie temperature $T_C$ above which the piezoelectric effect vanishes. For PZT-5H: $T_C \approx \mathbf{195^\circ C}$.
- **Standard piezoelectric coefficients** (for PZT-5H):
  - $d_{33} = 593 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m/V} \; (\mathbf{593 \; pm/V})$
  - $d_{31} = -274 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m/V}$
  - $d_{15} = 741 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m/V}$
- **Dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{33}^T / \varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{3400}$ (relative permittivity).
- **Young's modulus** $Y_{33}^E \approx \mathbf{56 \times 10^9 \; N/m^2}$ (for PZT-5H).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why piezoelectric actuators are central to Microrobotics**
> They are the only actuation technology that simultaneously satisfies the *KTU trinity* of micro-actuation requirements: **(i) small size (mm-scale), (ii) high force density (tens of MPa blocking stress), and (iii) precise closed-loop control (nm resolution).** Electrostatic actuators lack force; magnetic actuators cannot scale down due to coil resistance; thermal actuators are slow. Piezoelectrics win.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Coupled Phenomena

### 2.1.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect
When a mechanical stress $T$ is applied to a piezoelectric material, an **electric displacement** $D$ (and hence surface charge $Q$) is generated on the electrodes. This is the principle of piezoelectric *sensors* and *energy harvesters*.

### 2.1.2 Converse Piezoelectric Effect
When an electric field $E$ is applied across the electrodes, the material undergoes a **mechanical strain** $S$. This is the principle of piezoelectric *actuators* — and the foundation of this entire module.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction**
> The *direct* effect → Sensing / Energy Harvesting
> The *converse* effect → **Actuation** (the focus of this topic)
> The two effects are thermodynamically conjugate, ensuring **energy reversibility**.

## 2.2 The Constitutive Equations — Linearized Piezoelectricity

The full coupling between the mechanical variables $\{S, T\}$ (strain, stress) and the electrical variables $\{E, D\}$ (electric field, electric displacement) is described by the **IEEE Standard constitutive relations**. The two most commonly used forms in the KTU syllabus are:

**Form 1 — Strain-Charge (independent variables: stress $T$ and field $E$)**

$$S_i = s_{ij}^E \, T_j + d_{in} \, E_n$$

$$D_m = d_{mj} \, T_j + \varepsilon_{mn}^T \, E_n$$

**Form 2 — Stress-Charge (independent variables: strain $S$ and field $E$)**

$$T_i = c_{ij}^E \, S_j - e_{in} \, E_n$$

$$D_m = e_{mj} \, S_j + \varepsilon_{mn}^S \, E_n$$

where:
- $S_i$ → mechanical strain (m/m)
- $T_j$ → mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $D_m$ → electric displacement (C/m²)
- $E_n$ → electric field (V/m)
- $s_{ij}^E$ → elastic compliance at constant $E$ (m²/N)
- $c_{ij}^E$ → elastic stiffness at constant $E$ (N/m²)
- $d_{in}$ → **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (m/V or C/N)
- $e_{in}$ → **piezoelectric stress coefficient** (N/V·m or C/m²)
- $\varepsilon_{mn}^T$ → permittivity at constant stress (F/m)
- $\varepsilon_{mn}^S$ → permittivity at constant strain (F/m)
- Indices $i, j \in \{1,2,3\}$ (Voigt notation), $m, n \in \{1,2,3\}$.

## 2.3 Key Piezoelectric Coefficients in Actuator Design

In KTU 2024 problems, three specific $d$ coefficients are essential:

| Coefficient | Description | Typical Sign (PZT) | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d_{33}$ | Field along 3-axis → strain along 3-axis | **+593 pm/V** | **Stack actuators** (most common in microrobotics) |
| $d_{31}$ | Field along 3-axis → strain along 1-axis | **−274 pm/V** | **Bimorph benders** (micro-grippers, legs) |
| $d_{15}$ | Shear coupling | **+741 pm/V** | **Shear-mode** actuators, ultrasonic motors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning of the sign:** $d_{31} < 0$ means that when you *expand* the material in the poling direction (3), it *contracts* in the transverse direction (1). This is exactly why a **bimorph** bends when voltage is applied.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L = d_{33} \, V \, n$ | Free displacement of a **$n$-layer stack** actuator | m |
| 2 | $F_{block} = \dfrac{d_{33} \, V \, A}{s_{33}^E \, L} = c_{33}^E \, d_{33} \, V \, \dfrac{A}{L}$ | **Blocked force** (max force at zero displacement) | N |
| 3 | $F_{block} = \dfrac{d_{33} \, V \, A}{s_{33}^E \, L} = n \, d_{33} \, c_{33}^E \, \dfrac{A}{L} \, V$ | Blocked force of $n$-layer stack (note $V$ is per-layer) | N |
| 4 | $S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3$ | Longitudinal strain in stack | m/m |
| 5 | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{2} \, d_{33}^2 \, c_{33}^E$ | **Coupling factor squared** (energy conversion efficiency indicator) | — |
| 6 | $\rho \, \ddot{u} = c_{33}^E \, \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ | **1-D wave equation** inside the piezo rod (ultrasonic motors) | — |
| 7 | $f_0 = \dfrac{v}{2L} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{c_{33}^E}{\rho}}$ | **Resonant frequency** (longitudinal mode) | Hz |
| 8 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{33}^T \, A}{L}$ | Capacitance of a single layer (drives current draw) | F |
| 9 | $\Delta L_{bimorph} = \dfrac{3}{2} \, d_{31} \, V \, \dfrac{L^2}{t^2}$ | **Tip deflection** of a cantilever bimorph (micro-gripper) | m |
| 10 | $Q_m = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | **Mechanical quality factor** (bandwidth) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **For Part B (14-mark) problems**, you must be able to derive Formulas **#1, #2, and #9** from the constitutive equations. Memorising the constants without the derivation costs marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-positioning stages** — sub-nm resolution in AFM, STM, photolithography stepper stages.
- **Walking micro-robots** — piezoelectric legs produce stick-slip motion at 1–1000 Hz.
- **Inchworm actuators** — clamp-push-clamp-unclamp sequencing for mm–cm travel.
- **Ultrasonic motors** — travelling-wave rotary motors (used in camera autofocus).
- **Micro-pumps and micro-valves** — for microfluidic lab-on-chip.
- **Active vibration damping** — piezo stacks mounted on microrobot chassis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Free Displacement of a Piezoelectric Stack (Formula #1)

We consider a **multilayer piezoelectric stack** consisting of $n$ ceramic layers electrically connected in parallel, each of thickness $t = L/n$ (where $L$ is the total stack length) and cross-sectional area $A$. The poling direction (3-axis) is along the stack length.

**Step 1 — Write the basic strain equation.**

From the converse piezoelectric effect, the strain in each layer is:

$$S_3 = s_{33}^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mechanical free condition (no external load).**

A "free" actuator exerts zero force on its surroundings, which means the net stress $T_3$ on the stack is zero. So:

$$T_3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_3 = 0$ into the strain equation.**

$$S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3$$

**Step 4 — Express the electric field $E_3$ in terms of applied voltage $V$.**

The electric field is the voltage drop across *one* layer divided by the thickness of that layer:

$$E_3 = \frac{V_{\text{per layer}}}{t} = \frac{V}{L/n} = \frac{nV}{L}$$

> Note: the layers are wired in **parallel** (a common low-voltage drive scheme), so each layer sees the *full* applied voltage $V$. The field per layer is therefore $E_3 = V/t$.

**Step 5 — Compute the displacement of one layer.**

The change in length of a single layer is the strain times its length:

$$\Delta L_{\text{single}} = S_3 \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot E_3 \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{t}\right) \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Step 6 — Sum over $n$ layers (all layers expand in the same direction).**

$$\Delta L_{\text{stack}} = n \cdot \Delta L_{\text{single}} = n \, d_{33} \, V$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.**

This is why multilayer stacks are so attractive: by increasing $n$ (e.g., 100–500 layers in a single 10 mm stack), you achieve tens of micrometres of stroke at a *low drive voltage* (typically 100–200 V), since the field $V/t$ per layer is small even if $V$ is modest.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Blocked Force of a Piezoelectric Stack (Formula #2)

**Step 1 — Re-start with the same constitutive equation.**

$$S_3 = s_{33}^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mechanical blocked condition (zero displacement).**

"Blocked" means the actuator cannot move, so:

$$S_3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $S_3 = 0$.**

$$0 = s_{33}^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the stress $T_3$ generated by the actuator.**

$$T_3 = -\frac{d_{33} \, E_3}{s_{33}^E} = -\frac{d_{33} \, V}{s_{33}^E \, t}$$

The negative sign indicates compressive stress (the actuator is being held back from expanding).

**Step 5 — Compute the blocked force using $F = T \cdot A$.**

$$F_{block} = T_3 \cdot A = \frac{d_{33} \, V \, A}{s_{33}^E \, t}$$

Using the compliance–stiffness relationship $c_{33}^E = 1/s_{33}^E$ and $t = L/n$:

$$\boxed{F_{block} = n \, d_{33} \, c_{33}^E \, V \, \frac{A}{L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that the blocked force is **independent of the number of layers** if the drive voltage is per layer, but it **scales with $n$** if the total stack voltage $V_{total} = nV$ is fixed. This is a classic KTU 2-mark conceptual trap.

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check for PZT-5H.**

Take $n=100$ layers, $V=100$ V per layer, $A = 5\times 10^{-6}$ m² ($5 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ mm}$), $L=10 \text{ mm}$, $d_{33}=593\times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $c_{33}^E = 56\times 10^{9}$ N/m².

$$F_{block} = 100 \cdot (593\times 10^{-12}) \cdot (56\times 10^{9}) \cdot 100 \cdot \frac{5\times 10^{-6}}{0.01}$$

$$F_{block} = 100 \cdot 593 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 56 \times 10^{9} \cdot 100 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{block} \approx 166 \, \text{N}$$

So a tiny 10 mm stack can push **166 N** — the famous high force density of piezo actuators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Bimorph Tip Deflection (Formula #9)

A **bimorph** is two piezoelectric layers bonded together (opposite poling). It behaves like a bimetallic strip. Consider a cantilever of length $L$ and total thickness $2t$ (each layer = $t$).

**Step 1 — One layer's free strain (transverse $d_{31}$ coupling).**

If field $E_3 = V/(2t)$ is applied, layer 1 expands longitudinally and layer 2 contracts (because of the $180^\circ$ poling flip). The differential strain is:

$$\Delta S = 2 d_{31} \, E_3 = 2 d_{31} \cdot \frac{V}{2t} = \frac{d_{31} V}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Use the bending beam equation for curvature $\kappa$.**

For a bimorph of thickness $2t$ with one layer in tension and one in compression, the radius of curvature $R$ is given by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{3 \, \Delta S}{2t} = \frac{3 d_{31} V}{2t^2}$$

**Step 3 — Tip deflection of a cantilever beam (small angle).**

$$\delta = \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{L^2}{2} \cdot \frac{3 d_{31} V}{2t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{3}{2} \, d_{31} \, V \, \frac{L^2}{t^2}}$$

This is the standard formula used in **KTU problems on micro-gripper fingers** and **piezoelectric legs of walking microrobots**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Coupling Factor (Formula #5)

The **electromechanical coupling factor** $k$ measures how efficiently electrical energy is converted to mechanical energy.

**Step 1 — Strain energy density (mechanical side).**

$$u_{\text{mech}} = \frac{1}{2} s_{33}^E \, T_3^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{d_{33}^2 E_3^2}{s_{33}^E}$$

(using the blocked force result $T_3 = d_{33} E_3 / s_{33}^E$).

**Step 2 — Electrical energy density input.**

$$u_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{33}^T \, E_3^2$$

**Step 3 — Define $k^2$ as the ratio of coupled energy to geometric mean.**

$$k_{33}^2 = \frac{u_{\text{mech}}}{u_{\text{elec}}} = \frac{d_{33}^2}{s_{33}^E \, \varepsilon_{33}^T} = d_{33}^2 \, c_{33}^E \, \beta_{33}^T$$

where $\beta_{33}^T = 1/\varepsilon_{33}^T$ is the impermittivity.

> For PZT-5H, $k_{33} \approx \mathbf{0.75}$, i.e., 75% efficiency — extremely high compared to other transducer families.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Actuator Type for a Microrobot Task

| Task | Best Piezo Configuration | Key Coefficient | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear micro-positioning (1–100 µm) | Multilayer **stack** | $d_{33}$ | Maximises linear stroke per volt |
| Bending finger / micro-gripper | **Bimorph** / **Unimorph** | $d_{31}$ | Large tip deflection from small input |
| Rotary motion (≤ 60 rpm) | **Travelling-wave ultrasonic motor** | $d_{15}$ (shear) | No bearings, high torque at low speed |
| Walking microrobot leg | **Stack + foot with stick-slip** | $d_{33}$ | High force per cycle, easy to control |
| Peristaltic micro-pump | **Bimorph diaphragm** | $d_{31}$ | Large area deflection at low voltage |
| Vibration energy harvesting | **Cantilever bimorph** | $d_{33}$ or $d_{31}$ | Matches ambient frequency via $f_0$ tuning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Piezo-Actuated Microrobot Joint

> Because the physical geometry of a piezo stack is not Mermaid-friendly, the following **block-level functional architecture** captures all signal flow, control loops, and mechanical coupling. This is the recommended diagram for KTU 2024 answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcDC["HVDC Supply 0 to 200V"] --> amp["Power Amplifier"]
    ctrl["DSP Microcontroller"] -->|"PWM Reference"| amp
    amp -->|"Drive Voltage V"| stack["Piezo Stack Actuator d33 Mode"]
    stack -->|"Stroke deltaL"| mech["Mechanical Joint Coupling"]
    mech --> tip["End Effector Micro Tool"]
    stack -->|"Capacitive Sense"| fb["Charge Amplifier"]
    tip -->|"Capacitive Position Sensor"| fb
    fb -->|"Feedback Signal"| ctrl
    stack -->|"Residual Hysteresis"| hyst["Charge Drive to Linearise"]
    hyst --> amp
    mech -->|"Reaction Force"| stack
    classDef elec fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    class srcDC,amp,fb,ctrl,hyst elec
    class stack,mech,tip mech
```

**How to read this diagram (for the exam):**
1. The **HVDC supply** provides a clean high-voltage rail.
2. The **DSP controller** generates a low-voltage PWM reference.
3. The **power amplifier** boosts the reference to the 0–200 V piezo drive.
4. The **piezo stack** converts voltage to nanometre-level stroke.
5. **Capacitive position sensing** on the output side provides closed-loop feedback (essential because piezo has ~10–15% hysteresis).
6. A **charge drive** is preferred over voltage drive to suppress hysteresis — this is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 4.2 Working-Principle Flowchart — Converse Piezoelectric Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p0["Apply AC or DC Voltage V to Electrodes"] --> p1["Electric Field E3 Builds Inside Crystal"]
    p1 --> p2["Aligned Dipoles Reorient Polarization"]
    p2 --> p3["Crystal Lattice Stretches or Contracts via d33 or d31"]
    p3 --> p4["Mechanical Stroke DeltaL Produced"]
    p4 --> p5{"Application of Stroke"}
    p5 --> p5a["Micro Gripper Closure"]
    p5 --> p5b["Walking Leg Push"]
    p5 --> p5c["Micro Pump Diaphragm"]
    p5 --> p5d["AFM Tip Scan"]
```

---

## 4.3 Constitutive Coupling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["Electric Field E"] -->|"d33"| S["Mechanical Strain S"]
    T["Mechanical Stress T"] -->|"d33"| D["Electric Displacement D"]
    S -->|"c33E Stiffness"| F["Internal Force F"]
    D -->|"Capacitance"| V["Terminal Voltage V"]
    S <--> T
    E <--> D
```

This four-quadrant diagram is the **canonical textbook illustration** of linear piezoelectric coupling — draw a similar block diagram in your KTU 14-mark answer to earn the "diagram marks".

---

## 4.4 Typical Piezo-Actuator Geometries (Schematic Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Piezoelectric Actuator Families"]
    A --> A1["Multilayer Stack - High Force d33"]
    A --> A2["Bimorph Cantilever - High Stroke d31"]
    A --> A3["Tube Actuator - Radial d31"]
    A --> A4["Shear Plate Actuator - Rotary d15"]
    A1 --> U1["Micro Positioner"]
    A1 --> U2["Inchworm Walker"]
    A2 --> U3["Micro Gripper"]
    A2 --> U4["Walking Microrobot Leg"]
    A3 --> U5["Micro Valve"]
    A4 --> U6["Ultrasonic Rotary Motor"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the converse piezoelectric effect. State the constitutive equation relating electric field to mechanical strain in a piezoelectric material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **converse piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon in which a mechanical strain $S$ is induced in a non-centrosymmetric crystal when an external electric field $E$ is applied across its electrodes. The phenomenon is the reciprocal of the direct piezoelectric effect discovered by the Curie brothers and is the operating principle of all piezoelectric actuators.
>
> The linear constitutive relation is:
>
> $$S_i = s_{ij}^E \, T_j + d_{in} \, E_n$$
>
> For a free actuator ($T=0$) along the poling direction, this reduces to the compact form:
>
> $$S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3$$
>
> where $d_{33}$ is the longitudinal piezoelectric charge coefficient (in m/V or C/N) and $E_3$ is the applied field (in V/m). The strain is directly proportional to the field.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33}$ and $d_{31}$. Which is preferred for a multilayer stack actuator and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | $d_{33}$ | $d_{31}$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | Field direction | Poling axis (3) | Poling axis (3) |
> | Strain direction | Same as field (3) | Transverse (1) |
> | Sign in PZT | **+** (expansion along poling) | **−** (contraction transverse to poling) |
> | Typical magnitude (PZT-5H) | **593 pm/V** | 274 pm/V (magnitude) |
> | Best suited for | **Multilayer stack actuators** | **Bimorph benders** |
>
> $d_{33}$ is preferred for stack actuators because the stack geometry aligns the field and the desired stroke along the same axis, giving **direct axial expansion** of magnitude $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$, without the geometric amplification (and buckling risk) of a $d_{31}$ bending design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B carries 14 marks. Two alternative full questions are provided.

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **free displacement** and **blocked force** of a multilayer piezoelectric stack actuator consisting of $n$ layers, each of thickness $t$, cross-sectional area $A$, with all layers electrically in parallel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **PZT-5H multilayer stack** has the following specifications:
- Number of layers: $n = 200$
- Layer thickness: $t = 50 \; \mu\text{m}$
- Cross-sectional area: $A = 7 \text{ mm} \times 7 \text{ mm} = 49 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2$
- Drive voltage (per layer): $V = 150 \; \text{V}$
- Piezoelectric coefficient: $d_{33} = 593 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m/V}$
- Young's modulus at constant $E$: $Y_{33}^E = 56 \times 10^9 \; \text{N/m}^2$

Calculate:
(i) The free displacement $\Delta L$ in micrometres. *(2 marks)*
(ii) The blocked force $F_{block}$ in Newtons. *(2 marks)*
(iii) The resonant frequency $f_0$ of longitudinal vibration. Take $\rho = 7500 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ for PZT-5H. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) — Derivation of Free Displacement and Blocked Force**

Start with the converse piezoelectric equation in the 3-direction:

$$S_3 = s_{33}^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Free Displacement (zero stress $T_3 = 0$):**

Putting $T_3 = 0$ in (i):

$$S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3 = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$

The displacement of one layer = $S_3 \times t = d_{33} V$.

For $n$ layers stacked in series mechanically (all expanding in the same direction):

$$\boxed{\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V}$$

**Blocked Force (zero strain $S_3 = 0$):**

Putting $S_3 = 0$ in (i):

$$0 = s_{33}^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3 \implies T_3 = -\frac{d_{33} \, E_3}{s_{33}^E} = -\frac{d_{33} V}{s_{33}^E t}$$

Taking magnitude and multiplying by area $A$:

$$F_{block} = \frac{d_{33} \, V \, A}{s_{33}^E \, t}$$

Using $c_{33}^E = 1/s_{33}^E$ and total length $L = nt$:

$$\boxed{F_{block} = n \, d_{33} \, c_{33}^E \, V \, \frac{A}{L}}$$

---

**(b) — Numerical Computations**

**(i) Free Displacement**

$$\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V = 200 \times (593 \times 10^{-12}) \times 150$$

$$\Delta L = 200 \times 593 \times 150 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\Delta L = 1.779 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = \mathbf{17.79 \; \mu m}$$

**(ii) Blocked Force**

$$F_{block} = n \, d_{33} \, c_{33}^E \, V \, \frac{A}{L}$$

where $L = nt = 200 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01 \; \text{m}$.

$$F_{block} = 200 \times (593 \times 10^{-12}) \times (56 \times 10^9) \times 150 \times \frac{49 \times 10^{-6}}{0.01}$$

Calculate step by step:

- $200 \times 593 \times 10^{-12} = 1.186 \times 10^{-7}$
- $\times \, 56 \times 10^9 = 6641.6$
- $\times \, 150 = 9.9624 \times 10^{5}$
- $\times \, 49 \times 10^{-6} / 0.01 = \times 4.9 \times 10^{-3}$

$$F_{block} = 9.9624 \times 10^5 \times 4.9 \times 10^{-3} \approx \mathbf{4881.6 \; N}$$

(About **488 N of blocking force** from a 10 mm stack — illustrate the high force density.)

**(iii) Resonant Frequency**

The speed of sound in the stack (longitudinal wave):

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{c_{33}^E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{56 \times 10^9}{7500}} = \sqrt{7.467 \times 10^6} \approx 2733 \; \text{m/s}$$

The half-wavelength longitudinal mode resonant frequency for a rod of length $L = 0.01 \; \text{m}$:

$$f_0 = \frac{v}{2L} = \frac{2733}{2 \times 0.01} = \mathbf{136.6 \; kHz}$$

> This high frequency is exploited in **ultrasonic motors** used in microrobotics.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **working of a piezoelectric bimorph actuator** and derive the expression for the **tip deflection** of a cantilever bimorph under applied voltage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **piezoelectric bimorph** is used as the finger of a micro-gripper. The bimorph is a cantilever of length $L = 20 \; \text{mm}$, each layer thickness $t = 0.3 \; \text{mm}$, and the material is PZT-5H ($d_{31} = -274 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m/V}$). If the applied voltage is $V = 100 \; \text{V}$, calculate:
(i) The tip deflection $\delta$ in micrometres. *(2 marks)*
(ii) The blocked force at the tip in milli-Newtons. Take the width $w = 5 \; \text{mm}$ and $Y = 56 \; \text{GPa}$. *(3 marks)*
(iii) Comment on how the choice of **charge drive** versus **voltage drive** affects hysteresis in this actuator. *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) — Bimorph Working and Derivation**

A **bimorph** consists of two piezoelectric strips of equal thickness bonded together with **opposite poling directions**. When a voltage $V$ is applied:
- One strip expands longitudinally (using $d_{31}$).
- The other strip contracts longitudinally.
- The differential strain bends the structure, much like a bimetallic thermostat strip.

The curvature is established (for small deflections):

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{3 \, d_{31} \, V}{2 \, t^2}$$

The tip deflection of a cantilever of length $L$:

$$\delta = \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{L^2}{2} \cdot \frac{3 d_{31} V}{2t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{3}{2} \, d_{31} \, V \, \frac{L^2}{t^2}}$$

*(Block diagram reference: see Section 4.1 functional architecture, replace stack with bimorph.)*

---

**(b) — Numerical Computations**

**(i) Tip Deflection**

Use magnitude of $d_{31}$ (deflection direction is governed by polarity):

$$\delta = \frac{3}{2} \times 274 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times \frac{(0.02)^2}{(0.0003)^2}$$

Step by step:
- $\frac{3}{2} \times 274 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 4.11 \times 10^{-8}$
- $\frac{(0.02)^2}{(0.0003)^2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{9 \times 10^{-8}} = 4444.4$

$$\delta = 4.11 \times 10^{-8} \times 4444.4 \approx 1.827 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} = \mathbf{182.7 \; \mu m}$$

**(ii) Blocked Force at the Tip**

For a cantilever bimorph, the equivalent tip stiffness (combining both layers) is:

$$k = \frac{3 \, Y \, I}{L^3}$$

where moment of inertia for the bimorph cross-section of width $w$ and total thickness $2t$:

$$I = \frac{w (2t)^3}{12} = \frac{2 \, w \, t^3}{3}$$

So:

$$k = \frac{3 Y \cdot (2 w t^3 / 3)}{L^3} = \frac{2 Y w t^3}{L^3}$$

Plug in values: $Y = 56 \times 10^9$ Pa, $w = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $L = 0.02$ m.

- $t^3 = 2.7 \times 10^{-11}$
- $L^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6}$
- $k = \frac{2 \times 56 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.7 \times 10^{-11}}{8 \times 10^{-6}}$
- $k = \frac{2 \times 56 \times 5 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15120 \times 10^{-5}}{8 \times 10^{-6}}$
- $k = 15120 \times \frac{10^{-5}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 15120 \times 1.25 = 18900 \; \text{N/m}$

Blocked force at tip = $k \cdot \delta$:

$$F_{block, tip} = 18900 \times 1.827 \times 10^{-4} \approx \mathbf{3.45 \; N}$$

(Note: even a 20 mm bender produces several newtons — sufficient for gripping micro-objects.)

**(iii) Charge Drive vs Voltage Drive**

> **Voltage drive** is the simpler circuit, but the actuator exhibits **10–15% hysteresis** in the stroke-vs-voltage curve because polarization switching and domain wall motion are *history-dependent*. This causes open-loop position errors.
>
> **Charge drive** (current-controlled) effectively replaces $E$ with $D$ in the constitutive equations. The relation between $D$ and $S$ at constant $T$ is **linear and hysteresis-free** to first order, because $D$ is the *integral* of the true independent electrical state variable. Hence, charge drive linearises the actuator and is mandatory in **sub-micrometre precision applications** (e.g., AFM scanners, KTU problem contexts).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam!**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** The $d_{31}$ coefficient is **negative** for PZT. If you write $\delta = +\frac{3}{2} d_{31} V L^2/t^2$ without the magnitude, you may lose 1 mark. **Always state "magnitude of $d_{31}$"** when computing deflection magnitude.
> 2. **Units trap:** $d_{33} = 593$ pm/V = $593 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Forgetting the $10^{-12}$ factor is the most common mistake in 14-mark numerical answers — answer comes out **1000× too large** and is awarded **zero marks**.
> 3. **Capacitance versus resistance:** Piezo is a *capacitive* load. Your amplifier must be able to sink/source displacement current; resistive calculations are wrong.
> 4. **Hysteresis:** A "voltage controlled" piezo is *not* a linear device. If a question asks about linearity, you must mention **hysteresis** and the **charge-drive solution**.
> 5. **Frequency response:** Piezo actuators have a mechanical resonance. Above ~30% of $f_0$, the stroke collapses. Don't write "piezo is good for any frequency".
> 6. **Curie temperature:** Exceeding $T_C$ *depoles* the material permanently. Always include in a "drawbacks" answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Discovered:** 1880, Curie brothers (direct effect); 1881, Lippmann predicted converse.
- **Core physics:** Reorientation of non-centrosymmetric unit cell dipoles under applied field → strain $S = d \cdot E$.
- **Key constitutive equations (linear):**
  - $S = s^E T + d_t E$
  - $D = d T + \varepsilon^T E$
- **Stack displacement:** $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$ — increase $n$ for low-voltage high-stroke.
- **Stack blocked force:** $F_{block} = n \, d_{33} \, c_{33}^E \, V \, A/L$ — high force density (~tens of MPa).
- **Bimorph tip deflection:** $\delta = \frac{3}{2} d_{31} V L^2/t^2$ — large stroke, small force.
- **Material classes:** PZT ceramics (most common), single crystals (PMN-PT, highest $d$), polymers (PVDF, flexible).
- **Material constants to memorise (PZT-5H):** $d_{33} = 593$ pm/V, $d_{31} = -274$ pm/V, $Y_{33}^E = 56$ GPa, $\rho \approx 7500$ kg/m³, $T_C = 195$°C.
- **Configurations:** Stack ($d_{33}$), Bimorph ($d_{31}$), Shear plate ($d_{15}$), Tube, Disc.
- **Drive schemes:** Voltage drive (simple, but hysteresis), Charge drive (linear, used in precision).
- **Resonance:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{c_{33}^E/\rho}$ — sets the upper usable frequency.
- **Coupling factor:** $k_{33}^2 = d_{33}^2 c_{33}^E / \varepsilon_{33}^T \approx 0.5$ for PZT-5H (i.e., $k_{33} \approx 0.75$).
- **Microrobotic applications:** AFM scanners, micro-grippers, walking micro-robots, inchworm actuators, ultrasonic rotary motors, micro-pumps.
- **Advantages:** nm resolution, high force density, fast response, vacuum compatible, no magnetic field.
- **Disadvantages:** Small stroke (mitigated by amplification stages or multilayer design), hysteresis (~10–15%), depoling above $T_C$, brittle ceramics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Piezoelectric Motors (PEMs)** are *electromechanical actuators* that exploit the **converse piezoelectric effect** — the ability of certain crystalline materials (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT, Barium Titanate — BaTiO₃, and relaxor ferroelectrics like PMN-PT) to undergo **controlled mechanical strain** when subjected to an externally applied electric field. Unlike electromagnetic motors, PEMs generate motion **without the need for magnetic fields, commutation, or gears**, making them ideal for high-precision, high-force-density, and magnetically silent microrobotic actuation.

In the context of **KTU PERAT746 (Microrobotics)** and KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, piezoelectric motors are classified based on the operating mechanism into three principal families:

1. **Stick–Slip (Inchworm / Inertial) Motors** — quasi-static, low-frequency operation.
2. **Standing-Wave Ultrasonic Motors (SWUSM)** — high-frequency resonance.
3. **Traveling-Wave Ultrasonic Motors (TWUSM)** — rotary and linear, using superposition of two orthogonal standing waves.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Snail's Foot & The Violin Bow:**
> Imagine pushing a small glass marble across a smooth table. If you *slide* your finger across it, friction stops it. But if you *stick* your finger to it, push it forward (the **stick** phase), then quickly *slip* your finger backward (the **slip** phase) — the marble creeps forward in tiny bursts. This is the **stick–slip principle**, the foundation of inchworm-type piezoelectric motors. Conversely, a **traveling-wave motor** works like a snail's foot: a *traveling deformation wave* ripples through the stator surface, and the rotor is "pushed" by the wave like a surfboard riding an ocean swell — yet never physically touches the wave's peak.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

The following engineering metrics characterize piezoelectric motors:

- **Piezoelectric strain constant:** $d_{33}$ (typically **300–600 pC/N** for soft PZT-5H).
- **Piezoelectric voltage constant:** $g_{33}$ (typically **20–40 × 10⁻³ Vm/N**).
- **Electromechanical coupling factor:** $k_{33}$ or $k_{31}$ (typically **0.5–0.7**).
- **Mechanical Quality Factor:** $Q_m$ (typically **50–1000** for hard PZT).
- **Operating Frequency:** **20 kHz – 10 MHz** (ultrasonic regime).
- **Resonance Frequency:** depends on geometry; for thin plate, $f_r \approx 1$ MHz.
- **Power Density:** **up to 40 W/cm³** — an order of magnitude higher than EM motors.
- **Holding Torque (no current):** **non-zero**, due to static friction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three Physical Effects

> [!NOTE]
> A piezoelectric material couples **electrical** and **mechanical** domains. Three coupled effects exist:
> 1. **Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** Mechanical stress → electrical charge (used in **sensors**).
> 2. **Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** Applied electric field → mechanical strain (used in **actuators / PEMs**).
> 3. **Pyroelectric Effect:** Temperature change → surface charge (parasitic in PEMs).

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown of a Piezoelectric Motor

Every piezoelectric motor consists of four functional blocks:

| Block | Function |
|-------|---------|
| **Piezoelectric Stator** | Converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration (actuation element). |
| **Rotor / Slider / Railed Carriage** | Receives motion via friction contact with the stator. |
| **Drive Electronics** | Generates high-frequency AC voltage (e.g., 20–200 V peak, 20 kHz – 2 MHz). |
| **Preload Mechanism** | Maintains contact pressure between stator and rotor (typically a spring, $F_p \approx 5\text{–}50$ N). |

### 2.3 Mathematical Description — Constitutive Equations

The IEEE Standard on Piezoelectricity (ANSI/IEEE Std 176-1987) defines two equivalent forms. The **strain-charge form** is most relevant for *actuator design*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= s^E_{ij}\, T_j + d_{ik}\, E_k \\[4pt]
D_m &= d_{mj}\, T_j + \varepsilon^T_{mk}\, E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain tensor (unitless, m/m).
- $T_j$ = mechanical stress (N/m²).
- $E_k$ = applied electric field (V/m).
- $D_m$ = electric displacement (C/m²).
- $s^E_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant $E$ (m²/N).
- $d_{ik}$ = **piezoelectric strain constant** (m/V or C/N).
- $\varepsilon^T_{mk}$ = dielectric permittivity at constant $T$ (F/m).

For the **stress-charge form** (sensors):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= c^E_{ji}\, S_i - e_{kj}\, E_k \\[4pt]
D_m &= e_{mi}\, S_i + \varepsilon^S_{mk}\, E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes every equation you must know for the University Exam.** KTU examiners frequently test these directly or indirectly in Part B (14-mark) questions.

| \# | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Resonance frequency of a thin piezo plate (thickness mode) | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\,t_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}}$ | $t_p$ = thickness (m); $c^E_{33}$ = stiffness (N/m²); $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) |
| 2 | Mechanical strain in actuator | $S = d \cdot E = d \cdot \dfrac{V}{t_p}$ | $d$ = strain constant (m/V); $V$ = applied voltage (V) |
| 3 | Free displacement of stack actuator | $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ | $n$ = number of layers; $d_{33}$ in m/V |
| 4 | Electromechanical Coupling Factor (k) | $k^2 = \dfrac{\text{Stored mechanical energy}}{\text{Total input electrical energy}}$ | Dimensionless; $k_{33}$ for longitudinal, $k_{31}$ for transverse |
| 5 | Blocking Force of stack | $F_b = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V}{s^E_{33}}$ | $F_b$ in N |
| 6 | Linear (TWUSM) wave superposition | $w(x,t) = \xi_0\,[\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) - \cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)] = \xi_0\, \sin(kx - \omega t)$ | $k$ = wave number; $\omega$ = angular frequency |
| 7 | Stick-slip velocity (asymmetric sawtooth drive) | $\langle v \rangle = \dfrac{f_s \cdot \Delta x_{\text{step}}}{1}$ where $\Delta x_{\text{step}} = d_{33} V - \mu \, d_{33} V_{\text{back}}$ | $f_s$ = step frequency (Hz) |
| 8 | Rotational speed (TWUSM) | $n_r = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N}$ (rpm) | $f$ = drive frequency (Hz); $N$ = number of wavelengths around circumference |
| 9 | Friction-limited torque | $\tau_{\max} = \mu \, F_p \, R$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient; $F_p$ = preload (N); $R$ = mean rotor radius (m) |
| 10 | Energy density of PZT-5H | $e_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\, k_{33}^2 \cdot \varepsilon^T_{33} \cdot E^2$ | J/m³ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** NASA Mars Rover actuators use piezoelectric stepper motors for sample manipulation due to **vacuum compatibility** (no outgassing, no brushes).
- **Medical Robotics:** Piezoelectric motors drive **surgical micromanipulators** in ophthalmic surgery (e.g., hand tremor compensation at 8–12 Hz).
- **Camera Autofocus:** Canon and Nikon's silent-wave motors (SWM / USM) are ultrasonic PEMs.
- **Haptic Devices:** Piezo actuators provide localized skin deformation feedback in VR gloves.
- **Microrobotic Locomotion:** Inchworm and stick-slip walkers in pipe-inspection microrobots.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** Voice-coil actuation has been augmented by dual-stage piezoelectric actuators for sub-nanometer positioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Plate (Thickness Mode)

Consider a thin piezoelectric plate polarized along the $z$-axis (thickness direction). The 1D wave equation along the thickness is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \, \frac{\partial^2 u(z,t)}{\partial t^2} &= \frac{\partial T(z,t)}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the constitutive relation $T = c^E_{33} \cdot S$ where $S = \partial u / \partial z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} &= c^E_{33}\, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** This is the standard 1D acoustic wave equation with phase velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &= \sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a freely vibrating plate of thickness $t_p$ with stress-free boundaries, the natural boundary conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(0,t) = T(t_p,t) = 0 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right|_{0} = \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right|_{t_p} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Assume a harmonic standing-wave solution $u(z,t) = U_0 \cos(k z)\cos(\omega t)$. The derivative $\partial u/\partial z = -U_0\, k\, \sin(k z)\cos(\omega t)$. For this to vanish at $z=0$ and $z=t_p$, we require $\sin(k t_p) = 0$, i.e.:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k t_p &= \dfrac{\pi}{2},\ \dfrac{3\pi}{2},\ \dfrac{5\pi}{2},\ \dots = \dfrac{(2m-1)\pi}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $k = \omega / v_p$ into the fundamental mode ($m=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_r &= \dfrac{\pi}{2\, t_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** The **fundamental resonance frequency** is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r = \dfrac{\omega_r}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{4\, t_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favorite):** For PZT-5H with $c^E_{33} = 5.5 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $\rho = 7750$ kg/m³, $t_p = 1$ mm:
> $f_r = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 10^{-3}}\sqrt{\dfrac{5.5 \times 10^{10}}{7750}} = 250 \times 2662 \approx \mathbf{665\,kHz}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Traveling Wave Formation in TWUSM

A traveling-wave ultrasonic motor is built by **superposing two orthogonal standing waves** that are 90° apart in both **space and time**.

**Step 1:** Define the two standing waves on a circular stator (polarized mode $(p,1)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1(x,t) &= \xi_0 \, \sin(p x) \cos(\omega t) \\[4pt]
w_2(x,t) &= \xi_0 \, \cos(p x) \sin(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $p$ is the number of nodal diameters, and $\xi_0$ is the vibration amplitude.

**Step 2:** Superpose $w_1$ and $w_2$ to form the resultant wave:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(x,t) &= w_1 + w_2 = \xi_0 \left[ \sin(px)\cos(\omega t) + \cos(px)\sin(\omega t) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **trigonometric identity** $\sin(A)\cos(B) + \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A+B)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(x,t) &= \xi_0 \, \sin(px + \omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** To generate motion in the **opposite direction**, reverse the time-phase of $w_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w'(x,t) &= \xi_0 \left[ \sin(px)\cos(\omega t) - \cos(px)\sin(\omega t) \right] \\[4pt]
&= \xi_0 \, \sin(px - \omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Surface velocity at the stator (where the rotor contacts):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{surface}}(x,t) &= \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z = h/2} = \xi_0 \, \omega \cos(px - \omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** The peak velocity of the surface particle is $\xi_0 \omega$. With $\xi_0 = 2\,\mu$m and $\omega = 2\pi \times 40{,}000$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\max} &= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2\pi \times 4 \times 10^4 \approx 0.503 \,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The peak displacement of a typical PZT stator is only **1–10 µm**, but the high frequency ($\sim$40 kHz) converts this micro-vibration into a macroscopic surface velocity ($\sim$0.5 m/s). This is why TWUSM motors can spin rotors at **3000 rpm** despite sub-micron vibration amplitudes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stick–Slip Step Size (Inchworm Motor)

**Setup:** A piezo stack with free displacement $\Delta L = n\, d_{33}\, V$ pushes a slider of mass $m$ against a guide rail with friction coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Stick Phase:** The piezo stack expands slowly (quasi-statically) at velocity $v_{\text{slow}}$. The friction force $F_{\text{friction}} = \mu\, m\, g$ holds the slider fixed relative to the rail only if the stack acceleration does not exceed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{stick}} < \mu\, g
\end{aligned}
$$

The slider therefore **moves with the stack** by $\Delta x_{\text{stick}} = \Delta L$.

**Step 2 — Slip Phase:** The piezo rapidly contracts with a sawtooth waveform of period $T_{\text{back}}$. The slider, due to inertia, **remains in place** while the stack retracts.

**Step 3 — Net Displacement per Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_{\text{step}} = \Delta L_{\text{forward}} - \Delta L_{\text{backward}} \approx \Delta L \quad \text{(if backward slip is negligible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Velocity:** For a step frequency $f_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v \rangle = f_s \cdot \Delta L = f_s \cdot n\, d_{33}\, V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Resolution:** Resolution is *not* limited to $\Delta L$ but to the **minimum drive voltage step**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta x_{\min} = n \, d_{33} \, \delta V
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\delta V = 1$ mV, $n=100$, $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta x_{\min} = 100 \times 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-3} = 40 \times 10^{-12} \,\text{m} = \mathbf{40 \,pm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **picometer resolution** is why piezoelectric stick–slip stages are used in **scanning probe microscopy (SPM/AFM)**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Stick–Slip (Inchworm) | Standing Wave USM | Traveling Wave USM |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Drive Frequency | DC – 10 kHz | 20 – 100 kHz | 30 – 100 kHz |
| Displacement per step | µm – mm | Sub-µm (resonant) | Sub-µm (resonant) |
| Maximum Speed | 10 mm/s | 50 mm/s | 500 mm/s |
| Holding Force | High (preload-friction) | Medium | Medium-High |
| Resolution | Sub-nm to pm | nm | nm |
| Lifetime | > 10⁹ cycles | > 5000 hrs | > 5000 hrs |
| Vacuum Compatibility | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Complexity of Drive | Simple (HV op-amp) | Moderate (L-C tank) | Complex (2-phase drive) |
| Typical Application | AFM stages, micro-positioners | Linear stages | Camera AF, robotics joints |

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TWUSM Piezoelectric Motor


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric motor. Mention any two advantages over electromagnetic motors.**

**Model Answer:**
A piezoelectric motor operates on the **converse piezoelectric effect**, in which certain crystals (e.g., PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) undergo a controlled mechanical deformation when subjected to an externally applied electric field. A high-frequency alternating voltage (typically 20 kHz – 2 MHz) is applied to electrodes patterned on the piezoelectric stator, which then vibrates at ultrasonic frequencies. A rotor (or slider) pressed against the stator by a preload spring is driven by **friction** at the contact zone, converting micro-scale vibrations into macro-scale linear or rotary motion.

**Advantages over electromagnetic motors:**
1. **No magnetic fields** — safe in MRI environments and resistant to EMI; can operate in vacuum without outgassing or brush wear.
2. **High holding torque at zero current** — friction-locked rotor position is maintained without continuous power, giving excellent energy efficiency in standby.
3. **High power density** (up to ~40 W/cm³), an order of magnitude greater than EM motors of the same volume.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between stick–slip and ultrasonic piezoelectric motors based on operating principle, frequency range, and resolution.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Stick–Slip Motor | Ultrasonic Motor (TWUSM/SWUSM) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| Operating Principle | Asymmetric sawtooth excitation; friction alternates between *stick* (slow expansion, slider moves with stack) and *slip* (fast contraction, slider stays by inertia) | Two phase-shifted standing waves superpose into a traveling deformation wave; surface particles trace **elliptical trajectories** driving the rotor via friction |
| Frequency Range | DC – 10 kHz (quasi-static to low audio) | 20 kHz – 2 MHz (ultrasonic) |
| Resolution | Sub-nanometer (limited only by drive voltage DAC resolution, e.g., 40 pm @ 1 mV step) | Limited to nanometer scale (resonant amplitude constrains minimum step) |
| Stroke | Long (mm to m, multi-stack cascade) | Limited (~1 cm per stage) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the constitutive equations of a piezoelectric material in the **strain-charge form**, clearly defining each term. Show how these equations reduce to the case of a pure actuator (no external mechanical stress).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A multilayer PZT-5H stack actuator has $n = 200$ layers, each of thickness $t_p = 80\,\mu$m and $d_{33} = 450 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Compute:
(i) the free displacement at $V = 150$ V,
(ii) the resonance frequency, given $c^E_{33} = 5.5 \times 10^{10}$ N/m² and $\rho = 7750$ kg/m³,
(iii) the blocking force, given $s^E_{33} = 18 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The piezoelectric constitutive equations couple mechanical and electrical variables. The **strain-charge form** is preferred for actuators:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= s^E_{ij}\, T_j + d_{ik}\, E_k \\[4pt]
D_m &= d_{mj}\, T_j + \varepsilon^T_{mk}\, E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Definitions:**
- $S_i$ — mechanical strain (m/m), with $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ (Voigt notation).
- $T_j$ — mechanical stress (N/m²).
- $E_k$ — applied electric field (V/m).
- $D_m$ — electric displacement (C/m²).
- $s^E_{ij}$ — elastic compliance tensor at **constant electric field** (m²/N).
- $d_{ik}$ — piezoelectric strain coefficient tensor (m/V or C/N).
- $\varepsilon^T_{mk}$ — dielectric permittivity at **constant stress** (F/m).

**Reduction to pure actuator (free mechanical condition, $T_j = 0$):**

Setting $T_j = 0$ in equation (1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= d_{ik}\, E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the fundamental **actuator equation** — strain is directly proportional to the applied electric field. The proportionality constant is the piezoelectric strain coefficient $d$.

For a simple longitudinal configuration with field along the polarization axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_3 = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $n = 200$, $t_p = 80\,\mu$m $= 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $d_{33} = 450 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $V = 150$ V, $c^E_{33} = 5.5 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $\rho = 7750$ kg/m³, $s^E_{33} = 18 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N.

**(i) Free Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= n \, d_{33} \, V \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times (450 \times 10^{-12}) \times 150 \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times 6.75 \times 10^{-8} \\[4pt]
&= 1.35 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{13.5 \,\mu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Resonance Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= \dfrac{1}{2\, t_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}\sqrt{\dfrac{5.5 \times 10^{10}}{7750}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}\sqrt{7.097 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt]
&= 6250 \times 2664.16 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{16.65 \,\text{MHz}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Blocking Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot V}{s^E_{33}} = \dfrac{450 \times 10^{-12} \times 150}{18 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8 \times 10^{-11}} \\[4pt]
&= 3750 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for a *multilayer stack*, the effective compliance is $s^E_{33,\text{stack}} = s^E_{33}/n$, and the blocking force is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \dfrac{n \, d_{33} \, V}{s^E_{33}} = \dfrac{200 \times 450 \times 10^{-12} \times 150}{18 \times 10^{-12}} = \mathbf{750{,}000 \,\text{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is physically impossible due to material limits — meaning **the compliance value given assumes single-layer**, so the **realistic blocking force for the whole stack** is bounded by the **layer compression limit**. The intended answer (using single-layer $s^E_{33}$) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \dfrac{n \, d_{33} \, V}{s^E_{33}} = \boxed{\mathbf{750\,kN \, \text{(ideal)}}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dfrac{d_{33}\, V}{s^E_{33}/n} = 3750 \times 200 = 750{,}000\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a labeled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a traveling-wave ultrasonic motor (TWUSM)**. Explain why two phases 90° apart in time and space are necessary.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **stick–slip mechanism** in inchworm-type piezoelectric actuators. Derive the expression for the average velocity in terms of step frequency and free displacement. Why is backward motion a concern, and how is it minimized?

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction:** A TWUSM consists of:
1. A **piezoelectric stator** (typically a PZT ring or annular plate) with two sets of electrodes patterned on its top surface. The electrodes are spatially arranged such that when excited, they generate two orthogonal **standing-wave bending modes** (often the $(p,1)$ and $(p,-1)$ modes, where $p$ is the number of nodal diameters, typically 7–11).
2. A **backing ring** (metal or composite) bonded to the underside for mechanical support and Q-factor enhancement.
3. A **friction layer** (boron nitride, polymer, or ceramic) bonded to the top surface for rotor contact.
4. A **rotor** pressed against the stator by a **preload spring** (typically a Belleville disc spring).
5. A **two-phase drive electronics** generating sine voltages 90° out of phase.

**Working:**
- Electrode set A is excited with $V_A = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
- Electrode set B is excited with $V_B = V_0 \cos(\omega t)$ (90° phase shift).
- Each set generates a **standing bending wave** in the stator ring; the superposition produces a **traveling deformation wave** that rotates around the stator circumference.
- Surface particles of the stator trace **elliptical trajectories** in the radial-tangential plane.
- The tangential component of the elliptical motion drags the rotor via friction, producing rotary motion.

**Why 90° Phase and Space Shift are Necessary:**
A *single* standing wave produces only **oscillatory** (back-and-forth) surface motion — no net directional movement. By combining two standing waves **90° apart in time AND 90° apart in space** (one-quarter wavelength offset on the electrode pattern), the mathematical identity $\sin(A)\cos(B) + \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A+B)$ gives a **traveling wave** $w = \xi_0 \sin(px - \omega t)$, whose crests move unidirectionally. Without this orthogonality, the rotor would merely oscillate.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Stick–Slip Mechanism:**
The inchworm piezoelectric actuator uses a **sawtooth voltage waveform** to drive a PZT stack. One cycle consists of:

- **Stick Phase (Slow Ramp):** The voltage rises linearly at low $dV/dt$, slowly extending the stack. The slider, in contact with a friction rail, is dragged along because the inertial forces are below the friction limit ($a_{\text{stack}} < \mu g$). The slider advances by $\Delta L$.
- **Slip Phase (Fast Drop):** The voltage drops abruptly. The stack contracts rapidly. Because the inertia of the slider is high compared to the contraction impulse, the slider **does not move**; only the stack slips back underneath it.

The net displacement per cycle is $\Delta L$, but the cycle is repeated at frequency $f_s$.

**Average Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v \rangle = f_s \cdot \Delta L = f_s \cdot n \, d_{33} \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Backward Motion Concern:**
During the rapid contraction, residual vibration and finite slider inertia may cause **partial backward slip**. This is minimized by:
1. **Asymmetric sawtooth waveforms** (slow ramp up + sharp drop), keeping the ratio of rise/fall time > 100:1.
2. **Preload force tuning** — ensuring friction is sufficient during stick but allowing inertial breakaway during slip.
3. **Feedforward compensation** with a small overshoot pulse to clear residual vibration.
4. **Mechanical damping** at the slider interface.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per missing unit** in numerical problems. Always append units (Hz, m, N, V).
> 2. **Confusing $d_{33}$ vs $d_{31}$** — $d_{33}$ is along the polarization axis (longitudinal); $d_{31}$ is perpendicular (transverse). Mixing them produces a factor-of-3 to 10 error.
> 3. **Missing the 1/2 vs 1/4 factor** in the resonance formula. KTU 2024 Scheme expects the **1/4** for a freely suspended thin plate ($f_r = \frac{1}{4 t_p}\sqrt{c^E_{33}/\rho}$).
> 4. **Drawing stick–slip motion as a sine wave** instead of a *sawtooth* — examiners specifically check for the asymmetric excitation. Drawing a symmetric waveform = **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Omitting the 90° space-shift requirement** when explaining TWUSM — this is a frequently repeated part-question.
> 6. **Mixing up the form of constitutive equations** — examiners ask explicitly whether you are writing the *strain-charge* or *stress-charge* form. State it explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Piezoelectric Motors:**

- **Piezoelectric motors** use the **converse piezoelectric effect** to convert electrical energy directly into linear or rotary mechanical motion.
- **No magnetic fields, no commutation, no gears** — making them ideal for clean, vacuum, MRI, and silent-operation environments.
- Three principal types: **(i) Stick–Slip (Inchworm)**, **(ii) Standing-Wave USM**, **(iii) Traveling-Wave USM**.
- **Constitutive equations (strain-charge form):** $S_i = s^E_{ij} T_j + d_{ik} E_k$, $D_m = d_{mj} T_j + \varepsilon^T_{mk} E_k$.
- **Free displacement** of multilayer stack: $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$.
- **Resonance frequency** of thin plate (thickness mode): $f_r = \dfrac{1}{4\, t_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}}$.
- **Blocking force:** $F_b = \dfrac{d_{33} V}{s^E_{33}}$.
- **TWUSM working principle** = superposition of two orthogonal standing waves 90° apart in time and space → **traveling wave** $w = \xi_0 \sin(px - \omega t)$.
- **Surface particle motion in TWUSM** is **elliptical** — this is what couples to the rotor via friction.
- **Stick–Slip Principle:** slow stick + fast slip = net displacement per cycle = $\Delta L$.
- **Average velocity** of stick–slip: $\langle v \rangle = f_s \, \Delta L = f_s \, n \, d_{33} V$.
- **Resolution of stick–slip** is governed by DAC voltage step: $\delta x_{\min} = n \, d_{33} \, \delta V$ → **sub-nanometer achievable**.
- **Drive frequency** for ultrasonic motors lies between **20 kHz – 2 MHz** (above human hearing).
- **Preload force** is essential for friction coupling — typically **5–50 N** in TWUSM.
- **Common materials:** PZT-5H (soft, high strain), PZT-8 (hard, high power), PMN-PT (relaxor, ultra-high strain).
- **Key advantages:** high power density (~40 W/cm³), high holding torque without power, vacuum & MRI compatible, silent operation.
- **Key disadvantages:** limited stroke (cascading needed for >1 cm), friction wear, complex drive electronics, thermal drift.
- **Applications:** camera autofocus (Canon USM, Nikon SWM), AFM scanners, surgical robots, space actuators, micro-positioning stages, haptic devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys (MSMAs)** are a class of ferromagnetic smart materials that undergo large, recoverable, magnetically-induced strains (typically **1% to 10%**) through the **reorientation of martensite twin variants** under an applied external magnetic field, without any change in temperature or crystallographic phase.

The most widely studied MSMA is the **Heusler-type Ni–Mn–Ga** ternary system, although other systems such as Ni–Fe–Ga, Co–Ni–Ga, Fe–Pd, and Fe–Pt are also recognized as MSMAs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** MSMAs are *not* conventional thermally-activated Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs). The strain is produced by **magnetic-field-driven twin boundary motion**, not by martensite–austenite phase transformation at constant stress.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **book of loose pages lying on a table**:
- Each page is like a *martensite variant* (a region of crystal with a particular orientation).
- In the rest state, the pages stick out in every direction — short and long dimensions are randomly mixed.
- When you sweep a strong magnet across the book, the pages "align" with the field. The book's overall length **grows along the field direction** and shrinks perpendicular to it.
- Remove the magnet → the elastic energy of the twin boundaries tries to pull it back. Apply a small biasing mechanical stress (or another field pulse in reverse), and the book returns to its original size.

This is exactly what happens inside an MSMA: the **magnetic moment in each variant couples to the external field**, the **easy axis of magnetization rotates** in variants that are not aligned, and the **twin boundaries sweep across the crystal** to grow the variants whose easy axis lies along the field. The macroscopic result is a controlled, reversible strain.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Ni–Mn–Ga) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Saturation Magnetization, $M_s$ | **480 – 700** | kA/m |
| Curie Temperature, $T_C$ | **370 – 380** | K |
| Martensite Start, $M_s^{th}$ | **280 – 340** | K |
| Austenite Finish, $A_f$ | **310 – 360** | K |
| Maximum Magnetic Field-Induced Strain, $\varepsilon_{mag}$ | **6 – 10** | % |
| Twinning Stress, $\sigma_{tw}$ | **1 – 3** | MPa |
| Blocking Stress, $\sigma_{bl}$ | **2 – 8** | MPa |
| Operating Bandwidth | **DC – 1** | kHz |
| Energy Density | **~ 100 – 200** | kJ/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Microrobotics:** The strain of MSMAs is **~ 100× larger than piezoelectrics** (~ 0.1%) and the response time is in **milliseconds**, making them attractive for **micro-actuators, micro-pumps, micro-valves, and wireless micro-robots** that need large strokes in a tiny volume.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Parent Austenite Phase (Heusler L2₁ cubic):** Above the martensitic transformation temperature, the crystal is a highly ordered cubic structure (Fm3m). It is ferromagnetic.
2. **Cooling to Martensite Phase:** On cooling below $M_s^{th}$, the cubic austenite transforms into a lower-symmetry martensite (commonly **10M modulated** or **non-modulated tetragonal L1₀-like**). The transformation is **twin-related** — multiple crystallographic variants form self-accommodating groups to minimize macroscopic strain.
3. **Self-Accommodated State (Zero-Field):** Variants are arranged so that the net macroscopic strain is near zero. Each variant has a **magnetic easy axis** aligned with its short crystallographic axis ($c$-axis).
4. **Application of Magnetic Field $\vec{H}$:** A Zeeman energy difference $\Delta E_Z = -\mu_0 \Delta M \cdot H$ develops between variants whose easy axis is parallel to $\vec{H}$ and those where it is not.
5. **Variant Reorientation (Twin Boundary Motion):** When $\Delta E_Z$ exceeds the twinning energy barrier, **twin boundaries sweep through the crystal**, expanding the favorably-oriented variants at the cost of others.
6. **Macroscopic Strain Output:** The crystal elongates along $\vec{H}$ by an amount determined by the **crystallographic lattice distortion** $(a-c)/a$.
7. **Recovery (Biased Reset):** A small opposing **mechanical bias stress** (or a reverse magnetic pulse) restores the original variant arrangement, making the actuation fully reversible.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why twin variants?** The martensitic transformation must accommodate a large lattice distortion; doing so with multiple orientations distributes the strain evenly.
- **Why a magnetic easy axis?** Magnetocrystalline anisotropy ($K_u$) makes one crystallographic direction energetically preferred for the magnetic moments.
- **Why is the strain so large (up to 10%)?** Because the *crystal lattice itself* reorients — it is not just a magnetic-domain effect (which gives only magnetostriction, < 0.2%). The crystal symmetry change provides the bulk of the displacement.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{a-c}{a} = 1 - \dfrac{c}{a}$ | Theoretical maximum lattice strain from variant reorientation (tetragonal martensite) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E_Z = -\mu_0 \, M_s \, H \cos\theta$ | Zeeman energy per unit volume of a variant with moment at angle $\theta$ to $H$ | J/m³ |
| 3 | $\Delta E_Z = \mu_0 \, M_s \, H \left(1 - \cos\theta\right)$ | Zeeman energy difference between two twin-related variants | J/m³ |
| 4 | $E_a = K_u \sin^2\theta$ | Magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy density | J/m³ |
| 5 | $\sigma_{mag} = \mu_0 M_s H$ | Equivalent magnetic stress (work-conjugate to strain $\varepsilon$) | Pa |
| 6 | $H_{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{tw}}{\mu_0 M_s}$ | Critical magnetic field to overcome twinning stress | A/m |
| 7 | $\sigma_{block} = \mu_0 M_s H - \sigma_{tw}$ | Maximum external stress the MSMA can lift under field $H$ | Pa |
| 8 | $w = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Work density per cycle (input mechanical + magnetic energy) | J/m³ |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{w_{out}}{w_{in}}$ | Energy conversion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{res} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Mechanical resonance of MSMA element of stiffness $k$, mass $m$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cos\theta \vert$ and $\vert M_s \vert$ are written as $\vert M_s \vert \cos\theta$ — never as $\vert\cos\theta\vert$ with bare pipe characters inside the table — to keep the markdown table syntax valid.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Microrobotics

- **Wireless Micro-Actuators:** An untethered micro-robot can be actuated by an external rotating/oscillating magnetic field — no on-board power or wiring needed.
