**Operating Principle:** An AC-driven coil generates an alternating magnetic field. A conductive target (the microrobot surface or an embedded metal mark) induces **eddy currents**, which oppose the primary field and reduce the coil's effective inductance $L$.

$$L = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l}$$

The change in inductance is detected by an **LCR bridge** or **oscillator circuit**. Eddy-current sensors are immune to non-conductive materials (oil, water, tissue), making them ideal for **in-pipe microrobot inspection**.

### 2.1.3 Piezoelectric (Piezoelectric Strain) Sensors

**Operating Principle:** Certain crystals (PZT, quartz, PVDF) generate a charge $Q$ proportional to applied mechanical strain $\varepsilon$:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \Delta x$$

where $d_{33}$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (≈ 400 pC/N for PZT-5H) and $k$ is the stiffness. Piezoelectric sensors have **zero power consumption** in static mode and are used for vibration-mode microrobots (e.g., piezoelectric walking microrobots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drift Problem:** Piezoelectric sensors are inherently AC-coupled (charge leaks away). They cannot measure **static displacement** without an ultra-high-impedance charge amplifier (10¹⁴ Ω). KTU questions often probe this limitation.

### 2.1.4 Optical Sensors (PSD, Encoders, Interferometers)

Three sub-categories exist:

| Sub-type | Working Principle | Typical Resolution |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** | Lateral photoeffect; light spot position → current division | **0.1 μm** |
| **Optical Encoder** | Moiré fringe counting on a grating | **10 nm – 1 μm** |
| **Laser Interferometer** | Heterodyne detection of optical path length | **λ/1000 ≈ 0.6 nm** |

**Laser Interferometer Equation (Heterodyne):**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi}$$

where $\lambda$ is the laser wavelength (typically **632.8 nm** for He-Ne) and $\Delta \phi$ is the measured phase shift. This is the **gold standard** for calibration.

### 2.1.5 Magnetic Sensors (Hall Effect, GMR, TMR)

**Hall Effect Sensor Output Voltage:**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

A permanent magnet attached to (or embedded in) the microrobot produces field $B$, and a stationary Hall/GMR sensor detects position. **Giant Magnetoresistive (GMR)** sensors achieve **nT-level field resolution**, equivalent to sub-μm position accuracy.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|----------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plate | F (Farad) | Capacitive sensor |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ | Capacitive sensitivity | F/m | Sensor design |
| 3 | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H I B}{t}$ | Hall voltage | V | Magnetic tracking |
| 4 | $Q = d_{33} F = d_{33} k \Delta x$ | Piezoelectric charge | C | Piezo sensor |
| 5 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi}$ | Interferometric displacement | m | Optical calibration |
| 6 | $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l}$ | Solenoid inductance | H | Inductive sensor |
| 7 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz | Oscillator readout |
| 8 | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Sensor performance |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\Delta x} = \dfrac{\sigma_V}{S}$ | Position uncertainty | m | Error propagation |
| 10 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2m}{r^3}$ | Dipole field (far-field) | T | Magnetic tracking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) allows a **non-programmable scientific calculator**. Memorize the structure of equations #1, #3, #5, and #8 — these are the four most frequently asked.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

These sensor principles are the foundation of:
- **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy)** — uses optical lever + piezo for sub-nm imaging
- **Surgical microrobots (e.g., magnetically steered capsule endoscopes)** — X-ray fluoroscopy + magnetic tracking fusion
- **MEMS gyroscopes & accelerometers** — capacitive + piezoelectric in a single chip
- **Scanning probe lithography** — interferometric closed-loop positioning
- **Soft microrobotics (hydrogel crawlers)** — visual servoing with deep learning

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitive Differential Sensor Output Voltage

**Setup:** A MEMS microrobot carries a proof mass that forms two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in a half-bridge configuration. At rest, $C_1 = C_2 = C_0$. Upon displacement $\Delta x$, one gap increases and the other decreases.

**Step 1 — Define the two capacitances after displacement:**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta x} \quad \text{(gap decreases)}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + \Delta x} \quad \text{(gap increases)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the differential capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{d_0 + \Delta x} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine over a common denominator:**

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{(d_0 + \Delta x) - (d_0 - \Delta x)}{(d_0 - \Delta x)(d_0 + \Delta x)}$$

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{2\Delta x}{d_0^2 - (\Delta x)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-signal approximation** $\Delta x \ll d_0$:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta x}{d_0^2}$$

**Step 5 — Connect to bridge output voltage** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{2C_0 + \Delta C}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{d_0} \quad \text{(for small } \Delta x \text{)}$$

**Conclusion:** The output is **linearly proportional** to displacement $\Delta x$, with sensitivity $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{d_0}$. This is why differential capacitive designs dominate industrial MEMS accelerometers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Magnetic Dipole Tracking Accuracy

**Setup:** A microrobot carries a small permanent magnet of dipole moment $m$. A 2D Hall sensor array at distance $z$ detects the field components $B_x$ and $B_z$.

**Step 1 — Field components of a magnetic dipole on its axis:**

$$B_z(r) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2m}{r^3}$$

**Step 2 — Off-axis field component:**

$$B_x(r, x) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{3 m x}{(x^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Position inversion using the ratio $R = B_x / B_z$:**

$$R = \frac{3 m x}{(x^2 + z^2)^{5/2}} \cdot \frac{(x^2 + z^2)^{3/2}}{2m} = \frac{3x}{2\sqrt{x^2 + z^2}}$$

$$R^2 = \frac{9 x^2}{4(x^2 + z^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$ in terms of $z$ and $R$:**

$$4 R^2 (x^2 + z^2) = 9 x^2$$

$$x^2 (9 - 4R^2) = 4 R^2 z^2$$

$$x = \frac{2 R z}{\sqrt{9 - 4R^2}}$$

**Conclusion:** By measuring $B_x / B_z$ and knowing the standoff distance $z$, the lateral position $x$ of the microrobot can be computed analytically — no iterative solver required. This closed-form solution is **favored in real-time microrobot control loops**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution Bound from Sensor Noise

**Setup:** A capacitive sensor with sensitivity $S = \partial V / \partial x$ is read out by an amplifier with input-referred noise $V_n$.

**Step 1 — Express position noise (RMS):**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{V_n}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute capacitive sensitivity $S = V_{ex} / d_0$:**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{V_n \cdot d_0}{V_{ex}}$$

**Step 3 — Insert typical MEMS numbers:** $V_n = 100$ nV/√Hz, $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $d_0 = 2$ μm, bandwidth $BW = 1$ kHz:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-9})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{5} = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m/}\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $\sqrt{BW}$:**

$$\sigma_x = 4 \times 10^{-14} \times \sqrt{1000} \approx 1.27 \text{ pm}$$

**Conclusion:** Theoretical noise floor is **picometer-class**, but practical resolution is limited by **mechanical-thermal noise (Brownian motion)** of the proof mass, not by the electronics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal-Mechanical Noise Floor (KTU Advanced):** $\sigma_{thermal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T B}{k}}$ where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. For $k = 1$ N/m and $T = 300$ K, $\sigma_{thermal} \approx 0.13$ pm/√Hz — the absolute physical limit.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Capacitive | Inductive | Piezoelectric | Optical (PSD) | Magnetic (Hall) | Interferometric |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Resolution** | 0.1 nm | 100 nm | 1 pm (dynamic) | 0.1 μm | 10 μm | 0.6 nm |
| **Bandwidth** | 10 kHz | 1 kHz | 100 kHz | 1 MHz | 10 kHz | 10 MHz |
| **Range** | 10 μm | 1 mm | 1 μm | 10 mm | 50 mm | ∞ |
| **Contact Required** | No | No | Yes | No | No | No |
| **CMOS Compatible** | **Yes** | No | Partial | No | Yes | No |
| **Tissue Penetration** | None | None | None | None | **Yes** | None |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | **Very High** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **resolution** with **accuracy**. Resolution = smallest detectable increment; accuracy = closeness to true value (affected by nonlinearity, hysteresis, and calibration). Interferometers are high in BOTH; Hall sensors are decent in resolution but poor in absolute accuracy.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Displacement Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microrobot Physical Displacement] --> B[Primary Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Digital Filter and Calibration]
    E --> F[Pose Estimation Algorithm]
    F --> G[6 DOF Position and Orientation Output]
    G --> H[Closed Loop Controller]
    H --> I[Actuator Command Signal]
    I --> J[MEMS Actuator]
    J -.-> A
```

```mermaid
    subgraph FUSION_LAYER[Sensor Fusion Layer]

    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3 --> C1
    A4 --> B4 --> C1

```

```mermaid

        M1[Fixed Electrode Top]
        M2[Movable Proof Mass]

    VEX[Excitation AC Voltage Vex] --> CAP1[Capacitor C1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question] *(3 Marks)*
**Define a capacitive displacement sensor and state TWO advantages of using it in microrobotic applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A capacitive displacement sensor is a non-contact transducer that measures the change in electrical capacitance between two electrodes, where the relative motion of the microrobot (or attached proof mass) alters the gap $d$ or overlap area $A$ between the plates according to $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.

**Two advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **High resolution (sub-nm):** The sensitivity scales as $1/d^2$, which is naturally large at micro-scale gaps.
2. **CMOS-compatible fabrication:** Allows monolithic integration with the microrobot's on-chip signal processing circuitry, reducing parasitics and cost.
3. **Zero DC power consumption:** No steady-state current flows, making it ideal for battery-powered microrobots.
4. **Low thermal drift:** Temperature coefficients of silicon-based capacitors are very small compared to piezoresistive sensors.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question] *(3 Marks)*
**What is the role of an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) in microrobot tracking? Mention ONE limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** is a recursive Bayesian estimator that fuses data from multiple sensors (vision, magnetic, inertial, capacitive) to produce an optimal real-time estimate of the microrobot's 6-DOF pose (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw). It predicts the next state using a motion model and corrects it using noisy measurements, minimizing the mean-squared error.

**Mathematical Form (for reference):**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}))$$

where $K_k$ is the **Kalman gain**, balancing prediction and measurement.

**Limitation:** EKF assumes **Gaussian noise** and requires linearization of nonlinear models, which can cause **divergence** when the system is highly nonlinear (e.g., near magnetic singularities) or in the presence of non-Gaussian disturbances (occlusion, multipath).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A *(14 Marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **differential capacitive displacement sensor** used in MEMS microrobotics. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of displacement. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **laser interferometry** as a calibration tool for microrobot positioning. Derive the relationship between phase shift and displacement, and state its resolution limit. *(7 Marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Differential Capacitive Displacement Sensor *(7 Marks)*

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A differential capacitive sensor uses a movable proof mass that forms **two capacitors** $C_1$ and $C_2$ with two fixed electrodes. When the proof mass is at rest (mid-position), $C_1 = C_2$. Upon displacement $\Delta x$, one gap decreases (say $C_1$) and the other increases ($C_2$). The **difference $C_1 - C_2$** is proportional to $\Delta x$, providing a linear output.

The capacitors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by an AC source $V_{ex}$. A **charge amplifier** or **difference amplifier** reads the differential output. Common-mode signals (temperature drift, supply noise) cancel out, leaving only the motion-induced signal.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + \Delta x}$$

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{2 \Delta x}{d_0^2 - (\Delta x)^2}$$

Applying small-signal approximation $\Delta x \ll d_0$:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta x}{d_0^2}$$

The bridge output voltage:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta C}{2 C_0} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{d_0}$$

Thus, **sensitivity** is $S = V_{ex} / d_0$, and output is linear in $\Delta x$.

#### Part (b) — Laser Interferometry for Calibration *(7 Marks)*

**Principle (3 marks):**
A **Michelson or heterodyne laser interferometer** splits a laser beam into a **reference path** and a **measurement path**. The measurement beam reflects off a mirror attached to the microrobot. When the microrobot moves by $\Delta x$, the optical path length changes by $2 \Delta x$ (round trip), producing a phase shift $\Delta \phi$ between the two beams.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2 \Delta x}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \pi \Delta x}{\lambda}$$

Solving for displacement:

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi}$$

Or equivalently (in the simplified heterodyne form):

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi}$$

**Resolution Limit (1 mark):**
The smallest detectable phase shift in modern interferometers is $\Delta \phi_{min} \approx 0.001$ rad. With $\lambda = 632.8$ nm:

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \pi} \times 0.001 \approx 50 \text{ pm}$$

This is the **gold standard** for calibrating capacitive and piezoelectric sensors.

---

### QUESTION B *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the **magnetic dipole tracking method** for microrobots. Derive the expression for lateral position $x$ in terms of the field ratio $B_x/B_z$ and the standoff distance $z$. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare **optical, capacitive, and magnetic** displacement sensors in terms of resolution, bandwidth, range, and applicability to microrobotic systems. *(7 Marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Magnetic Dipole Tracking *(7 Marks)*

**Principle (3 marks):**
A small permanent magnet (Neodymium N52, typically 0.5–2 mm³) is embedded in the microrobot. An array of **2D Hall sensors** (or GMR sensors) mounted externally detects the magnetic field components $B_x$ and $B_z$. From these field measurements, the 3D position of the magnet (and thus the microrobot) is reconstructed analytically.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
For a magnetic dipole of moment $m$ at distance $r$:

$$B_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2m}{r^3}$$

$$B_x = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{3 m x}{(x^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}$$

Compute ratio $R = B_x / B_z$:

$$R = \frac{3x}{2\sqrt{x^2 + z^2}}$$

Square both sides:

$$4 R^2 (x^2 + z^2) = 9 x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 (9 - 4R^2) = 4 R^2 z^2$$

$$\boxed{x = \frac{2 R z}{\sqrt{9 - 4R^2}}}$$

This closed-form solution enables **real-time** tracking with update rates exceeding **1 kHz** on modern DSPs.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table *(7 Marks)*

| Parameter | Optical (PSD/Encoder) | Capacitive | Magnetic (Hall) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Resolution** | 0.1 μm | 0.1 nm | 10 μm |
| **Bandwidth** | 1 MHz | 10 kHz | 10 kHz |
| **Range** | 10 mm | 10 μm | 50 mm |
| **Tissue Penetration** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Contact Required** | No | No | No |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Bench-top lab, clean-room | On-chip MEMS feedback | In-vivo medical |

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
- **Capacitive** sensors dominate **on-chip feedback** in MEMS microrobots due to monolithic integration.
- **Optical** sensors (vision + interferometer) are the **best for calibration and lab characterization**.
- **Magnetic** sensors are the **only viable option for in-vivo, non-line-of-sight** tracking of medical microrobots.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PERAT746:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in derivations:** Writing $\Delta x = \lambda \Delta \phi / 4\pi$ without specifying that $\lambda$ is in meters will cost you 0.5 to 1 mark. Always state units in the final boxed answer.
> 2. **Confusing the signs in the differential capacitor:** $C_1$ should have a *decreasing* gap, $C_2$ an *increasing* gap. Reversing them gives a wrong sign for $V_{out}$ — examiners penalize this.
> 3. **Ignoring the small-signal approximation:** Without stating $\Delta x \ll d_0$, the linearization step is incomplete and will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "magnetic sensor" without specifying GMR, TMR, or Hall:** Always name the *specific* technology. Hall sensors are cheap but low-resolution; GMR/TMR are modern and nano-Tesla-sensitive.
> 5. **Skipping the Mermaid-style block diagram in part (a) questions:** KTU explicitly awards **1 to 2 marks for the diagram**. Even a hand-drawn version earns partial credit.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention thermal-mechanical (Brownian) noise:** When asked about resolution *limits*, the *physical* limit is set by $k_B T / k$, not by the electronics. Toppers mention this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Capacitive Sensor Formula:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, sensitivity $\propto 1/d^2$
- [x] **Differential Capacitor Output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta x / d_0$ (linear for small $\Delta x$)
- [x] **Piezoelectric Sensor:** $Q = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \Delta x$, AC-coupled only, cannot measure static DC
- [x] **Hall Effect:** $V_H = R_H I B / t$, GMR/TMR preferred for sub-μm resolution
- [x] **Laser Interferometer Resolution:** $\Delta x_{min} = \lambda \Delta \phi_{min} / 4\pi \approx 50$ pm for $\lambda = 632.8$ nm
- [x] **Magnetic Dipole Position Inversion:** $x = 2 R z / \sqrt{9 - 4R^2}$ where $R = B_x / B_z$
- [x] **LVDT Principle:** Linear Variable Differential Transformer — three-coil transformer with movable core
- [x] **Sensor Fusion Algorithm:** Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for 6-DOF pose estimation
- [x] **Thermal-Mechanical Noise Floor:** $\sigma_{thermal} = \sqrt{4 k_B T B / k}$ — the absolute physical limit
- [x] **Microrobotic Sensing Trilemma:** Resolution ↔ Bandwidth ↔ Non-invasiveness (pick two)
- [x] **CMOS Compatibility:** Capacitive and Hall sensors are CMOS-friendly; piezoelectric and optical are not
- [x] **Tissue Penetration:** Only **magnetic** and **ultrasound** sensors work through opaque tissue
- [x] **Standard Reference Values:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for PERAT746 Module 4:**
> *"Know the formula, know the diagram, know the trade-off, know the noise floor."* These four 'knows' cover >80% of the marks in any KTU question on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT746 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT746 – Microrobotics, Module 4)**, the term **"Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots"** refers to the systematic study, design evaluation, and forward-looking analysis of **MEMS/Micro/Nano-scale robotic systems** that perform dedicated, autonomous, or semi-autonomous tasks in constrained, hazardous, or biologically sensitive environments. The syllabus explicitly maps these systems to four canonical domains:

1. **Biomedical applications** (targeted drug delivery, minimally invasive surgery, in-vivo diagnostics, and cell manipulation).
2. **Industrial micro-assembly and inspection** (sub-millimeter positioning, micro-factory operations, and wafer-level quality control).
3. **Environmental monitoring and search-and-rescue** (in-pipe inspection, contaminant tracing, and post-disaster rubble penetration).
4. **Defense, surveillance, and space exploration** (reconnaissance micro-aerial vehicles, swarm intelligence, and planetary subsurface probes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Approved Wording):**
> *A microrobot is an untethered (or minimally tethered) device whose characteristic dimension lies in the range of **1 µm to 10 mm**, designed to execute sensing, actuation, and decision-making tasks at scales where classical continuum and rigid-body assumptions begin to fail and surface-area-to-volume scaling laws dominate the dynamics.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine shrinking an **ant** (an organism that already operates in a world dominated by **viscous forces and surface tension**) to the size of a **grain of sand** and giving it the ability to:
- Carry a **payload** (a drug molecule or a micro-sensor) to a precise location inside the human body,
- Communicate with **thousands of its siblings** to form a coordinated swarm,
- Harvest **energy from the surrounding fluid** (blood, air, or industrial coolant) to sustain itself,
- And then **biodegrade** after completing its task without leaving a trace.

This is the engineering reality of microrobotics. The intuition to remember is:

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lilliput Effect":** As a robot's length scale $L$ shrinks, its **volume (and mass) shrinks as $L^3$**, but its **surface (and hence viscous drag, surface friction) shrinks only as $L^2$**. This means surface effects **dominate** body effects at the micro scale — exactly the opposite of what we experience in macro-world robotics where gravity and inertia dominate.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Mandatory Bold)

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly cited in KTU university exam answers and must be memorized:

- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa·s}$
- **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** $\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{Pa·s}$
- **Density of water:** $\rho \approx 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of air:** $\rho \approx 1.204 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Characteristic micro-robot dimension:** $L \in [1\,\mu\text{m},\ 10\,\text{mm}]$
- **Reynolds number at micro-scale:** $Re \ll 1$ (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$)
- **Scaling exponents:** Mass $\propto L^3$, Force $\propto L^2$, Power $\propto L^2$, Time $\propto L^1$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Micro Robots Need a Separate Application Theory

At macro scales, an engineer designs robots by balancing **inertial forces** (Newton's 2nd law) against **gravitational loads**. At micro scales, this paradigm **collapses entirely** because of three fundamental physics shifts:

1. **Inertia becomes negligible** → viscous drag dominates (Stokes regime).
2. **Gravity becomes negligible** → electrostatic, magnetic, and surface-tension forces dominate.
3. **Fabrication shifts from assembly to monolithic MEMS lithography** → design choices are constrained by IC-foundry process rules.

This forces the application of microrobots to be **problem-first, physics-second**, rather than the reverse.

## 2.2 Application Domain Breakdown (Structured Logic Steps)

### Step 1 — Biomedical Applications
- **Targeted drug delivery:** A magnetic or bio-hybrid microrobot navigates the vasculature and releases a payload at a tumor site, reducing systemic toxicity of chemotherapy.
- **Minimally invasive surgery:** Capsule endoscopes (PillCam-class) and steerable micro-catheters perform diagnostics in the GI tract without incisions.
- **Cell manipulation:** Optical / magnetic tweezers integrated onto MEMS grippers handle single cells for IVF and gene therapy.

### Step 2 — Industrial Micro-Assembly & Inspection
- **Micro-factory cells:** Sub-mm grippers position optical fibers, MEMS components, and watch parts.
- **In-pipe and in-reactor inspection:** Untethered micro-probes crawl inside nuclear reactor headers, heat exchangers, and aircraft engine borescopes.
- **Wafer-level testing:** MEMS probe cards make thousands of contact measurements per second on semiconductor wafers.

### Step 3 — Environmental Monitoring
- **Water-quality sensing:** Micro-swimmmers detect heavy-metal ions in lakes and rivers.
- **Soil & air micro-sensors:** Distributed micro-robots monitor agricultural micro-climates and pollution plumes.
- **Search-and-rescue (SAR):** Collapse-rubble-penetrating micro-robots locate survivors in post-earthquake scenarios.

### Step 4 — Defense, Space, and Swarm Intelligence
- **Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAV):** Sub-100 g flapping-wing or quad-rotor drones for reconnaissance.
- **Planetary subsurface explorers:** NASA-inspired "hedgehog" or "wheg" robots for Mars/Europa terrain.
- **Swarm operations:** Hundreds of micro-robots performing cooperative mapping, communication relay, or synchronized actuation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces | dimensionless | Determines if Stokes ($Re\!\ll\!1$) or Navier–Stokes regime applies. |
| 2 | $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes drag on a sphere | N | Drag force on a spherical micro-robot. |
| 3 | $F_{\text{mag}} = (V \Delta \chi / \mu_0) \, (\nabla B) B$ | Magnetic actuation force | N | Used in magnetic drug delivery. |
| 4 | $\tau_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{6 \pi \mu r^3}{k_B T}$ | Brownian relaxation time | s | Sets the upper speed limit for nano-swimmers. |
| 5 | $m \propto L^3$ | Mass scales with cube of length | kg | Underlies "Lilliput Effect". |
| 6 | $F \propto L^2$ | Force scales with surface area | N | Volume-dominated forces scale with $L^3$ but surface drag scales with $L^2$ → drag dominates. |
| 7 | $P \propto L^2$ | Power scales with surface | W | Power density $\propto L^{-1}$: smaller = more efficient. |
| 8 | $t \propto L$ | Characteristic time | s | Smaller systems respond faster (key for high-bandwidth actuators). |
| 9 | $\text{Capillary length } \ell_c = \sqrt{\gamma / \rho g}$ | Length below which surface tension beats gravity | m | Critical for micro-swimmer surface adhesion. |
| 10 | $P_{\text{actuator}} = \tau \, \omega$ | Rotary actuator power | W | Used when comparing MEMS vs nano actuation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The two single-most-tested formulas are **(1) the Reynolds-number scaling** and **(2) the Stokes drag relation**. Always write the **assumption ($Re \ll 1$, incompressible, Newtonian fluid)** before plugging numbers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surgical robotics** (Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci, Verb Surgical) and **capsule endoscopy** (Medtronic PillCam) trace their lineage directly to the microrobotics research of the late 1990s.
- **In the oil & gas industry**, in-pipe micro-robots (e.g., GE Inspection Robotics' PipeGuard) inspect pipelines without shutdown, saving millions of dollars per day.
- **In agriculture**, micro-drone swarms (e.g., XAG, DJI Agras) perform targeted pesticide spraying, reducing chemical use by up to 30–60 %.
- **In planetary science**, the **NASA Hedgehog** and **ESA Mole** concepts are direct microrobot designs for low-gravity surfaces where conventional rovers cannot operate.

## 2.5 Future Prospects — The 2025–2035 Roadmap

| Short (≤ 5 yr) | Bio-hybrid swimmers (bacteria + synthetic cargo) | Targeted tumor therapy in clinical trials. |

| Long (> 10 yr) | In-vivo nano-surgical platforms | Single-cell surgery inside the human body. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **fully worked analytical derivations** of the most exam-relevant formulae from Section 2.3. **No step is skipped**; every transition is justified in plain English below the equation block.

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaling Laws for Force, Power, and Time

### Step 1 — Start from a general "characteristic quantity" $Q$

A micro-robot's **mass $m$** is proportional to its **volume**, hence to the **cube of its characteristic length** $L$. This is because volume is a 3-D geometric measure.

$$m \propto L^3$$

### Step 2 — Recognize that the cross-sectional area (the surface facing the flow or the ground) scales as $L^2$

When a force is transmitted through a surface — e.g., aerodynamic drag, electrostatic attraction, magnetic pull — the magnitude of that force is proportional to the **cross-sectional area**, not the volume.

$$F \propto L^2$$

### Step 3 — Derive power from force × velocity

Velocity has units of $\text{m/s}$, but in the micro regime the characteristic velocity also scales linearly with $L$ (because both the actuation force and the inertia shrink together). Therefore:

$$P = F \cdot v \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P \propto L^2 \cdot L^1 = L^2$$

This is a famous result: **power scales as the surface, not as the volume.**

### Step 4 — Derive the characteristic response time

From Newton's second law applied to a single mode of motion, the natural frequency $\omega$ of a mechanical oscillator satisfies:

$$\omega \propto \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{L}{L^3}} = L^{-1}$$

Since $t \propto 1/\omega$, we obtain the **linear time-scaling law**:

$$t \propto L$$

Smaller robots **respond proportionally faster** — this is the physical basis of the legendary speed of fly wings and MEMS gyroscopes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stokes Drag on a Spherical Microrobot

Consider a rigid sphere of radius $r$ moving with steady velocity $v$ through an unbounded, incompressible Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu$, in the regime $Re \ll 1$ (inertial terms negligible). Solving the **Navier–Stokes equation** in this limit (a long derivation, the result of which is memorizable for KTU):

$$\nabla p = \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u}, \qquad \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$$

with the no-slip boundary condition $\mathbf{u}=0$ at the sphere surface and $\mathbf{u}=v\,\hat{z}$ at infinity, the drag force is found to be:

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

This is **Stokes' Law** (1851). It is the single most important formula for KTU microrobotics problems because it tells the student:

- The drag is **linear in velocity** (not quadratic, as in the high-$Re$ regime).
- The drag is **linear in $r$**, so halving the robot halves the drag.
- The drag is **independent of fluid density** in the low-$Re$ limit — a counter-intuitive result that examiners love to test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity envelope:** Stokes' Law holds for $Re < 1$ and for an **unbounded** fluid. Near walls, a correction factor must be applied (the **Faxén correction**), and at $Re > 1$ the **Oseen correction** is required. In KTU answers, always state these envelopes explicitly.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Microrobot Swimming in Blood

A spherical magnetic microrobot of radius $r = 250 \ \mu\text{m}$ is propelled through human blood (assume $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa·s}$, $\rho = 1060 \ \text{kg/m}^3$) at $v = 1 \ \text{mm/s}$. Compute the drag force and the Reynolds number.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the drag force using Stokes' Law:**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v = 6 \pi \times (3.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

Group the numerical factors:

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times 3.5 \times 250 \times 10^{-3-6-3} = 6 \pi \times 3.5 \times 250 \times 10^{-12}$$

Compute the scalar product:

$$6 \pi \times 3.5 = 21 \pi \approx 65.97$$

$$65.97 \times 250 = 16492.5$$

So:

$$F_d \approx 1.65 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-12} \ \text{N} = 1.65 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{N} = 16.5 \ \text{nN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 250 \times 10^{-6})}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

(The characteristic length $L = 2r = 500 \ \mu\text{m}$ is the diameter, per standard convention.)

$$Re = \frac{1060 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 10^{-6}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1060 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{5.30 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1514$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Stokes assumption:**

$$Re \approx 0.15 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

The low-Reynolds-number assumption holds; Stokes' Law is valid.

**Step 4 — Interpret physically:**

A drag force of $16.5$ nN is **easily produced** by a small permanent magnet (e.g., a 1 mm³ NdFeB cube in a 100 mT gradient generates ~µN-level forces), confirming the feasibility of magnetic drug-delivery microrobots in human blood.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Application ↔ Dominant Physics ↔ Enabling Technology

| Application | Dominant Physics | Enabling Technology | Typical $Re$ | Typical $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vascular drug delivery | Stokes drag + magnetic | Magnetic actuation | $10^{-3}$–$10^{-1}$ | $10$–$500 \ \mu\text{m}$ |
| GI capsule endoscopy | Inertia + low-friction rolling | MEMS + wireless RF | $10^{2}$–$10^{4}$ | $10$ mm |
| Micro-assembly gripper | Surface adhesion + electrostatic | MEMS electrostatic | n/a (quasi-static) | $0.1$–$5$ mm |
| Pipeline inspection | Rolling friction + magnetic | Magnetic caterpillar | $10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ | $10$–$50$ mm |
| Environmental swimmers | Diffusion + chemotaxis | Bio-hybrid bacteria | $10^{-6}$–$10^{-3}$ | $1$–$10 \ \mu\text{m}$ |
| Micro Aerial Vehicle (MAV) | Aero + flapping-wing | Smart-material actuators | $10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ | $50$–$100$ mm wingspan |
| Planetary hedgehog | Low-gravity + friction | Spiked inertia-driven | n/a | $100$–$300$ mm |
| Swarm mapping | Communication latency | BLE / UWB + IMU | varies | $10$–$50$ mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Observation:** Students often write "microrobots work in the low-Reynolds-number regime" as a blanket statement. The matrix above proves this is **true for swimmers** but **false for capsule endoscopes and MAVs** — the latter operate in the inertial regime. Always qualify the statement.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** write "microrobots work in low Reynolds number" as a blanket statement — it is true only for *fluid-borne* microrobots. Capsule endoscopes, MAVs, and MARS-rover-class microrobots are in the **high-$Re$ / inertial regime**.
> 2. Always **state the validity envelope** of Stokes' Law ($Re < 1$, unbounded fluid) before using it. Marks are lost if the envelope is omitted.
> 3. In the application-section answer, **do not** give a generic list. For every application, the examiner expects: **(a) the problem, (b) the dominant physics, (c) the enabling technology, (d) one quantitative metric.** Skipping (d) typically costs 1 mark per sub-part.
> 4. Numerical answers must include **units** in every line. A numerically correct answer with no unit = **0 marks** in KTU board valuation.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**List any three major biomedical applications of microrobots and state the dominant actuation principle used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Application | Dominant Actuation Principle |
|---|---|
| Targeted drug delivery in vasculature | **External magnetic field gradient** (since the body is opaque to RF and tethered wires are unacceptable) |
| Capsule endoscopy of the GI tract | **Peristaltic motion of the gut** + on-board imaging + wireless RF transmission (no active actuator needed) |
| Single-cell manipulation in IVF | **Optical / magnetic tweezers** integrated with MEMS micro-grippers |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**State the scaling laws for mass, force, and time for a micro-robot and explain their physical significance in a single sentence each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Mass** $m \propto L^3$ — because mass is a volumetric quantity.
- **Force** $F \propto L^2$ — because most actuation forces are transmitted through a surface (cross-section).
- **Time** $t \propto L$ — because natural frequency $\omega \propto \sqrt{k/m} \propto L^{-1}$.

**Physical significance:** As $L$ decreases, the **mass shrinks faster than the force**, so **surface / viscous effects dominate over inertial / gravitational effects** — this is the foundational physics of microrobotics.

The surrounding blood has viscosity $\mu = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa·s}$ and density $\rho = 1060 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. The robot is observed to swim at a terminal velocity of $v = 800 \ \mu\text{m/s}$ under a magnetic gradient force. Calculate the **Stokes drag force** on the robot and the **Reynolds number**. Verify whether the low-$Re$ assumption is valid.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **four major application domains** of microrobots (biomedical, industrial, environmental, and defense/space). For each domain, give one specific real-world example, the dominant physics, and the characteristic length scale.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **scaling laws** $m \propto L^3$, $F \propto L^2$, and $t \propto L$ for a microrobot, and explain how these scaling laws justify the design choice of using **magnetic actuation** (rather than electrical or mechanical) at the micro-scale.

---

## Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
External MRI-style gradient coil
        │
        ▼
Magnetic field B(x, y, z, t) ──► Magnetic microrobot (NdFeB core + drug coating)
                                          │
                                          ▼
                              Steering algorithm (closed-loop)
                                          │
                                          ▼
                              Image feedback (MRI / Ultrasound)
                                          │
                                          ▼
                              Drug release at target site
```

**Subsystem-wise description (1 mark each, 7 total):**

1. **External magnetic gradient source** — superconducting or permanent-magnet array; produces a spatially varying $B$ field.
2. **Magnetic microrobot body** — typically a NdFeB microparticle (50–500 µm) coated with a biocompatible polymer (PEG, chitosan) loaded with a drug (e.g., doxorubicin).
3. **Steering algorithm** — closed-loop controller that drives the gradient coil so that the robot follows a planned trajectory on a 2-D vascular roadmap.
4. **Imaging feedback** — real-time MRI or ultrasound, providing positional updates to the controller.
5. **Drug release mechanism** — pH-sensitive or temperature-sensitive polymer coating that releases the payload when the local environment matches the tumor signature.
6. **Power source** — *none on-board*; power is supplied externally via the magnetic field (a unique advantage over battery-powered micro-robots).
7. **Biodegradability** — the polymer and magnetic core are engineered to dissolve in weeks/months after task completion.

---

## Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the Stokes drag force (3 marks):**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times (4.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (800 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times 4.0 \times 200 \times 800 \times 10^{-3-6-6}$$

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times 4.0 \times 1.6 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-15}$$

$$F_d = 6 \pi \times 6.4 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$F_d \approx 1.206 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{N} = 12.06 \ \text{nN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

(Characteristic length $L = 2r = 400 \ \mu\text{m}$.)

$$Re = \frac{1060 \times 800 \times 400 \times 10^{-12}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.392 \times 10^{-4}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0848$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Stokes assumption (1 mark):**

$$Re \approx 0.085 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

The low-Reynolds-number assumption is valid; Stokes' Law is correctly applied.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):**

A drag force of **12 nN** is well within the achievable range of MRI-gradient magnetic actuation forces (typical 10–1000 nN), confirming the practical feasibility of vascular drug delivery at this scale.

---

| Domain | Real-world Example | Dominant Physics | Characteristic Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biomedical** | PillCam SB3 capsule endoscope (Medtronic) | Inertial motion + wireless RF communication | $\sim 11 \ \text{mm} \times 26 \ \text{mm}$ |
| **Industrial** | In-pipe inspection robot (GE PipeGuard) | Magnetic adhesion + rolling friction | $\sim 20$–$50 \ \text{mm}$ |

## Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Mass scaling (2 marks):**

Mass is a volumetric quantity, so for a body of characteristic linear dimension $L$:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot c L^3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m \propto L^3$$

**Step 2 — Force scaling (2 marks):**

Forces transmitted through a surface — drag, electrostatic, magnetic — are proportional to the cross-sectional area $A \propto L^2$:

$$F \propto L^2$$

**Step 3 — Time scaling (2 marks):**

From $m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = 0$, the natural frequency scales as:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{L}{L^3}} = L^{-1}$$

The characteristic time $t \propto 1/\omega_n$:

$$t \propto L$$

**Step 4 — Why this justifies magnetic actuation (1 mark):**

Because $F \propto L^2$ shrinks faster than $m \propto L^3$, **gravitational and inertial forces become negligible**, but **volume-distributed forces** (which scale as $L^3$ — e.g., magnetic force on a magnetized volume) **dominate**. Hence, magnetic actuation — which acts on the **volume** of a magnetized particle — is the most efficient actuation principle at the micro-scale, whereas cable-driven or battery-driven actuation becomes impossible (cables are too stiff, batteries are too large and heavy).

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU):**

- [ ] A microrobot's **characteristic length** lies between **1 µm and 10 mm**.
- [ ] At the micro-scale, **surface forces dominate over body forces** (the Lilliput Effect).
- [ ] The three foundational scaling laws are: **$m \propto L^3$, $F \propto L^2$, $t \propto L$**.
- [ ] **Stokes' drag** for a sphere: $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ — valid only for $Re \ll 1$ in an unbounded Newtonian fluid.
- [ ] **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v L / \mu$; swimmers operate at $Re \approx 10^{-6}$–$10^{-1}$, capsule endoscopes and MAVs at $Re \approx 10^{2}$–$10^{5}$.
- [ ] **Brownian motion** sets the *lower* size limit for deterministic microrobot control; viscosity sets the *upper* speed limit.
- [ ] The **four canonical application domains** are: biomedical, industrial, environmental, and defense/space.
- [ ] **Magnetic actuation** is preferred for in-vivo applications because: (a) the field penetrates tissue, (b) no on-board power is needed, and (c) the actuation force scales with the *volume* of the magnetized core ($L^3$), which dominates at the micro-scale.
- [ ] **Future prospects** include DNA-origami fabrication, bio-hybrid swimmers, AI-driven swarms, and self-healing materials with embedded micro-bots.
- [ ] Always write the **validity envelope** of any formula you use; always include **units** in every numerical answer.
- [ ] The **capillary length** $\ell_c = \sqrt{\gamma / \rho g} \approx 2.7 \ \text{mm}$ for water — below this scale, surface tension beats gravity.
- [ ] Power scales as $L^2$, not $L^3$ — so micro-robots have **higher power density** than macro-robots.
- [ ] Brownian relaxation time $\tau_{\text{diff}} = 6 \pi \mu r^3 / k_B T$ is the fundamental limit on how fast a nano-swimmer can move deterministically.
